topics.title: Definition of Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition of Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scheduling** is the process of **allocating limited resources (machines, labor, tools, time)** to a set of tasks or jobs over a defined time horizon, with the goal of **optimizing one or more performance objectives** (such as minimizing makespan, tardiness, or flow time) while satisfying a set of operational constraints.

In the context of **Industrial Scheduling (PEIET633)**, scheduling refers specifically to the **short-term decision-making process** that determines *what* will be done, *by which* machine or resource, *in what sequence*, and *at what time*, after the aggregate (long-term) and intermediate planning stages have been finalized.

The general scheduling problem can be expressed mathematically as:

$$
\mathcal{S} = \{J, M, T, C, \Pi\}
$$

Where:
- $J = \{J_1, J_2, \dots, J_n\}$ is the set of **Jobs** (tasks)
- $M = \{M_1, M_2, \dots, M_m\}$ is the set of **Machines** (resources)
- $T$ is the **Time horizon** over which allocation occurs
- $C$ represents the **Constraints** (precedence, capacity, availability)
- $\Pi$ is the **Performance objective** to be optimized (e.g., $C_{max}$, $\sum T_j$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The university explicitly treats scheduling as a *decision-making subsystem* within **Production Planning and Control (PPC)** hierarchy, sitting below **Aggregate Planning** and **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Train Station Manager:**
> Imagine you are the manager of a busy railway station with only **3 platforms** and **10 trains** that must depart today. Each train needs a platform, a specific departure time, and a certain dwell time. Passengers are getting restless, trains have deadlines, and platforms cannot host two trains at once. You must decide **which train goes to which platform, in what order, and when.**
> - The **trains** = Jobs ($J$)
> - The **platforms** = Machines ($M$)
> - The **departure deadlines** = Due dates ($d_j$)
> - Your goal of *"minimizing total passenger delay"* = Objective function ($\Pi$)
>
> **Scheduling is exactly this ŌĆö but on the factory floor.**

## 1.3 Why Scheduling Matters in Industry

- **Directly impacts** on-time delivery, machine utilization, and inventory cost.
- A **poor schedule** can increase makespan by **20ŌĆō40%** even with identical resources.
- **Scheduling decisions** affect shop-floor KPIs such as **Throughput**, **Work-In-Progress (WIP)**, and **Tardiness**.

---

**Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Terminology):**

- **Makespan ($C_{max}$)** ŌĆö Total time to complete all jobs (in **hours/days**).
- **Flow Time ($F_j$)** ŌĆö Time a job spends in the system (in **days**).
- **Tardiness ($T_j$)** ŌĆö $\max(0, C_j - d_j)$ measured in **days**.
- **Lateness ($L_j$)** ŌĆö $C_j - d_j$ (can be negative).
- **Machine Utilization ($U_k$)** ŌĆö Fraction of busy time = $\frac{\sum p_{jk}}{C_{max}}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Scheduling Concept

Scheduling, as defined in Module 1 of PEIET633, can be broken down into **four fundamental components**. Every scheduling problem, regardless of industry, is fully described by these four pillars:

1. **The Set of Jobs ($J$)** ŌĆö *What needs to be done?*
   - Each job $J_j$ has an associated processing requirement, often represented as a vector of processing times $(p_{j1}, p_{j2}, \dots, p_{jm})$ on each machine.
   - Jobs may have **due dates** ($d_j$), **weights** ($w_j$), and **release dates** ($r_j$).

2. **The Set of Machines/Resources ($M$)** ŌĆö *What are we working with?*
   - Machines can be **identical**, **uniform** (different speeds), or **unrelated** (different capability matrices).
   - Each machine $M_k$ can process **at most one job at a time** (binary capacity constraint).

3. **The Constraints ($C$)** ŌĆö *What rules must we obey?*
   - **Precedence constraints:** $J_i \rightarrow J_j$ (job $i$ must finish before $j$ starts).
   - **Resource constraints:** A machine cannot process two jobs simultaneously.
   - **No-pass / No-wait constraints** (in flow lines).
   - **Pre-emption allowed vs. non-pre-emptive.**

4. **The Objective ($\Pi$)** ŌĆö *What are we trying to achieve?*
   - Examples: minimize $C_{max}$, minimize $\sum w_j T_j$, maximize throughput, minimize setup time, balance machine loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** In every scheduling problem the examiner expects you to **first identify these four pillars** before stating the solution. Skipping this step costs you **2 marks** consistently.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Scheduling

The **why** of scheduling is rooted in the **scarcity of resources** in manufacturing environments:

- A factory has **finite machines, finite operators, and finite time**.
- Multiple jobs compete for these resources simultaneously.
- Without a schedule, conflicts arise ŌåÆ **idle time**, **bottlenecks**, and **missed deliveries**.

The **how** is procedural:

$$
\text{Plan (Aggregate)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Schedule (Detailed)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Dispatch (Real-time)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Execute}
$$

Scheduling sits at the **second stage**, translating strategic plans into **machine-level, hour-by-hour operational decisions**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $J_j$ | $j$-th job in the system | ŌĆö |
| $M_k$ | $k$-th machine in the system | ŌĆö |
| $p_{jk}$ | Processing time of job $j$ on machine $k$ | **hours / minutes** |
| $r_j$ | Release date (earliest start) of job $j$ | **days** |
| $d_j$ | Due date of job $j$ | **days** |
| $C_j$ | Completion time of job $j$ | **days** |
| $F_j$ | Flow time of job $j = C_j - r_j$ | **days** |
| $T_j$ | Tardiness of job $j = \max(0,\; C_j - d_j)$ | **days** |
| $L_j$ | Lateness of job $j = C_j - d_j$ | **days** |
| $C_{max}$ | Makespan = $\max_j(C_j)$ | **days** |
| $U_k$ | Utilization of machine $k$ | **dimensionless (0ŌĆō1)** |
| $\bar{F}$ | Mean flow time = $\frac{1}{n}\sum F_j$ | **days** |
| $\bar{T}$ | Mean tardiness = $\frac{1}{n}\sum T_j$ | **days** |
| $n$ | Total number of jobs | ŌĆö |
| $m$ | Total number of machines | ŌĆö |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, the $3$-field notation $\alpha \vert \beta \vert \gamma$ (Graham's notation) is the **standard** way to classify any scheduling problem. The examiner will expect familiarity with this notation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Scheduling theory is the backbone of:

- **Automotive assembly lines** ŌĆö sequencing mixed models on a single line.
- **Semiconductor fabrication (fabs)** ŌĆö where tool utilization exceeds **$90\%$** due to intelligent scheduling.
- **Hospital operating theatres** ŌĆö scheduling surgeries across specialty rooms.
- **Cloud computing data centers** ŌĆö job scheduling across servers.
- **Project management** ŌĆö critical path method (CPM) and PERT rely on scheduling principles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Definition (Expanded Mathematical Form)

A scheduling problem is a **combinatorial optimization problem** that can be stated as follows:

$$
\text{Given: } (J, M, p_{jk}, r_j, d_j, C, \Pi)
$$

$$
\text{Find: A feasible assignment } \sigma : J \times M \rightarrow T
$$

$$
\text{That minimizes (or maximizes) the objective } \Pi(\sigma)
$$

Where a **feasible assignment** $\sigma$ satisfies:

- Each job $J_j$ is processed on each required machine exactly once.
- No machine processes more than one job at any instant.
- All precedence, release, and due-date constraints are honored.

## 3.2 Derivation of Fundamental Performance Metrics

Below is the exhaustive derivation of the core scheduling metrics that the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists under Module 1.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Completion Time ($C_j$)

The completion time of job $j$ on machine $k$ is the sum of its own processing time plus any waiting time on that machine.

$$
C_{jk} \;=\; S_{jk} \;+\; p_{jk}
$$

Where $S_{jk}$ is the **start time** of job $j$ on machine $k$. For a single-machine environment:

$$
C_j \;=\; r_j \;+\; p_j \;+\; \text{waiting time}
$$

In the simple case where $r_j = 0$ and no waiting occurs:

$$
C_j \;=\; \sum_{i \in \text{predecessors of } j} p_i \;+\; p_j
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Flow Time ($F_j$)

Flow time is the total time a job spends inside the system (from arrival to departure).

$$
F_j \;=\; C_j \;-\; r_j
$$

**Derivation logic:** If a job arrives at the system at time $r_j$ and completes at time $C_j$, the difference $C_j - r_j$ is the total time it has occupied shop-floor resources ŌĆö i.e., its **flow time**.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Lateness ($L_j$)

Lateness measures how late (or early) a job finishes relative to its due date. Unlike tardiness, lateness can be negative.

$$
L_j \;=\; C_j \;-\; d_j
$$

**Sign convention:** $L_j < 0$ ŌåÆ job is **early**; $L_j = 0$ ŌåÆ job is **on time**; $L_j > 0$ ŌåÆ job is **late**.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Tardiness ($T_j$)

Tardiness is the *positive* part of lateness. A job finished early contributes **zero** tardiness.

$$
T_j \;=\; \max\bigl(0,\; L_j \bigr) \;=\; \max\bigl(0,\; C_j - d_j \bigr)
$$

**Derivation logic:** Define $x = C_j - d_j$. If $x \leq 0$, the job is not late, so $T_j = 0$. If $x > 0$, the job is late by exactly $x$ units, so $T_j = x$. Combining via the $\max(\cdot, 0)$ operator yields the formula above.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Makespan ($C_{max}$)

Makespan is the time at which the **last job** leaves the system.

$$
C_{max} \;=\; \max_{j \in J}\bigl(C_j\bigr)
$$

**Derivation logic:** Out of all $n$ completion times, the largest one dictates when the entire schedule "ends." This is the makespan.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Machine Utilization ($U_k$)

$$
U_k \;=\; \frac{\text{Total processing time on machine } k}{C_{max}}
$$

$$
U_k \;=\; \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{jk}}{C_{max}}
$$

**Logic:** Utilization is the **fraction of the makespan** during which machine $k$ was actually busy. A perfectly loaded machine has $U_k = 1$.

### Step 7 ŌĆö Mean Flow Time and Mean Tardiness

$$
\bar{F} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j
$$

$$
\bar{T} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} T_j
$$

These are aggregate KPIs used to evaluate a schedule's overall quality.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö Identifying a Scheduling Problem

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix to "classify" any scheduling question before solving it.** The KTU examiner rewards students who can correctly classify a problem in Step 1.

| Question to Ask | Possible Answers | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| How many machines? | 1, 2, ŌĆ”, $m$ | Single-machine vs. parallel-machine vs. multi-stage |
| What is the routing? | All jobs same route / different / no fixed route | Flow shop / Job shop / Open shop |
| Are jobs pre-emptible? | Yes / No | Non-pre-emptive is the default in KTU problems |
| Are there precedence relations? | Yes / No | Affects feasible sequence set |
| What is the objective? | $C_{max}$, $\sum T_j$, $\sum w_j C_j$, etc. | Drives the choice of algorithm |
| Are due dates present? | Yes / No | Distinguishes "static" vs. "due-date" scheduling |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Scheduling Decision Block Diagram

The figure below shows the **end-to-end architecture** of how scheduling fits into the broader manufacturing decision hierarchy, and what inputs feed into it.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Aggregate Planning] --> B[Master Production Schedule]
    B --> C[Material Requirements Planning]
    C --> D[Scheduling PEIET633 Core]
    D --> E[Shop Floor Dispatch]
    E --> F[Real Time Execution]
    D --> G[Performance Feedback KPI]
    G --> A
```

## 4.2 Internal Block Architecture of the Scheduling Function

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Inputs Jobs and Machines] --> nodeB[Constraints Module]
    nodeC[Processing Times pjk] --> nodeB
    nodeD[Due Dates dj] --> nodeE[Objective Function Selector]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Scheduler Engine]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Feasible Sequence Sigma]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Gantt Chart Output]
    nodeG --> nodeI[KPI Computation]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Performance Metrics Cmax T sum etc]
```

## 4.3 Mapping the Four Pillars of Scheduling

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[What to do Jobs J] --> SCH[Scheduling Decision Sigma]
    P2[With what Resources M] --> SCH
    P3[Under what rules Constraints C] --> SCH
    P4[To achieve what Objective Pi] --> SCH
    SCH --> OUT[Feasible Optimal Schedule]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every scheduling problem in PEIET633 can be represented as a transformation from the four inputs $\{J, M, C, \Pi\}$ into a single **feasible schedule** $\sigma$, which is then visualized as a **Gantt chart** and evaluated by KPIs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024, Model Question, Set A]**

**Q1. Define scheduling in the context of industrial production. Mention the four key elements that characterize any scheduling problem.**

**Model Answer:**

*Scheduling is the process of allocating limited resources (machines, labor) to a set of jobs over a defined time horizon to optimize one or more performance objectives (e.g., makespan, tardiness) while respecting operational constraints.*

*The four key elements are:*
1. *The set of **jobs** $J$ to be processed.*
2. *The set of **machines/resources** $M$.*
3. *The **constraints** $C$ (precedence, capacity, release dates).*
4. *The **performance objective** $\Pi$ to be optimized.*

*(3 marks ŌĆö Definition 1.5M, Four elements 1.5M)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Question, Set B]**

**Q2. Differentiate between *lateness* and *tardiness* of a job with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

*Lateness ($L_j = C_j - d_j$) can be **positive, zero, or negative**, capturing whether a job is late, on time, or early.*

*Tardiness ($T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$) is the **positive part only**; early jobs have $T_j = 0$.*

*Example: If a job is due on day 10 and completes on day 7, then $L_j = -3$ but $T_j = 0$. If it completes on day 12, $L_j = +2$ and $T_j = +2$.*

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Question Bank (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 | Module 1, Module Test]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four key elements** that completely define any scheduling problem in an industrial environment. Use mathematical notation and provide one real-world industry example for each element. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A small job shop has the following data for **3 jobs** on **2 machines** in a flow-shop configuration:

$$
p_{j1} = (4,\; 6,\; 2), \quad p_{j2} = (3,\; 5,\; 8)
$$

All jobs are released at time $r_j = 0$. Due dates are $d_1 = 12$, $d_2 = 14$, $d_3 = 10$. Compute, for **each job**, the values of $C_j$, $F_j$, $L_j$, and $T_j$ under the sequence $J_1 \rightarrow J_3 \rightarrow J_2$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)**

The four elements:

1. **Set of Jobs $J$** ŌĆö defines the work to be done. *Industrial example:* In an automotive plant, $J = \{$Hatchback, Sedan, SUV$\}$ represents the three car models scheduled on the paint line.

2. **Set of Machines $M$** ŌĆö defines available resources. *Industrial example:* In a CNC machine shop, $M = \{M_1, M_2, M_3\}$ are the three CNC mills.

3. **Constraints $C$** ŌĆö restrictions on feasible schedules. *Industrial example:* In a semiconductor fab, a photolithography tool cannot process two wafers simultaneously.

4. **Objective $\Pi$** ŌĆö what we optimize. *Industrial example:* An e-commerce warehouse minimizes $\sum T_j$ (total tardiness) of shipments.

*(7 marks ŌĆö 1.5M per element + 1M for the industry example overlay)*

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)**

Using the sequence $J_1 \rightarrow J_3 \rightarrow J_2$ in a 2-machine flow shop:

**Job 1 ($J_1$):**
- $C_{1,M_1} = p_{11} = 4$
- $C_{1,M_2} = C_{1,M_1} + p_{12} = 4 + 3 = 7$
- $C_1 = 7$, $F_1 = 7 - 0 = 7$, $L_1 = 7 - 12 = -5$, $T_1 = 0$

**Job 3 ($J_3$):** (executed after $J_1$)
- $C_{3,M_1} = C_{1,M_1} + p_{31} = 4 + 2 = 6$
- $C_{3,M_2} = \max(C_{3,M_1},\; C_{1,M_2}) + p_{32} = \max(6, 7) + 8 = 15$
- $C_3 = 15$, $F_3 = 15$, $L_3 = 15 - 10 = +5$, $T_3 = 5$

**Job 2 ($J_2$):**
- $C_{2,M_1} = C_{3,M_1} + p_{21} = 6 + 6 = 12$
- $C_{2,M_2} = \max(C_{2,M_1},\; C_{3,M_2}) + p_{22} = \max(12, 15) + 5 = 20$
- $C_2 = 20$, $F_2 = 20$, $L_2 = 20 - 14 = +6$, $T_2 = 6$

**Summary Table:**

| Job | $C_j$ | $F_j$ | $L_j$ | $T_j$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| $J_1$ | 7 | 7 | $-5$ | $0$ |
| $J_3$ | 15 | 15 | $+5$ | $5$ |
| $J_2$ | 20 | 20 | $+6$ | $6$ |

**Aggregate KPIs:**
- $C_{max} = 20$
- $\bar{F} = (7+15+20)/3 = 14$
- $\bar{T} = (0+5+6)/3 = 3.67$

*(7 marks ŌĆö correct application of flow-shop timing logic and metric formulas)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative choice)*

**(a)** "Scheduling is fundamentally a decision-making process." Discuss this statement with reference to the **hierarchy of production planning decisions**. Place scheduling at the correct level and justify its position. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With neat sketches, explain the difference between **flow shop**, **job shop**, and **open shop** scheduling configurations. Use processing-time matrices $p_{jk}$ to illustrate each. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)**

The Production Planning hierarchy is:

1. **Long-term (Strategic):** Aggregate planning ŌĆö capacity, workforce levels.
2. **Medium-term (Tactical):** Master Production Schedule (MPS), MRP.
3. **Short-term (Operational):** **Scheduling** ŌĆö what, where, when.
4. **Real-time:** Dispatch and execution.

Scheduling is a **short-term operational decision**. Justification: Aggregate plans set *what* and *how much*; scheduling determines *which specific job* goes on *which specific machine* in *which specific hour*. It translates strategy into shop-floor reality.

*(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)**

- **Flow shop:** All jobs follow the **same route** $M_1 \rightarrow M_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow M_m$.
  Example $p_{jk}$:
  $$
  \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 5 \\ 6 & 2 \\ 4 & 7 \end{bmatrix}
  $$
  Here every job visits $M_1$ then $M_2$.

- **Job shop:** Each job has a **distinct route**.
  Example: $J_1$: $M_2 \rightarrow M_1$; $J_2$: $M_1 \rightarrow M_2$; $J_3$: $M_1 \rightarrow M_3$.
  The $p_{jk}$ matrix has zeros where a job does not visit a machine.

- **Open shop:** Each job visits every machine, but the **order is unrestricted**.
  Example $p_{jk}$:
  $$
  \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 5 & 6 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
  $$
  Any permutation on each row is permitted.

*(7 marks ŌĆö 2M definitions + 3M matrices + 2M distinguishing logic)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing $L_j$ and $T_j$:** A common error is to assign $T_j = C_j - d_j$ directly. **Always remember:** $T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$. Marks lost: 1 per wrong row.
> 2. **Ignoring flow-shop idle time on $M_2$:** If $M_2$ finishes $J_1$ but $J_3$ has not yet arrived from $M_1$, $M_2$ must **wait**, not pre-empt. Use $\max(\cdot)$ correctly.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the objective:** When asked to "solve a scheduling problem," the first sentence of your answer must specify the objective ($C_{max}$? $\sum T_j$?). Examiners dock **1 mark** if the objective is not stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Definition of Scheduling**

- ŌśÉ **Scheduling** = allocation of *jobs* to *machines* over *time* to optimize an *objective*.
- ŌśÉ The four pillars are: **Jobs $J$, Machines $M$, Constraints $C$, Objective $\Pi$**.
- ŌśÉ Scheduling sits at the **short-term operational level** of the PPC hierarchy.
- ŌśÉ Key metrics you must know cold:
  - $C_{max} = \max(C_j)$
  - $F_j = C_j - r_j$
  - $L_j = C_j - d_j$ (can be negative)
  - $T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$ (always $\geq 0$)
- ŌśÉ **Tardiness is non-negative**; **lateness is signed** ŌĆö this is the #1 exam trap.
- ŌśÉ Three classical configurations: **Flow shop, Job shop, Open shop**.
- ŌśÉ The default in KTU numerical problems is **non-pre-emptive** unless stated otherwise.
- ŌśÉ Always classify a problem using **Graham's $\alpha \mid \beta \mid \gamma$** notation when asked.
- ŌśÉ Visualize every schedule as a **Gantt chart** for full marks.
- ŌśÉ Real-world analogy: **train station platform manager** ŌĆö multiple trains, limited platforms, deadlines.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Scheduling

In the context of Industrial Engineering and Production Management, **Scheduling** is formally defined as the **decision-making process of allocating limited resources (machines, labour, tools, materials) to competing tasks (jobs/operations) over a specific time horizon**, in order to optimize one or more predefined performance objectives (such as minimizing makespan, tardiness, or work-in-process inventory).

KTU 2024 Scheme officially defines scheduling as a *short-to-medium term operational activity* that translates the aggregate production plan into a concrete, time-phased sequence of operations executable on the shop floor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction: Planning vs. Scheduling**
> - **Planning** answers: *What* and *How much* to produce (aggregate, long-term, resource-agnostic).
> - **Scheduling** answers: *When* and *In which order* to produce (detailed, short-term, resource-specific).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen during the dinner rush:

- **The Master Plan (Aggregate Planning):** The head chef decides how many cooks to hire, how much raw material to buy for the week, and which dishes will be on the menu.
- **Scheduling:** The sous chef assigns each incoming order (e.g., 1 Pasta, 1 Steak, 1 Salad) to specific cooks and stations, decides the order in which they are cooked, and tracks time so that all dishes arrive at Table 12 at *exactly* the same time, hot and fresh.

If the kitchen is unscheduled, the steak might be served cold while the salad wilts. **Scheduling is the art of orchestrating tasks to flow in harmony with time.**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Scheduling (KTU 2024 Framework):**
> 1. **Jobs (J)** ŌĆö Tasks to be performed (e.g., Job 1, Job 2, ŌĆ”, Job n).
> 2. **Machines (M)** ŌĆö Constrained resources (e.g., M1, M2, ŌĆ”, Mm).
> 3. **Objective Function (Z)** ŌĆö The criterion to be optimized (e.g., min makespan, min tardiness).

## 1.3 Why Scheduling Matters ŌĆö The Industrial Reality

In any real-world manufacturing or service operation, three realities are unavoidable:

1. **Resources are scarce** (only 3 CNC machines, only 2 paint booths).
2. **Jobs compete** for these resources simultaneously.
3. **Time is finite** ŌĆö every delay costs money, reputation, and market share.

Without a schedule, chaos ensues: bottlenecks form, machines sit idle while queues grow, deadlines are missed, and customer satisfaction collapses. Scheduling transforms this chaos into a **predictable, optimized flow**.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Scheduling Metrics (Memorize These):**
> - **Makespan (CŌéśŌéÉŌéō)** ŌĆö Total time to complete all jobs.
> - **Tardiness (TßĄó)** ŌĆö How late a job is beyond its due date.
> - **Flow Time (FßĄó)** ŌĆö Time a job spends in the system.
> - **Utilization (U)** ŌĆö Percentage of time a resource is busy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Role of Scheduling in Manufacturing

Scheduling does not operate in isolation. It is the **operational brain** of the production system, sitting between planning and execution. KTU 2024 Scheme organizes manufacturing decisions into a strict hierarchy:

| Level | Time Horizon | Decision Type | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic Planning** | 1ŌĆō5 years | Capacity, facility location | Build a new plant |
| **Aggregate Planning** | 3ŌĆō18 months | Production rate, workforce levels | Produce 10,000 units/quarter |
| **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** | WeeksŌĆōMonths | What & when to produce | Produce 500 units of Model X in Week 3 |
| **Detailed Scheduling** ŌŁÉ | HoursŌĆōDays | Sequence & timing of operations | Job 7 ŌåÆ Machine 2 at 10:30 AM |
| **Execution & Control** | Real-time | Monitoring, dispatching | Operator starts Job 7 now |

> **Detailed scheduling (the focus of this subject) is where the *real* operational performance is determined.**

## 2.2 The Core Objectives of Scheduling

A schedule is evaluated against one or more of these **performance objectives** (KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus):

1. **Minimize Makespan (CŌéśŌéÉŌéō)** ŌĆö finish all jobs as early as possible.
2. **Minimize Total/Average Flow Time (F╠ä)** ŌĆö jobs spend minimal time in the system.
3. **Minimize Maximum/Number of Tardy Jobs** ŌĆö meet due-date commitments.
4. **Minimize Idle Time (IdleßĄó)** ŌĆö keep machines busy.
5. **Maximize Machine Utilization (U)** ŌĆö productive use of capital.
6. **Minimize Work-In-Process (WIP) Inventory** ŌĆö fewer jobs waiting.
7. **Minimize Setup Costs / Changeover Time** ŌĆö fewer interruptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conflict of Objectives:** Minimizing makespan often *increases* WIP, and minimizing tardiness may *increase* makespan. KTU 2024 questions frequently ask students to **identify which objective a given schedule prioritizes** ŌĆö a classic 7-mark sub-question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Completion Time of Job *i* | $C_i$ | Time units |
| 2 | Makespan | $C_{\max} = \max \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_n\}$ | Time units |
| 3 | Flow Time of Job *i* | $F_i = C_i - r_i$ | Time units |
| 4 | Average Flow Time | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i$ | Time units |
| 5 | Lateness of Job *i* | $L_i = C_i - d_i$ | Can be negative (early) |
| 6 | Tardiness of Job *i* | $T_i = \max \{0, L_i\}$ | $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | Earliness of Job *i* | $E_i = \max \{0, -L_i\}$ | $\geq 0$ |
| 8 | Maximum Tardiness | $T_{\max} = \max \{T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n\}$ | Time units |
| 9 | Number of Tardy Jobs | $N_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i$ where $U_i=1$ if $T_i>0$ | Count |
| 10 | Machine Utilization | $U_m = \dfrac{\sum p_{ij}}{m \cdot C_{\max}}$ | Fraction $\in [0,1]$ |
| 11 | Throughput | $\text{TP} = \dfrac{n}{C_{\max}}$ | Jobs / time unit |
| 12 | Total Weighted Completion | $\sum w_i C_i$ | Weighted time units |
| 13 | Total Tardiness | $\sum T_i$ | Time units |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Lateness vs. Tardiness ŌĆö the #1 Confusion Point in KTU papers:**
> - Lateness $L_i$ can be **negative** (job finishes early).
> - Tardiness $T_i$ is **never negative** (only counts lateness that exceeds the due date).

## 2.4 The Real-World Utility of Scheduling

Scheduling is the invisible engine behind virtually every time-sensitive system:

- **Aerospace:** Sequencing thousands of components into a single aircraft assembly line.
- **Hospitals:** Allocating operating theatres, surgeons, and recovery beds to patient surgeries.
- **Cloud Computing:** Dispatching millions of virtual machines across data center servers.
- **Supply Chain:** Allocating trucks, drivers, and loading docks for daily dispatches.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Sequencing 400+ process steps across 60+ machines with zero tolerance for delay.

> [!TIP]
> Every rupee invested in good scheduling software returns 3ŌĆō10 rupees in operational savings through reduced inventory, fewer delays, and higher machine utilization. This is why scheduling is a **mandatory Module 1 topic** in KTU 2024.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Basic Flow Time and Makespan Relationship

For a single machine processing **n** jobs in sequence, the completion time of the $i^{th}$ job (1-indexed) is derived as follows:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define processing time.** Let $p_i$ be the processing time of Job $i$ on the single machine.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define release time.** Let $r_i$ be the time Job $i$ arrives (becomes available) at the machine. For a single machine with all jobs available at time 0, $r_i = 0$ for all $i$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express completion time recursively.** The completion time of Job $i$ is the sum of its own processing time plus the completion time of the job that precedes it (or zero, if it is the first job).

$$
C_i = C_{i-1} + p_i \quad \text{with} \quad C_0 = 0
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Unroll the recursion.** Expanding the recurrence:

$$
C_i = p_1 + p_2 + p_3 + \dots + p_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} p_j
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Derive makespan.** Since the last job finishes at $C_n$, the makespan is:

$$
C_{\max} = C_n = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Derive flow time.** Flow time is the time a job spends in the system, which for a single machine with $r_i = 0$ is simply $F_i = C_i$ (because the job arrived at time 0 and completed at $C_i$).

$$
F_i = C_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} p_j
$$

> **Sanity Check (n=3, p = [4, 2, 5]):**
> $C_1 = 4$, $C_2 = 4+2 = 6$, $C_3 = 4+2+5 = 11$, so $C_{\max} = 11$. Ō£ō

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Deriving Utilization from First Principles

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define productive work.** The total productive work required is the sum of all processing times across all jobs and machines:

$$
W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} p_{ij}
$$

where $p_{ij}$ is the processing time of Job $i$ on Machine $j$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define available capacity.** If the schedule runs for $C_{\max}$ time units across $m$ parallel machines, the total available machine-time is $m \cdot C_{\max}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Form the utilization ratio.** Utilization is the fraction of available machine-time that was actually used for productive processing.

$$
U = \frac{W}{m \cdot C_{\max}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Bound the result.** Since $C_{\max} \geq W / m$ (the fastest possible makespan equals perfect load balancing), we have $0 \leq U \leq 1$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Tardiness, Earliness, and Lateness ŌĆö Boundary Analysis

For any job $i$ with completion $C_i$ and due date $d_i$:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Lateness is the signed difference** (can be positive, zero, or negative):

$$
L_i = C_i - d_i
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Tardiness is the positive part** of lateness (zero if early):

$$
T_i = \max \{0, L_i\} = \frac{L_i + \vert L_i \vert}{2}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Earliness is the positive part of "being early"** (zero if late):

$$
E_i = \max \{0, -L_i\} = \frac{-L_i + \vert L_i \vert}{2}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Key invariant.** For any job, exactly one of $T_i$ or $E_i$ is non-zero (except when $C_i = d_i$, where both are zero). The relationship can be written as:

$$
L_i = T_i - E_i
$$

> **Worked Example:** $C_i = 12$, $d_i = 10$ ŌåÆ $L_i = +2$ (late) ŌåÆ $T_i = 2$, $E_i = 0$. Ō£ō
> **Worked Example:** $C_i = 8$, $d_i = 10$ ŌåÆ $L_i = -2$ (early) ŌåÆ $T_i = 0$, $E_i = 2$. Ō£ō

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö "Which Objective Should I Use?"

| If the problem context is... | Then the primary objective is... | Secondary objective |
|---|---|---|
| One-off project, common deadline (e.g., shipbuilding) | Minimize Makespan $C_{\max}$ | Maximize Utilization $U$ |
| High-volume repetitive production (e.g., FMCG) | Maximize Throughput TP | Minimize WIP |
| Customer-driven with strict due dates (e.g., e-commerce) | Minimize Number of Tardy Jobs $N_T$ | Minimize Max Tardiness $T_{\max}$ |
| JIT / Lean environment (e.g., Toyota) | Minimize Average Flow Time $\bar{F}$ | Minimize WIP & Earliness $E_i$ |
| Penalty-based contracts (e.g., aerospace MRO) | Minimize Total Weighted Tardiness $\sum w_i T_i$ | Meet $N_T$ target |
| Capacity-constrained bottleneck (e.g., bottleneck CNC) | Maximize Bottleneck Utilization | Minimize Idle Time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **high-yield KTU 2024 exam tool**. In Part B questions, when given a scenario, students must justify the chosen objective by referencing the operational context. Generic answers like "minimize makespan" without justification score 4/7, not 7/7.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example ŌĆö Multi-Objective Schedule Evaluation

**Problem:** 4 jobs on a single machine, processed in order J1ŌåÆJ2ŌåÆJ3ŌåÆJ4.

| Job | Processing Time $p_i$ | Due Date $d_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| J1 | 3 | 7 |
| J2 | 5 | 10 |
| J3 | 2 | 4 |
| J4 | 4 | 8 |

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute completion times.**

$$
C_1 = 3, \quad C_2 = 3+5 = 8, \quad C_3 = 3+5+2 = 10, \quad C_4 = 3+5+2+4 = 14
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute makespan.**

$$
C_{\max} = 14
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute flow time (here $r_i = 0$ so $F_i = C_i$).**

$$
F_1 = 3, \quad F_2 = 8, \quad F_3 = 10, \quad F_4 = 14
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute average flow time.**

$$
\bar{F} = \frac{3 + 8 + 10 + 14}{4} = \frac{35}{4} = 8.75
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute lateness, tardiness, earliness.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= 3 - 7 = -4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_1 = 0,\; E_1 = 4 \\
L_2 &= 8 - 10 = -2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_2 = 0,\; E_2 = 2 \\
L_3 &= 10 - 4 = +6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_3 = 6,\; E_3 = 0 \\
L_4 &= 14 - 8 = +6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_4 = 6,\; E_4 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute aggregate performance measures.**

$$
N_T = 2 \;\;(\text{J3 and J4}), \quad T_{\max} = 6, \quad \sum T_i = 12
$$

> **Result Interpretation:** J1 and J2 finished early (good for JIT, but may indicate inventory-holding cost); J3 and J4 are late (delivery penalties apply). The schedule is **not optimal** ŌĆö a different sequence (e.g., SPT: J3ŌåÆJ1ŌåÆJ4ŌåÆJ2) would yield different metrics. (Detailed sequence optimization is covered in Module 2.)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. Define scheduling. How is it different from aggregate planning? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Scheduling is the short-term operational decision-making process that allocates specific jobs to specific machines at specific times, with the goal of optimizing performance criteria such as makespan, tardiness, or utilization. It differs from aggregate planning in three key ways:

| Aspect | Aggregate Planning | Scheduling |
|---|---|---|
| Time Horizon | 3ŌĆō18 months | Hours to days |
| Granularity | Product families | Individual jobs/operations |
| Decisions | Production rate, workforce | Sequence and timing |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for the comparison table + 1 for the time horizon distinction.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Q1(b)]**

**Q2. List and briefly explain any three performance measures used to evaluate a schedule. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Makespan ($C_{\max}$):** Total elapsed time from the start of the first job to the completion of the last job. *Minimizing makespan maximizes throughput.*

2. **Tardiness ($T_i$):** The amount of time by which the completion of a job exceeds its due date. *Tardiness is never negative; lateness can be negative.*

3. **Machine Utilization ($U$):** The ratio of productive processing time to total available time. *High utilization means the resource is being used efficiently.*

*(3 marks: 1 mark per measure with correct definition + relevant formula + one-line interpretation.)*

---

## PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Q2]**

### **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **hierarchical role of scheduling** within a manufacturing planning and control system. Use a block diagram to illustrate the relationships. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A job shop has 4 jobs to be processed on a single machine in the sequence J1 ŌåÆ J2 ŌåÆ J3 ŌåÆ J4. The processing times (in hours) and due dates are:

| Job | $p_i$ | $d_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| J1 | 6 | 10 |
| J2 | 4 | 12 |
| J3 | 8 | 15 |
| J4 | 3 | 8 |

Compute the **makespan, average flow time, lateness of each job, and total tardiness**. Comment on whether the schedule satisfies the due-date commitments. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Hierarchical Role of Scheduling (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the hierarchy.** Manufacturing planning and control operates across five interconnected levels, moving from long-term strategic decisions to real-time execution.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Describe each level.**

1. **Strategic Planning (1ŌĆō5 years):** Decides on capital investments, facility location, and long-term capacity. *Example: opening a new plant in Bangalore.*
2. **Aggregate Planning (3ŌĆō18 months):** Sets overall production rates, workforce levels, and inventory policies at the product-family level. *Example: producing 50,000 units in Q1.*
3. **Master Production Schedule (MPS) (weeksŌĆōmonths):** Disaggregates the aggregate plan into specific end-items and time buckets. *Example: 1,200 units of Model X in Week 4.*
4. **Detailed Scheduling (hoursŌĆōdays) ŌŁÉ:** Converts the MPS into a precise sequence and timing of operations on individual machines. *Example: Job J7 on CNC-2 from 10:00 to 11:30.*
5. **Execution and Control (real-time):** Dispatches jobs to operators, monitors progress, and reschedules in response to disruptions (machine breakdown, rush order).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Explain the interconnections.** Information flows downward (plans become schedules) and feedback flows upward (actual performance is reported back). The **detailed schedule is the operational interface** between planning and the shop floor.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Justify the importance of scheduling.** The detailed schedule is the level at which the aggregate plan's promises are either realized or broken. Poor scheduling negates excellent planning; excellent scheduling can rescue a mediocre plan.

> **Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1) ŌĆö reproduce in exam.**

*(7 marks: 1 mark per level description + 2 marks for diagram + 1 mark for feedback loop explanation.)*

---

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given data:**

| Job | $p_i$ | $d_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| J1 | 6 | 10 |
| J2 | 4 | 12 |
| J3 | 8 | 15 |
| J4 | 3 | 8 |

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute completion times recursively.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 6 \\
C_2 &= 6 + 4 = 10 \\
C_3 &= 6 + 4 + 8 = 18 \\
C_4 &= 6 + 4 + 8 + 3 = 21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute makespan.**

$$
C_{\max} = C_4 = 21 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute flow times.** With $r_i = 0$, $F_i = C_i$.

$$
F_1 = 6, \quad F_2 = 10, \quad F_3 = 18, \quad F_4 = 21
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute average flow time.**

$$
\bar{F} = \frac{6 + 10 + 18 + 21}{4} = \frac{55}{4} = 13.75 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute lateness and tardiness for each job.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= 6 - 10 = -4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_1 = 0, \;\; E_1 = 4 \;\;\text{(Early)} \\
L_2 &= 10 - 12 = -2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_2 = 0, \;\; E_2 = 2 \;\;\text{(Early)} \\
L_3 &= 18 - 15 = +3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_3 = 3, \;\; E_3 = 0 \;\;\text{(Late)} \\
L_4 &= 21 - 8 = +13 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_4 = 13, \;\; E_4 = 0 \;\;\text{(Late)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute total tardiness.**

$$
\sum T_i = 0 + 0 + 3 + 13 = 16 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Comment on due-date compliance.**

- J1 and J2 meet their due dates (early).
- **J3 is 3 hours late.**
- **J4 is severely late by 13 hours** ŌĆö this is a critical due-date violation.
- The sequence J1 ŌåÆ J2 ŌåÆ J3 ŌåÆ J4 is clearly **not optimal**. A better sequence (such as SPT: J4 ŌåÆ J2 ŌåÆ J1 ŌåÆ J3, or EDD: J1 ŌåÆ J4 ŌåÆ J2 ŌåÆ J3) would significantly reduce tardiness.

*(7 marks: 1 for completion times + 1 for makespan + 1 for flow times + 2 for lateness/tardiness table + 1 for total tardiness + 1 for the qualitative comment.)*

---

### **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **classification of scheduling objectives** with suitable examples. Why is it not always possible to minimize all objectives simultaneously? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A factory has 3 machines and 5 jobs. The total processing time required across all jobs is **42 hours**. The schedule completes all jobs in a makespan of **18 hours**. Compute the **average machine utilization**. If the same workload were scheduled optimally, what is the **theoretical lower bound on the makespan**? Explain why the actual makespan cannot go below this bound. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Classification of Scheduling Objectives (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the classification logic.** Scheduling objectives are classified into **five families** based on what they measure.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Enumerate and exemplify.**

1. **Time-Based Objectives:** Minimize makespan, flow time, and lateness. *Used in project-oriented industries like construction and shipbuilding.*
2. **Due-Date-Based Objectives:** Minimize tardiness, number of tardy jobs, and maximum tardiness. *Used in customer-driven industries like e-commerce and food delivery.*
3. **Resource-Based Objectives:** Maximize machine utilization; minimize idle time and setup time. *Used in capital-intensive industries like semiconductor fabs.*
4. **Inventory-Based Objectives:** Minimize Work-In-Process (WIP) and earliness penalty. *Used in JIT/Lean environments like Toyota Production System.*
5. **Cost-Based Objectives:** Minimize total weighted tardiness, penalty costs; maximize throughput revenue. *Used in penalty-driven contracts like aerospace MRO.*

**Step 3 ŌĆö Explain the conflict (the trade-off).**

These objectives often **conflict** with each other because they measure different things:

- Minimizing makespan may require running machines continuously, which **increases WIP** (more jobs piled up waiting).
- Minimizing tardiness may force the schedule to **insert idle time** to wait for a due date, which **reduces utilization**.
- Minimizing earliness may require **delaying job starts**, which **increases flow time**.

> **Mathematical Intuition:** The objectives live on a Pareto frontier ŌĆö improving one often worsens another. KTU 2024 questions frequently ask students to **identify which objective dominates in a given scenario** and to justify the choice.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per family + 1 for the conflict explanation + 1 for the Pareto-frontier insight.)*

---

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Utilization and Makespan Lower Bound (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Total workload $W = 42$ hours
- Number of machines $m = 3$
- Actual makespan $C_{\max}^{\text{actual}} = 18$ hours

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute average machine utilization.**

$$
U = \frac{W}{m \cdot C_{\max}^{\text{actual}}} = \frac{42}{3 \times 18} = \frac{42}{54} = 0.7778
$$

$$
U \approx 77.78\%
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the theoretical lower bound on makespan.**

The **fastest possible makespan** is achieved when the workload is perfectly balanced across all machines (every machine finishes at exactly the same time). This is called the **load-balancing bound** (or LB1):

$$
C_{\max}^{\min} \geq \frac{W}{m} = \frac{42}{3} = 14 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Explain why actual makespan cannot go below this bound.**

The total available machine-time per unit clock time is $m$ (i.e., 3 machine-hours every real hour). To process 42 machine-hours of work, the clock must advance by at least $42 / 3 = 14$ hours. No sequence, no algorithm, no matter how clever, can do better than this because **work cannot be created or destroyed ŌĆö it can only be redistributed across machines**. Additional lower bounds (LB2 from longest job, LB3 from job-machine precedence) may raise the bound further, but the load-balancing bound is the **fundamental physical limit**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the optimality gap.**

$$
\text{Gap} = \frac{C_{\max}^{\text{actual}} - C_{\max}^{\min}}{C_{\max}^{\min}} \times 100\% = \frac{18 - 14}{14} \times 100\% = 28.57\%
$$

> The current schedule is **28.57% worse than the theoretical optimum** ŌĆö there is significant room for improvement through better sequencing.

*(7 marks: 2 for utilization calculation + 2 for lower-bound derivation + 2 for explanation of the bound + 1 for the optimality-gap bonus insight.)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Traps in "Role of Scheduling" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing lateness with tardiness.** Lateness is *signed* (can be negative); tardiness is *unsigned* (Ōēź 0). Examiners deduct 1 mark per instance.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit of $C_{\max}$.** Always write "hours" or "days" ŌĆö a number alone is incomplete.
> 3. **Omitting the objective justification.** Saying "minimize makespan" without explaining *why* this objective fits the scenario is a 4/7 answer, not 7/7.
> 4. **Confusing makespan with total processing time.** Makespan $C_{\max} = C_n$ (the *last* job's completion), not $\sum p_i$ (which equals it only for a single machine).
> 5. **Skipping the load-balance bound.** In utilization questions, the lower bound $C_{\max} \geq W/m$ is mandatory ŌĆö forgetting it is an automatic 1-mark loss.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Role of Scheduling**

- Ō£ģ Scheduling is the **short-term, operational** decision of *what* runs on *which* machine, *when*, and in *what sequence*.
- Ō£ģ It is **one level below** the Master Production Schedule and **one level above** shop-floor execution.
- Ō£ģ The **three core elements** of every scheduling problem are: **Jobs**, **Machines**, **Objective Function**.
- Ō£ģ The **eight standard metrics** are: makespan, flow time, lateness, tardiness, earliness, utilization, throughput, and number of tardy jobs.
- Ō£ģ **Lateness is signed; tardiness is unsigned** ŌĆö the most-tested distinction.
- Ō£ģ Scheduling objectives are classified into **five families**: time-based, due-date-based, resource-based, inventory-based, cost-based.
- Ō£ģ The **fundamental makespan lower bound** is $C_{\max} \geq W/m$ (load-balancing bound).
- Ō£ģ **Utilization** is computed as $U = W / (m \cdot C_{\max})$ and is bounded in $[0, 1]$.
- Ō£ģ **Real-world applications** include manufacturing, hospitals, cloud computing, supply chain, and semiconductor fabs.
- Ō£ģ **KTU 2024 expects** definitions, hierarchy diagrams, formulas, numerical computations, and trade-off analysis ŌĆö practice all four.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminologies Involved in Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Terminologies Involved in Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Scheduling** is the process of allocating limited resources (machines, labor, time) to competing tasks (jobs) over a specific time horizon, with the objective of optimizing one or more performance measures. In the context of **Production and Operations Management**, scheduling is the short-term execution phase of production planning that determines **what** will be done, **when** it will be done, **by which** machine or resource, and **in what order**.

In formal mathematical notation, a scheduling problem is denoted by the three-field classification **╬▒ | ╬▓ | ╬│**, where:
- **╬▒** describes the machine environment (e.g., single machine, parallel machines, flow shop, job shop)
- **╬▓** describes the job characteristics and constraints (e.g., processing times, release dates, precedence)
- **╬│** describes the objective function to be minimized

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The terminologies discussed in this module form the **foundational vocabulary** for every subsequent module (sequencing, Gantt charts, Johnson's rule, flow shop scheduling, etc.). Examiners frequently test these definitions for **2ŌĆō3 mark short-answer questions**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of scheduling like **planning a dinner party in a small kitchen**:
- You (the **scheduler**) have multiple **dishes (jobs)** that need to be cooked.
- Each dish requires use of the **stove, oven, and mixer (machines)**.
- Each dish needs a certain **cooking time (processing time)**.
- Some dishes (like marinating) must be done **before** others (precedence constraints).
- Guests arrive at a fixed **time (due date)** ŌĆö you want everything ready.
- The **Gantt chart** is like your kitchen timeline taped to the wall.

The vocabulary below is the language you use to *describe* and *measure* how well your kitchen (or factory) is performing.

### 1.3 Why Terminology Matters

> [!NOTE]
> Without a standardized vocabulary, scheduling problems cannot be:
> 1. **Modeled mathematically** (you cannot write an objective function without symbols like $C_j$, $T_j$).
> 2. **Communicated precisely** between engineers, managers, and researchers.
> 3. **Classified** into solvable categories (NP-hard vs. polynomial).
> 4. **Benchmarked** against standard test problems in literature.

The **standard performance metrics** evaluated in KTU examinations are highlighted in **bold** throughout these notes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Basic Entities: Jobs, Machines, Operations

- **Job ($J_j$)**: An independent unit of work to be processed. A job consists of one or more **operations**.
- **Operation ($O_{ij}$)**: The $i$-th task of job $j$, typically performed on a specific machine $M_i$.
- **Machine ($M_i$)**: A physical or logical resource capable of processing one operation at a time.
- **Processing Time ($p_{ij}$)**: The time required to complete operation $O_{ij}$ on machine $M_i$. It is a **deterministic, known, non-negative** parameter in classical scheduling theory.
- **Setup Time ($s_{ij}$)**: The time to prepare machine $M_i$ before processing job $J_j$. May be **sequence-dependent** or **sequence-independent**.

### 2.2 Time-Based Parameters

- **Release Date ($r_j$)**: The earliest time at which job $J_j$ becomes available for processing. Also called *ready time* or *availability time*.
- **Due Date ($d_j$)**: The committed delivery or promised completion time for job $J_j$.
- **Start Time ($S_{ij}$)**: The time at which operation $O_{ij}$ actually begins.
- **Completion Time ($C_j$)**: The time at which job $J_j$ finishes all its operations. This is the **primary output** of any schedule.
- **Flow Time ($F_j$)**: The total time job $J_j$ spends in the system: $F_j = C_j - r_j$.

### 2.3 Performance/Deviation Measures

These measures quantify *how well* a schedule meets its deadlines.

- **Lateness ($L_j$)**: Signed deviation from due date: $L_j = C_j - d_j$. Can be negative (early) or positive (late).
- **Tardiness ($T_j$)**: Non-negative lateness: $T_j = \max(0,\ C_j - d_j)$. **Cannot be negative.**
- **Earliness ($E_j$)**: Non-negative measure of being early: $E_j = \max(0,\ d_j - C_j)$.
- **Waiting Time ($W_j$)**: Time job waits in queue before processing starts.

### 2.4 System-Level Performance Measures

- **Makespan ($C_{\max}$)**: The completion time of the **last job** to leave the system:
$$C_{\max} = \max_{j} \{C_j\}$$
This is the **most commonly used** objective in KTU problems.
- **Total Completion Time** ($\sum C_j$), **Total Flow Time** ($\sum F_j$), **Maximum Tardiness** ($T_{\max}$), **Number of Tardy Jobs** ($U_j = 1$ if $C_j > d_j$, else $0$).

### 2.5 Idle Time and Utilization

- **Machine Idle Time**: Period when a machine is available but no job is being processed. Measured as:
$$\text{Idle Time} = C_{\max} - \sum_{j \in \text{assigned}} p_{ij}$$
- **Machine Utilization**: Fraction of available time the machine is productive.

### 2.6 Schedule Classifications

- **Feasible Schedule**: Satisfies all precedence, capacity, and resource constraints.
- **Optimal Schedule**: A feasible schedule that minimizes (or maximizes) the chosen objective.
- **Permutation Schedule**: The order of jobs is identical across all machines (common in flow shops).
- **Non-Permutation Schedule**: Job order differs between machines ŌĆö sometimes provably better, but harder to handle.
- **Active Schedule**: No job can be moved earlier without delaying another.
- **Semi-Active Schedule**: No idle time can be inserted without delaying some job.
- **Non-Delay Schedule**: No machine is intentionally kept idle while a job waits.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula** | **Range / Unit** |
|:----------:|:------------|:-----------:|:----------------:|
| $p_{ij}$ | Processing time | Given input | Time units |
| $r_j$ | Release date | Given input | Time units |
| $d_j$ | Due date | Given input | Time units |
| $S_j$ | Start time of job $j$ | Computed | Time units |
| $C_j$ | Completion time of job $j$ | $C_j = S_j + p_j$ | Time units |
| $C_{\max}$ | Makespan | $\max_j C_j$ | Time units |
| $F_j$ | Flow time | $C_j - r_j$ | Time units |
| $L_j$ | Lateness | $C_j - d_j$ | $\in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $T_j$ | Tardiness | $\max(0,\ C_j - d_j)$ | $\geq 0$ |
| $E_j$ | Earliness | $\max(0,\ d_j - C_j)$ | $\geq 0$ |
| $U_j$ | Unit penalty | $1$ if $C_j > d_j$, else $0$ | $\{0, 1\}$ |
| $W_j$ | Waiting time | $S_j - r_j$ | Time units |
| $\bar{F}$ | Mean flow time | $\frac{1}{n}\sum F_j$ | Time units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always remember the relationship chain:**
> $$r_j \leq S_j \leq C_j \quad \text{and} \quad C_j = S_j + \sum_i p_{ij}$$

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

These terminologies are the backbone of:
- **Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES)** in automotive and semiconductor plants.
- **CPU scheduling** in operating systems (similar $C_j$, $T_j$ concepts).
- **Project management tools** (Primavera, MS Project) for critical path calculations.
- **Hospital operating-room scheduling**, **airline crew rostering**, and **cloud computing job dispatchers**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Performance Measures

We now derive the **fundamental identity** that connects flow time, lateness, tardiness, and earliness. This identity appears in many KTU problems.

**Step 1:** Define lateness using completion time and due date.
By definition:
$$L_j = C_j - d_j$$

**Step 2:** Split lateness into positive and negative parts.
Every real number can be decomposed as:
$$L_j = \underbrace{\max(0,\ L_j)}_{T_j} - \underbrace{\max(0,\ -L_j)}_{E_j}$$

This means lateness equals tardiness minus earliness:
$$L_j = T_j - E_j$$

**Step 3:** Verify boundary cases.
- If $C_j > d_j$ (late): $L_j > 0$, $T_j = L_j$, $E_j = 0$. Ō£ō
- If $C_j = d_j$ (on time): $T_j = E_j = 0$. Ō£ō
- If $C_j < d_j$ (early): $L_j < 0$, $T_j = 0$, $E_j = -L_j$. Ō£ō

**Step 4:** Express in terms of $C_j$ and $d_j$.
Substituting the definitions:
$$\boxed{T_j = \max(0,\ C_j - d_j)}$$
$$\boxed{E_j = \max(0,\ d_j - C_j)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Makespan Lower Bound

**Step 1:** Recognize that every machine $i$ must process all jobs assigned to it.
If machine $i$ processes a subset $J^{(i)}$ of jobs, then:
$$C_{\max} \geq \sum_{j \in J^{(i)}} p_{ij}$$

**Step 2:** Take the maximum over all machines to get a **machine-bound lower bound**:
$$C_{\max} \geq \max_i \sum_j p_{ij}$$

**Step 3:** Account for precedence. If job $j$ depends on a chain of operations, the chain length gives another bound:
$$C_{\max} \geq \max_{c \in \text{chains}} \sum_{(i,j) \in c} p_{ij}$$

**Step 4:** The true makespan lower bound is the **maximum** of all such bounds:
$$C_{\max}^* = \max\left(\max_i \sum_j p_{ij},\ \max_{\text{chains}} \sum p_{ij}\right)$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Scenarios

| **Problem Type** | **Given** | **Compute** | **Objective** |
|:-----------------|:----------|:------------|:--------------|
| Single machine, $n$ jobs | $p_j, d_j$ | $C_j, T_j, L_j$ | Minimize $\sum T_j$ |
| Two-machine flow shop | $p_{1j}, p_{2j}$ | $C_j$ for all $j$ | Minimize $C_{\max}$ (Johnson's rule) |
| Parallel identical machines | $p_j$ | Assignment + sequence | Minimize $C_{\max}$ (LPT rule) |
| Job shop | $p_{ij}$, route | $S_j, C_j$ | Minimize $C_{\max}$ |
| $n/1/\sum T_j$ | $p_j, d_j$ | Schedule | Minimize total tardiness |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Example (KTU Style):** *Three jobs $J_1, J_2, J_3$ are processed on a single machine in the order 2 ŌåÆ 1 ŌåÆ 3. The processing times are $p_1 = 4$, $p_2 = 6$, $p_3 = 3$, and the due dates are $d_1 = 10$, $d_2 = 8$, $d_3 = 14$. Compute $C_j$, $L_j$, $T_j$, $E_j$, $F_j$ (assume $r_j = 0$).*

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute start and completion times sequentially.

$$S_2 = 0,\quad C_2 = 0 + 6 = 6$$

$$S_1 = 6,\quad C_1 = 6 + 4 = 10$$

$$S_3 = 10,\quad C_3 = 10 + 3 = 13$$

**Step 2:** Compute makespan.
$$C_{\max} = \max(6,\ 10,\ 13) = 13$$

**Step 3:** Compute lateness for each job.
$$L_1 = C_1 - d_1 = 10 - 10 = 0$$
$$L_2 = C_2 - d_2 = 6 - 8 = -2$$
$$L_3 = C_3 - d_3 = 13 - 14 = -1$$

**Step 4:** Compute tardiness and earliness.
$$T_1 = \max(0, 0) = 0,\quad E_1 = \max(0, 0) = 0$$
$$T_2 = \max(0, -2) = 0,\quad E_2 = \max(0, 2) = 2$$
$$T_3 = \max(0, -1) = 0,\quad E_3 = \max(0, 1) = 1$$

**Step 5:** Compute flow times (with $r_j = 0$).
$$F_1 = 10 - 0 = 10,\quad F_2 = 6,\quad F_3 = 13$$
$$\sum F_j = 29,\quad \bar{F} = \frac{29}{3} \approx 9.67$$

**Step 6:** Aggregate performance.
$$\sum T_j = 0,\quad T_{\max} = 0,\quad \sum C_j = 29$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Since all jobs are completed *before or at* their due dates, this schedule is **on-time perfect** ŌĆö no tardiness penalty. This is the ideal scenario.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Taxonomy of Scheduling Terminologies

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Scheduling Terminologies] --> B[Entities]
    A --> C[Time Parameters]

    B --> B1[Job Jj]
    B --> B2[Machine Mi]

    C --> C1[Release Date rj]

    C --> C3[Start Time Sj]
    C --> C4[Completion Time Cj]

    D --> D1[Lateness Lj]
    D --> D2[Tardiness Tj]

    E --> E1[Feasible]

    F --> F1[Makespan Cmax]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Gantt Chart Example

```mermaid

    dateFormat HH:mm
    axisFormat %H:%M

    section Machine M1
    Job J2 (p=6)   :a2, 00:00, 6h
    Job J1 (p=4)   :a1, after a2, 4h
    Job J3 (p=3)   :a3, after a1, 3h

    section Due Dates
    Due J2 (d=8)    :crit, milestone, 00:08, 0h
    Due J1 (d=10)   :crit, milestone, 00:10, 0h
    Due J3 (d=14)   :crit, milestone, 00:14, 0h
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Makespan, (ii) Tardiness, (iii) Due date.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Makespan ($C_{\max}$):** The completion time of the last job to leave the system in a schedule. It represents the total time required to finish all jobs. *Example:* If 3 jobs complete at times 6, 10, and 13, then $C_{\max} = 13$.

(ii) **Tardiness ($T_j$):** The non-negative lateness of a job, defined as $T_j = \max(0,\ C_j - d_j)$. It measures how late a job is beyond its due date, and is never negative. *Example:* If $C_j = 15$ and $d_j = 12$, then $T_j = 3$.

(iii) **Due Date ($d_j$):** The promised or committed date/time by which job $J_j$ should ideally be completed. It is a contractual or planning parameter. *Example:* A customer order promised for delivery on the 20th of the month has $d_j = 20$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
*Differentiate between **lateness**, **tardiness**, and **earliness**. Explain with a formula the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

The three measures quantify deviations from the due date:

- **Lateness ($L_j = C_j - d_j$)** is a *signed* measure ŌĆö can be positive, negative, or zero.
- **Tardiness ($T_j = \max(0,\ C_j - d_j)$)** is the *positive part* of lateness ŌĆö measures lateness only.
- **Earliness ($E_j = \max(0,\ d_j - C_j)$)** is the *positive part* of negative lateness ŌĆö measures how early.

**Relationship:**
$$L_j = T_j - E_j$$

If $C_j > d_j$: $T_j = L_j$ and $E_j = 0$.
If $C_j < d_j$: $T_j = 0$ and $E_j = -L_j$.
If $C_j = d_j$: $T_j = E_j = L_j = 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Flow Shop Computation Focus

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022 Style]**

> **(a)** *[7 Marks]* Define the following scheduling terminologies with standard notation: processing time, due date, completion time, makespan, flow time, and tardiness. For each, state whether it is an *input parameter* or an *output measure* of a scheduling problem.
>
> **(b)** *[7 Marks]* Four jobs are to be processed on two machines $M_1$ and $M_2$ in the same order (flow shop). The processing times and due dates are given below. Determine the **completion time** and **tardiness** of each job under the sequence $J_2 \rightarrow J_1 \rightarrow J_4 \rightarrow J_3$, and compute the **makespan** and **total tardiness**.

| Job | $p_{1j}$ (M1) | $p_{2j}$ (M2) | $d_j$ |
|:---:|:-------------:|:-------------:|:-----:|
| $J_1$ | 5 | 3 | 9 |
| $J_2$ | 2 | 6 | 7 |
| $J_3$ | 4 | 4 | 12 |
| $J_4$ | 6 | 2 | 15 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions (7 marks):**

| **Term** | **Notation** | **Definition** | **Type** |
|:---------|:------------:|:--------------|:--------:|
| Processing time | $p_{ij}$ | Time to process op. $O_{ij}$ on machine $i$ | Input |
| Due date | $d_j$ | Promised completion time for job $j$ | Input |
| Completion time | $C_j$ | Time when job $j$ finishes all ops | Output |
| Makespan | $C_{\max}$ | Maximum completion time over all jobs | Output |
| Flow time | $F_j$ | $C_j - r_j$, total time in system | Output |
| Tardiness | $T_j$ | $\max(0,\ C_j - d_j)$ | Output |

**(b) Step-by-step computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Build the two-machine table for the sequence $J_2 \rightarrow J_1 \rightarrow J_4 \rightarrow J_3$.

**Step 2:** Compute $C_{1j}$ on machine $M_1$ (jobs in sequence):
- $C_{1,2} = p_{1,2} = 2$
- $C_{1,1} = 2 + p_{1,1} = 2 + 5 = 7$
- $C_{1,4} = 7 + p_{1,4} = 7 + 6 = 13$
- $C_{1,3} = 13 + p_{1,3} = 13 + 4 = 17$

**Step 3:** Compute $C_{2j}$ on machine $M_2$ (job starts only after its M1 completion):
- $C_{2,2} = \max(C_{1,2},\ C_{2,\text{prev}}) + p_{2,2} = \max(2,\ 0) + 6 = 8$
- $C_{2,1} = \max(C_{1,1},\ C_{2,2}) + p_{2,1} = \max(7,\ 8) + 3 = 11$
- $C_{2,4} = \max(C_{1,4},\ C_{2,1}) + p_{2,4} = \max(13,\ 11) + 2 = 15$
- $C_{2,3} = \max(C_{1,3},\ C_{2,4}) + p_{2,3} = \max(17,\ 15) + 4 = 21$

**Step 4:** Compute tardiness $T_j = \max(0,\ C_j - d_j)$:
- $T_2 = \max(0,\ 8 - 7) = 1$
- $T_1 = \max(0,\ 11 - 9) = 2$
- $T_4 = \max(0,\ 15 - 15) = 0$
- $T_3 = \max(0,\ 21 - 12) = 9$

**Step 5:** Aggregate metrics.
$$C_{\max} = \max(8,\ 11,\ 15,\ 21) = 21$$
$$\sum T_j = 1 + 2 + 0 + 9 = 12$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often incorrectly start $M_2$ processing **before** the corresponding $M_1$ operation finishes. Always enforce: $S_{2j} \geq C_{1j}$ **and** $S_{2j} \geq C_{2,\text{previous}}$. Both conditions must hold.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Conceptual + Computational

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023 Style]**

> **(a)** *[7 Marks]* With a neat sketch, explain the difference between **active**, **semi-active**, and **non-delay** schedules. Give one example each where a schedule is active but not semi-active, and semi-active but not non-delay.
>
> **(b)** *[7 Marks]* For the single-machine problem below, compute $C_j$, $L_j$, $T_j$, $E_j$, and $\sum T_j$ for the sequence $J_3 \rightarrow J_1 \rightarrow J_4 \rightarrow J_2$. All $r_j = 0$.

| Job | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $J_1$ | 8 | 12 |
| $J_2$ | 5 | 10 |
| $J_3$ | 3 | 7 |
| $J_4$ | 6 | 18 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual answer (7 marks):**

- **Semi-Active Schedule:** No idle time can be inserted between any two consecutive operations without delaying some job. Equivalently, every operation starts as early as possible given the job sequence.
- **Active Schedule:** A semi-active schedule where no job can be moved earlier (by swapping with another or shifting within the same machine) without delaying another job or violating precedence.
- **Non-Delay Schedule:** An active schedule where no machine is kept idle while there is at least one job waiting to be processed on it.

**Inclusion hierarchy:**
$$\text{Non-Delay} \subset \text{Active} \subset \text{Semi-Active}$$

**Examples:**
- *Active but not semi-active*: Schedule with an idle gap that could be removed ŌĆö e.g., jobs at times [0ŌĆō5, 7ŌĆō10] with idle gap 5ŌĆō7 when another job could fit.
- *Semi-active but not non-delay*: Schedule where a machine stays idle at time 3ŌĆō4 even though a job is waiting in queue.

**(b) Step-by-step computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Sequential start and completion times in order $J_3 \rightarrow J_1 \rightarrow J_4 \rightarrow J_2$:

$$C_3 = 0 + p_3 = 0 + 3 = 3$$
$$C_1 = 3 + p_1 = 3 + 8 = 11$$
$$C_4 = 11 + p_4 = 11 + 6 = 17$$
$$C_2 = 17 + p_2 = 17 + 5 = 22$$

**Step 2:** Lateness $L_j = C_j - d_j$:
$$L_3 = 3 - 7 = -4,\quad L_1 = 11 - 12 = -1$$
$$L_4 = 17 - 18 = -1,\quad L_2 = 22 - 10 = +12$$

**Step 3:** Tardiness $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$:
$$T_3 = 0,\quad T_1 = 0,\quad T_4 = 0,\quad T_2 = 12$$

**Step 4:** Earliness $E_j = \max(0, -L_j)$:
$$E_3 = 4,\quad E_1 = 1,\quad E_4 = 1,\quad E_2 = 0$$

**Step 5:** Aggregate:
$$\sum T_j = 12,\quad T_{\max} = 12$$
$$C_{\max} = \max(3, 11, 17, 22) = 22$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** A common mistake is to treat **lateness and tardiness as the same thing**. Tardiness is *always non-negative*. If a student writes "$L_j = -4$" but calls it tardiness, they lose marks. Also, do not forget to compute $C_{\max}$ and $\sum T_j$ at the end ŌĆö KTU examiners award **1 mark each** for these aggregate metrics.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- Ō£ģ **Job** = unit of work; **Operation** = task of a job; **Machine** = resource.
- Ō£ģ **Processing time $p_{ij}$** is always an **input**; **$C_j$** is always an **output**.
- Ō£ģ **Makespan** $C_{\max}$ = maximum completion time ŌĆö most common KTU objective.
- Ō£ģ **Lateness** $L_j = C_j - d_j$ can be negative; **Tardiness** $T_j$ cannot.
- Ō£ģ **Earliness** $E_j$ measures how early a job finishes.
- Ō£ģ **Flow time** $F_j = C_j - r_j$ (zero if all $r_j = 0$).
- Ō£ģ The **identity** $L_j = T_j - E_j$ is essential and frequently tested.
- Ō£ģ **Hierarchy:** Non-Delay ŌŖé Active ŌŖé Semi-Active ŌŖé Feasible.
- Ō£ģ **Permutation schedule** = same order on all machines (used in flow shop).
- Ō£ģ **Lower bound on makespan:** $C_{\max} \geq \max\left(\sum_j p_{ij}\right)$.
- Ō£ģ Three-field classification **╬▒ | ╬▓ | ╬│** is the standard way to denote scheduling problems.
- Ō£ģ Common objectives in KTU: minimize $C_{\max}$, $\sum C_j$, $\sum T_j$, $T_{\max}$, or number of tardy jobs $\sum U_j$.
- Ō£ģ Always **draw a Gantt chart** to verify your schedule visually ŌĆö examiners reward neatness.
- Ō£ģ **Units matter:** keep all times in the same unit (hours, days, minutes) throughout a calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Scheduling Models - Graham et al. ╬▒/╬▓/╬│ Notations to Classify Scheduling Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Scheduling Models - Graham et al. ╬▒/╬▓/╬│ Notations to Classify Scheduling Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In deterministic machine scheduling theory, scheduling problems can become extraordinarily diverse. Different industries face different shop configurations (one machine, many identical machines, flow shops, job shops, etc.), different operational constraints (precedence constraints, release dates, preemption, setup times, etc.), and different optimization goals (minimize makespan, minimize tardiness, minimize completion time, etc.). To bring order to this vast universe of problems, **Graham, Lawler, Lenstra, and Rinnooy Kan** proposed a standardized three-field classification scheme in their seminal 1979 paper *"Optimization and Approximation in Deterministic Sequencing and Scheduling: A Survey."*

The classification is denoted as:

$$\alpha \;\vert\; \beta \;\vert\; \gamma$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Graham's ╬▒/╬▓/╬│ Notation** is a compact, universal shorthand used worldwide in scheduling literature to precisely describe any deterministic scheduling problem. The ╬▒ field specifies the **machine environment**, the ╬▓ field specifies the **job characteristics and processing constraints**, and the ╬│ field specifies the **objective function to be optimized**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine you walk into a busy restaurant. To describe how the kitchen operates, you would naturally answer three questions:

1. **What kind of kitchen is it?** (╬▒ field) ŌĆö Is it a single chef preparing one dish at a time? Multiple identical grills cooking in parallel? Or a structured assembly-line (flow shop) where each station handles one step? This is the **machine environment**.

2. **What are the rules and constraints on the orders?** (╬▓ field) ŌĆö Do some dishes need to be cooked *before* others (precedence constraints)? Can the chef interrupt a dish to start another (preemption)? Are some ingredients only delivered later (release dates)? These are the **job characteristics**.

3. **What does the manager want to optimize?** (╬│ field) ŌĆö Is it total finishing time (makespan)? Minimize late orders (tardiness)? Maximize customer satisfaction weighted by importance (weighted completion time)? This is the **objective function**.

The beauty of Graham's notation is that **any scheduling scenario** ŌĆö from a single-machine repair shop to a complex automobile assembly plant ŌĆö can be described precisely using just three short fields.

---

## 1.3 The Three-Field Notation at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Convention:** The notation ╬▒ | ╬▓ | ╬│ is read as "Given machine environment ╬▒, with job characteristics ╬▓, minimize (or maximize) objective ╬│." The vertical bar `|` is a separator, not division.

| Field | Symbol | What It Describes | Example |
|-------|--------|-------------------|---------|
| 1st | **╬▒** | Machine environment | $1$, $P_m$, $Q_m$, $R_m$, $F_m$, $J_m$, $O$ |
| 2nd | **╬▓** | Job processing constraints | $prec$, $pmtn$, $r_j$, $d_j$ |
| 3rd | **╬│** | Objective function | $C_{\max}$, $L_{\max}$, $\sum C_j$ |

A complete problem statement, e.g., $1 \;\vert\; pmtn, r_j \;\vert\; \sum C_j$, can be read as: *"Given a single machine environment, with preemption allowed and release dates, minimize the total completion time."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Three Fields

### 2.1.1 The ╬▒ Field ŌĆö Machine Environment

The ╬▒ field classifies the **physical processing infrastructure**. It is the most fundamental aspect because the resource structure dictates the search space of all feasible schedules.

- **Single Machine ($1$):** Exactly one machine processes all jobs. Jobs are queued and processed one at a time. The scheduling decision is purely the *order* in which jobs are sequenced on the lone machine.

- **Identical Parallel Machines ($P_m$):** $m$ machines run in parallel, each with **identical** processing speeds. A job can be processed on any machine, and processing time is machine-independent.

- **Uniform Parallel Machines ($Q_m$):** $m$ machines run in parallel, but each machine $i$ has a different *speed* $s_i$. The time to process job $j$ on machine $i$ is $p_j / s_i$.

- **Unrelated Parallel Machines ($R_m$):** $m$ machines run in parallel with arbitrary speeds. The processing time of job $j$ on machine $i$ is a fully independent value $p_{ij}$.

- **Flow Shop ($F_m$):** All jobs follow the **same sequence** of $m$ machines, in the same order. Each machine acts as a station in a production line.

- **Job Shop ($J_m$):** Each job has its **own predetermined route** through the $m$ machines. Different jobs visit machines in different orders.

- **Open Shop ($O$):** Each job must be processed on each of the $m$ machines, but the **order is not fixed** ŌĆö the scheduler decides the routing.

### 2.1.2 The ╬▓ Field ŌĆö Job Characteristics and Constraints

The ╬▓ field is often empty (denoted $\beta = \emptyset$ or simply left blank), which means "no special constraints." When present, it is a comma-separated list of attributes:

- **$prec$ (Precedence Constraints):** A partial order exists among jobs. Job $i$ must complete before job $j$ can begin. Modeled as a directed acyclic graph (DAG).

- **$pmtn$ (Preemption):** Processing of a job may be interrupted and resumed later (possibly on a different machine). Used for dynamic rescheduling.

- **$r_j$ (Release Dates):** Job $j$ cannot start processing before its ready time $r_j$. Models job arrivals in a real-time system.

- **$d_j$ (Due Dates):** Each job $j$ has a due date $d_j$ by which it should ideally be completed. Used in tardiness-based objectives.

- **$s_{jk}$ (Sequence-Dependent Setup Times):** If job $j$ is processed immediately after job $k$, a setup time $s_{jk}$ is incurred. Distinguishes from $s_j$ (sequence-independent setups).

- **$p_j = p$ (Identical Processing Times):** All jobs have equal processing time $p$.

- **$M_j$ (Arbitrary Preemptions to a Fixed Limit):** Each job $j$ may be preempted at most $M_j$ times.

### 2.1.3 The ╬│ Field ŌĆö Objective Function

The ╬│ field specifies the **criterion to be optimized**. Common objectives include:

- **$C_{\max}$ (Makespan):** Maximum completion time across all jobs. Measures the total time to complete the entire schedule.

- **$L_{\max}$ (Maximum Lateness):** Worst-case lateness, defined as $\max_j \{C_j - d_j\}$. Captures the most delayed job.

- **$\sum C_j$ (Total Completion Time):** Sum of all job completion times. Equivalent to minimizing average flow time (since average = sum / $n$).

- **$\sum w_j C_j$ (Total Weighted Completion Time):** Weighted sum, where $w_j$ reflects job importance or cost.

- **$\sum T_j$ (Total Tardiness):** Sum of tardy amounts $T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$.

- **$\sum U_j$ (Number of Late Jobs):** Count of jobs completed after their due date. A binary penalty.

- **$\sum w_j T_j$ (Total Weighted Tardiness):** Weighted tardiness variant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The *combination* of ╬▒, ╬▓, and ╬│ determines the computational complexity of the problem. Some combinations are solvable in polynomial time (e.g., $1 \mid \vert \sum C_j$ via SPT rule), while others are **NP-hard** (e.g., $1 \mid r_j \mid \sum C_j$, $F_m \mid \vert C_{\max}$, $J_m \mid \vert C_{\max}$).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the most critical notation, definitions, and complexity classifications encountered in Graham's notation.

| Element | Symbol / Notation | Definition | Common Use |
|---------|-------------------|------------|------------|
| Machine env. | $1$ | Single machine | Base case for proofs |
| Machine env. | $P_m$ | Identical parallel machines | Batch processing |
| Machine env. | $Q_m$ | Uniform parallel machines | Variable-speed machines |
| Machine env. | $R_m$ | Unrelated parallel machines | Heterogeneous machines |
| Machine env. | $F_m$ | Flow shop with $m$ machines | Assembly lines |
| Machine env. | $J_m$ | Job shop with $m$ machines | Custom manufacturing |
| Machine env. | $O$ | Open shop | Flexible routing |
| Constraint | $prec$ | Precedence among jobs | Project scheduling |
| Constraint | $pmtn$ | Preemption allowed | Real-time systems |
| Constraint | $r_j$ | Release date of job $j$ | Job arrivals |
| Constraint | $d_j$ | Due date of job $j$ | Delivery deadlines |
| Constraint | $s_{jk}$ | Setup time between $j$ and $k$ | Production changeovers |
| Objective | $C_{\max}$ | Makespan | Production capacity |
| Objective | $\sum C_j$ | Total completion time | Work-in-progress |
| Objective | $L_{\max}$ | Maximum lateness | Service guarantees |
| Objective | $\sum T_j$ | Total tardiness | Delivery penalties |
| Objective | $\sum U_j$ | Number of late jobs | Penalty scheduling |
| Complexity | $P$ | Polynomial-time solvable | Easy cases |
| Complexity | $NP$-hard | No known poly-time algorithm | Hard cases |

---

## 2.3 Why This Notation Matters in Real Engineering

In real-world industrial engineering applications, the Graham notation is not just academic ŌĆö it is the **lingua franca** of:

- **Manufacturing scheduling:** Determining how to allocate jobs to machines in an automotive assembly plant ($J_m \mid \vert C_{\max}$).
- **Project management:** Sequencing activities with precedence in construction ($1 \mid prec \mid C_{\max}$, equivalent to the **Critical Path Method**).
- **Supply chain and logistics:** Sequencing truck deliveries at a single loading dock ($1 \mid r_j \mid L_{\max}$).
- **Semiconductor manufacturing:** Wafer fabrication involves reentrant flow shops with complex setups.
- **Cloud computing and data centers:** Scheduling jobs on parallel servers ($P_m \mid r_j, pmtn \mid C_{\max}$).

By mapping a real problem to a Graham notation, practitioners can immediately tap into a vast literature of known algorithms, complexity results, and heuristics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Earliest Use of Graham's Notation

Graham's original 1966 paper introduced the notation for problems on **a single machine**. He denoted the problem as:

$$n / 1 / F$$

where $n$ was the number of jobs, $1$ denoted a single machine, and $F$ denoted an objective function (regular, non-decreasing in completion times). This was later generalized in 1979 by Graham, Lawler, Lenstra, and Rinnooy Kan (GLLR) into the modern three-field form:

$$\alpha \;\vert\; \beta \;\vert\; \gamma$$

The transition was driven by two realizations:

1. **Machine environments vary widely** ŌĆö moving beyond single machine to parallel machines, flow shops, and job shops is essential.

2. **Constraints and objectives need separate fields** ŌĆö multiple constraints often co-exist (e.g., release dates *and* precedence), and they must be enumerated independently of the optimization target.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: The SPT Optimality for $1 \mid \mid \sum C_j$

To demonstrate how Graham's notation directs the choice of an exact solution method, let us derive the **Shortest Processing Time (SPT) rule** for the problem $1 \mid \mid \sum C_j$.

### Step 1: Define the Problem Using Graham's Notation

We have:
- $\alpha = 1$: a single machine.
