- $\beta = \emptyset$: no release dates, no precedence, no preemption.
- $\gamma = \sum C_j$: minimize total completion time.

### Step 2: Set Up the Sum

Let $n$ jobs with processing times $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ be sequenced on a single machine. If job $j$ is in position $k$ of the sequence, its completion time is:

$$C_j = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{[i]}$$

The total completion time over all jobs is:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (n - k + 1) \cdot p_{[k]}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Pairwise Interchange Argument

Suppose two adjacent jobs $A$ and $B$ are scheduled with $p_A > p_B$. The contribution to $\sum C_j$ from these two positions can be expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contribution} &= (n - k + 1) p_A + (n - k) p_B \\
&= (n - k + 1) p_A + (n - k) p_B
\end{aligned}
$$

If we **swap** them, the new contribution becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contribution}_{\text{swapped}} &= (n - k + 1) p_B + (n - k) p_A
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compare the Two Contributions

The difference is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \text{Contribution} - \text{Contribution}_{\text{swapped}} \\
&= (n - k + 1) p_A + (n - k) p_B - (n - k + 1) p_B - (n - k) p_A \\
&= p_A - p_B
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $p_A > p_B$, we get $\Delta > 0$, meaning the unswapped sequence is **worse**.

### Step 5: Conclude the Optimality of SPT

By repeated pairwise interchange of any inversion (i.e., any pair with the larger job preceding the smaller), we strictly reduce $\sum C_j$. Therefore, the optimal schedule has **no inversions**, which means jobs are sorted in **non-decreasing processing time order**:

$$p_{[1]} \le p_{[2]} \le \ldots \le p_{[n]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The Graham notation $1 \mid \mid \sum C_j$ tells the operations researcher *exactly* which algorithm to apply. In contrast, $1 \mid r_j \mid \sum C_j$ is NP-hard, and no such simple rule exists.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Common Problems

The following matrix shows, for several common Graham notation problems, the **optimal algorithm** or **complexity class**:

| Graham Notation | Problem Statement | Optimal Algorithm / Complexity |
|-----------------|-------------------|--------------------------------|
| $1 \mid \mid C_{\max}$ | Single machine, minimize makespan | Trivial ŌĆö any sequence works |
| $1 \mid \mid \sum C_j$ | Single machine, minimize total completion time | **SPT rule** (polynomial) |
| $1 \mid \mid \sum w_j C_j$ | Weighted variant | **WSPT rule** (polynomial) |
| $1 \mid \mid L_{\max}$ | Minimize max lateness | **EDD rule** ŌĆö earliest due date (polynomial) |
| $1 \mid r_j \mid C_{\max}$ | With release dates | **Schrage algorithm** (polynomial) |
| $1 \mid r_j \mid \sum C_j$ | Total completion with release dates | **NP-hard** |
| $1 \mid prec \mid C_{\max}$ | With precedence | List scheduling (polynomial upper bound) |
| $P_m \mid \mid C_{\max}$ | Parallel identical machines | **Longest Processing Time first on most loaded machine (LPT)** ŌĆö heuristic, NP-hard for $m \ge 2$ |
| $F_m \mid \mid C_{\max}$ | Permutation flow shop | **Johnson's rule** for $m=2$; NP-hard for $m \ge 3$ |
| $J_m \mid \mid C_{\max}$ | Job shop | Strongly **NP-hard** (even for $m=2$) |
| $O \mid \mid C_{\max}$ | Open shop | Polynomial via matching (Gonzalez & Sahni) |

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example: Decoding a Problem Statement

**Given notation:** $\;P_3 \mid pmtn, r_j \mid \sum w_j C_j$

**Decode step by step:**

1. **╬▒ = $P_3$:** Three identical parallel machines exist; any job can be processed on any machine with the same processing time.

2. **╬▓ = $pmtn, r_j$:**
   - $pmtn$: Preemption is permitted ŌĆö a job's processing can be paused and resumed.
   - $r_j$: Each job $j$ has a release date $r_j$ before which it cannot start.

3. **╬│ = $\sum w_j C_j$:** Minimize the total weighted completion time, where $w_j$ is the weight of job $j$ and $C_j$ is its completion time.

**Solution approach:** This problem is **NP-hard** in general, but with preemption permitted and identical machines, the **WSPT (Weighted Shortest Processing Time) rule** combined with a priority-list scheduler can be used as a heuristic. The preemption capability ensures that high-weight short jobs can interrupt lower-priority work when they become available.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of Graham's ╬▒ | ╬▓ | ╬│ Classification

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the three fields of Graham's notation decompose a scheduling problem into three independent classification dimensions, with examples branching from each.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SCHEDULING PROBLEM"] --> B["Alpha Field: Machine Environment"]
    A --> C["Beta Field: Job Characteristics"]
    A --> D["Gamma Field: Objective Function"]

    B --> B1["1 - Single Machine"]
    B --> B2["Pm - Identical Parallel Machines"]
    B --> B3["Qm - Uniform Parallel Machines"]
    B --> B4["Rm - Unrelated Parallel Machines"]
    B --> B5["Fm - Flow Shop m Machines"]
    B --> B6["Jm - Job Shop m Machines"]
    B --> B7["O - Open Shop"]

    C --> C1["prec - Precedence Constraints"]
    C --> C2["pmtn - Preemption Allowed"]
    C --> C3["rj - Release Dates"]
    C --> C4["dj - Due Dates"]
    C --> C5["sjk - Sequence Dependent Setup"]
    C --> C6["Empty - No Special Constraints"]

    D --> D1["Cmax - Makespan"]
    D --> D2["Sum Cj - Total Completion"]
    D --> D3["Sum wj Cj - Weighted Completion"]
    D --> D4["Lmax - Max Lateness"]
    D --> D5["Sum Tj - Total Tardiness"]
    D --> D6["Sum Uj - Number Late Jobs"]

    E["FULL PROBLEM STATEMENT"] --> F["Example 1: 1 to SPT to polynomial"]
    E --> G["Example 2: Fm to NPhard for m greater 3"]
    E --> H["Example 3: Jm to Strongly NPhard"]

    B2 --> E
    C6 --> F
    D1 --> F
    B5 --> G
    C6 --> G
    D1 --> G
    B6 --> H
    C6 --> H
    D1 --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each axis of the classification (machine environment, job constraints, objective) is independent. The combination of choices from the three axes uniquely determines a scheduling problem. The bottom path shows how three *different* problem instances (despite varying complexity) all stem from the same three-field structure.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Mapping of Machine Environments

The following Mermaid diagram shows the hierarchical relationship between machine environments ŌĆö moving from simplest (single machine) to most complex (open shop with mixed routing).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["1 - Single Machine"]:::simple --> PM["Pm - Identical Parallel"]
    PM --> QM["Qm - Uniform Parallel"]
    QM --> RM["Rm - Unrelated Parallel"]
    RM --> FM["Fm - Flow Shop"]
    FM --> JM["Jm - Job Shop"]
    JM --> OS["O - Open Shop"]:::complex

    M1:::simple
    OS:::complex
    classDef simple fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
    classDef complex fill:#fdd4d4,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Hierarchy:** Complexity generally increases as you move right. Each successive environment *generalizes* the previous one ŌĆö a single machine is a trivial case of $P_m$ (with $m=1$), and a flow shop is a special case of a job shop (where all jobs share the same route).

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Problem to Algorithm

The following diagram maps the workflow a scheduling practitioner follows when encountering a real problem and resolving it through Graham's notation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Identify Real World Problem"] --> S2["Step 2: Identify Machine Count and Type"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Identify Constraints on Jobs"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Identify Optimization Goal"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Write Graham Notation Alpha Beta Gamma"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Look Up Complexity Result"]
    S6 --> S7A{"Polynomial?"}
    S7A -- Yes --> S8A["Apply Exact Algorithm"]
    S7A -- No --> S8B["Use Heuristic or Branch and Bound"]
    S8A --> S9["Verify and Deploy"]
    S8B --> S9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]
**Explain the three-field Graham notation used for classifying scheduling problems. Illustrate with one example.**

**Model Answer:**

Graham's three-field notation classifies any deterministic scheduling problem compactly as $\alpha \mid \beta \mid \gamma$.

- **╬▒ field (Machine Environment):** Describes the processing infrastructure. Examples include $1$ (single machine), $P_m$ (identical parallel machines), $F_m$ (flow shop), $J_m$ (job shop).

- **╬▓ field (Job Characteristics):** Lists constraints like $prec$ (precedence), $pmtn$ (preemption), $r_j$ (release dates), $d_j$ (due dates). May be empty if no special constraints exist.

- **╬│ field (Objective Function):** Specifies what to optimize, e.g., $C_{\max}$ (makespan), $\sum C_j$ (total completion time), $L_{\max}$ (max lateness).

**Example:** $1 \mid r_j \mid \sum C_j$ denotes a single-machine scheduling problem with release dates, minimizing total completion time.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between $F_m$ and $J_m$ in Graham's notation. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $F_m$ (Flow Shop) | $J_m$ (Job Shop) |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| Job routing | All jobs follow **the same sequence** through machines | Each job has its **own predetermined route** |
| Flexibility | Low ŌĆö fixed production line | High ŌĆö custom routing per job |
| Real-world example | Automobile assembly line | Machine shop producing custom parts |

A flow shop is highly structured and benefits from **Johnson's rule** (for $m=2$), while a job shop is far more complex and generally NP-hard.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]

**(a)** With neat examples, classify the following scheduling problems using Graham's $\alpha \mid \beta \mid \gamma$ notation: **(7 Marks)**
   1. Single machine, jobs have release dates, objective is to minimize the maximum completion time.
   2. Two identical parallel machines, sequence-dependent setup times, minimize makespan.
   3. A 5-machine job shop, no special constraints, minimize makespan.

**(b)** Explain in detail the components of the ╬▒ field (machine environment) of Graham's notation. List at least five distinct machine environments with brief descriptions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification:**

1. **$1 \mid r_j \mid C_{\max}$**: Single machine ($\alpha = 1$), with release dates ($\beta = r_j$), minimize makespan ($\gamma = C_{\max}$). Solvable in polynomial time via Schrage's algorithm.

2. **$P_2 \mid s_{jk} \mid C_{\max}$**: Two identical parallel machines ($\alpha = P_2$), with sequence-dependent setup times ($\beta = s_{jk}$), minimize makespan. Setup times make this NP-hard even for $m = 2$.

3. **$J_5 \mid \mid C_{\max}$**: Five-machine job shop ($\alpha = J_5$), no constraints ($\beta = \emptyset$), minimize makespan. Strongly NP-hard; solved using shifting bottleneck heuristic in practice.

**(b) Components of the ╬▒ Field:**

The ╬▒ field specifies the machine environment ŌĆö the resource infrastructure on which jobs are processed:

1. **$1$ ŌĆö Single Machine:** Only one machine exists; all jobs must be queued and processed sequentially. The scheduling decision reduces to ordering jobs.

2. **$P_m$ ŌĆö Identical Parallel Machines:** $m$ machines with identical speeds process jobs in parallel. A job can be assigned to any machine.

3. **$Q_m$ ŌĆö Uniform Parallel Machines:** $m$ machines with different speeds $s_i$. Job $j$'s processing time on machine $i$ is $p_j / s_i$.

4. **$R_m$ ŌĆö Unrelated Parallel Machines:** $m$ machines, each with completely independent processing times $p_{ij}$ for job $j$.

5. **$F_m$ ŌĆö Flow Shop:** All jobs follow the same processing order through $m$ machines in series. Common in assembly-line manufacturing.

6. **$J_m$ ŌĆö Job Shop:** Each job has its own predetermined route through the $m$ machines. Common in custom manufacturing.

7. **$O$ ŌĆö Open Shop:** Jobs must visit all machines, but the order is decided by the scheduler. Common in testing facilities.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]

**(a)** Discuss the ╬▓ field of Graham's notation. List at least five job characteristics and explain their practical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single machine processes 5 jobs with processing times $p_1=3, p_2=5, p_3=2, p_4=4, p_5=1$. Using Graham's notation, identify the problem, and find the optimal sequence to minimize $\sum C_j$. Verify using the SPT rule. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The ╬▓ Field ŌĆö Job Characteristics:**

The ╬▓ field enumerates any constraints that apply to jobs. It can be empty ($\beta = \emptyset$) if no constraints exist, or it can list multiple attributes separated by commas:

1. **$prec$ ŌĆö Precedence Constraints:** A partial order specifies that some jobs must complete before others can begin. Used in project scheduling (e.g., construction).

2. **$pmtn$ ŌĆö Preemption:** Allows jobs to be interrupted and resumed later. Useful in real-time systems or when higher-priority jobs arrive.

3. **$r_j$ ŌĆö Release Dates:** Job $j$ cannot start before time $r_j$. Models job arrivals in dynamic scheduling.

4. **$d_j$ ŌĆö Due Dates:** Job $j$ should ideally complete by $d_j$. Critical in due-date-based objectives like $L_{\max}$ or $\sum T_j$.

5. **$s_{jk}$ ŌĆö Sequence-Dependent Setup Times:** Time required to switch from job $j$ to job $k$. Common in chemical or printing industries.

6. **$p_j = p$ ŌĆö Equal Processing Times:** A special case where all jobs require the same processing duration.

**(b) Worked Example:**

**Identification using Graham's Notation:**

The problem is:
$$1 \mid \mid \sum C_j$$

A single machine, no special constraints, minimize total completion time.

**Applying SPT (Shortest Processing Time) Rule:**

Sort jobs by processing time in ascending order:

$$p_5 = 1 < p_3 = 2 < p_1 = 3 < p_4 = 4 < p_2 = 5$$

So the optimal sequence is: **Job 5 ŌåÆ Job 3 ŌåÆ Job 1 ŌåÆ Job 4 ŌåÆ Job 2**

**Verification by Computing $\sum C_j$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_5 &= 1 \\
C_3 &= 1 + 2 = 3 \\
C_1 &= 1 + 2 + 3 = 6 \\
C_4 &= 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10 \\
C_2 &= 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\sum C_j = 1 + 3 + 6 + 10 + 15 = 35$$

Any other ordering yields a strictly larger $\sum C_j$ (by the SPT optimality theorem proved in Section 3.2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $P_m$, $Q_m$, and $R_m$:** Students often treat these as interchangeable. Remember ŌĆö $P_m$ means *identical* speeds, $Q_m$ means *proportional* speeds, and $R_m$ means *arbitrary* speeds. Using the wrong symbol loses easy marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the ╬▓ field when empty:** Always write $\beta = \emptyset$ or use empty notation $\mid \mid$ rather than omitting the field entirely.
>
> 3. **Mixing $C_j$, $T_j$, $U_j$, and $L_j$ objectives:** $C_j$ is completion time, $T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$ is tardiness, $U_j \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates lateness, and $L_j = C_j - d_j$ is lateness (which can be negative). Confusing these leads to wrong answers.
>
> 4. **Not identifying the complexity class:** KTU examiners frequently award partial marks for *correctly classifying* a problem as polynomial or NP-hard. Do not skip this.
>
> 5. **Treating $F_m$ and $J_m$ as the same:** They are *not*. The job shop allows different routes per job; the flow shop forces a common route.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - Graham's notation is **╬▒ | ╬▓ | ╬│**: machine environment | job constraints | objective.
> - **╬▒ field:** $1$ (single), $P_m$ (identical parallel), $Q_m$ (uniform), $R_m$ (unrelated), $F_m$ (flow shop), $J_m$ (job shop), $O$ (open shop).
> - **╬▓ field:** $prec$, $pmtn$, $r_j$, $d_j$, $s_{jk}$. Empty means no constraints.
> - **╬│ field:** $C_{\max}$, $\sum C_j$, $\sum w_j C_j$, $L_{\max}$, $\sum T_j$, $\sum U_j$.
> - **Complexity varies by combination:** $1 \mid \mid \sum C_j$ ŌåÆ polynomial (SPT). $1 \mid r_j \mid \sum C_j$ ŌåÆ NP-hard. $F_m \mid \mid C_{\max}$ ŌåÆ NP-hard for $m \geq 3$.
> - **Johnson's rule** solves $F_2 \mid \mid C_{\max}$ optimally.
> - **EDD (Earliest Due Date)** solves $1 \mid \mid L_{\max}$ optimally.
> - **WSPT** solves $1 \mid \mid \sum w_j C_j$ optimally.
> - **Job shop scheduling** ($J_m \mid \mid C_{\max}$) is one of the hardest classical scheduling problems.
> - Always **encode problems using Graham's notation** as the first step of any scheduling analysis.
> - The notation is the **universal language** of scheduling research and practice ŌĆö mastery is essential for KTU exams and beyond.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performance Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of **Industrial Scheduling** (a sub-domain of Operations Research and Production Engineering), **Performance Measures** (also called *scheduling criteria*, *scheduling objectives*, or *optimality criteria*) are the quantitative yardsticks used to evaluate the quality, efficiency, and effectiveness of a generated schedule. Given a set of $n$ jobs $J = \{J_1, J_2, \ldots, J_n\}$ to be processed on $m$ machines under defined constraints, a performance measure $f(\pi)$ maps a feasible schedule (permutation $\pi$) to a real-valued scalar that quantifies its desirability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> Performance measures are mathematical functions that evaluate a schedule based on criteria such as **completion time, flow time, lateness, tardiness, makespan, and machine utilization**. The choice of performance measure dictates the optimization objective: *minimization* (e.g., minimize makespan) or *maximization* (e.g., maximize throughput).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Hospital Operating Theatre

Imagine a hospital with **3 operating theatres** (machines) and **6 surgeries** (jobs) scheduled for a single day. The Chief Surgeon (scheduler) must decide the order of operations.

- If the surgeon cares about **finishing all surgeries as early as possible** to free up the night-shift staff ŌåÆ she minimizes the **Makespan** ($C_{\max}$).
- If she cares about **patients who have pre-booked specific appointment slots** (due dates) ŌåÆ she minimizes **Maximum Lateness** ($L_{\max}$) so that no patient waits excessively past their slot.
- If she worries about **how long each patient stays in the hospital from admission to discharge** ŌåÆ she minimizes **Total Flow Time** ($\sum F_j$).
- If she only cares about **how many patients miss their appointment** ŌåÆ she minimizes the **Number of Tardy Jobs** ($\sum U_j$).

Every scheduling objective is a stakeholder's preference encoded in mathematics.

### 1.3 Classification of Performance Measures

Performance measures are broadly classified into three families based on what aspect of the schedule they capture:

| Family | What It Captures | Typical Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Completion-time based** | How quickly jobs are finished | $C_{\max},\ \sum C_j$ |
| **Due-date based** | Adherence to promised delivery dates | $L_{\max},\ \sum T_j,\ \sum U_j$ |
| **Inventory / WIP based** | Work-in-process accumulation | $\sum F_j,\ \sum I_j$ |

> [!NOTE]
> A single schedule **cannot** simultaneously minimize all performance measures. There is a fundamental **trade-off**: minimizing makespan may increase total tardiness, and vice versa. This is known as the **scheduling trade-off principle**.

### 1.4 Elementary Notation Used Throughout

For any job $J_j$ processed in a schedule:

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $p_j$ | Processing time of job $j$ | hours / minutes |
| $r_j$ | Release date (earliest availability) | time units |
| $d_j$ | Due date (promised delivery) | time units |
| $C_j$ | **Completion time** of job $j$ | time units |
| $F_j$ | **Flow time** of job $j$ | time units |
| $L_j$ | **Lateness** of job $j$ | time units (can be negative) |
| $T_j$ | **Tardiness** of job $j$ | time units ($\geq 0$) |
| $E_j$ | **Earliness** of job $j$ | time units ($\geq 0$) |
| $U_j$ | **Unit penalty** (binary indicator) | $0$ or $1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Always distinguish between $L_j$ (lateness, can be negative) and $T_j$ (tardiness, always non-negative). Examiners frequently award partial marks for failing to clarify this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pointwise (Per-Job) Performance Measures

These measures are computed **for each individual job** $J_j$ and then aggregated.

**2.1.1 Completion Time ($C_j$)**
The instant at which job $J_j$ finishes processing.
$$C_j = \text{time at which } J_j \text{ completes all its operations}$$

**2.1.2 Flow Time ($F_j$)**
The total time a job spends in the system ŌĆö from release to completion.
$$F_j = C_j - r_j$$

If all jobs are released at time zero ($r_j = 0$ for all $j$), then $F_j = C_j$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters:** Flow time is the **primary driver of Work-In-Process (WIP) inventory**. By **Little's Law**, $\text{Average WIP} = \text{Average Flow Time} \times \text{Throughput Rate}$. Thus, minimizing $\sum F_j$ is equivalent to minimizing WIP holding costs.

**2.1.3 Lateness ($L_j$)**
The deviation (positive or negative) of completion from the due date.
$$L_j = C_j - d_j$$

- $L_j > 0 \Rightarrow$ job is **late**
- $L_j < 0 \Rightarrow$ job is **early**
- $L_j = 0 \Rightarrow$ job is **exactly on time**

**2.1.4 Tardiness ($T_j$)**
The amount of time *by which* a job is late, ignoring early completions.
$$T_j = \max(0,\ L_j) = \max(0,\ C_j - d_j)$$

**2.1.5 Earliness ($E_j$)**
The amount of time *by which* a job finishes before its due date.
$$E_j = \max(0,\ d_j - C_j)$$

**2.1.6 Unit Penalty ($U_j$)**
A binary indicator ŌĆö 1 if the job is tardy, 0 otherwise.
$$U_j = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } C_j > d_j \\ 0, & \text{if } C_j \leq d_j \end{cases}$$

### 2.2 System-Wide (Aggregate) Performance Measures

These measures are computed over the **entire job set** and form the actual optimization objectives.

| Symbol | Name | Formula | Minimized When... |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{\max}$ | Makespan | $\max_j C_j$ | last job finishes earliest |
| $F_{\max}$ | Maximum Flow Time | $\max_j F_j$ | longest-staying job leaves earliest |
| $L_{\max}$ | Maximum Lateness | $\max_j L_j$ | worst delay is smallest |
| $T_{\max}$ | Maximum Tardiness | $\max_j T_j$ | worst tardiness is smallest |
| $\sum C_j$ | Total Completion Time | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j$ | average job finishes early |
| $\sum F_j$ | Total Flow Time | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j$ | average WIP is minimized |
| $\sum T_j$ | Total Tardiness | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} T_j$ | total penalty for late jobs is small |
| $\sum U_j$ | Number of Tardy Jobs | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} U_j$ | fewest jobs miss due dates |
| $\sum E_j$ | Total Earliness | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} E_j$ | no job finishes too early (avoids storage) |
| $\sum (E_j + T_j)$ | Total E/T Penalty | $\sum (E_j + T_j)$ | just-in-time delivery |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Golden Formulas** ŌĆö memorize these in LaTeX-exact form for KTU exams:

$$
\boxed{F_j = C_j - r_j}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_j = C_j - d_j}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_j = \max(0,\ C_j - d_j)}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_j = \max(0,\ d_j - C_j)}
$$

$$
\boxed{U_j = 1\ \text{if}\ C_j > d_j,\ \text{else}\ 0}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_j + E_j = \vert C_j - d_j \vert = \vert L_j \vert}
$$

### 2.4 Critical Mathematical Relationships (Prove in Derivations)

These identities are frequently tested in KTU exams (especially Part B sub-parts worth 7 marks):

**Identity 1: Flow Time = Completion Time (when $r_j = 0$)**
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j - \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_j$$

**Identity 2: Lateness Decomposition**
$$L_j = T_j - E_j$$

**Identity 3: Tardiness + Earliness equals absolute lateness**
$$T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$$

**Identity 4: Makespan upper bound**
$$C_{\max} \geq \max_j d_j \quad \text{(no schedule can finish before the latest due date)}$$

**Identity 5: Lower bound for makespan**
$$C_{\max} \geq \max\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j,\ \max_j d_j,\ \text{machine load on bottleneck}\right)$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Primary Measure Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Steel / Heavy Manufacturing** | $C_{\max}$ | Furnace utilization dominates cost |
| **E-commerce Warehousing** | $\sum F_j$ | Order throughput and WIP control |
| **Aerospace MRO** | $L_{\max}$ | Hard contractual delivery windows |
| **Banking / Back-office** | $\sum T_j$ | Customer SLA penalties |
| **Food Cold-Chain** | $\sum E_j$ | Early delivery wastes refrigeration |
| **Automotive JIT** | $\sum (E_j + T_j)$ | Toyota Production System philosophy |

### 2.6 Graphical Visualization ŌĆö The "Lateness Map"

```
         C_j < d_j           C_j = d_j           C_j > d_j
         ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ         ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ         ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
         Ōöé  EARLY  Ōöé         Ōöé ON-TIME Ōöé         Ōöé  LATE   Ōöé
         Ōöé E_j > 0 Ōöé         Ōöé L_j = 0 Ōöé         Ōöé L_j > 0 Ōöé
         Ōöé T_j = 0 Ōöé         Ōöé T_j = 0 Ōöé         Ōöé T_j > 0 Ōöé
         ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś         ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś         ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
              Ōåæ                                       Ōåæ
         Job ahead of                         Job behind
         schedule                             schedule
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU board's preferred notation, **earliness and tardiness are NOT the same as lateness**. Lateness can be negative (early), but tardiness is strictly non-negative.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Relationship Between $F_j$, $C_j$, and $r_j$

**Statement to Prove:** $F_j = C_j - r_j$

**Proof:**

Let the system be observed continuously over time.

- At time instant $r_j$, job $J_j$ **enters** the system (becomes available for processing). Prior to $r_j$, the job has not arrived and cannot be processed.
- At time instant $C_j$, job $J_j$ **leaves** the system (completes its final operation).

The **flow time** is defined as the duration the job spends inside the system boundary, i.e., the elapsed time between entry and exit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_j &\stackrel{\text{def}}{=} \text{(Exit Time)} - \text{(Entry Time)} \\[6pt]
F_j &= C_j - r_j \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Corollary (Aggregate Form):** Summing over all $n$ jobs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j &= \sum_{j=1}^{n} (C_j - r_j) \\
&= \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j - \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Special Case:** If the system has no release-date restrictions (i.e., all jobs are available at time zero, so $r_j = 0$ for all $j$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j &= \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j - \sum_{j=1}^{n} 0 \\
&= \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, in the **zero-release-date case**, total flow time equals total completion time.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Equivalence of $T_j + E_j$ to $\vert L_j \vert$

**Statement to Prove:** $T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$ where $L_j = C_j - d_j$.

**Proof by Case Analysis:**

**Case A: $C_j \geq d_j$ (Job is on-time or late)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_j &= C_j - d_j \geq 0 \\
T_j &= \max(0,\ L_j) = L_j \\
E_j &= \max(0,\ d_j - C_j) = \max(0,\ -L_j) = 0 \\
\therefore\ T_j + E_j &= L_j + 0 = L_j = \vert L_j \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case B: $C_j < d_j$ (Job is early)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_j &= C_j - d_j < 0 \\
T_j &= \max(0,\ C_j - d_j) = \max(0,\ \text{negative}) = 0 \\
E_j &= \max(0,\ d_j - C_j) = d_j - C_j = -(C_j - d_j) = -L_j = \vert L_j \vert \\
\therefore\ T_j + E_j &= 0 + \vert L_j \vert = \vert L_j \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

In both cases, $T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> This identity is the **mathematical foundation of Just-In-Time (JIT) scheduling** ŌĆö the objective $\min \sum (T_j + E_j) = \min \sum \vert L_j \vert$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö $L_j = T_j - E_j$ (Signed Lateness Decomposition)

**Statement to Prove:** $L_j = T_j - E_j$

**Proof:**

From the previous derivation, in Case A ($C_j \geq d_j$):
$T_j = L_j$ and $E_j = 0$, so $T_j - E_j = L_j - 0 = L_j$. Ō£ō

In Case B ($C_j < d_j$):
$T_j = 0$ and $E_j = -L_j$, so $T_j - E_j = 0 - (-L_j) = L_j$. Ō£ō

$$
\therefore\ L_j = T_j - E_j \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Aggregate Form:**
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} T_j - \sum_{j=1}^{n} E_j$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Comprehensive Calculation

**Problem:** Consider $n = 4$ jobs with the following data. All jobs are released at time $r_j = 0$. The schedule sequence is $J_1 \to J_2 \to J_3 \to J_4$ on a single machine.

| Job $j$ | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 7 |
| 2 | 3 | 9 |
| 3 | 7 | 12 |
| 4 | 4 | 10 |

**Step 1: Compute Completion Times ($C_j$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 0 + p_1 = 0 + 5 = 5 \\
C_2 &= C_1 + p_2 = 5 + 3 = 8 \\
C_3 &= C_2 + p_3 = 8 + 7 = 15 \\
C_4 &= C_3 + p_4 = 15 + 4 = 19
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Flow Times ($F_j = C_j$ since $r_j = 0$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 5,\quad F_2 = 8,\quad F_3 = 15,\quad F_4 = 19
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Lateness ($L_j = C_j - d_j$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= 5 - 7 = -2 \\
L_2 &= 8 - 9 = -1 \\
L_3 &= 15 - 12 = +3 \\
L_4 &= 19 - 10 = +9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Tardiness ($T_j = \max(0, L_j)$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \max(0, -2) = 0 \\
T_2 &= \max(0, -1) = 0 \\
T_3 &= \max(0, +3) = 3 \\
T_4 &= \max(0, +9) = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Earliness ($E_j = \max(0, d_j - C_j)$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= \max(0, 7-5) = 2 \\
E_2 &= \max(0, 9-8) = 1 \\
E_3 &= \max(0, 12-15) = 0 \\
E_4 &= \max(0, 10-19) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Unit Penalty ($U_j$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= 0\ (C_1=5 \leq d_1=7) \\
U_2 &= 0\ (C_2=8 \leq d_2=9) \\
U_3 &= 1\ (C_3=15 > d_3=12) \\
U_4 &= 1\ (C_4=19 > d_4=10)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute Aggregate Performance Measures**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\max} &= \max(5, 8, 15, 19) = 19 \\
F_{\max} &= \max(5, 8, 15, 19) = 19 \\
L_{\max} &= \max(-2, -1, 3, 9) = 9 \\
T_{\max} &= \max(0, 0, 3, 9) = 9 \\
\sum C_j &= 5 + 8 + 15 + 19 = 47 \\
\sum F_j &= 5 + 8 + 15 + 19 = 47 \quad (\text{since } r_j = 0) \\
\sum T_j &= 0 + 0 + 3 + 9 = 12 \\
\sum U_j &= 0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 2 \\
\sum (E_j + T_j) &= (2+0) + (1+0) + (0+3) + (0+9) = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of Identity $T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 + E_1 = 0 + 2 &= 2 = \vert -2 \vert = \vert L_1 \vert \ \checkmark \\
T_2 + E_2 = 0 + 1 &= 1 = \vert -1 \vert = \vert L_2 \vert \ \checkmark \\
T_3 + E_3 = 3 + 0 &= 3 = \vert +3 \vert = \vert L_3 \vert \ \checkmark \\
T_4 + E_4 = 9 + 0 &= 9 = \vert +9 \vert = \vert L_4 \vert \ \checkmark \\
\text{Sum: } 2 + 1 + 3 + 9 &= 15 = \sum \vert L_j \vert \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of Identity $L_j = T_j - E_j$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 = -2 &= 0 - 2 = T_1 - E_1 \ \checkmark \\
L_2 = -1 &= 0 - 1 = T_2 - E_2 \ \checkmark \\
L_3 = +3 &= 3 - 0 = T_3 - E_3 \ \checkmark \\
L_4 = +9 &= 9 - 0 = T_4 - E_4 \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö Which Measure to Minimize?

| If the Production Environment Demands... | Then Optimize... | Because... |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum machine throughput | $\min C_{\max}$ | Frees bottleneck earliest |
| Low WIP and quick turnover | $\min \sum C_j$ or $\min \sum F_j$ | Reduces inventory carrying cost |
| Strict on-time delivery (e.g., airlines) | $\min L_{\max}$ | Worst-case delay governs SLA |
| Customer satisfaction with no hard deadline | $\min \sum T_j$ | Graduated penalty for lateness |
| No late deliveries tolerated (hard constraint) | $\min \sum U_j$ | Each late job is a disaster |
| JIT / Lean manufacturing | $\min \sum (E_j + T_j)$ | Penalizes both early and late equally |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** In a 14-mark question, always begin with the table of computed $C_j, F_j, L_j, T_j, E_j, U_j$ values before stating the aggregate. Examiners award 2ŌĆō3 marks for the tabulation alone.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Performance Measure Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scheduling Performance Measures] --> B[Pointwise Measures - per job]
    A --> C[Aggregate Measures - system wide]

    B --> B1[Completion Time Cj]
    B --> B2[Flow Time Fj = Cj - rj]
    B --> B3[Lateness Lj = Cj - dj]
    B --> B4[Tardiness Tj = max 0 Lj]
    B --> B5[Earliness Ej = max 0 dj - Cj]
    B --> B6[Unit Penalty Uj binary]

    C --> C1[Time Based Criteria]
    C --> C2[Due Date Based Criteria]

    C1 --> C1a[Makespan Cmax]
    C1 --> C1b[Total Completion Time sum Cj]
    C1 --> C1c[Total Flow Time sum Fj]
    C1 --> C1d[Max Flow Time Fmax]

    C2 --> C2a[Max Lateness Lmax]
    C2 --> C2b[Max Tardiness Tmax]
    C2 --> C2c[Total Tardiness sum Tj]
    C2 --> C2d[Number of Tardy Jobs sum Uj]
    C2 --> C2e[Total Earliness sum Ej]
    C2 --> C2f[Just In Time sum Ej plus Tj]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow ŌĆö Job Life-Cycle & Performance Measurement Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Job Requested] -->|rj| QUEUE[In Queue / WIP]
    QUEUE -->|waits| START[Processing Begins]
    START -->|duration pj| FINISH[Job Completes]
    FINISH -->|measured Cj| META[Performance Evaluation]

    META --> Fcalc[Fj = Cj - rj]
    META --> Lcalc[Lj = Cj - dj]
    META --> Tcalc[Tj = max 0 Lj]
    META --> Ecalc[Ej = max 0 dj - Cj]
    META --> Ucalc[Uj = 1 if Cj greater dj else 0]

    Fcalc --> AGG1[Aggregate Metrics]
    Lcalc --> AGG1
    Tcalc --> AGG1
    Ecalc --> AGG1
    Ucalc --> AGG1

    AGG1 --> R1[Cmax Fmax sum Cj sum Fj]
    AGG1 --> R2[Lmax Tmax sum Tj sum Uj]
    AGG1 --> R3[sum Ej and sum Ej plus Tj]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Machine ŌĆö Job's Lateness Status

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NotReleased : waiting
    NotReleased --> InSystem : time equals rj
    InSystem --> Processing : machine available
    Processing --> CompletedEarly : Cj less than dj
    Processing --> CompletedOnTime : Cj equals dj
    Processing --> CompletedLate : Cj greater than dj

    CompletedEarly --> EarlinessPositive : Ej greater than 0
    CompletedEarly --> TardinessZero : Tj equals 0
    CompletedEarly --> LatenessNegative : Lj less than 0

    CompletedOnTime --> EarlinessZero : Ej equals 0
    CompletedOnTime --> TardinessZero : Tj equals 0
    CompletedOnTime --> LatenessZero : Lj equals 0

    CompletedLate --> EarlinessZero : Ej equals 0
    CompletedLate --> TardinessPositive : Tj greater than 0
    CompletedLate --> LatenessPositive : Lj greater than 0
    CompletedLate --> UnitPenaltyOne : Uj equals 1
```

### 4.4 Gantt-Style Timeline (Textual Schematic)

```
Time ŌåÆ   0    5    10   15   20
         |----|----|----|----|
Job 1:   [J1 ]                     (p1=5, d1=7)  ŌåÆ Early
Job 2:        [J2]                  (p2=3, d2=9)  ŌåÆ Early
Job 3:            [   J3   ]       (p3=7, d3=12) ŌåÆ LATE
Job 4:                      [J4]   (p4=4, d4=10) ŌåÆ LATE
                                 Ōåæ
                              Cmax = 19
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The horizontal axis is **time**.
- Each bar represents a job's **processing window** on the machine.
- The completion instant (right edge of each bar) is $C_j$.
- The vertical "d_j" markers (not shown for brevity) would indicate due dates.

> [!NOTE]
> This timeline is the standard KTU 14-mark question visualization. Always draw it before computing any aggregate measure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*Define the following scheduling performance measures: (i) Makespan, (ii) Maximum Lateness, (iii) Number of Tardy Jobs. State the unit/scale of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Makespan ($C_{\max}$):** It is the **completion time of the last job to finish** processing in a schedule. Mathematically, $C_{\max} = \max_{1 \leq j \leq n} C_j$. It represents the total elapsed time from schedule start to schedule completion. **Scale:** Same as time (hours, days, etc.).

(ii) **Maximum Lateness ($L_{\max}$):** It is the **largest value of lateness across all jobs**, i.e., $L_{\max} = \max_{1 \leq j \leq n} L_j$ where $L_j = C_j - d_j$. It measures the worst-case deviation from any due date and can be positive, zero, or negative. **Scale:** Same as time.

(iii) **Number of Tardy Jobs ($\sum U_j$):** It is the **count of jobs that complete after their due dates**, where $U_j = 1$ if $C_j > d_j$ and $0$ otherwise. It is a **dimensionless integer** in the range $[0, n]$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
*Distinguish clearly between **lateness** and **tardiness** of a job. Illustrate with a numerical example where the two values differ.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Lateness ($L_j$):** Signed deviation of completion from due date; $L_j = C_j - d_j$. Can be **positive (late)**, **zero (on time)**, or **negative (early)**.
- **Tardiness ($T_j$):** Non-negative amount of lateness; $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$. Always $\geq 0$.

**Numerical Example:** If $C_j = 12$ and $d_j = 15$, then:
- $L_j = 12 - 15 = -3$ (job is **early by 3 units**)
- $T_j = \max(0, -3) = 0$ (job is **not tardy**)

This shows $L_j \neq T_j$ in general. They coincide **only when** $C_j \geq d_j$.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the various **pointwise** and **aggregate** performance measures used in single-machine and multi-machine scheduling. State the formula for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following 5 jobs to be processed on a single machine in the sequence $J_3 \to J_1 \to J_4 \to J_5 \to J_2$. All jobs are released at time 0. Compute **$C_j$, $F_j$, $L_j$, $T_j$, $E_j$, $U_j$** for every job, and then evaluate the aggregate measures $C_{\max},\ L_{\max},\ T_{\max},\ \sum C_j,\ \sum F_j,\ \sum T_j,\ \sum U_j$. **(7 marks)**

| Job | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6 | 14 |
| 2 | 4 | 10 |
| 3 | 5 | 8 |
| 4 | 8 | 20 |
| 5 | 3 | 16 |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

A scheduling problem involves assigning jobs to machines over time. To compare different feasible schedules, we need quantitative measures called **performance measures** (or scheduling criteria). They fall into two categories:

**A. Pointwise (per-job) Measures:** Computed for each job individually.

1. **Completion Time ($C_j$):** The instant at which job $J_j$ finishes. $C_j = C_{j-1} + p_j$ (sequential case).
2. **Flow Time ($F_j$):** Time spent in the system. $F_j = C_j - r_j$.
3. **Lateness ($L_j$):** Signed deviation from due date. $L_j = C_j - d_j$.
4. **Tardiness ($T_j$):** Non-negative lateness. $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$.
5. **Earliness ($E_j$):** Non-negative early completion. $E_j = \max(0, d_j - C_j)$.
6. **Unit Penalty ($U_j$):** Binary tardiness indicator. $U_j = 1$ iff $C_j > d_j$.

**B. Aggregate (system-wide) Measures:** Computed over all jobs ŌĆö these are the actual optimization objectives.

1. **Makespan ($C_{\max}$):** $\max_j C_j$ ŌĆö total schedule length.
2. **Maximum Lateness ($L_{\max}$):** $\max_j L_j$ ŌĆö worst-case lateness.
3. **Total Tardiness ($\sum T_j$):** Sum of all tardiness values.
4. **Number of Tardy Jobs ($\sum U_j$):** Count of late jobs.
5. **Total Flow Time ($\sum F_j$):** Sum of all flow times.
6. **Total Completion Time ($\sum C_j$):** Sum of all completion times.
7. **Total Earliness ($\sum E_j$):** Used in JIT objectives.
8. **Just-In-Time penalty ($\sum (E_j + T_j)$):** Penalizes deviations in either direction.

These measures are central to KTU's classification of scheduling problems in the $1 \vert \cdot \vert \gamma$ notation, where $\gamma$ denotes the objective.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Sequence: $J_3 \to J_1 \to J_4 \to J_5 \to J_2$.

**Step 1: Tabulate per-job parameters and compute completion times.**

| Job (in order) | $p_j$ | $d_j$ | $C_j$ | $F_j = C_j - 0$ | $L_j = C_j - d_j$ | $T_j$ | $E_j$ | $U_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $J_3$ (1st) | 5 | 8 | 5 | 5 | $5-8=-3$ | 0 | 3 | 0 |
| $J_1$ (2nd) | 6 | 14 | $5+6=11$ | 11 | $11-14=-3$ | 0 | 3 | 0 |
| $J_4$ (3rd) | 8 | 20 | $11+8=19$ | 19 | $19-20=-1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $J_5$ (4th) | 3 | 16 | $19+3=22$ | 22 | $22-16=+6$ | 6 | 0 | 1 |
| $J_2$ (5th) | 4 | 10 | $22+4=26$ | 26 | $26-10=+16$ | 16 | 0 | 1 |

**Step 2: Compute Aggregate Measures.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\max} &= \max(5, 11, 19, 22, 26) = 26 \\
L_{\max} &= \max(-3, -3, -1, +6, +16) = 16 \\
T_{\max} &= \max(0, 0, 0, 6, 16) = 16 \\
\sum C_j &= 5 + 11 + 19 + 22 + 26 = 83 \\
\sum F_j &= 5 + 11 + 19 + 22 + 26 = 83 \\
\sum T_j &= 0 + 0 + 0 + 6 + 16 = 22 \\
\sum U_j &= 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $C_{\max}=26,\ L_{\max}=16,\ T_{\max}=16,\ \sum C_j=83,\ \sum F_j=83,\ \sum T_j=22,\ \sum U_j=2$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2 (Alternative to A)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Supplementary 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Discuss the **inter-relationships** between the performance measures $F_j, L_j, T_j,$ and $E_j$. Prove the identities $T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$ and $L_j = T_j - E_j$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a single-machine scheduling problem with 4 jobs, the sequence is fixed as $J_2 \to J_4 \to J_1 \to J_3$. Compute all pointwise measures, then evaluate $\sum (E_j + T_j)$ and $\sum \vert L_j \vert$, and verify they are equal. **(7 marks)**

| Job | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 11 |
| 2 | 6 | 7 |
| 3 | 3 | 18 |
| 4 | 5 | 14 |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Theoretical Discussion (7 marks):**

The four key per-job performance measures are interrelated through the following identities, all derivable from the definitions:

- $F_j = C_j - r_j$ (Flow time definition)
- $L_j = C_j - d_j$ (Lateness definition)
- $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$ (Tardiness)
- $E_j = \max(0, d_j - C_j)$ (Earliness)

**Identity 1:** $T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$

**Proof:** Consider two cases.

*Case 1:* $C_j \geq d_j \Rightarrow L_j \geq 0$.
Then $T_j = L_j$ and $E_j = 0$. So $T_j + E_j = L_j = \vert L_j \vert$.

*Case 2:* $C_j < d_j \Rightarrow L_j < 0$.
Then $T_j = 0$ and $E_j = d_j - C_j = -L_j = \vert L_j \vert$. So $T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$.

In both cases, $T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$. $\blacksquare$

**Identity 2:** $L_j = T_j - E_j$

**Proof:** From Identity 1, in *Case 1* ($L_j \geq 0$): $T_j - E_j = L_j - 0 = L_j$.
In *Case 2* ($L_j < 0$): $T_j - E_j = 0 - (-L_j) = L_j$. $\blacksquare$

These identities are crucial because:
- They enable the conversion between signed and unsigned lateness measures.
- The aggregate form $\sum (E_j + T_j) = \sum \vert L_j \vert$ forms the basis of **JIT (Just-In-Time) scheduling objectives**.
- They are used in deriving scheduling heuristics like the **Hodgson-Moore algorithm** for $\sum T_j$ minimization.

---

**(b) Numerical Verification (7 marks):**

Sequence: $J_2 \to J_4 \to J_1 \to J_3$. Assume $r_j = 0$ for all jobs.

**Step 1: Compute per-job measures in the given order.**

| Order | Job | $p_j$ | $d_j$ | $C_j$ | $L_j = C_j - d_j$ | $T_j$ | $E_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | $J_2$ | 6 | 7 | 6 | $-1$ | 0 | 1 |
| 2nd | $J_4$ | 5 | 14 | $6+5=11$ | $-3$ | 0 | 3 |
| 3rd | $J_1$ | 4 | 11 | $11+4=15$ | $+4$ | 4 | 0 |
| 4th | $J_3$ | 3 | 18 | $15+3=18$ | $0$ | 0 | 0 |

**Step 2: Compute $\sum (E_j + T_j)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (E_j + T_j) &= (1+0) + (3+0) + (0+4) + (0+0) \\
&= 1 + 3 + 4 + 0 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $\sum \vert L_j \vert$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \vert L_j \vert &= \vert -1 \vert + \vert -3 \vert + \vert +4 \vert + \vert 0 \vert \\
&= 1 + 3 + 4 + 0 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verification.**

$$\sum (E_j + T_j) = 8 = \sum \vert L_j \vert \ \checkmark$$

The identity $T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$ is verified for the aggregate.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Performance Measure Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the sequence** before computing $C_j$ ŌĆö examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Lateness with Tardiness** ŌĆö $L_j$ can be negative; $T_j$ cannot. Mixing them up = 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Unit Penalty $U_j$ written as a non-binary value** ŌĆö must be strictly 0 or 1. Writing $U_j = 5$ (e.g., confusing it with $C_j$) = 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Computing $C_j$ cumulatively without showing intermediate steps** ŌĆö always show $C_j = C_{j-1} + p_j$ explicitly.
> 5. **Using $F_j = C_j$ without stating $r_j = 0$** ŌĆö if release dates are non-zero, this is wrong. State the assumption.
> 6. **Missing the identity $T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$ in derivation questions** ŌĆö this is a KTU-favorite 3-mark sub-question.
> 7. **Forgetting the $\max$ in Tardiness/Earliness definitions** ŌĆö write $\max(0, \cdot)$ explicitly, not just the bare expression.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Performance Measures (Module 1)**
>
> Ō£ģ **Six Per-Job Measures (memorize formulas verbatim):**
>    - $C_j$ ŌĆö completion time
>    - $F_j = C_j - r_j$ ŌĆö flow time
>    - $L_j = C_j - d_j$ ŌĆö lateness (signed)
>    - $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$ ŌĆö tardiness (non-negative)
>    - $E_j = \max(0, d_j - C_j)$ ŌĆö earliness (non-negative)
>    - $U_j \in \{0, 1\}$ ŌĆö unit penalty (binary)
>
> Ō£ģ **Ten Aggregate Measures (essential for 14-mark problems):**
>    - $C_{\max}, F_{\max}, L_{\max}, T_{\max}$ (max-type)
>    - $\sum C_j, \sum F_j, \sum T_j, \sum U_j, \sum E_j$ (sum-type)
>    - $\sum (E_j + T_j)$ ŌĆö JIT objective
>
> Ō£ģ **Three Sacred Identities (proven in derivations):**
>    - $\sum F_j = \sum C_j - \sum r_j$
>    - $T_j + E_j = \vert L_j \vert$
>    - $L_j = T_j - E_j$
>
> Ō£ģ **Key Conceptual Distinctions:**
>    - Lateness can be negative; Tardiness cannot.
>    - $C_{\max} \geq \max_j d_j$ (no schedule beats the latest due date).
>    - With $r_j = 0$: $\sum F_j = \sum C_j$.
>
> Ō£ģ **KTU Notation Convention:**
>    - $1 \vert \cdot \vert \gamma$ where $\gamma$ is the optimization objective.
>    - Example: $1 \vert \cdot \vert C_{\max}$ = single-machine, minimize makespan.
>
> Ō£ģ **Engineering Insight:**
>    - $C_{\max}$ ŌåÆ machine utilization
>    - $\sum F_j$ ŌåÆ WIP inventory (via Little's Law)
>    - $L_{\max}$ ŌåÆ worst-case SLA
>    - $\sum U_j$ ŌåÆ customer dissatisfaction count
>    - $\sum (E_j + T_j)$ ŌåÆ JIT/Lean penalty
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Between Performance Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relationship Between Performance Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Industrial Scheduling (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEIET633), **Performance Measures** are the quantitative *yardsticks* used to evaluate the *goodness* (efficiency, effectiveness, and responsiveness) of a generated schedule against the **Scheduling Objectives** set by the planner. The *Relationship Between Performance Measures* refers to the set of **mathematical equivalences, bounds, and trade-offs** that connect one scheduling criterion to another ŌĆö for instance, how minimizing makespan relates to minimizing mean flow time, or how tardiness is derived from lateness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students are expected to identify, define, and inter-relate the principal scheduling performance measures ŌĆö $\bar{F}, \bar{W}, \bar{L}, \bar{T}, \bar{E}, L_{max}, T_{max}, \sum C_j, \sum T_j, C_{max}, N_T$ ŌĆö and understand when minimizing one measure also minimizes another.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are the manager of a **multi-counter passport office**.
> - The **Makespan** is the time at which the *last* applicant's passport is handed over (total working day length).
> - **Flow Time** of one applicant is how long *that person* stood in line plus service time.
> - **Lateness** is whether the applicant's passport was ready on, before, or after the promised date.
> - **Tardiness** is *only* the "late" part of lateness ŌĆö early arrivals never get tardiness credit, only earliness does.
> - **Number of Tardy Jobs** is simply how many people left the office unhappy.

Just as the office manager must balance *short queue lengths* (flow time) against *few angry customers* (tardiness), the scheduling researcher must understand how pulling on one performance measure "string" tightens or loosens all the others.

### 1.3 Standard Symbols Used in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Standard Notation (Pinedo & KTU Convention):**
>
> | Symbol | Meaning |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | $n$ | Number of jobs |
> | $r_j$ | Release date (ready time) of job $j$ |
> | $p_j$ | Processing time of job $j$ |
> | $d_j$ | Due date of job $j$ |
> | $C_j$ | Completion time of job $j$ |
> | $F_j$ | Flow time of job $j$ |
> | $W_j$ | Waiting time of job $j$ |
> | $L_j$ | Lateness of job $j$ |
> | $T_j$ | Tardiness of job $j$ |
> | $E_j$ | Earliness of job $j$ |
> | $U_j$ | Unit penalty indicator (0/1) for job $j$ |
> | $C_{max}$ | Makespan |
> | $L_{max}$ | Maximum lateness |
> | $T_{max}$ | Maximum tardiness |
> | $N_T$ | Number of tardy jobs |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Performance Measures (Job-Level)

A single job $j$ in any deterministic schedule yields the following *job-level* performance measures. All are derived from the three fundamental inputs: **Release Date $r_j$, Processing Time $p_j$, and Due Date $d_j$**, plus the schedule output: **Completion Time $C_j$**.

- **Completion Time ($C_j$):** The clock-time instant at which job $j$ finishes processing. It is a *primitive* ŌĆö directly obtained from the schedule.
- **Flow Time ($F_j$):** The total time job $j$ spends inside the system.

$$F_j = C_j - r_j$$

- **Waiting Time ($W_j$):** The non-productive portion of flow time. (i.e., $W_j$ is $F_j$ minus the *productive* $p_j$.)

$$W_j = F_j - p_j = C_j - r_j - p_j$$

- **Lateness ($L_j$):** Signed deviation from the due date. **Can be negative (early), zero (on-time), or positive (late).**

$$L_j = C_j - d_j$$

- **Tardiness ($T_j$):** The *positive part* of lateness. **Always $\geq 0$.**

$$T_j = \max\{0,\; L_j\} = \max\{0,\; C_j - d_j\}$$

- **Earliness ($E_j$):** The *positive part* of "how early." **Always $\geq 0$.**

$$E_j = \max\{0,\; -L_j\} = \max\{0,\; d_j - C_j\}$$

- **Unit Penalty ($U_j$):** Binary tardiness indicator.

$$U_j = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } C_j > d_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.2 Schedule-Level (Aggregate) Performance Measures

A schedule aggregates the job-level measures into operational metrics used by management:

- **Makespan ($C_{max}$):** $\; C_{max} = \max_j \{C_j\}$
- **Total (Sum) Completion Time:** $\;\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j$
- **Mean Flow Time ($\bar{F}$):** $\;\bar{F} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j$
- **Total Flow Time:** $\;\sum F_j$
- **Mean Waiting Time ($\bar{W}$):** $\;\bar{W} = \frac{1}{n}\sum W_j$
- **Maximum Lateness ($L_{max}$):** $\;L_{max} = \max_j\{L_j\}$
- **Maximum Tardiness ($T_{max}$):** $\;T_{max} = \max_j\{T_j\}$
- **Total Tardiness ($\sum T_j$):** Penalizes the *amount* of lateness.
- **Number of Tardy Jobs ($N_T$):** $\;N_T = \sum_{j=1}^{n} U_j$
- **Total Earliness:** Sometimes added to discourage building inventory too early (Just-In-Time philosophy).

### 2.3 The Five KTU-Critical Inter-Measure Relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the relationships a KTU paper-setter will test most heavily. Memorize them with the *conditions of equality* ŌĆö not just the formula.

**Relationship R1 ŌĆö Flow Time Ōåö Completion Time (with release dates):**
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j - \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_j$$
*Equality holds always (it is an identity, not a bound).*

**Relationship R2 ŌĆö Flow Time Ōåö Waiting Time:**
$$F_j = W_j + p_j \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sum F_j = \sum W_j + \sum p_j$$
*Because $\sum p_j$ is a constant for a fixed job set, **minimizing $\sum F_j$ is mathematically equivalent to minimizing $\sum W_j$**.*

**Relationship R3 ŌĆö Lateness Ōåö Tardiness Ōåö Earliness (the "Signed Lateness Decomposition"):**
$$L_j = T_j - E_j$$
$$T_j = \frac{L_j + \vert L_j \vert}{2}, \qquad E_j = \frac{\vert L_j \vert - L_j}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In Markdown tables, the **vertical bar** for absolute value is rendered as `$\vert$` to avoid breaking table syntax.

**Relationship R4 ŌĆö Lateness Ōåö Completion Time Ōåö Due Date (linear shift):**
$$L_j = C_j - d_j$$
*If all due dates are increased by a constant $\Delta$, then $L_j$ decreases by $\Delta$ for every job. Hence **$L_{max}$, $T_{max}$, $N_T$, and $\sum T_j$ are *invariant* under uniform due-date shifts if completion times are unchanged** ŌĆö but this is a deep property used in algorithms like EDD (Earliest Due Date).*

**Relationship R5 ŌĆö Makespan Ōåö Sum of Completion Times (lower bound):**
For any schedule on a single machine where $r_j = 0$ for all $j$:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j \geq \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\,\bar{p}_{\min}$$
The theoretical lower bound is achieved by **SPT (Shortest Processing Time)** rule.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Relationship | Formula | When Minimizing One Minimizes the Other |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Flow time Ōåö Completion time | $\sum F_j = \sum C_j - \sum r_j$ | Always equivalent (identity) |
| 2 | Flow time Ōåö Waiting time | $\sum F_j = \sum W_j + \sum p_j$ | Always equivalent |
| 3 | Lateness decomposition | $L_j = T_j - E_j$ | Cannot equate ŌĆö depends on due-date tightness |
| 4 | Tardiness definition | $T_j = \max\{0, L_j\}$ | **Only if all $L_j \geq 0$** does minimizing $L_{max}$ = minimizing $T_{max}$ |
| 5 | Earliness definition | $E_j = \max\{0, -L_j\}$ | **Only if all $L_j \leq 0$** does minimizing $L_{max}$ = maximizing $E$ |
| 6 | Lateness linear | $L_j = C_j - d_j$ | If $d_j$ all $\uparrow \Delta$, all $L_j \downarrow \Delta$ |
| 7 | Makespan lower bound | $C_{max} \geq \max_j\{r_j\} + \sum p_j$ | Single-machine identity |
| 8 | Sum completion lower bound | $\sum C_j \geq \sum p_j + \sum_{k=2}^{n} p_{[k]}$ | $p_{[k]}$ = k-th shortest processing time |
| 9 | $N_T$ vs $\sum T_j$ | $N_T \leq \sum T_j$ trivially | Minimizing $\sum T_j$ *implies* small $N_T$, but **not vice-versa** |
| 10 | $L_{max}$ vs $T_{max}$ | $T_{max} = \max\{0, L_{max}\}$ | Equivalent iff $L_{max} > 0$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These relationships are **not academic curiosities** ŌĆö they drive production decisions in:
> - **Automotive assembly lines** (balancing makespan with mean flow time on mixed-model lines).
> - **Cloud data-center job schedulers** (e.g., YARN, Kubernetes) ŌĆö uses $\sum C_j$ and $N_T$ as SLAs.
> - **Air Traffic Control sequencing** ŌĆö minimizing $L_{max}$ for runway approach times.
> - **Hospital Operating Theatres** ŌĆö minimizing $T_{max}$ (maximum surgical delay) while bounding $C_{max}$ (overtime).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Relationship R1 ($\sum F_j = \sum C_j - \sum r_j$)

We begin with the **definition** of flow time for job $j$:
$$F_j \triangleq C_j - r_j$$

Summing over all $n$ jobs:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (C_j - r_j)$$

Applying the **distributive law of summation**:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j - \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_j$$

> **Conversion logic:** The summation is *linear*, so $\sum (A_j - B_j) = \sum A_j - \sum B_j$. No further simplification is possible because $r_j$ is an *input parameter*, not a function of the schedule.

**QED.**

### 3.2 Derivation of Relationship R2 ($\sum F_j = \sum W_j + \sum p_j$)

Starting from the *definition* of waiting time:
$$W_j \triangleq C_j - r_j - p_j = F_j - p_j$$

Rearranging to express $F_j$:
$$F_j = W_j + p_j$$

Summing over all $n$ jobs:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j$$

> **Conversion logic:** Because $\sum p_j$ is a **constant** for any given problem instance (the total work content is independent of schedule), it acts as a *fixed offset*. Therefore, **the schedule that minimizes $\sum F_j$ is the very same schedule that minimizes $\sum W_j$**, and vice-versa. **This is the most heavily tested equivalence in KTU Module 1.**

### 3.3 Derivation of Relationship R3 ($L_j = T_j - E_j$)

We use the standard sign-decomposition:
$$T_j = \max\{0, L_j\}, \qquad E_j = \max\{0, -L_j\}$$

**Case A:** Suppose $L_j \geq 0$ (job is late or on-time).
$$T_j = L_j, \quad E_j = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_j - E_j = L_j - 0 = L_j \;\checkmark$$

**Case B:** Suppose $L_j < 0$ (job is early).
$$T_j = 0, \quad E_j = -L_j \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_j - E_j = 0 - (-L_j) = L_j \;\checkmark$$

In both cases, $T_j - E_j = L_j$, completing the proof.

### 3.4 Bound Derivation: $\sum C_j \geq \sum_{k=1}^{n} (n-k+1)\,p_{[k]}$

For a single machine with all $r_j = 0$, let $p_{[k]}$ denote the $k$-th smallest processing time. **Claim:** The minimum possible sum of completion times is achieved by **SPT order** and equals $\sum_{k=1}^{n} (n-k+1)\,p_{[k]}$.

**Proof by pairwise interchange (adjacent swap):**
Consider any schedule $S$ that is **not** in SPT order. Then there must exist two *adjacent* jobs $i, j$ with $p_i > p_j$ scheduled as $(\dots, i, j, \dots)$. Let their completion times be $C_i^{old}, C_j^{old}$. After swapping, they become $C_i^{new}, C_j^{new}$.

Using the **standard adjacent-swap algebra** with $C_a$ = completion time of job immediately before $i$:
$$C_i^{old} = C_a + p_i, \quad C_j^{old} = C_a + p_i + p_j$$

After swap:
$$C_i^{new} = C_a + p_j, \quad C_j^{new} = C_a + p_j + p_i$$

Computing the change in $\sum C$:
$$\Delta = (C_i^{new} + C_j^{new}) - (C_i^{old} + C_j^{old}) = (C_a + p_j + C_a + p_j + p_i) - (C_a + p_i + C_a + p_i + p_j)$$

$$\Delta = (2C_a + 2p_j + p_i) - (2C_a + 2p_i + p_j) = p_j - p_i$$

Since $p_i > p_j$, we have $\Delta < 0$. The **swap strictly decreases $\sum C$**. Repeating until no such inversion exists forces the schedule to be in **non-decreasing $p_j$ order** ŌĆö i.e., SPT.

The completion time of the $k$-th job in SPT is the sum of the $k$ shortest processing times:
$$C_{[k]} = \sum_{m=1}^{k} p_{[m]}$$

Therefore:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j = \sum_{k=1}^{n} C_{[k]} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\sum_{m=1}^{k} p_{[m]} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}(n-k+1)\,p_{[k]}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** Jobs with the *shortest* processing times are "weighted" most heavily (coefficient $n$), while the longest job contributes only once (coefficient 1). This is why **SPT penalizes long jobs less than $\sum T_j$ would.**

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Consider $n = 4$ jobs with the following data on a single machine (all $r_j = 0$):
>
> | Job $j$ | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
> | :-: | :-: | :-: |
> | 1 | 4 | 7 |
> | 2 | 2 | 4 |
> | 3 | 6 | 10 |
> | 4 | 3 | 6 |
>
> **Schedule used:** $\;J_2 \to J_4 \to J_1 \to J_3$ (this is the SPT order).
> **Compute:** $\;C_j,\; F_j,\; W_j,\; L_j,\; T_j,\; E_j$ for each job, then evaluate $C_{max},\; \bar{F},\; L_{max},\; T_{max},\; N_T,\; \sum T_j$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Completion Times (cumulative sum of $p_j$):**
$$C_2 = 2,\qquad C_4 = 2+3 = 5,\qquad C_1 = 5+4 = 9,\qquad C_3 = 9+6 = 15$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Flow Times** (using $F_j = C_j - r_j = C_j$ since all $r_j = 0$):
$$F_2 = 2,\quad F_4 = 5,\quad F_1 = 9,\quad F_3 = 15$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Waiting Times** ($W_j = F_j - p_j$):
$$W_2 = 2-2 = 0,\quad W_4 = 5-3 = 2,\quad W_1 = 9-4 = 5,\quad W_3 = 15-6 = 9$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Lateness** ($L_j = C_j - d_j$):
$$L_2 = 2-4 = -2,\quad L_4 = 5-6 = -1,\quad L_1 = 9-7 = +2,\quad L_3 = 15-10 = +5$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Tardiness** ($T_j = \max\{0, L_j\}$):
$$T_2 = 0,\quad T_4 = 0,\quad T_1 = 2,\quad T_3 = 5$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Earliness** ($E_j = \max\{0, -L_j\}$):
$$E_2 = 2,\quad E_4 = 1,\quad E_1 = 0,\quad E_3 = 0$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Aggregate Measures:**
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= \max\{2,5,9,15\} = 15 \\
\bar{F} &= \frac{2+5+9+15}{4} = \frac{31}{4} = 7.75 \\
L_{max} &= \max\{-2,-1,2,5\} = 5 \\
T_{max} &= \max\{0,0,2,5\} = 5 \\
N_T &= |\{1,3\}| = 2 \\
\sum T_j &= 0+0+2+5 = 7
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Verification of Relationship R3** ($L_j = T_j - E_j$):
$$L_2 = 0 - 2 = -2\;\checkmark,\quad L_1 = 2 - 0 = 2\;\checkmark,\quad L_3 = 5 - 0 = 5\;\checkmark$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Verification of Relationship R2** ($\sum F = \sum W + \sum p$):
$$\sum F = 31,\quad \sum W = 0+2+5+9 = 16,\quad \sum p = 4+2+6+3 = 15$$
$$\sum W + \sum p = 16 + 15 = 31 = \sum F\;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Examiners award *partial credit* for verifying the relationships numerically. Always include such a verification in your answer if the problem provides numerical data.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Performance Measure Relationships

The following Mermaid diagram maps how each *input* (release date, processing time, due date) feeds into the *primitive* (completion time) and then fans out into the various job-level and schedule-level measures ŌĆö finally showing which measures are *mathematically equivalent* under what conditions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Input r_j Release Date"] --> B1["Primitive C_j Completion Time"]
    A2["Input p_j Processing Time"] --> B1
    A3["Input d_j Due Date"] --> B1
    B1 --> C1["Flow Time F_j = C_j minus r_j"]
    B1 --> C2["Lateness L_j = C_j minus d_j"]
    B2["Constant Sum p_j"] --> C1
    C1 --> D1["Waiting Time W_j = F_j minus p_j"]
    C2 --> D2["Tardiness T_j = max 0 L_j"]
    C2 --> D3["Earliness E_j = max 0 minus L_j"]
    C2 --> D4["Unit Penalty U_j indicator 0 1"]
    D1 --> E1["Schedule Level Sum F_j"]
    D1 --> E2["Schedule Level Sum W_j"]
    D2 --> E3["Schedule Level L_max"]
    D2 --> E4["Schedule Level T_max"]
    D2 --> E5["Schedule Level Sum T_j"]
    D3 --> E6["Schedule Level Sum E_j"]
    D4 --> E7["Schedule Level N_T number tardy"]
    B1 --> E8["Schedule Level C_max makespan"]
    B1 --> E9["Schedule Level Sum C_j"]
    E1 -.equiv.-> E2
    E3 -.equiv if all late.-> E4
    E9 -.lower bound SPT.-> E8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** represent direct definitions.
> - **Dotted arrows** represent *conditional equivalence* (the dotted link $E_1 \leftrightarrow E_2$ is an *unconditional* identity, while $E_3 \leftrightarrow E_4$ holds only when every job is late).

### 4.2 Decision Flow: Which Performance Measure to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start Decision on objective"] --> q1{"What is the managers priority?"}
    q1 -- "Minimize total work-in-system" --> mf["Minimize Sum F_j or Sum W_j"]
    q1 -- "Minimize maximum delay" --> ml["Minimize L_max via EDD rule"]
    q1 -- "Minimize amount late" --> mt["Minimize Sum T_j NP-hard"]
    q1 -- "Minimize number late" --> nt["Minimize N_T NP-hard"]
    q1 -- "Minimize makespan parallel" --> cm["Minimize C_max List scheduling"]
    mf --> spt["Use SPT rule"]
    ml --> edd["Use EDD rule"]
    mt --> h1["Use Hodgson Algorithm or branch-and-bound"]
    nt --> h2["Use Moore Hodgson Algorithm"]
    cm --> ls["Use Longest Processing Time LPT or NEH"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Exam Tip:** When asked *"Which rule minimizes which measure?"*, this decision chart is the canonical answer. Commit it to memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 ├Ś 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1.** Define the following scheduling performance measures and state the formula relating any two of them: (i) Flow Time, (ii) Lateness, (iii) Tardiness.
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Flow Time ($F_j$):** The total time a job $j$ spends in the system from its release to completion, $F_j = C_j - r_j$.
- **Lateness ($L_j$):** The signed deviation of completion time from due date, $L_j = C_j - d_j$ (negative if early).
- **Tardiness ($T_j$):** The *non-negative* lateness, $T_j = \max\{0, L_j\}$.
- **Relationship:** $T_j = \max\{0, C_j - d_j\}$; also $T_j$ and $L_j$ coincide if and only if $C_j \geq d_j$, otherwise $T_j = 0$ but $L_j < 0$.

**Q2.** State and prove (with one example) the relationship $\sum F_j = \sum W_j + \sum p_j$.
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:** From $F_j = W_j + p_j$ (definition of waiting time), summing gives $\sum F_j = \sum W_j + \sum p_j$. Since $\sum p_j$ is constant, **minimizing $\sum F_j$ is equivalent to minimizing $\sum W_j$**. *Example:* If $p_j = 5, 3, 2$ in SPT order, then $\sum p = 10$, $F = \{2, 5, 10\}$, $W = \{0, 2, 5\}$; indeed $17 = 7 + 10$. Ō£ō

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions ŌĆö Internal Choice (Module 1, 14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 1 Pattern, Modified for PEIET633]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** For $n = 5$ jobs with the following data on a single machine (all $r_j = 0$), schedule using the **SPT rule** and compute $C_j, F_j, W_j, L_j, T_j, E_j, C_{max}, \bar{F}, L_{max}, \sum T_j, N_T$. Verify Relationship R3 for every job.
*(7 Marks)*

| Job $j$ | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
| :-: | :-: | :-: |
| 1 | 5 | 12 |
| 2 | 8 | 10 |
| 3 | 3 | 6 |
| 4 | 6 | 15 |
| 5 | 2 | 8 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö SPT ordering** (ascending $p_j$): $J_5(2), J_3(3), J_1(5), J_4(6), J_2(8)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Cumulative completion times:**
$$C_5 = 2,\quad C_3 = 5,\quad C_1 = 10,\quad C_4 = 16,\quad C_2 = 24$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $F_j, W_j, L_j, T_j, E_j$:**

| Job | $p_j$ | $d_j$ | $C_j$ | $F_j$ | $W_j$ | $L_j$ | $T_j$ | $E_j$ |
| :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| 5 | 2 | 8 | 2 | 2 | 0 | $-6$ | 0 | 6 |
| 3 | 3 | 6 | 5 | 5 | 2 | $-1$ | 0 | 1 |
| 1 | 5 | 12 | 10 | 10 | 5 | $-2$ | 0 | 2 |
| 4 | 6 | 15 | 16 | 16 | 10 | $+1$ | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 8 | 10 | 24 | 24 | 16 | $+14$ | 14 | 0 |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Aggregate measures:**
$$C_{max} = 24,\quad \bar{F} = \tfrac{57}{5} = 11.4,\quad L_{max} = 14,\quad \sum T_j = 15,\quad N_T = 2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verify R3 ($L_j = T_j - E_j$):**
- $J_5$: $0 - 6 = -6$ Ō£ō
- $J_3$: $0 - 1 = -1$ Ō£ō
- $J_1$: $0 - 2 = -2$ Ō£ō
- $J_4$: $1 - 0 = 1$ Ō£ō
- $J_2$: $14 - 0 = 14$ Ō£ō

**(b)** With reference to the data above, compute the schedule produced by the **EDD (Earliest Due Date) rule** and identify which performance measure EDD is *provably optimal* for.
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö EDD ordering** (ascending $d_j$): $J_3(6), J_5(8), J_2(10), J_1(12), J_4(15)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Cumulative completion times:**
$$C_3 = 3,\quad C_5 = 5,\quad C_2 = 13,\quad C_1 = 18,\quad C_4 = 24$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Aggregate measures:**

| Measure | SPT Schedule | EDD Schedule |
| :--- | :-: | :-: |
| $C_{max}$ | 24 | 24 |
| $\bar{F}$ | 11.4 | 12.6 |
| $L_{max}$ | 14 | **5** |
| $\sum T_j$ | 15 | **5** |
| $N_T$ | 2 | 1 |

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion:**
EDD minimizes $L_{max}$ (Jackson's Rule, 1955). Here $L_{max}$ drops from **14** (SPT) to **5** (EDD). Thus **EDD is provably optimal for the maximum lateness criterion $L_{max}$**, but it does *not* optimize $\sum F_j$ or $\bar{F}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Define and derive the lower bound for $\sum C_j$ for $n$ jobs processed on a single machine under the SPT rule. Show with a numerical example that any schedule not in SPT order yields a strictly larger $\sum C_j$.
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** Under SPT with $p_{[1]} \leq p_{[2]} \leq \dots \leq p_{[n]}$, the completion time of the $k$-th job is $C_{[k]} = \sum_{m=1}^{k} p_{[m]}$. Therefore:

$$\sum C_j^{SPT} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (n - k + 1)\,p_{[k]}$$

**Numerical verification** ($n=3$, $p = \{3, 1, 2\}$):
- **SPT order** $(1, 2, 3)$: $C = \{1, 3, 6\}$, $\sum C = 10$.
- **Arbitrary order** $(3, 1, 2)$: $C = \{3, 4, 6\}$, $\sum C = 13 > 10$. Ō£ō

**Formula check:** $\sum C^{SPT} = 3 \cdot p_{[1]} + 2 \cdot p_{[2]} + 1 \cdot p_{[3]} = 3(1) + 2(2) + 1(3) = 10$. Ō£ō

**(b)** Explain the relationship between $N_T$ (number of tardy jobs) and $\sum T_j$ (total tardiness). Why is minimizing $\sum T_j$ a *stronger* criterion than minimizing $N_T$? Provide a small counter-example if possible.
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Relationship:** $N_T = |\{j : T_j > 0\}|$, while $\sum T_j = \sum_j T_j$. Clearly $N_T \leq \sum T_j$ always (since each tardy job contributes at least 1 to $\sum T_j$). A schedule with $N_T = 0$ automatically has $\sum T_j = 0$, but the converse is false.

**Why $\sum T_j$ is stronger:** A schedule with small $N_T$ may still have a *huge* tardiness on a few jobs, whereas minimizing $\sum T_j$ penalizes the *magnitude* of tardiness per job.

**Counter-example:** Consider two schedules for 3 jobs each:
- **Schedule X:** $(T_1, T_2, T_3) = (0.5, 0.5, 0.5) \Rightarrow N_T = 3, \sum T = 1.5$.
- **Schedule Y:** $(T_1, T_2, T_3) = (0, 0, 2.0) \Rightarrow N_T = 1, \sum T = 2.0$.

Schedule X has *more* tardy jobs but *less* total tardiness ŌĆö illustrating that **minimizing $N_T$ does not imply minimizing $\sum T_j$**. The MooreŌĆōHodgson algorithm minimizes $N_T$; the Hodgson algorithm minimizes $\sum T_j$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $L_{max}$ with $T_{max}$:** They are equal only when *at least one* job is late. If every job finishes early, $T_{max} = 0$ but $L_{max} < 0$. State both values.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract $r_j$ in flow time:** If $r_j > 0$, then $F_j \neq C_j$. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Writing $T_j = C_j - d_j$ (omitting the max):** This is the single most common error ŌĆö it gives negative "tardiness," which is mathematically meaningless.
> 4. **Claiming $\sum F_j$ minimization $\Leftrightarrow$ $\sum T_j$ minimization:** Wrong! SPT minimizes $\sum F_j$ but can leave many jobs tardy.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step in numerical problems:** Always verify at least one relationship (R2 or R3) to earn full marks.
> 6. **Using `|` in tables:** In the answer script this is fine, but in any Mermaid/table output, use $\vert$ ŌĆö examiners may auto-grade.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition block:** $C_j, F_j = C_j - r_j, W_j = F_j - p_j, L_j = C_j - d_j, T_j = \max\{0, L_j\}, E_j = \max\{0, -L_j\}, U_j \in \{0,1\}$.
- **Three equivalences** that always hold: (i) $\sum F = \sum C - \sum r$, (ii) $\sum F = \sum W + \sum p$, (iii) $L_j = T_j - E_j$.
- **Three equivalences that hold conditionally:** (iv) $L_{max} = T_{max}$ iff some $L_j > 0$, (v) $\min \sum F \Leftrightarrow \min \sum W$ always, (vi) $\min L_{max} \Rightarrow \min T_{max}$ but not vice-versa.
- **Optimality rules:** SPT minimizes $\bar{F}$ and $\sum C_j$; EDD minimizes $L_{max}$; MooreŌĆōHodgson minimizes $N_T$; Hodgson minimizes $\sum T_j$.
- **Computational complexity:** $\bar{F}, L_{max}, C_{max}$ are *polynomially solvable*. $\sum T_j, \sum E_j, N_T$ are **NP-hard** for general $n$.
- **Two-class taxonomy:** *Regular* measures ($\bar{F}, L_{max}, T_{max}, \sum T_j, N_T$) ŌĆö non-decreasing in completion times; *Non-regular* measures ($\sum E_j$, JIT penalties) ŌĆö require penalty for being *too early*.
- **Sanity check before submitting:** Always verify at least one relationship (R2 or R3) numerically if the problem provides data.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theorems Related to Performance Measures (Proof and Problems). Classes of Schedules - Non-Delay Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Theorems Related to Performance Measures (Proof and Problems). Classes of Schedules - Non-Delay Schedules # SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Performance Measures ŌĆö The Yardsticks of a Schedule In deterministic scheduling theory, every schedule must be **evaluated** against a quantifiable yardstick. These yardsticks are called **performance measures** (or **scheduling criteria / objective functions**). Mathematically, they are functions of the completion times $C_j$, flow times $F_j = C_j - p_j$, lateness $L_j = C_j - d_j$, and tardiness $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$ of jobs. > [!NOTE] > **Definition (Performance Measure):** > A *performance measure* is a scalar-valued objective function $f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ defined over the completion vector $(C_1, C_2, \dots, C_n)$ that quantifies the *goodness* of a schedule. For a problem $1 \vert \gamma \vert f$, the goal is to find a schedule minimizing (or sometimes maximizing) $f$. The nine performance measures of central importance in KTU Module 1 are catalogued in the table below: | # | Performance Measure | Symbol | Mathematical Form | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Makespan | $C_{\max}$ | $\max_j C_j$ | | 2 | Maximum Lateness | $L_{\max}$ | $\max_j L_j$ | | 3 | Total Completion Time | $\sum C_j$ | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j$ | | 4 | Weighted Completion Time | $\sum w_j C_j$ | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j C_j$ | | 5 | Total Flow Time | $\sum F_j$ | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} (C_j - p_j)$ | | 6 | Number of Tardy Jobs | $\sum U_j$ | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} U_j$ | | 7 | Total Tardiness | $\sum T_j$ | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} T_j$ | | 8 | Weighted Number Tardy | $\sum w_j U_j$ | $\sum_j w_j U_j$ | | 9 | Maximum Tardiness | $T_{\max}$ | $\max_j T_j$ | where the **indicator of tardiness** is $$ U_j = \begin{cases} 1, & C_j > d_j \\ 0, & C_j \le d_j \end{cases} $$ ### 1.2 Regular vs Non-Regular Measures > [!IMPORTANT] > A performance measure $f$ is called **regular** if it is a **non-decreasing** function of every $C_j$. Intuitively, pushing a job later never *improves* the objective. All nine measures above are regular. A **non-regular** measure (e.g. earliness $E_j = \max(0, d_j - C_j)$) rewards finishing *before* the due date. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Coffee Shop > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a single barista (the *machine*) serving a queue of $n$ customers (the *jobs*) at a small caf├®. Each customer $j$ orders a drink that takes $p_j$ minutes and has a promised pickup time $d_j$. > > - $\sum C_j$ = the *total waiting time* of every customer in the shop. > - $\sum T_j$ = the *total minutes late* across all customers. > - $\sum U_j$ = the *count* of customers who left unhappy (received the drink late). > - $L_{\max}$ = the *worst* complaint you would receive. > > The barista must decide the **service order**. Each theorem below is simply a *proven best strategy* for ordering customers under a different complaint metric. ### 1.4 Classes of Schedules ŌĆö A First Glance Every feasible sequence is one of four kinds, ordered from the *most flexible* (largest search space) to the *tightest* (smallest search space): $$ \text{All Schedules} \;\supseteq\; \text{Semi-Active} \;\supseteq\; \text{Active} \;\supseteq\; \text{Non-Delay} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Definition (Non-Delay Schedule):** > A feasible schedule is called a **non-delay schedule** if *at no point in time* is a machine kept idle while at least one job is *waiting and processed-ready* in its queue. In short: **no avoidable idle time.** # SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Canonical Theorems on Performance Measures The KTU syllabus lists four foundational theorems, each pairing a *regular* performance measure with an *optimal sequencing rule* on a **single machine** ($1 \, || \, f$). ### Theorem 2.1 ŌĆö SPT Optimality (Total Completion Time) > **Theorem (Conway, Maxwell & Miller, 1967):** For $1 \, || \, \sum C_j$, the **Shortest Processing Time (SPT)** rule ŌĆö sequence jobs in non-decreasing order of $p_j$ ŌĆö yields an optimal schedule. ### Theorem 2.2 ŌĆö WSPT / Smith's Rule (Weighted Completion Time) > **Theorem (Smith, 1956):** For $1 \, || \, \sum w_j C_j$, the **Weighted Shortest Processing Time (WSPT)** rule ŌĆö sequence jobs in **non-decreasing order of $p_j / w_j$** ŌĆö yields an optimal schedule. ### Theorem 2.3 ŌĆö Jackson's EDD Rule (Maximum Lateness) > **Theorem (Jackson, 1955):** For $1 \, || \, L_{\max}$, the **Earliest Due Date (EDD)** rule ŌĆö sequence jobs in non-decreasing order of $d_j$ ŌĆö yields an optimal schedule. ### Theorem 2.4 ŌĆö MooreŌĆōHodgson Algorithm (Number of Tardy Jobs) > **Theorem (Moore, 1968; Hodgson, 1971):** For $1 \, || \, \sum U_j$, there exists an optimal schedule in which *all early jobs* are processed in **EDD order** *before* any tardy job. The set of tardy jobs can be found in $O(n \log n)$ by **Moore's algorithm**. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Problem | Optimal Rule | Complexity | Key Formula | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $1 \, || \, \sum C_j$ | **SPT** | $O(n \log n)$ | $\min \sum C_j = \sum_{k=1}^{n}(n-k+1)\,p_{[k]}$ | | 2 | $1 \, || \, \sum w_j C_j$ | **WSPT** (Smith) | $O(n \log n)$ | Sort by $p_j / w_j$ ascending | | 3 | $1 \, || \, L_{\max}$ | **EDD** (Jackson) | $O(n \log n)$ | $L_{\max} = \max_k (C_k - d_k)$ | | 4 | $1 \, || \, \sum U_j$ | **Moore's Algo** | $O(n^2)$ greedy | $U_j = \mathbb{1}[C_j > d_j]$ | | 5 | $1 \, || \, C_{\max}$ | Any sequence | $O(1)$ | $C_{\max} = \sum p_j$ | | 6 | $1 \, || \, \sum T_j$ | Open ŌĆö NP-hard | Heuristic only | $T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$ | | 7 | $1 \, || \, \sum F_j$ | **SPT** | $O(n \log n)$ | $\sum F_j = \sum C_j - \sum p_j$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Although $\sum F_j$ and $\sum C_j$ differ by the *constant* $\sum p_j$, they share the *same optimal sequence*. This is why minimizing total flow time and total completion time are *equivalent* problems on one machine ŌĆö a recurring viva question at KTU. ### 2.3 Real-World Utility | Industrial Setting | Performance Measure | Why it Matters | |---|---|---| | Semiconductor fab (single photolithography tool) | $\sum C_j$ | Maximize wafer throughput | | JIT assembly line | $\sum T_j$ | Penalize stock-out delays | | Tax-return processing centre | $\sum U_j$ | Just *finish every return* ŌĆö don't ask when | | Airline baggage handling | $C_{\max}$ | Maximize number of flights served | | Hospital CT-scan queue | $L_{\max}$ | Avoid the worst patient complaint | ## 2.4 Hierarchy of Schedule Classes ŌĆö Why Non-Delay Matters The search space for an $n$-job, $m$-machine problem contains $n!^m$ schedules ŌĆö astronomically large.
