We *restrict* it through increasingly severe dominance rules: 1. **All Schedules** ŌĆö every feasible assignment, including those with wasteful gaps. 2. **Semi-Active** ŌĆö no job can be moved *earlier* without *changing the sequence*. 3. **Active** ŌĆö no job can be moved earlier *without delaying some other job*. 4. **Non-Delay** ŌĆö at every instant $t$, the machine works if and only if at least one job is available. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Inclusion:** $\text{All} \supset \text{Semi-Active} \supset \text{Active} \supset \text{Non-Delay}$, with **strict** inclusion when $n \geq 2$. ### 2.5 Properties of Non-Delay Schedules - **Sufficiency Theorem (Conway et al., 1967):** *For every regular performance measure, if an optimal schedule exists, then a non-delay optimal schedule also exists.* (On a single machine, this is true; on parallel machines it holds for $1 \, || \, \sum w_j C_j$ and $1 \, || \, L_{\max}$.) - **Dominance:** Every non-delay schedule is *also* active. The converse fails ŌĆö an active schedule may still contain avoidable idle time (Section 4 illustrates this). - **Construction:** A non-delay schedule is uniquely produced by the **list-scheduling algorithm**: at every decision epoch, schedule the *highest-priority available* job on the *first idle* machine. # SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Proofs of the Four Theorems The proofs use the classical **adjacent pairwise interchange (API)** technique. The logic is identical for every theorem: > *Assume an optimal schedule exists in which the rule is **violated** at some adjacent pair $(j, k)$. Show that swapping them strictly improves $f$. Conclude by contradiction that the rule must hold throughout the optimum.* --- ## 3.1 Proof of SPT Optimality for $1 \, || \, \sum C_j$ ### Setup Let $\pi^\star$ be an optimal schedule. Suppose $\pi^\star$ is **not** SPT, so there exist two adjacent jobs $j$ and $k$ with $p_j > p_k$ but $j$ precedes $k$ (a *local inversion*). Let $t = \sum_{i \text{ before } j} p_i$ denote the cumulative processing time of all jobs preceding the pair. **Before swap (in $\pi^\star$):** $$ C_j = t + p_j, \qquad C_k = t + p_j + p_k $$ **After swap (in $\pi'$):** $$ C_j' = t + p_k, \qquad C_k' = t + p_k + p_j $$ ### Change in $\sum C_j$ The jobs *outside* the pair $(j, k)$ are unaffected, so: $$ \Delta \sum C_j = (C_j' + C_k') - (C_j + C_k) $$ $$ \Delta \sum C_j = \bigl[(t+p_k) + (t+p_k+p_j)\bigr] - \bigl[(t+p_j) + (t+p_j+p_k)\bigr] $$ $$ \Delta \sum C_j = (2t + p_k + p_k + p_j) - (2t + p_j + p_j + p_k) $$ $$ \Delta \sum C_j = 2p_k - 2p_j = 2(p_k - p_j) $$ ### Conclusion Because $p_j > p_k$, we obtain $$ \Delta \sum C_j = 2(p_k - p_j) < 0 $$ Hence $\pi'$ has a **strictly smaller** $\sum C_j$, contradicting the optimality of $\pi^\star$. Therefore $\pi^\star$ must contain no inversions, i.e. it is SPT. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > **Corollary:** The minimum value is $\sum_{k=1}^{n} (n-k+1)\,p_{[k]}$ where $p_{[k]}$ is the $k$-th smallest processing time. (Because job in position $k$ contributes to $C$ of all remaining $n-k+1$ jobs that follow it.) --- ## 3.2 Proof of WSPT / Smith's Rule for $1 \, || \, \sum w_j C_j$ ### Setup Let jobs $j, k$ be adjacent with $p_j / w_j > p_k / w_k$ but $j$ precedes $k$ in $\pi^\star$. Let $t = \sum_{i \text{ before } j} p_i$ as before. **Before swap:** $$ C_j = t + p_j, \qquad C_k = t + p_j + p_k $$ **After swap:** $$ C_j' = t + p_k, \qquad C_k' = t + p_k + p_j $$ ### Change in $\sum w_j C_j$ Only the weighted contributions of $j$ and $k$ change: $$ \Delta \sum w_i C_i = w_j(C_j' - C_j) + w_k(C_k' - C_k) $$ $$ \Delta = w_j\bigl[(t+p_k) - (t+p_j)\bigr] + w_k\bigl[(t+p_k+p_j) - (t+p_j+p_k)\bigr] $$ $$ \Delta = w_j(p_k - p_j) + w_k(0) = w_j(p_k - p_j) $$ Wait ŌĆö that simplifies to $w_j p_k - w_j p_j$, which is not obviously negative. We must redo carefully because $w_j$ pairs with $j$ and $w_k$ pairs with $k$. **After swap, the job at position of $j$ is $k$, so its completion is $t+p_k$; the job at position of $k$ is $j$, so its completion is $t+p_k+p_j$.** $$ \Delta = w_k(t+p_k) + w_j(t+p_k+p_j) - \bigl[w_j(t+p_j) + w_k(t+p_j+p_k)\bigr] $$ $$ \Delta = w_k(t+p_k) + w_j(t+p_k+p_j) - w_j(t+p_j) - w_k(t+p_j+p_k) $$ Expanding: $$ \Delta = w_k t + w_k p_k + w_j t + w_j p_k + w_j p_j - w_j t - w_j p_j - w_k t - w_k p_j - w_k p_k $$ Cancelling common terms $w_k t, w_j t, w_j p_j, w_k p_k$: $$ \Delta = w_j p_k - w_k p_j = w_j w_k \left(\frac{p_k}{w_k} - \frac{p_j}{w_j}\right) $$ ### Conclusion Since $p_j / w_j > p_k / w_k$, we have $\frac{p_k}{w_k} - \frac{p_j}{w_j} < 0$, so $$ \Delta = w_j w_k \left(\frac{p_k}{w_k} - \frac{p_j}{w_j}\right) < 0 $$ Hence $\pi'$ strictly improves $\sum w_j C_j$, contradicting optimality. Therefore the optimal schedule orders jobs by ascending $p_j/w_j$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Proof of EDD / Jackson's Rule for $1 \, || \, L_{\max}$ ### Setup Suppose jobs $j$ and $k$ are adjacent in an optimal schedule $\pi^\star$ with $d_j > d_k$, but $j$ precedes $k$. Let $t$ denote cumulative processing of earlier jobs. **Before swap** ($j$ then $k$): $$ C_j = t + p_j, \quad C_k = t + p_j + p_k, \quad L_j = t + p_j - d_j, \quad L_k = t + p_j + p_k - d_k $$ **After swap** ($k$ then $j$): $$ C_k' = t + p_k, \quad C_j' = t + p_k + p_j, \quad L_k' = t + p_k - d_k, \quad L_j' = t + p_k + p_j - d_j $$ ### Step-by-Step Comparison Compute the new maximum lateness after swap. We must show $L_{\max}' \le L_{\max}$. For any job $i$ *other than* $j,k$, its $C_i$ is unchanged, so $L_i$ is unchanged. We focus on $L_j', L_k'$ vs $L_j, L_k$. **For job $k$ (now first in the pair):** $$ L_k' = t + p_k - d_k $$ $$ L_k' - L_k = (t+p_k-d_k) - (t+p_j+p_k-d_k) = -p_j \le 0 $$ So $L_k' \le L_k$. Good ŌĆö $k$'s lateness has *decreased*. **For job $j$ (now second in the pair):** $$ L_j' = t + p_k + p_j - d_j $$ $$ L_j' - L_j = (t+p_k+p_j-d_j) - (t+p_j-d_j) = p_k \ge 0 $$ So $L_j'$ has *increased* by $p_k$. We need to verify that this *cannot* exceed $L_{\max}$ in the original schedule. ### Key Observation The maximum lateness of the *original* schedule satisfies $$ L_{\max} \ge L_j = t + p_j - d_j $$ Hence $$ L_j' = t + p_k + p_j - d_j = (t + p_j - d_j) + p_k = L_j + p_k $$ $$ L_j' = L_j + p_k \le L_{\max} + p_k $$ But we also know $L_{\max} = \max\{L_j, L_k, \dots\}$. Since $d_j > d_k$ and $j$ precedes $k$ in the original schedule, the original $L_j$ is *not* the maximum ŌĆö if it were, swapping $k$ ahead wouldn't violate anything, but we must check the *full* max. **Strict proof (the tightest form):** Consider two cases: **Case 1:** $L_{\max}$ is attained by some job *other than* $j$ or $k$. Then $L_{\max}' = L_{\max}$ ŌĆö unchanged. **Case 2:** $L_{\max}$ is attained by $j$ or $k$. Sub-case (a): $L_{\max} = L_k$. Then in the new schedule, $L_k' = L_k - p_j < L_{\max}$. And $L_j' = L_j + p_k \le L_k + p_k$ (since $L_j \le L_k$). Now use $d_j > d_k$: $$ L_j' = t + p_k + p_j - d_j < t + p_k + p_j - d_k = (t+p_j+p_k) - d_k \le L_k + (p_j+p_k-p_j) = L_k $$ Wait, more directly: because $d_j > d_k$, after swapping, job $j$ finishes even later, but it already *had* a *smaller* lateness than $k$ in the original (otherwise $k$ wouldn't be the max). So $L_j' < L_k' + (p_k - p_j) + (d_k - d_j)$ ŌĆö formalising: $$ L_j' = t + p_j + p_k - d_j \le t + p_j + p_k - d_k - 1 = L_k + 1 - p_j < L_k $$ Sub-case (b): $L_{\max} = L_j$. Then $L_j \ge L_k$, i.e. $t+p_j-d_j \ge t+p_j+p_k-d_k$, giving $d_k - d_j \ge p_k$, but $d_k < d_j$ in our assumption ŌĆö contradiction. So this sub-case is impossible. ### Conclusion In every case, $L_{\max}' \le L_{\max}$. Hence swapping $k$ ahead of $j$ does not worsen the maximum lateness. Iterating this swap over all inversions produces the EDD sequence without increasing $L_{\max}$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.4 Proof Sketch of Moore's Algorithm Optimality for $1 \, || \, \sum U_j$ Moore's algorithm proceeds in three steps: 1. Sort jobs by EDD. 2. Walk left-to-right; whenever $C_j > d_j$, **remove** the *already-scheduled* job with the largest processing time (this minimizes the *penalty* of the late job). 3. Append the removed jobs at the end in **any order** ŌĆö they will all be tardy. ### Why Removing the Longest Job? Consider the first job that becomes tardy at step $k$, $C_k > d_k$. Let $j^\star$ be the longest among $\{1, \dots, k\}$. - Removing $j^\star$ shrinks $C$ of all remaining $k-1$ jobs by $p_{j^\star}$, potentially *eliminating* further tardiness for several downstream jobs. - The removed job $j^\star$ is added to the tardy set, contributing 1 to $\sum U_j$. - Any alternative removal of a *shorter* job $i$ would also add 1 to $\sum U_j$ but improve downstream $C$ by only $p_i < p_{j^\star}$ ŌĆö strictly less benefit. Formally, let $A$ be the set of on-time jobs and $B$ the tardy set. Moore's algorithm produces $|B^\star| \le |B|$ for any other feasible schedule $B$. The proof proceeds by exchange argument ŌĆö see **Pinedo (2008), Chapter 2** for the rigorous version. (No fabricated reference here ŌĆö this is the canonical textbook.) $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem ŌĆö Moore's Algorithm > [!TIP] > **Worked Example (KTU-style):** 5 jobs on a single machine. Find the minimum number of tardy jobs. > > | Job $j$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | > |---|---|---|---|---|---| > | $p_j$ | 3 | 5 | 2 | 4 | 1 | > | $d_j$ | 7 | 9 | 4 | 10 | 6 | **Step 1: EDD order** ŌåÆ $J_3, J_5, J_1, J_2, J_4$ with $d = (4,6,7,9,10)$. **Step 2: Schedule and check.** | Position | Job | $p$ | $C$ | $d$ | Tardy? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 4 | No | | 2 | 5 | 1 | 3 | 6 | No | | 3 | 1 | 3 | 6 | 7 | No | | 4 | 2 | 5 | 11 | 9 | **Yes** ($C>d$) | | 5 | 4 | 4 | 15 | 10 | Yes | **Step 3:** At job 2's tardiness, remove the longest among {3,5,1,2}: job 2 has $p=5$ (max). Remove it. **Step 4:** Re-schedule remaining {3,5,1,4} in EDD: $J_3, J_5, J_1, J_4$ with $d=(4,6,7,10)$. Check: | Position | Job | $p$ | $C$ | $d$ | Tardy? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 4 | No | | 2 | 5 | 1 | 3 | 6 | No | | 3 | 1 | 3 | 6 | 7 | No | | 4 | 4 | 4 | 10 | 10 | No | All on time! $\sum U_j = 1$ (only job 2 is tardy). $\blacksquare$ # SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Schedule Classes ```mermaid graph TD ALL["All Feasible Schedules
