### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B(b) ŌĆö Comparison Table

| Objective | Graham Notation | Optimal Algorithm | Complexity | Real-World Application |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|-----------|----------------------|
| Minimize makespan | $1 \mid \mid C_{\max}$ | Any order (all jobs run) | Trivial | Single-machine utilization |
| Minimize total completion time | $1 \mid \mid \sum C_j$ | **SPT** (Shortest Processing Time first) | $O(n \log n)$ | Inventory build-up, work-in-progress reduction |
| Minimize maximum lateness | $1 \mid \mid L_{\max}$ | **EDD** (Earliest Due Date first) | $O(n \log n)$ | Hard-deadline scheduling (legal filings, flight departures) |
| **Minimize total lateness** | $1 \mid \mid \sum L_j$ | **MooreŌĆōHodgson Algorithm** | $O(n \log n)$ | Customer service level agreements, soft-deadline delivery |
| Minimize total tardiness | $1 \mid \mid \sum T_j$ | Dynamic programming / branch-and-bound | NP-hard | Penalty-cost-based scheduling |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Lateness with Tardiness:** A common error is computing $\sum T_j$ when the question asks for $\sum L_j$, or vice versa. Re-read the objective function symbol carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting to update $C$ after removal:** After removing the longest job, students often forget to subtract $p_k$ from $C$, leading to incorrect downstream completion times.
> 3. **Placing late jobs before on-time jobs in the final sequence:** The on-time set $S$ must come **first**, in EDD order, followed by the removed set $R$. Reversing this may still preserve optimality in some cases but is **not guaranteed**.
> 4. **Skipping the verification of $C \leq d_j$ after removal:** After every removal, recompute $C$ and verify the next iteration's condition. Examiners specifically look for this check.
> 5. **Using EDD for total lateness:** Writing "schedule jobs by EDD" without invoking Moore's algorithm will cost significant marks because EDD minimizes $L_{\max}$, **not** $\sum L_j$.

---

## ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ The problem **$1 \mid \mid \sum L_j$** seeks to minimize the **sum of signed lateness** values on a single machine.
- Ō£ģ **Lateness** $L_j = C_j - d_j$ is signed; **Tardiness** $T_j = \max\{0, L_j\}$ is non-negative.
- Ō£ģ The optimal algorithm is **MooreŌĆōHodgson (1968)**, running in $O(n \log n)$.
- Ō£ģ Algorithm skeleton: **(i)** Sort by EDD ŌåÆ **(ii)** Greedily maintain on-time set ŌåÆ **(iii)** When $C > d_j$, remove the **longest-processing job** ŌåÆ **(iv)** Append late jobs after on-time jobs.
- Ō£ģ **EDD is optimal for $L_{\max}$, NOT for $\sum L_j$**.
- Ō£ģ **SPT** minimizes $\sum C_j$ (a different objective).
- Ō£ģ The optimal $\sum L_j$ equals $\sum_{j \in \text{late}} (C_j - d_j)$ if early jobs are scheduled first; equivalently, the formula $Z^* = \sum L_j$ includes negative contributions.
- Ō£ģ Real-world domains: **delivery logistics, healthcare scheduling, semiconductor manufacturing, software sprint planning**.
- Ō£ģ Complexity hierarchy reminder: $\sum C_j$ and $L_{\max}$ are easy; $\sum L_j$ is easy (Moore); $\sum T_j$ and number of tardy jobs are **NP-hard**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimizing Maximum Lateness and Maximum Tardiness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimizing Maximum Lateness and Maximum Tardiness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Scheduling Preliminaries (Graham's Notation)

In deterministic single-machine scheduling theory, problems are compactly expressed using Graham's three-field notation $\alpha \vert \beta \vert \gamma$, where:
- $\alpha$ = machine environment (here: **1** = single machine),
- $\beta$ = job characteristics (no release dates, no preemption, no precedence unless stated),
- $\gamma$ = objective function to be minimized.

The two problems in this module are formally written as:

$$
1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max} \qquad \text{and} \qquad 1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; T_{\max}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** Minimizing **Maximum Lateness** ($L_{\max}$) and **Maximum Tardiness** ($T_{\max}$) on a single machine is the cornerstone of deterministic scheduling. KTU expects (i) rigorous definitions, (ii) proof of optimality of the EDD rule, and (iii) solution of numerical problems using Jackson's algorithm and its preemptive extensions (McNaughton / Lawler).

## 1.2 Formal Definitions

Let $n$ jobs be processed on a single machine. For job $j$:
- $p_j$ = processing time,
- $d_j$ = due date,
- $C_j$ = completion time in a given schedule,
- $S_j$ = start time.

### Lateness
$$
L_j \;=\; C_j - d_j
$$
Lateness may be **negative (early completion)**, **zero (on time)**, or **positive (late)**.

### Tardiness
$$
T_j \;=\; \max\bigl(0,\; C_j - d_j\bigr) \;=\; \max(0,\; L_j)
$$
Tardiness is **always non-negative** and equals the *positive part* of lateness.

### Earliness
$$
E_j \;=\; \max\bigl(0,\; d_j - C_j\bigr)
$$

### Aggregate Performance Measures
$$
L_{\max} \;=\; \max_{1 \le j \le n}\bigl\{L_j\bigr\} \qquad\qquad T_{\max} \;=\; \max_{1 \le j \le n}\bigl\{T_j\bigr\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** $L_{\max}$ penalises a job that is *late* (positive $L_j$), but a strongly *early* job can "compensate" only arithmetically ŌĆö the maximum operator picks the worst single job. $T_{\max}$, however, ignores early completions entirely.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Doctor's Clinic" Intuition

Imagine **Dr. Priya** running a single consultation room. Five patients arrive at time **0** with appointments at $d_1,\dots,d_5$. The doctor processes them one by one. $L_j$ is the *signed* delay of patient $j$ from the appointment; $T_j$ is the *unavoidable* wait that Dr. Priya's receptionist would apologise for.

- $L_{\max}$ = the *worst signed slip* on the schedule ŌĆö a "boss complaint" if the CEO's appointment slips by 90 minutes while a peon's slips by only 5 minutes.
- $T_{\max}$ = the *worst absolute slip* a non-technical observer cares about.

The doctor asks: **"In what order should I call patients so that the *worst* appointment slip is as small as possible?"** ŌĆö exactly the $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max}$ problem. The answer is the **Earliest Due Date (EDD)** rule ŌĆö see the patient whose appointment is earliest gets seen first.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Throughout the Module

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $p_j$ | Processing time of job $j$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ |
| $d_j$ | Due date of job $j$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ |
| $C_j$ | Completion time | $\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ |
| $L_j$ | Lateness | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $T_j$ | Tardiness | $\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ |
| $E_j$ | Earliness | $\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ |
| $L_{\max}$ | Maximum lateness | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $T_{\max}$ | Maximum tardiness | $\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ |
| $U_j$ | Unit penalty, $1$ if $T_j>0$ else $0$ | $\{0,1\}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Earliest Due Date (EDD) Rule ŌĆö Jackson's Theorem (1955)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jackson's Rule (1955):** For the problem $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max}$, an optimal schedule is obtained by sequencing jobs in **non-decreasing order of their due dates**:
> $$d_{\sigma(1)} \le d_{\sigma(2)} \le \cdots \le d_{\sigma(n)}$$
> where $\sigma$ is the permutation. The minimum value of $L_{\max}$ is
> $$L_{\max}^{*} \;=\; \max_{1 \le k \le n}\Bigl\{\;\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{\sigma(i)} \;-\; d_{\sigma(k)}\Bigr\}$$

### Why EDD is Optimal ŌĆö The Exchange (Adjacent-Pair) Argument

Suppose an optimal schedule $\pi^{*}$ contains an adjacent inversion: job $i$ is scheduled immediately before job $j$, yet $d_i > d_j$. Let the start time of $i$ be $t$. Then:
- In the inverted order: $C_i = t + p_i$, $C_j = t + p_i + p_j$
- Swap them: $C_i' = t + p_i + p_j$, $C_j' = t + p_j$

Lateness values:
- Before swap: $L_i = t + p_i - d_i$, $\;L_j = t + p_i + p_j - d_j$
- After swap: $L_i' = t + p_i + p_j - d_i$, $\;L_j' = t + p_j - d_j$

Maximum after the swap:
$$
\max\bigl(L_i', L_j'\bigr) \;\le\; \max\bigl(L_i, L_j\bigr)
$$
because $L_j' \le L_j$ and $L_i' \le L_i$ (since $d_i > d_j$, the larger due date absorbs the extra $p_j$).

**Conclusion:** No inversion can exist in an optimal schedule. Therefore EDD is optimal. $\blacksquare$

## 2.2 Maximum Tardiness ŌĆö Why EDD is Again Optimal for $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; T_{\max}$

Since $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$ and the maximum operator commutes with $\max(0, \cdot)$:
$$
T_{\max} \;=\; \max_j T_j \;=\; \max\bigl(0,\; L_{\max}\bigr)
$$
Hence minimising $T_{\max}$ is equivalent to minimising $\max(0, L_{\max})$, which is achieved by the **same EDD schedule** that minimises $L_{\max}$. The two problems $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max}$ and $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; T_{\max}$ share the same optimal permutation; they differ only in how the optimal value is *reported*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** $T_{\max}$ is the metric in *just-in-time* (JIT) supply chains where a single excessively late delivery can trigger a *line stoppage penalty* disproportionate to a small delay. The largest single tardiness ŌĆö not the average ŌĆö is what auditors fine.

## 2.3 The Lower Bound on $L_{\max}^{\*}$

A *trivial* lower bound, used in branch-and-bound and dynamic programming, is:
$$
LB \;=\; \max_{1 \le k \le n}\Bigl\{\; \sum_{j:\,d_j \le d_{(k)}} p_j \;-\; d_{(k)} \;\Bigr\}
$$
where $d_{(k)}$ is the $k$-th smallest due date. For the EDD schedule this bound is **tight**.

## 2.4 McNaughton's Rule for $1 \,\vert\, pmtn \,\vert\, L_{\max}$

When **preemption is allowed**, the bound $LB$ can be achieved by the following algorithm (McNaughton, 1959):

1. Sort jobs by EDD.
2. Maintain a *current partial schedule* and a *cumulative processing load* $P_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{\sigma(i)}$.
3. If $P_k > d_{\sigma(k)}$, preempt job $\sigma(k)$ ŌĆö run it for $(d_{\sigma(k)} - P_{k-1})$ time units, then **start the next job from time $d_{\sigma(k)}$**.
4. Repeat until all jobs are placed.

The minimum achievable $L_{\max}$ equals the lower bound above. The algorithm runs in $O(n \log n)$ time.

## 2.5 Lawler's Algorithm for $1 \,\vert\, pmtn,\, prec \,\vert\, L_{\max}$

When both **preemption** and **precedence constraints** are present, Lawler (1973) gave a backward-scheduling algorithm:

1. Let $S = \{\,j : j \text{ has no successor}\,\}$ be the set of jobs eligible to be placed last.
2. Choose $j^{\*} = \arg\max_{j \in S} p_j$ and fix it in the last unfilled position.
3. Remove $j^{\*}$ from the precedence graph and update $S$.
4. Repeat until all jobs are placed (working backwards from the last position to the first).

This algorithm runs in $O(n^2)$ and produces an **optimal preemptive schedule with precedence**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| \# | Concept | Formula / Rule | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lateness | $L_j = C_j - d_j$ | Signed |
| 2 | Tardiness | $T_j = \max(0,\; L_j)$ | Non-negative |
| 3 | Earliness | $E_j = \max(0,\; -L_j)$ | Non-negative |
| 4 | Maximum Lateness | $L_{\max} = \max_j L_j$ | Worst single job |
| 5 | Maximum Tardiness | $T_{\max} = \max_j T_j = \max(0, L_{\max})$ | Worst single job |
| 6 | EDD Optimality (Jackson) | Sequence by $d_j \uparrow$ | Minimises $L_{\max}$ & $T_{\max}$ |
| 7 | Optimal $L_{\max}^{\*}$ value | $L_{\max}^{*} = \max_k \bigl\{ P_k - d_{(k)} \bigr\}$ | After EDD order |
| 8 | Lower Bound (LB) | $LB = \max_k \bigl\{ \sum_{j:\,d_j \le d_{(k)}} p_j - d_{(k)} \bigr\}$ | For B&B |
| 9 | McNaughton rule | Preempt when $P_k > d_{(k)}$ | $1\,\vert\,pmtn\,\vert\,L_{\max}$ |
| 10 | Lawler rule | Place largest-$p$ eligible job last | $1\,\vert\,pmtn,prec\,\vert\,L_{\max}$ |
| 11 | Identity | $L_j = E_j \cdot (-1) \cdot [\text{negated}] + T_j$ | $L_j = T_j - E_j$ |
| 12 | Equivalence | $T_{\max} = \max(0,\; L_{\max})$ | Single machine only |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor wafer fabrication:** a single lot exceeding its due window disrupts the *hot lot* queue; $T_{\max}$ is the operational KPI.
- **Cloud job schedulers:** SLAs penalise the *worst* overdue VM, not the average ŌĆö direct $T_{\max}$ minimisation.
- **Airline turnaround:** a single flight delayed by 4 hours triggers EU261 compensation of Ōé¼600/passenger; $L_{\max}$ is the regulator-facing metric.
- **Construction rebar delivery:** EDD sequencing on a single fabrication line keeps the *worst* site wait bounded.
- **Ship-building dry-dock:** Lawler's rule schedules the longest blocking operations last, respecting hull-block assembly precedence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max}$ and $T_{\max}$ via EDD

### Given Data

| Job $j$ | Processing time $p_j$ | Due date $d_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 7 |
| 2 | 5 | 4 |
| 3 | 2 | 10 |
| 4 | 4 | 8 |
| 5 | 1 | 6 |

### Step 1 ŌĆö Apply EDD (sort by $d_j$ ascending)

$$
d_{(1)}=4\ (J2),\quad d_{(2)}=6\ (J5),\quad d_{(3)}=7\ (J1),\quad d_{(4)}=8\ (J4),\quad d_{(5)}=10\ (J3)
$$

EDD order: $\;\sigma = (J2,\, J5,\, J1,\, J4,\, J3)$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute completion times

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{J2} &= p_{J2} = 5 \\
C_{J5} &= C_{J2} + p_{J5} = 5 + 1 = 6 \\
C_{J1} &= C_{J5} + p_{J1} = 6 + 3 = 9 \\
C_{J4} &= C_{J1} + p_{J4} = 9 + 4 = 13 \\
C_{J3} &= C_{J4} + p_{J3} = 13 + 2 = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $L_j$ and $T_j$ for each job

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{J2} &= 5 - 4 = 1, \quad &T_{J2} &= \max(0,\,1) = 1 \\
L_{J5} &= 6 - 6 = 0, \quad &T_{J5} &= \max(0,\,0) = 0 \\
L_{J1} &= 9 - 7 = 2, \quad &T_{J1} &= \max(0,\,2) = 2 \\
L_{J4} &= 13 - 8 = 5, \quad &T_{J4} &= \max(0,\,5) = 5 \\
L_{J3} &= 15 - 10 = 5, \quad &T_{J3} &= \max(0,\,5) = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Aggregate the maxima

$$
L_{\max}^{*} \;=\; \max\{1,\,0,\,2,\,5,\,5\} \;=\; 5
$$

$$
T_{\max}^{*} \;=\; \max\{1,\,0,\,2,\,5,\,5\} \;=\; 5
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Verify optimality by the explicit formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\max}^{*} &= \max_{k=1}^{5}\bigl\{\, P_k - d_{(k)}\,\bigr\} \\
&= \max\bigl\{(5-4),\;(6-6),\;(9-7),\;(13-8),\;(15-10)\bigr\} \\
&= \max\{1,\;0,\;2,\;5,\;5\} \;=\; 5 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The explicit closed-form $L_{\max}^{*} = \max_k(P_k - d_{(k)})$ is the **examiner's shortcut**: once you have the EDD order and the cumulative $P_k$, you do not even need to compute $L_j$ for every job. The maximum of the five differences is the answer.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max}$ with Preemption (McNaughton's Rule)

### Given Data (modified example)

| Job $j$ | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 6 |
| 2 | 5 | 9 |
| 3 | 3 | 11 |

### Step 1 ŌĆö Sort by EDD

EDD order: $J1,\;J2,\;J3$ with $d = 6,\;9,\;11$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $P_k$ and compare to $d_{(k)}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 4, \quad &d_{(1)} &= 6, \quad &P_1 - d_{(1)} &= -2 \;\;(\text{OK, no preemption}) \\
P_2 &= 4+5 = 9, \quad &d_{(2)} &= 9, \quad &P_2 - d_{(2)} &= 0 \;\;(\text{OK}) \\
P_3 &= 4+5+3 = 12, \quad &d_{(3)} &= 11, \quad &P_3 - d_{(3)} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

No preemptions needed. $L_{\max}^{*} = 1$. Schedule: $J1$ at $[0,4)$, $J2$ at $[4,9)$, $J3$ at $[9,12)$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö A case *requiring* preemption

Now take $p_1 = 5, p_2 = 5, p_3 = 1$ with due dates $d_1=4, d_2=8, d_3=10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 5 > d_{(1)}=4 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{PREEMPT } J_1 \text{ at } t=4 \\
&\quad\text{Run } J_1 \text{ on }[0,4),\;J_2 \text{ on }[4,9),\;J_3 \text{ on }[9,10),\;J_1 \text{ on }[10,11)
\end{aligned}
$$

Final schedule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_1 &: [0,\,4) \cup [10,\,11) \\
J_2 &: [4,\,9) \\
J_3 &: [9,\,10)
\end{aligned}
$$

Verifying: $C_{J1}=11, C_{J2}=9, C_{J3}=10$. Latenesses: $L_{J1}=7, L_{J2}=1, L_{J3}=0$. With preemption the bound $L_{\max}^{*}=7$ is unavoidable because $\sum p_j = 11$ and the smallest due date is $d_1=4$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of Algorithms

| Problem | Best Algorithm | Time Complexity | Optimal? | KTU Typical Sub-question |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max}$ | EDD (Jackson) | $O(n \log n)$ | Yes (proof by exchange) | "Sequence to minimise $L_{\max}$" |
| $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; T_{\max}$ | EDD (Jackson) | $O(n \log n)$ | Yes (same schedule) | "Compute minimum $T_{\max}$" |
| $1 \,\vert\, pmtn \,\vert\, L_{\max}$ | McNaughton | $O(n \log n)$ | Yes | "With preemption, find min $L_{\max}$" |
| $1 \,\vert\, pmtn, prec \,\vert\, L_{\max}$ | Lawler | $O(n^2)$ | Yes | "With precedence DAG, sequence jobs" |
| $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; \sum C_j$ | SPT (Smith) | $O(n \log n)$ | Yes (different problem) | Comparison question |
| $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; \sum T_j$ | Hodgson (branch & bound) | NP-hard | Yes (exact) | "Tardiness" in KTU Module 3 |
| $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; \sum U_j$ | Moore (1968) | $O(n \log n)$ | Yes | "Number of tardy jobs" |

## 3.4 Derivation: From $L_{\max}$ Optimality to $T_{\max}$ Optimality

We can derive the second from the first formally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\max} &= \max_{1 \le j \le n}\bigl\{\max(0,\, L_j)\bigr\} \\
&= \max\Bigl\{\, 0,\; \max_{j:\,L_j>0} L_j \Bigr\} \\
&= \max\bigl\{\, 0,\; L_{\max}\,\bigr\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the $\max(0, \cdot)$ function is monotonically non-decreasing, the schedule that minimises $L_{\max}$ also minimises $\max(0,\, L_{\max}) = T_{\max}$. Hence **EDD is simultaneously optimal for both objectives on a single machine.** $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equivalence is *false* on parallel machines ($P_m \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max}$ vs $P_m \;\vert\;\ \vert\; T_{\max}$). On identical parallel machines, the two problems diverge, and the $T_{\max}$ problem is strictly harder (Jackson's rule no longer applies). KTU restricts Module 2 to the single-machine case.

## 3.5 Lower-Bound Verification ŌĆö A Sub-Examination Drill

Using the data of ┬¦3.1, compute the lower bound $LB$ without scheduling:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LB &= \max_k\Bigl\{\sum_{j:\,d_j \le d_{(k)}} p_j \;-\; d_{(k)}\Bigr\} \\
&= \max\Bigl\{
\sum_{j:\,d_j \le 4} p_j - 4,\;
\sum_{j:\,d_j \le 6} p_j - 6,\;
\sum_{j:\,d_j \le 7} p_j - 7,\;
\sum_{j:\,d_j \le 8} p_j - 8,\;
\sum_{j:\,d_j \le 10} p_j - 10
\Bigr\} \\
&= \max\{\,5-4,\;(5+1)-6,\;(5+1+3)-7,\;(5+1+3+4)-8,\;(5+1+3+4+2)-10\,\} \\
&= \max\{1,\;0,\;2,\;5,\;5\} \;=\; 5
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches $L_{\max}^{*} = 5$ computed in ┬¦3.1 Step 4. The lower bound is **tight** ŌĆö confirming EDD is optimal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Decision Flow for $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max}$ / $T_{\max}$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input n jobs with pj and dj] --> B{Sort jobs by dj ascending}
    B --> C[Construct EDD sequence sigma]
    C --> D[Compute cumulative processing Pk]
    D --> E{Preemption allowed?}
    E -- No --> F[Compute Cj in EDD order]
    E -- Yes --> G{Precedence constraints?}
    G -- No --> H[Apply McNaughton preemption rule]
    G -- Yes --> I[Apply Lawler backward rule]
    F --> J[Compute Lj = Cj - dj]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Tj = max 0 Lj]
    K --> L[Compute Lmax and Tmax]
    L --> M[Output optimal sequence and Lmax star]
```

## 4.2 Gantt-Style Timeline Schematic (Worked Example ┬¦3.1)

```mermaid
gantt
    title EDD Schedule Gantt Chart (5 jobs, single machine)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section EDD order
    Job2 (p=5, d=4) :a1, 0, 5
    Job5 (p=1, d=6) :a2, 5, 1
    Job1 (p=3, d=7) :a3, 6, 3
    Job4 (p=4, d=8) :a4, 9, 4
    Job3 (p=2, d=10) :a5, 13, 2
```

The horizontal axis represents the **time line** (in arbitrary units); each bar represents the time-window during which a job occupies the single machine. The right edge of each bar is the **completion time** $C_j$.

## 4.3 Precedence DAG and Lawler's Backward Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Job A: p=3] --> C[Job C: p=4]
    B[Job B: p=5] --> C
    C --> D[Job D: p=2]
    A --> D
    B --> E[Job E: p=6]
    E --> F[Job F: p=3]
    D --> F
```

**Lawler interpretation (for $1 \,\vert\, pmtn, prec \,\vert\, L_{\max}$):**

- Step 1: Set of jobs with no successor = $\{F\}$ (largest $p$ is itself). Place **F** last.
- Step 2: Remove F. New sink set = $\{D, E\}$. Largest $p$ = **E** (p=6). Place **E** in the second-last position.
- Step 3: Remove E. New sink set = $\{D\}$. Place **D** third-last.
- Step 4: Sink set = $\{A, B\}$. Largest $p$ = **B** (p=5). Place **B** fourth-last.
- Step 5: Remaining = **A**, place first.

**Backward sequence:** F, E, D, B, A ŌåÆ **Forward execution order:** A, B, D, E, F.

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix Diagram ŌĆö Why EDD Wins

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Arbitrary sequence pi] --> check{Any adjacent inversion exists?}
    check -- Yes --> swap[Swap the inverted pair]
    swap --> delta[Delta max L_i L_j decreases or unchanged]
    delta --> check
    check -- No --> opt[EDD sequence reached, Lmax is minimal]
```

This loop captures the **exchange-argument proof**: a finite number of inversions exist, each swap is non-deteriorating, and termination yields an optimal schedule.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define *lateness*, *tardiness*, and *earliness* of a job in a schedule. How are they related? `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

For job $j$ in a schedule, with completion time $C_j$ and due date $d_j$:

- **Lateness:** $L_j = C_j - d_j$ (signed; may be negative, zero, or positive).
- **Tardiness:** $T_j = \max(0,\; C_j - d_j) = \max(0,\; L_j)$ (non-negative; equals the *positive part* of lateness).
- **Earliness:** $E_j = \max(0,\; d_j - C_j) = \max(0,\; -L_j)$ (non-negative; equals the *positive part* of the negative of lateness).

**Relation:** $L_j = T_j - E_j$ for every $j$, and consequently $L_{\max} = T_{\max} - E_{\min}$ where $E_{\min} = \min_j E_j$ (the *most early* job). On a single machine, $T_{\max} = \max(0,\; L_{\max})$ because the $\max$ operator is monotone and the negative parts are absorbed. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. State Jackson's rule for the $1 \,\vert\, \vert\, L_{\max}$ problem. Why is EDD optimal? `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Jackson's Rule (1955):** Sequence the $n$ jobs in **non-decreasing order of their due dates** $d_j$. Let $\sigma$ be the EDD permutation. Then

$$
L_{\max}^{*} \;=\; \max_{1 \le k \le n}\Bigl\{\,P_k - d_{\sigma(k)}\,\Bigr\}
$$

where $P_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{\sigma(i)}$.

**Optimality (Exchange Argument):** Suppose an optimal schedule has an adjacent pair $(i, j)$ with $d_i > d_j$ ŌĆö an *inversion*. Let the start time of $i$ be $t$. Before the swap: $L_i = t + p_i - d_i$, $L_j = t + p_i + p_j - d_j$. After the swap: $L_i' = t + p_i + p_j - d_i$, $L_j' = t + p_j - d_j$. We have $L_j' \le L_j$ (earlier) and $L_i' \le L_i$ (since $d_i > d_j$, the *larger* due date absorbs the extra $p_j$). Hence $\max(L_i', L_j') \le \max(L_i, L_j)$, so the swap does not worsen the objective. Repeating bubble-style eliminates all inversions in $O(n^2)$ swaps, yielding the EDD order with the *minimum* $L_{\max}$. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]` ŌĆö 14 Marks

**(a)** *Seven jobs are to be processed on a single machine. The processing times and due dates are:*

| Job | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $p_j$ | 4 | 2 | 6 | 1 | 8 | 3 | 5 |
| $d_j$ | 10 | 4 | 13 | 6 | 18 | 9 | 15 |

*Determine the sequence that minimises $L_{\max}$ and compute the resulting $L_{\max}^{*}$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For the same data, also compute $T_{\max}$ and the per-job tardiness list. Verify that $T_{\max} = \max(0, L_{\max})$.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Step 1: Sort jobs by due date (EDD).**

$$
d_{(1)}=4\;(J2),\;\;d_{(2)}=6\;(J4),\;\;d_{(3)}=9\;(J6),\;\;d_{(4)}=10\;(J1),\;\;d_{(5)}=13\;(J3),\;\;d_{(6)}=15\;(J7),\;\;d_{(7)}=18\;(J5)
$$

**Step 2: EDD order:** $\sigma = (J2, J4, J6, J1, J3, J7, J5)$.

**Step 3: Compute completion times.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{J2} &= 2 \\
C_{J4} &= 2 + 1 = 3 \\
C_{J6} &= 3 + 3 = 6 \\
C_{J1} &= 6 + 4 = 10 \\
C_{J3} &= 10 + 6 = 16 \\
C_{J7} &= 16 + 5 = 21 \\
C_{J5} &= 21 + 8 = 29
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $L_j$ for each job.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{J2} &= 2 - 4 = -2 \\
L_{J4} &= 3 - 6 = -3 \\
L_{J6} &= 6 - 9 = -3 \\
L_{J1} &= 10 - 10 = 0 \\
L_{J3} &= 16 - 13 = 3 \\
L_{J7} &= 21 - 15 = 6 \\
L_{J5} &= 29 - 18 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Aggregate.**

$$
L_{\max}^{*} = \max\{-2,\,-3,\,-3,\,0,\,3,\,6,\,11\} = 11
$$

**Verification by closed form:**

$$
L_{\max}^{*} = \max\bigl\{(2-4),\,(3-6),\,(6-9),\,(10-10),\,(16-13),\,(21-15),\,(29-18)\bigr\} = \max\{-2,\,-3,\,-3,\,0,\,3,\,6,\,11\} = 11 \checkmark
$$

**Answer:** EDD sequence $J2 \to J4 \to J6 \to J1 \to J3 \to J7 \to J5$ achieves $L_{\max}^{*} = 11$ time units. **(7 marks ŌĆö 1 for ordering, 3 for completion times, 2 for lateness, 1 for aggregation)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1: Compute $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$.**

$$
T_{J2}=0,\;\;T_{J4}=0,\;\;T_{J6}=0,\;\;T_{J1}=0,\;\;T_{J3}=3,\;\;T_{J7}=6,\;\;T_{J5}=11
$$

**Step 2: Aggregate $T_{\max}$.**

$$
T_{\max} = \max\{0,\,0,\,0,\,0,\,3,\,6,\,11\} = 11
$$

**Step 3: Verification of identity.**

$$
\max(0,\,L_{\max}) = \max(0,\,11) = 11 = T_{\max} \quad\checkmark
$$

**Answer:** Tardiness list is $(0,\,0,\,0,\,0,\,3,\,6,\,11)$, and $T_{\max} = 11$ matches $\max(0, L_{\max}) = 11$. The single most-delayed job is $J5$ at $11$ time units late. **(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for $T_j$ list, 2 for $T_{\max}$, 3 for verification and conclusion)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]` ŌĆö 14 Marks

**(a)** *Explain the **McNaughton preemption rule** for $1 \,\vert\, pmtn \,\vert\, L_{\max}$. For the data $J_1:(p=6, d=4)$, $J_2:(p=5, d=8)$, $J_3:(p=3, d=11)$, determine the minimum $L_{\max}$ and the preemptive schedule.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss the conditions under which preemption is required. What is the minimum $L_{\max}$ if preemption is **forbidden**? Compare the two answers.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**McNaughton's Rule (Recap):** Sort jobs by EDD. Let $P_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{\sigma(i)}$. If at any stage $P_k > d_{\sigma(k)}$, preempt the current job ŌĆö process it only up to time $d_{\sigma(k)}$, then start the next job from there. The algorithm is optimal in $O(n \log n)$ time, and the resulting $L_{\max}^{*} = \max_k (P_k - d_{(k)})$.

**Step 1: EDD order.** Due dates: $d_1=4, d_2=8, d_3=11$. Order is $J_1, J_2, J_3$.

**Step 2: Cumulative processing.**

$$
P_1 = 6,\quad P_2 = 11,\quad P_3 = 14
$$

**Step 3: Compare to due dates.**

$$
P_1 - d_1 = 6 - 4 = 2 \quad(\text{preempt at } t=4)
$$
$$
P_2 - d_2 = 11 - 8 = 3 \quad(\text{preempt at } t=8)
$$
$$
P_3 - d_3 = 14 - 11 = 3
$$

**Step 4: Construct preemptive schedule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_1 &: [0,\,4) \\
J_2 &: [4,\,9) \\
J_3 &: [9,\,11) \\
J_1 &: [11,\,13) \quad(\text{remaining } 2 \text{ units})
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait ŌĆö re-checking: $J_2$ needs 5 units, $J_1$ already used 4. So $J_2$ occupies $[4, 9)$. Then $J_3$ needs 3 units ŌåÆ $[9, 12)$. Then $J_1$ remaining 2 units ŌåÆ $[12, 14)$.

**Step 5: Completion times and $L_{\max}$.**

$$
C_{J_1} = 14,\quad C_{J_2} = 9,\quad C_{J_3} = 12
$$
$$
L_{J_1} = 14 - 4 = 10,\quad L_{J_2} = 9 - 8 = 1,\quad L_{J_3} = 12 - 11 = 1
$$
$$
L_{\max}^{*} = \max\{10,\,1,\,1\} = 10
$$

**Answer:** The minimum $L_{\max}$ with preemption is **10 time units**, attained by the schedule $[J_1(0{-}4),\,J_2(4{-}9),\,J_3(9{-}12),\,J_1(12{-}14)]$. **(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for rule, 3 for computation, 2 for schedule)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1: Non-preemptive EDD schedule.**

EDD order $J_1, J_2, J_3$ without preemption:
$C_{J_1}=6,\;C_{J_2}=11,\;C_{J_3}=14$.

**Step 2: Latenesses.**

$$
L_{J_1}=6-4=2,\quad L_{J_2}=11-8=3,\quad L_{J_3}=14-11=3
$$
$$
L_{\max}^{\text{non-pmtn}} = 3
$$

**Step 3: Compare.**

With preemption: $L_{\max}^{*} = 10$.
Without preemption: $L_{\max}^{\text{non-pmtn}} = 3$.

**Surprise observation:** Preemption *worsens* the worst-case lateness in this example! This is because the **first** job $J_1$ has a very early due date ($d_1=4$) and a long processing time ($p_1=6$). Even with preemption, $J_1$ *must* finish after 4 time units, and its remaining 2 units can only be placed at the end, pushing $C_{J_1}$ to 14. The total processing load is 14, and $J_1$ is *forced* to slip.

The condition for preemption to help is: $\sum p_j \le \max_j d_j + \min p_j$ (loosely). When a single job's processing time exceeds *all* earlier due dates, preemption cannot save it.

**Conclusion:** Preemption does **not** always improve $L_{\max}$. The result $L_{\max}^{\text{non-pmtn}} = 3 < 10 = L_{\max}^{\text{pmtn}}$ is possible and is a common *exam trap*. **(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for non-preemptive schedule, 2 for comparison, 3 for the conceptual insight)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Identity confusion:** Many students write $L_{\max} = T_{\max}$ *always*. This is FALSE ŌĆö $L_{\max}$ can be negative; $T_{\max}$ cannot. They coincide only when *all* jobs in the schedule are late (i.e., $L_{\max} \ge 0$).
> 2. **Mis-sorted EDD:** A frequent mistake is sorting by $d_j$ in *decreasing* order. Recheck the *sign of inequality* before writing the permutation.
> 3. **Forgetting the cumulative $P_k$:** Some students compute $C_j - d_j$ per job correctly but miss the *closed-form shortcut* $\max_k(P_k - d_{(k)})$ that examiners love to award bonus marks.
> 4. **Preemption ŌēĀ always better:** As Question B(b) shows, preemption can *worsen* $L_{\max}$ for jobs with very early due dates and long processing times. Do not assume preemption helps.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify optimality:** KTU examiners expect a one-line *exchange-argument justification* after the EDD ordering ŌĆö losing 1ŌĆō2 marks if omitted.
> 6. **Misreporting $T_j$ as $L_j$:** $T_j$ is the *positive part*; do not write negative tardiness values.

---

## ­¤ōŗ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Lateness** is signed: $L_j = C_j - d_j \in \mathbb{R}$. **Tardiness** is non-negative: $T_j = \max(0, L_j) \ge 0$.
- **Jackson's Rule (1955):** For $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max}$, sequence jobs by **EDD** (smallest $d_j$ first). The same sequence is optimal for $T_{\max}$.
- **Closed-form value:** $L_{\max}^{*} = \max_k(P_k - d_{(k)})$ over the EDD order.
- **Proof technique:** *Exchange argument* ŌĆö swapping an adjacent inversion never worsens the maximum; iterating gives the EDD permutation.
- **$L_{\max}$ and $T_{\max}$ coincide on a single machine** when no job finishes early; they diverge when early completions exist.
- **McNaughton's Rule (preemption allowed):** Preempt whenever $P_k > d_{(k)}$; run the job only up to the due date, then start the next. Optimal in $O(n \log n)$.
- **Lawler's Rule (preemption + precedence):** Backward-schedule; at each step, place the *largest-$p$ eligible* (sink) job in the current last slot. Optimal in $O(n^2)$.
- **Equivalence on 1 machine:** $T_{\max} = \max(0, L_{\max})$; not true on parallel machines.
- **Time complexity of EDD:** Sorting is $O(n \log n)$; the rest is $O(n)$.
- **Preemption is NOT always beneficial** for $L_{\max}$ ŌĆö counterexample: one early-due, long-processing job ruins the bound.
- **Worst single job dominates:** $L_{\max}$ and $T_{\max}$ are *max-type* objectives; a single mis-sequenced high-$d$ job can blow the bound.
- **Graham's notation:** $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; L_{\max}$ and $1 \;\vert\;\ \vert\; T_{\max}$ are *polynomially solvable* (unlike $\sum T_j$ which is NP-hard).
- **Engineering takeaway:** In JIT / SLA-bound systems, the *worst tardiness* ŌĆö not the average ŌĆö drives penalties; hence $T_{\max}$ is the regulator's metric.
- **Examiner's golden phrases to memorise:** *"Sort by EDD,"* *"exchange argument shows optimality,"* *"closed-form $L_{\max}^{*} = \max_k(P_k - d_{(k)})$,"* *"McNaughton's preemption rule,"* *"Lawler's backward scheduling."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimizing the Number of Tardy Jobs - Moore and Hodgson Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimizing the Number of Tardy Jobs - Moore and Hodgson Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Machine Scheduling Problem 1ŌĆ¢ŌłæUŌéÖ**: Given *n* jobs to be processed on a **single machine**, where each job *j* has a processing time $p_j$ and a **due date** $d_j$, the objective is to find a sequence that **minimizes the number of tardy jobs** (jobs completed after their due date).

The indicator variable for tardiness is defined as:

$$
U_j = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } C_j \leq d_j \text{ (on time)} \\ 1 & \text{if } C_j > d_j \text{ (tardy)} \end{cases}
$$

Where $C_j$ is the **completion time** of job $j$ in the chosen sequence. The objective function to minimize is:

$$
\min Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} U_j
$$

### Moore-Hodgson Algorithm

The **Moore-Hodgson Algorithm** (1968) is a classic **polynomial-time optimal algorithm** (complexity $O(n \log n)$) that solves the 1ŌĆ¢ŌłæUŌéÖ problem. It uses the **Earliest Due Date (EDD)** rule as a starting point and refines it by removing jobs that cause tardiness.

### Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are a **custom tailor** with 5 customers walking in one morning. Each has a different garment requiring different time to stitch (processing time $p_j$) and a different promised pickup time (due date $d_j$). You must decide the order of stitching. Some customers may have to be told "we are sorry, your dress will be late" (tardy). Moore-Hodgson helps you **disappoint the minimum number of customers** ŌĆö those you tell "sorry" to should ideally have the **longest stitching times** so the remaining schedule flows smoothly.

### Conceptual Intuition

- **EDD order** is the natural starting point because scheduling jobs with earlier due dates first **minimizes the maximum lateness** $L_{max}$.
- However, EDD does **not** directly minimize the *count* of tardy jobs. A long job sandwiched in the middle of EDD can delay several short jobs and make all of them tardy.
- The Moore-Hodgson fix: when tardiness arises, **kick out the longest job among those currently scheduled** because that single removal frees the most time, repairing the schedule with minimal disruption.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Foundational Setup

Consider *n* jobs indexed $j = 1, 2, \dots, n$. Each job has:
- **Processing time** $p_j > 0$
- **Due date** $d_j \geq 0$

The completion time of job *j* when scheduled at position *k* (in some sequence $\sigma$) is:

$$
C_{\sigma(k)} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{\sigma(i)}
$$

A job is **tardy** if its completion time exceeds its due date:

$$
\text{Tardy} \iff C_j > d_j
$$

### Algorithmic Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Moore-Hodgson Procedure (Sequential Logic)**:

1. **Sort the jobs in non-decreasing order of due dates** (EDD ordering).
   - This is the baseline sequence.
2. **Initialize two sets**:
   - $S$ = set of jobs scheduled on time (initially empty)
   - $T$ = set of jobs marked tardy (initially empty)
3. **Iterate through jobs in EDD order**. For each job $j_k$ being considered:
   - Tentatively add $j_k$ to set $S$.
   - Compute the cumulative completion time $C_{j_k}$ using the jobs currently in $S$.
4. **Tardiness check**: If $C_{j_k} > d_{j_k}$ (i.e., adding $j_k$ makes it tardy), then:
   - From the set $S \cup \{j_k\}$, **identify the job with the largest processing time** $p_{\max}$.
   - **Remove** that job from $S$ and **add it to set $T$** (tardy set).
5. **Repeat** until all *n* jobs have been examined.
6. **Final output**:
   - Schedule jobs in $S$ (in their EDD order) ŌåÆ all on time.
   - Jobs in $T$ are tardy and can be appended in any order at the end.

### The "Why" Behind the Rule

The decision to remove the **largest-processing-time job** (not the job currently causing tardiness) is mathematically optimal because:

- Removing any job frees exactly its processing time from the cumulative schedule.
- A larger $p$ frees **more time**, providing the **maximum benefit** for the remaining jobs.
- This guarantees that no future job is made tardier than it would have been by removing any other job ŌĆö establishing **optimality**.

### KTU Formula Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Rule** |
|---|---|---|
| $C_j$ | Completion time of job $j$ | $C_j = \sum_{i \in \text{predecessors of } j} p_i + p_j$ |
| $U_j$ | Tardiness indicator | $U_j = 1$ if $C_j > d_j$, else $0$ |
| $Z$ | Number of tardy jobs (objective) | $Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} U_j$ |
| EDD | Earliest Due Date rule | Sequence jobs by ascending $d_j$ |
| SPT | Shortest Processing Time rule | Sequence jobs by ascending $p_j$ |
| $L_j$ | Lateness of job $j$ | $L_j = C_j - d_j$ |
| $T_j$ | Tardiness of job $j$ | $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$ |
| Algorithm Complexity | Moore-Hodgson runtime | $O(n \log n)$ |

### Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world applications** of 1ŌĆ¢ŌłæUŌéÖ / Moore-Hodgson:
> - **Production planning** in a single-machine bottleneck work cell.
> - **Out-patient scheduling** in hospitals (minimize missed appointments).
> - **Logistics dispatching** for time-critical courier deliveries.
> - **Software sprint backlogs** where missed deadlines are penalized by story-point loss.
> - **Job-shop scheduling** when a single critical machine gates the entire workflow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Example ŌĆö Detailed Execution

**Problem Statement (KTU Exam-Style):**

Consider 5 jobs to be processed on a single machine. The processing times and due dates are:

| Job | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| $J_1$ | 3 | 5 |
| $J_2$ | 6 | 8 |
| $J_3$ | 4 | 9 |
| $J_4$ | 2 | 7 |
| $J_5$ | 5 | 11 |

Find the sequence that **minimizes the number of tardy jobs** using the Moore-Hodgson algorithm.

---

### Step 1 ŌĆö Sort by EDD (Earliest Due Date)

Sorting jobs by ascending $d_j$:

$$
J_1 (d=5) \;\prec\; J_4 (d=7) \;\prec\; J_2 (d=8) \;\prec\; J_3 (d=9) \;\prec\; J_5 (d=11)
$$

The sorted processing sequence: $(p_1, p_4, p_2, p_3, p_5) = (3, 2, 6, 4, 5)$.

---

### Step 2 ŌĆö Initialize Sets

$$
S = \emptyset, \quad T = \emptyset
$$

---

### Step 3 ŌĆö Iterate Through Sorted Jobs

#### **Iteration 1 ŌĆö Consider $J_1$:**

- Tentative schedule: $S = \{J_1\}$
- Completion time: $C_1 = 3$
- Compare with due date: $C_1 = 3 \leq d_1 = 5$ Ō£ō **On time**
- Final: $S = \{J_1\}$, $T = \{\;\}$

#### **Iteration 2 ŌĆö Consider $J_4$:**

- Tentative schedule: $S = \{J_1, J_4\}$
- Completion time for $J_4$: $C_4 = p_1 + p_4 = 3 + 2 = 5$
- Compare with due date: $C_4 = 5 \leq d_4 = 7$ Ō£ō **On time**
- Final: $S = \{J_1, J_4\}$, $T = \{\;\}$

#### **Iteration 3 ŌĆö Consider $J_2$:**

- Tentative schedule: $S = \{J_1, J_4, J_2\}$
- Completion time for $J_2$: $C_2 = p_1 + p_4 + p_2 = 3 + 2 + 6 = 11$
- Compare with due date: $C_2 = 11 > d_2 = 8$ Ō£Ś **Tardy!**
- **Removal action**: Find the job with largest $p$ in $S \cup \{J_2\} = \{J_1, J_4, J_2\}$.
  - $p_1 = 3$, $p_4 = 2$, $p_2 = 6$ ŌåÆ max is $J_2$ with $p_2 = 6$.
- **Remove $J_2$** from $S$, add to $T$.
- Final: $S = \{J_1, J_4\}$, $T = \{J_2\}$

#### **Iteration 4 ŌĆö Consider $J_3$:**

- Tentative schedule: $S = \{J_1, J_4, J_3\}$
- Completion time for $J_3$: $C_3 = p_1 + p_4 + p_3 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9$
- Compare with due date: $C_3 = 9 \leq d_3 = 9$ Ō£ō **On time** (equality allowed)
- Final: $S = \{J_1, J_4, J_3\}$, $T = \{J_2\}$

#### **Iteration 5 ŌĆö Consider $J_5$:**

- Tentative schedule: $S = \{J_1, J_4, J_3, J_5\}$
- Completion time for $J_5$: $C_5 = p_1 + p_4 + p_3 + p_5 = 3 + 2 + 4 + 5 = 14$
- Compare with due date: $C_5 = 14 > d_5 = 11$ Ō£Ś **Tardy!**
- **Removal action**: Find the job with largest $p$ in $S \cup \{J_5\} = \{J_1, J_4, J_3, J_5\}$.
  - $p_1 = 3$, $p_4 = 2$, $p_3 = 4$, $p_5 = 5$ ŌåÆ max is $J_5$ with $p_5 = 5$.
- **Remove $J_5$** from $S$, add to $T$.
- Final: $S = \{J_1, J_4, J_3\}$, $T = \{J_2, J_5\}$

---

### Step 4 ŌĆö Construct Final Optimal Schedule

- **On-time jobs in $S$** (in EDD order): $J_1 \to J_4 \to J_3$
- **Tardy jobs in $T$**: $J_2, J_5$ (can be appended at the end in any order)

**Optimal sequence**: $J_1 \to J_4 \to J_3 \to J_2 \to J_5$

---

### Step 5 ŌĆö Verification Table

| Position | Job | $p_j$ | $C_j$ | $d_j$ | $U_j$ | Status |
|----------|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------|--------|
| 1 | $J_1$ | 3 | 3 | 5 | 0 | On time |
| 2 | $J_4$ | 2 | 5 | 7 | 0 | On time |
| 3 | $J_3$ | 4 | 9 | 9 | 0 | On time |
| 4 | $J_2$ | 6 | 15 | 8 | 1 | Tardy |
| 5 | $J_5$ | 5 | 20 | 11 | 1 | Tardy |

$$
\min Z = \sum U_j = 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 2
$$

**Number of tardy jobs = 2** (which is optimal for this instance).

### Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Action | $S$ | $T$ | Cumulative $C$ |
|------|--------|-----|-----|----------------|
| Init | ŌĆö | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | 0 |
| 1 | Add $J_1$ | $\{J_1\}$ | $\emptyset$ | 3 |
| 2 | Add $J_4$ | $\{J_1, J_4\}$ | $\emptyset$ | 5 |
| 3 | Add $J_2$, remove $J_2$ | $\{J_1, J_4\}$ | $\{J_2\}$ | 5 |
| 4 | Add $J_3$ | $\{J_1, J_4, J_3\}$ | $\{J_2\}$ | 9 |
| 5 | Add $J_5$, remove $J_5$ | $\{J_1, J_4, J_3\}$ | $\{J_2, J_5\}$ | 9 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Flowchart ŌĆö Moore-Hodgson Algorithm Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input n jobs with p_j and d_j] --> B[Sort all jobs by EDD ascending d_j]
    B --> C[Initialize S equals empty set, T equals empty set]
    C --> D[Current cumulative time C equals 0]
    D --> E{All n jobs processed?}
    E -- No --> F[Tentatively add next EDD job j to S]
    F --> G[C equals C plus p_j]
    G --> H{C greater than d_j?}
    H -- No --> I[Keep j in S]
    I --> J[Move to next job]
    J --> E
    H -- Yes --> K[Find job m in S union j with largest p_m]
    K --> L[Remove m from S, add m to T]
    L --> M[Update C equals C minus p_m]
    M --> J
    E -- Yes --> N[Final Schedule: S in EDD order followed by T jobs]
    N --> O[Output: Min number of tardy jobs equals cardinality of T]
```

### Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"]
        A1[Job Set: n jobs]
        A2[Processing Times p_j]
        A3[Due Dates d_j]
    end

    subgraph SORT["Preprocessing"]
        B1[EDD Sort Module]
        B2[Sorted Sequence sigma]
    end

    subgraph CORE["Moore Hodgson Core Engine"]
        C1[Set S: on time buffer]
        C2[Set T: tardy accumulator]
        C3[Removal Logic: argmax p in S union j]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Stage"]
        D1[Optimal Sequence sigma star]
        D2[Number of Tardy Jobs equals cardinality of T]
        D3[Completion Time Vector C_j]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
```

### Conceptual State Diagram of Set S

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty: Initialize S equals empty
    Empty --> OnTimeSet: Add jobs satisfying C_j less than or equal to d_j
    OnTimeSet --> OnTimeSet: Add new on time job
    OnTimeSet --> ReducedSet: Tardiness detected, remove job with max p
    ReducedSet --> OnTimeSet: Add next on time job
    OnTimeSet --> [*]: All jobs processed
    ReducedSet --> [*]: All jobs processed
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*Define the 1ŌĆ¢ŌłæUŌéÖ scheduling problem. What does the Moore-Hodgson algorithm achieve?*

**Model Answer:**
The 1ŌĆ¢ŌłæUŌéÖ problem is a single-machine deterministic scheduling problem in which *n* jobs, each with a known processing time $p_j$ and due date $d_j$, must be sequenced to minimize the number of jobs completed after their due dates. The objective function is $Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} U_j$ where $U_j = 1$ if $C_j > d_j$ and $U_j = 0$ otherwise. The Moore-Hodgson algorithm is a polynomial-time optimal algorithm of complexity $O(n \log n)$ that finds such a minimum-tardy-job sequence by combining EDD ordering with selective removal of the largest processing-time job whenever tardiness occurs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*In the Moore-Hodgson algorithm, why is the job with the largest processing time removed when tardiness is detected, instead of the job that caused the tardiness?*

**Model Answer:**
Removing the job with the largest processing time maximizes the amount of processing time freed from the cumulative schedule, which in turn provides the greatest relief to the remaining on-time jobs. This greedy removal choice has been proven to be optimal because any alternative removal would either leave the schedule equally or more constrained, making additional future jobs tardy. Hence, the algorithm exchanges "tardy status" from the currently failing job to whichever already-scheduled job has the largest $p_j$, ensuring the global optimum for minimizing the count of tardy jobs.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024]**

The following 6 jobs are to be processed on a single machine:

| Job | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| $J_1$ | 4 | 8 |
| $J_2$ | 2 | 6 |
| $J_3$ | 7 | 12 |
| $J_4$ | 5 | 10 |
| $J_5$ | 3 | 9 |
| $J_6$ | 6 | 15 |

**(a)** Apply the Moore-Hodgson algorithm to obtain the optimal sequence that minimizes the number of tardy jobs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the number of tardy jobs and the total tardiness $\sum T_j$ for the obtained sequence. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Moore-Hodgson Algorithm:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö EDD Sort** (ascending $d_j$):

$$
J_2(d=6) \prec J_1(d=8) \prec J_5(d=9) \prec J_4(d=10) \prec J_3(d=12) \prec J_6(d=15)
$$

Sorted $p$ sequence: $(2, 4, 3, 5, 7, 6)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Iteration Table:**

| Iter | Job Added | $S$ after add | $C$ | Compare $C$ vs $d$ | Action |
|------|-----------|---------------|-----|--------------------|--------|
| 1 | $J_2$ | $\{J_2\}$ | 2 | $2 \leq 6$ Ō£ō | Keep |
| 2 | $J_1$ | $\{J_2, J_1\}$ | 6 | $6 \leq 8$ Ō£ō | Keep |
| 3 | $J_5$ | $\{J_2, J_1, J_5\}$ | 9 | $9 \leq 9$ Ō£ō | Keep |
| 4 | $J_4$ | $\{J_2, J_1, J_5, J_4\}$ | 14 | $14 > 10$ Ō£Ś | Find max $p$: $J_4$($p=5$), remove |
| 5 | $J_3$ | $\{J_2, J_1, J_5, J_3\}$ | 16 | $16 > 12$ Ō£Ś | Find max $p$: $J_3$($p=7$), remove |
| 6 | $J_6$ | $\{J_2, J_1, J_5, J_6\}$ | 15 | $15 \leq 15$ Ō£ō | Keep |

**Final state:**
- $S = \{J_2, J_1, J_5, J_6\}$
- $T = \{J_4, J_3\}$

**Optimal sequence:** $J_2 \to J_1 \to J_5 \to J_6 \to J_4 \to J_3$

**(b) Tardiness Calculation:**

| Pos | Job | $p_j$ | $C_j$ | $d_j$ | $T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$ |
|-----|-----|-------|-------|-------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | $J_2$ | 2 | 2 | 6 | 0 |
| 2 | $J_1$ | 4 | 6 | 8 | 0 |
| 3 | $J_5$ | 3 | 9 | 9 | 0 |
| 4 | $J_6$ | 6 | 15 | 15 | 0 |
| 5 | $J_4$ | 5 | 20 | 10 | 10 |
| 6 | $J_3$ | 7 | 27 | 12 | 15 |

$$
\sum U_j = 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 2
$$

$$
\sum T_j = 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 10 + 15 = 25 \text{ time units}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State and prove the optimality property of the Moore-Hodgson algorithm for the 1ŌĆ¢ŌłæUŌéÖ problem. Why is EDD the initial ordering choice? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the following 4-job instance, find the optimal sequence using Moore-Hodgson and verify it by enumerating all $4! = 24$ possible sequences:

| Job | $p_j$ | $d_j$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| A | 5 | 8 |
| B | 3 | 5 |
| C | 4 | 10 |
| D | 2 | 7 |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Optimality Property:**

**Statement:** For the 1ŌĆ¢ŌłæUŌéÖ problem, the Moore-Hodgson algorithm produces an optimal schedule in $O(n \log n)$ time.

**Why EDD as initial ordering:**
The EDD rule is optimal for minimizing maximum lateness $L_{max}$ on a single machine. In the 1ŌĆ¢ŌłæUŌéÖ problem, EDD provides a **necessary (but not sufficient) baseline** that places the most constrained jobs earliest ŌĆö any optimal sequence can be transformed into an EDD-prefixed sequence without increasing tardy count.

**Optimality Proof (Sketch via exchange argument):**

Assume the algorithm outputs $S$ on-time and $T$ tardy. Suppose an alternative sequence $\sigma^*$ has fewer tardy jobs. Consider the first position $k$ where $\sigma$ and $\sigma^*$ differ. By the exchange argument, swapping any out-of-order pair in $\sigma^*$ to bring it closer to EDD does not increase the number of tardy jobs because the job with the smaller $d_j$ benefits more from being scheduled earlier. Eventually $\sigma^*$ can be transformed into an EDD-like prefix without losing its tardiness count. The Moore-Hodgson removal rule (largest-$p$ extraction) preserves feasibility because removing the largest-$p$ job from a tardy-prefix maximizes the time remaining for subsequent EDD jobs, ensuring no extra jobs become tardy. Hence no sequence can have fewer tardy jobs than $\sigma$ ŌĆö proving optimality.

**(b) Moore-Hodgson Application:**

**EDD sort:** $B(d=5) \prec D(d=7) \prec A(d=8) \prec C(d=10)$

$p$ sequence: $(3, 2, 5, 4)$

| Iter | Job | $S$ | $C$ | Check | Action |
|------|-----|-----|-----|-------|--------|
| 1 | B | $\{B\}$ | 3 | $3 \leq 5$ Ō£ō | Keep |
| 2 | D | $\{B,D\}$ | 5 | $5 \leq 7$ Ō£ō | Keep |
| 3 | A | $\{B,D,A\}$ | 10 | $10 > 8$ Ō£Ś | Max $p$ = $A$($p=5$), remove |
| 4 | C | $\{B,D,C\}$ | 9 | $9 \leq 10$ Ō£ō | Keep |

**Result:** $S = \{B, D, C\}$, $T = \{A\}$

**Optimal sequence:** $B \to D \to C \to A$, with **1 tardy job**.

**Verification by enumeration** (selected critical sequences):

| Sequence | $C_B$ | $C_D$ | $C_A$ | $C_C$ | Tardy Count |
|----------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------------|
| B-D-A-C | 3 | 5 | 10 | 14 | 1 (A) Ō£ō |
| B-D-C-A | 3 | 5 | 9 | 14 | 1 (A) Ō£ō |
| B-A-D-C | 3 | 8 | 13 | 15 | 2 (A, D) |
| A-B-C-D | 5 | 8 | 12 | 14 | 1 (A) Ō£ō |
| D-A-B-C | 2 | 7 | 12 | 15 | 1 (A) Ō£ō |

The Moore-Hodgson sequence achieves the **minimum tardy count of 1**, confirmed optimal.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to remove the LARGEST-$p$ job** (not the tardy job itself) ŌĆö even if the tardy job has a small $p$, the algorithm demands removing the maximum. Marks deducted if logic is reversed.
> 2. **Skipping the EDD sort** ŌĆö without sorting, the algorithm is invalid. Always show the EDD ordering first.
> 3. **Not updating the cumulative completion time $C$** after removing a job ŌĆö students often forget to subtract the removed $p_j$.
> 4. **Confusing tardiness $T_j$ with lateness $L_j$** ŌĆö $T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$ (always non-negative), while $L_j$ can be negative.
> 5. **Equating on-time with completion exactly at due date** ŌĆö $C_j = d_j$ is **on time** ($U_j = 0$), not tardy. Only strict inequality $C_j > d_j$ counts as tardy.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Problem type:** Single-machine, deterministic, no release dates, regular measure $\sum U_j$.
- **Algorithm name:** Moore-Hodgson Algorithm (1968).
- **Time complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ ŌĆö dominated by the EDD sort.
- **Two key actions:**
  1. Sort by **EDD** (ascending due date).
  2. Remove the **largest-processing-time** job whenever a tardiness is triggered.
- **Optimality:** Provably optimal via an exchange argument; no known heuristic outperforms it for 1ŌĆ¢ŌłæUŌéÖ.
- **Output structure:** On-time set $S$ (EDD order) followed by tardy set $T$.
- **Equality rule:** $C_j = d_j$ is **NOT tardy** (closed interval on the right).
- **Companion rules to know:**
  - **SPT** ŌĆö minimizes $\sum C_j$ (not $\sum U_j$).
  - **EDD** ŌĆö minimizes $L_{max}$ (not $\sum U_j$).
  - **WSPT** ŌĆö minimizes $\sum w_j C_j$ (weighted case).
- **Tardiness vs Lateness:**
  - $L_j = C_j - d_j$ (can be negative).
  - $T_j = \max(0, L_j)$ (always $\geq 0$).
- **Weighted extension:** 1ŌĆ¢Ōłæ$w_j U_j$ is **NP-hard** (no polynomial algorithm known).
- **Real-world use:** Custom manufacturing, hospital scheduling, courier dispatch, software sprints.
- **Verification tip:** Always cross-check the final sequence by computing all $C_j$ values and tallying $U_j$ explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimizing Mean Tardiness - Wilkerson-Irwin Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimizing Mean Tardiness - Wilkerson-Irwin Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 ŌĆö Minimizing Mean Tardiness: Wilkerson-Irwin Algorithm

## 1.1 Formal Definition & Problem Context

In KTU's **PEIET633 ŌĆö Industrial Scheduling** framework, this topic falls under **single-machine deterministic scheduling** with the objective of controlling the **lateness profile** of a set of $n$ jobs on one machine. The classical notation used by examiners is:

$$1 \ \vert \ \vert \ \sum U_j \quad \text{and} \quad 1 \ \vert \ \vert \ \sum T_j$$

Where the three-field notation reads as: **1 machine | no intermediate constraints | objective function**.

- **Tardiness** of job $j$, denoted $T_j$, is defined as:
$$T_j \ = \ \max\{0,\ C_j - d_j\}$$
where $C_j$ is the **completion time** and $d_j$ is the **due date**.
- **Mean Tardiness** (the KTU Module-2 metric) is:
$$\bar{T} \ = \ \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} T_j \ = \ \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\max\{0,\ C_j - d_j\}$$
- A binary lateness indicator is also used:
$$U_j \ = \ \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } C_j > d_j \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The **Wilkerson-Irwin Algorithm (1971)** is a **dynamic-programming (DP)** procedure that *exactly* solves $1 \ \vert \ \vert \ \sum U_j$ ŌĆö i.e., it identifies the **maximum cardinality subset of jobs that can be completed on time** when scheduled in EDD order. Because the on-time subset is identified optimally, the residual schedule of tardy jobs yields the **minimum achievable mean tardiness** for the problem. Hence, in the KTU syllabus it is positioned as the algorithmic backbone for *mean-tardiness minimization*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Distinction (Read Carefully for Exams):**
> Wilkerson-Irwin **does not minimise mean tardiness directly**. It first solves the *companion* problem ŌĆö **minimise the number of tardy jobs** ($1 \ \vert \ \vert \ \sum U_j$). Once the optimal on-time set is found, the *mean tardiness* of the resulting schedule is the lowest possible value. Examiners love to test whether students confuse these two objectives.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Customs Inspection Counter"

Imagine a **single customs inspection booth** at an airport. $n$ passengers (jobs) arrive with **service times** $p_j$ and **flight deadlines** $d_j$. The officer must sequence them in **EDD (Earliest Due Date)** to give everyone a fair chance. However, not every passenger can be cleared before their flight ŌĆö the goal is to:

1. Let the **maximum number of passengers** catch their flights on time (the $\sum U_j$ objective), and
2. Make sure those who **miss** their flights face the **shortest possible average delay** (the $\bar{T}$ objective).

The Wilkerson-Irwin DP table is like the officer's ledger: it tracks, for every possible *cumulative workload* $T$, the **largest number of passengers served by their deadlines** if only the first $j$ passengers are considered. Once the ledger shows the best workload configuration, the officer backtracks to know *which* passengers to serve first.

> [!NOTE]
> **State-space logic in plain English:** "After considering the first $j$ passengers, if I have spent exactly $T$ minutes serving them in EDD order, the maximum number I could have cleared on time is $F(j, T)$."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions and Pre-conditions

1. **Single machine**, **no pre-emption**, jobs available at time $0$.
2. **Deterministic** processing times $p_j$ and due dates $d_j$ (no stochasticity).
3. Jobs are **first sorted in non-decreasing order of due date** (EDD order). After sorting, the indices $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ satisfy $d_1 \leq d_2 \leq \ldots \leq d_n$.
4. All jobs in the *on-time subset* are scheduled contiguously in EDD order; tardy jobs follow.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Algorithmic Logic

The algorithm builds a 2-D DP table $F(j, T)$ where each cell stores the **maximum number of jobs (out of the first $j$) that can be completed on time** if the cumulative processing time of those on-time jobs equals exactly $T$.

**Why a 2-D table?** Each cell answers: "Given jobs $1, \ldots, j$ and a budget of $T$ time-units, how many deadlines can I honour?" The recurrence is binary ŌĆö *include* or *exclude* job $j$.

| Step | Action | Reasoning |
|------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | Sort all jobs by non-decreasing $d_j$ (EDD order). | EDD is optimal for $1 \ \vert \ \vert \ \ L_{\max}$; it provides the natural feasibility frame. |
| 2 | Initialise $F(0, 0) = 0$ and $F(0, T) = -\infty$ for all $T > 0$. | Base case: with zero jobs, only zero work yields zero on-time completions. |
| 3 | For $j = 1$ to $n$, for each feasible $T \in [0, d_j]$: | $T \leq d_j$ ensures that even the *last* on-time job among the first $j$ does not exceed its own deadline. |
| 4 | Apply recurrence: $F(j, T) = \max\{F(j-1, T),\ F(j-1, T-p_j) + 1\}$. | **Exclude** job $j$ ŌåÆ same count; **Include** job $j$ ŌåÆ adds 1 to count and consumes $p_j$ time (only if $T \geq p_j$). |
| 5 | Optimal value: $F^* = \max\{F(n, T) \mid 0 \leq T \leq d_n\}$. | The best on-time subset among *all* $n$ jobs. |
| 6 | Backtrack from $(n, T^*)$ where $T^*$ attains $F^*$, recording decisions. | Recovers the membership of the optimal on-time subset. |
| 7 | Schedule on-time jobs in EDD order at time $0$. | Earliest deadlines first. |
| 8 | Schedule tardy jobs (in any order ŌĆö EDD retained by convention) **after** the on-time block. | They are by definition already late; ordering among them affects only $C_j$, not feasibility. |
| 9 | Compute $C_j$, $T_j$, and $\bar{T}$. | Final tardiness reporting. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All symbols use $\leq$, $\geq$, $\vert$, \mid instead of the pipe character to preserve table syntax.**

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning / Usage |
|---|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $T_j = \max\{0,\ C_j - d_j\}$ | Tardiness of job $j$ |
| 2 | $\bar{T} = \dfrac{1}{n}\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} T_j$ | **Mean Tardiness** (KTU Module-2 objective) |
| 3 | $U_j = 1$ if $C_j > d_j$, else $0$ | Lateness indicator for $1 \ \vert \ \vert \ \sum U_j$ |
| 4 | $\sum U_j = n - F^*$ | Number of tardy jobs in optimal schedule |
| 5 | $F(j, T) = \max\{F(j-1, T),\ F(j-1, T-p_j) + 1\}$ | **DP recurrence** (Wilkerson-Irwin core) |
| 6 | $F(0, 0) = 0;\ F(0, T) = -\infty$ for $T > 0$ | **Boundary condition** |
| 7 | $T \leq d_j$ | **Feasibility window** for cell $(j, T)$ |
| 8 | $F^* = \max\limits_{0 \leq T \leq d_n} F(n, T)$ | **Optimal cardinality** of on-time set |
| 9 | $C_j = \sum_{k \in S_{on}} p_k + \sum_{k \in S_t,\ k \prec j} p_k$ | Completion time of $j$ in final schedule |
| 10 | $\text{Complexity} = O(n \cdot d_{\max})$ where $d_{\max} = \max_j d_j$ | Time/space ŌĆö pseudo-polynomial |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Job-shop floor scheduling** in SME manufacturing (one bottleneck machine).
- **Aircraft turnaround sequencing** at a single runway.
- **Order-fulfilment centres** where a picker processes one order at a time with customer-promised delivery slots.
- **Compiler register allocation** problems reducible to 1-machine scheduling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Worked Derivation

## 3.1 Problem Data (Sorted by EDD)

We consider $n = 5$ jobs, already sorted in non-decreasing order of due date $d_j$.

| Job $j$ | Processing Time $p_j$ | Due Date $d_j$ |
|:-------:|:---------------------:|:--------------:|
| 1       | 3                     | 5              |
| 2       | 4                     | 7              |
| 3       | 2                     | 9              |
| 4       | 5                     | 11             |
| 5       | 1                     | 13             |

## 3.2 Initialisation

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(0, 0) &= 0 \\
F(0, T) &= -\infty \quad \text{for all } T > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Stage $j = 1$: $p_1 = 3$, $d_1 = 5$, feasible $T \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(1, 0) &= \max\{F(0, 0),\ F(0, -3) + 1\} = \max\{0,\ -\infty\} = 0 \\
F(1, 1) &= F(0, 1) = -\infty \\
F(1, 2) &= F(0, 2) = -\infty \\
F(1, 3) &= \max\{F(0, 3),\ F(0, 0) + 1\} = \max\{-\infty,\ 1\} = 1 \\
F(1, 4) &= F(0, 4) = -\infty \\
F(1, 5) &= F(0, 5) = -\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* Job 1 can be on time only if it consumes exactly 3 units and is included.

## 3.4 Stage $j = 2$: $p_2 = 4$, $d_2 = 7$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(2, 0) &= \max\{F(1, 0),\ F(1, -4)+1\} = 0 \\
F(2, 3) &= F(1, 3) = 1 \\
F(2, 4) &= \max\{F(1, 4),\ F(1, 0)+1\} = \max\{-\infty,\ 1\} = 1 \\
F(2, 7) &= \max\{F(1, 7),\ F(1, 3)+1\} = \max\{-\infty,\ 1+1\} = 2 \\
F(2, T) &= -\infty \quad \text{for } T \in \{1, 2, 5, 6\}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* Best we can do with jobs $\{1, 2\}$ is **2 on-time jobs** consuming 7 units (schedule both).

## 3.5 Stage $j = 3$: $p_3 = 2$, $d_3 = 9$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(3, 0) &= F(2, 0) = 0 \\
F(3, 2) &= F(2, 0) + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1 \quad \text{(only job 3)} \\
F(3, 3) &= F(2, 3) = 1 \\
F(3, 4) &= F(2, 4) = 1 \\
F(3, 5) &= F(2, 3) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \quad \text{(jobs 1, 3)} \\
F(3, 6) &= F(2, 4) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \quad \text{(jobs 2, 3)} \\
F(3, 7) &= F(2, 7) = 2 \\
F(3, 9) &= F(2, 7) + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \quad \text{(jobs 1, 2, 3)} \\
F(3, T) &= -\infty \quad \text{for } T \in \{1, 8\}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Stage $j = 4$: $p_4 = 5$, $d_4 = 11$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(4, 0) &= F(3, 0) = 0 \\
F(4, 2) &= F(3, 2) = 1 \\
F(4, 3) &= F(3, 3) = 1 \\
F(4, 4) &= F(3, 4) = 1 \\
F(4, 5) &= \max\{F(3, 5),\ F(3, 0) + 1\} = \max\{2, 1\} = 2 \\
F(4, 6) &= F(3, 6) = 2 \\
F(4, 7) &= \max\{F(3, 7),\ F(3, 2) + 1\} = \max\{2, 2\} = 2 \\
F(4, 8) &= F(3, 3) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
F(4, 9) &= \max\{F(3, 9),\ F(3, 4) + 1\} = \max\{3, 2\} = 3 \\
F(4, 10) &= F(3, 5) + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
F(4, 11) &= F(3, 6) + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
F(4, 1) &= -\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.7 Stage $j = 5$: $p_5 = 1$, $d_5 = 13$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(5, 0) &= F(4, 0) = 0 \\
F(5, 1) &= F(4, 0) + 1 = 1 \\
F(5, 2) &= F(4, 2) = 1 \\
F(5, 3) &= \max\{F(4, 3),\ F(4, 2) + 1\} = \max\{1, 2\} = 2 \\
F(5, 4) &= \max\{F(4, 4),\ F(4, 3) + 1\} = \max\{1, 2\} = 2 \\
F(5, 5) &= \max\{F(4, 5),\ F(4, 4) + 1\} = \max\{2, 2\} = 2 \\
F(5, 6) &= \max\{F(4, 6),\ F(4, 5) + 1\} = \max\{2, 3\} = 3 \\
F(5, 7) &= \max\{F(4, 7),\ F(4, 6) + 1\} = \max\{2, 3\} = 3 \\
F(5, 8) &= \max\{F(4, 8),\ F(4, 7) + 1\} = \max\{2, 3\} = 3 \\
F(5, 9) &= \max\{F(4, 9),\ F(4, 8) + 1\} = \max\{3, 3\} = 3 \\
F(5, 10) &= \max\{F(4, 10),\ F(4, 9) + 1\} = \max\{3, 4\} = 4 \\
F(5, 11) &= \max\{F(4, 11),\ F(4, 10) + 1\} = \max\{3, 4\} = 4 \\
F(5, 12) &= F(4, 11) + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \\
F(5, 13) &= \max\{F(4, 13),\ F(4, 12) + 1\} = \max\{-\infty,\ -\infty\} = -\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.8 Optimality and Backtracking

The maximum value attained at $j = 5$ is:
$$F^* \ = \ \max_{0 \leq T \leq 13} F(5, T) \ = \ 4 \quad \text{at } T^* = 10$$

Backtrack from $(5, 10)$:

| Step | Cell | Decision | Next State |
|------|------|----------|------------|
| 1 | $F(5, 10) = F(4, 9) + 1$ | **Include** job 5 | $(4, 9)$ |
| 2 | $F(4, 9) = F(3, 9)$ | **Exclude** job 4 | $(3, 9)$ |
| 3 | $F(3, 9) = F(2, 7) + 1$ | **Include** job 3 | $(2, 7)$ |
| 4 | $F(2, 7) = F(1, 3) + 1$ | **Include** job 2 | $(1, 3)$ |
| 5 | $F(1, 3) = F(0, 0) + 1$ | **Include** job 1 | $(0, 0)$ |

**Result:** On-time set $S_{on} = \{1, 2, 3, 5\}$; Tardy set $S_t = \{4\}$.

## 3.9 Final Schedule and Mean Tardiness Calculation

Schedule on-time jobs in EDD order, then place tardy job after:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 3, & T_1 &= \max\{0,\ 3 - 5\} = 0 \\
C_2 &= 3 + 4 = 7, & T_2 &= \max\{0,\ 7 - 7\} = 0 \\
C_3 &= 7 + 2 = 9, & T_3 &= \max\{0,\ 9 - 9\} = 0 \\
C_5 &= 9 + 1 = 10, & T_5 &= \max\{0,\ 10 - 13\} = 0 \\
C_4 &= 10 + 5 = 15, & T_4 &= \max\{0,\ 15 - 11\} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum T_j &= 0 + 0 + 0 + 4 + 0 = 4 \\
\bar{T} &= \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \ \text{time units} \\
\sum U_j &= 1 \quad (\text{minimum possible})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verifying Optimality:** Any other schedule that puts job 4 on time would need at least 14 cumulative units among the first four jobs ($3+4+2+5$), but $d_4 = 11$ ŌĆö infeasible. Hence **$F^* = 4$ is provably optimal**, and $\bar{T} = 0.8$ is the **minimum achievable mean tardiness**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagram ŌĆö Algorithm Flow & DP Architecture

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Wilkerson-Irwin

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["START - Input n jobs with processing time p sub j and due date d sub j"] --> B0["STEP 1 - Sort jobs in non decreasing EDD order"]
    B0 --> C0["STEP 2 - Initialise DP table F of 0 comma 0 equals 0 and F of 0 comma T equals minus infinity for T greater than 0"]
    C0 --> D0["STEP 3 - Set outer loop variable j equals 1"]
    D0 --> E0["STEP 4 - Set inner loop variable T equals 0"]
    E0 --> F0{"DECISION A - Is T less than or equal to d sub j"}
    F0 -->|"YES"| G0{"DECISION B - Is T greater than or equal to p sub j"}
    G0 -->|"YES"| H0["Compute Option 1 equals F of j minus 1 comma T"]
    H0 --> I0["Compute Option 2 equals F of j minus 1 comma T minus p sub j plus 1"]
    I0 --> J0["STEP 5 - Store F of j comma T equals max of Option 1 and Option 2"]
    G0 -->|"NO"| K0["STEP 6 - Store F of j comma T equals F of j minus 1 comma T"]
    J0 --> L0["STEP 7 - Increment T"]
    K0 --> L0
    L0 --> M0{"DECISION C - Has T reached d sub j"}
    M0 -->|"NO"| E0
    M0 -->|"YES"| N0["STEP 8 - Increment j"]
    N0 --> O0{"DECISION D - Has j reached n"}
    O0 -->|"NO"| D0
    O0 -->|"YES"| P0["STEP 9 - Find optimal T star that maximises F of n comma T"]
    P0 --> Q0["STEP 10 - Backtrack from cell n comma T star to recover on time subset S sub on"]
    Q0 --> R0["STEP 11 - Identify tardy subset S sub t equals complement of S sub on"]
    R0 --> S0["STEP 12 - Schedule S sub on in EDD order starting at time 0"]
    S0 --> T0["STEP 13 - Schedule S sub t in EDD order after completion of S sub on"]
    T0 --> U0["STEP 14 - Compute completion times C sub j and tardiness T sub j"]
    U0 --> V0["STEP 15 - Compute mean tardiness T bar equals 1 over n times sum of T sub j"]
    V0 --> W0["FINISH - Output schedule and minimum mean tardiness"]
```

## 4.2 DP Table State-Space Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Job 1 sub table F of 1 comma T for T equals 0 to 5"] --> B1["Job 2 sub table F of 2 comma T for T equals 0 to 7"]
    B1 --> C1["Job 3 sub table F of 3 comma T for T equals 0 to 9"]
    C1 --> D1["Job 4 sub table F of 4 comma T for T equals 0 to 11"]
    D1 --> E1["Job 5 sub table F of 5 comma T for T equals 0 to 13"]
    E1 --> F1["Extraction of F star equals max F of 5 comma T"]
    F1 --> G1["Backtracking Engine"]
    G1 --> H1["On time Subset S sub on equals 1 comma 2 comma 3 comma 5"]
    G1 --> I1["Tardy Subset S sub t equals 4"]
    H1 --> J1["Schedule Builder EDD Order"]
    I1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1["Output Mean Tardiness equals 0 point 8"]
```

## 4.3 Decision-Branch Pseudocode Logic (No Programming Language Used)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol, no Python/source code is included for non-CS engineering topics. The algorithm logic is conveyed through mathematical pseudocode expressions already embedded in Section 3.

---
