| **Exhaustive Enumeration** | $\displaystyle\binom{n+m-1}{m-1} = \binom{6}{2} = 15$ | $O(m^n)$ ŌĆö grows exponentially |
| **Dynamic Programming** | $O(n \cdot m^2) = 3 \times 16 = 48$ | Polynomial ŌĆö tractable |
| **Speedup Factor** | $\approx 3\times$ here, exponential in general | DP scales, enumeration explodes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö DP Scheduling Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Input: n jobs, m states, costs] --> B{Stage Decomposition}
    B --> C[Stage N]
    B --> D[Stage N-1]
    B --> E[Stage 2]
    B --> F[Stage 1]
    C --> G[Compute f_N from boundary f_{N+1} = 0]
    D --> H[Compute f_{N-1} using f_N table]
    E --> I[Compute f_2 using f_3 table]
    F --> J[Compute f_1 using f_2 table]
    G --> K[Build Value Table V n times m]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Trace-Back via Optimal Decisions x*]
    L --> M[Output: Optimal Policy and Minimum Cost Z*]
    M --> N[Apply to Schedule: Sequence, Lot Sizes, Allocations]
    N --> O[Industrial Scheduler MES / ERP Engine]
```

## 4.2 Recursion Tree (Three-Stage Resource Allocation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S3["Stage 3: lambda = 4"] --> D3a["x3 = 0: 0 + 29 = 29"]
    S3 --> D3b["x3 = 1: 6 + 23 = 29"]
    S3 --> D3c["x3 = 2: 11 + 16 = 27"]
    S3 --> D3d["x3 = 3: 15 + 8 = 23"]
    S3 --> D3e["x3 = 4: 19 + 0 = 19"]
    D3a --> R3a["f2 value 29"]
    D3b --> R3b["f2 value 23"]
    D3c --> R3c["f2 value 16"]
    D3d --> R3d["f2 value 8"]
    D3e --> R3e["f2 value 0"]
    R3a --> OPT["Optimal: x3* = 0"]
    R3b --> ALTB["Alt: x3* = 1"]
    R3a --> S2["Stage 2: remaining lambda = 4"]
    D3b --> S2B["Stage 2: remaining lambda = 3"]
    S2 --> P["Process recursively: f2 lambda=4 -> x2* = 1"]
    S2B --> PB["Process recursively: f2 lambda=3 -> x2* = 1"]
    P --> F["Stage 1: x1* = 3"]
    PB --> FB["Stage 1: x1* = 2"]
    F --> OUT["Z* = 29: Allocate 3 to Line 1, 1 to Line 2"]
    FB --> OUTB["Z = 28: Sub-optimal"]
```

## 4.3 State Transition Graph ŌĆö Generic DP for Single-Machine Scheduling

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    state0[State S0: No Job Done]
    state1[State S1: Job A Done]
    state2[State S2: Job B Done]
    state3[State S3: Job C Done]
    stateF[State SF: All Jobs Done]
    state0 --> state1: Process A, cost dA
    state0 --> state2: Process B, cost dB
    state0 --> state3: Process C, cost dC
    state1 --> stateF: Process remaining
    state2 --> stateF: Process remaining
    state3 --> stateF: Process remaining
    note right of stateF
        Optimal value Z* = min over all paths
        of sum of stage costs
    end note
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each node is a **state** (set of jobs already processed). Each edge is a **decision** (process the next job) with an associated **stage cost** $d_k$. DP finds the **minimum-cost path** from $S_0$ to $S_F$. This is the standard *shortest-path formulation* of single-machine scheduling.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. State Bellman's Principle of Optimality.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *"An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision."*
>
> In simpler terms: **the tail of an optimal sequence is itself optimal for the sub-problem defined by the state reached after the first decision.** This is the *optimal substructure* property on which Dynamic Programming rests. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between forward and backward recursion in Dynamic Programming.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Backward Recursion | Forward Recursion |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting point** | Last stage (n) ŌåÆ First stage (1) | First stage (1) ŌåÆ Last stage (n) |
| **Boundary** | $f_0(s) = 0$ | $f_{N+1}(s) = 0$ |
| **State info needed** | $f_{n-1}$ of *previous* stage | $f_{n+1}$ of *next* stage |
| **Use case in scheduling** | Lot sizing, Johnson's flow shop | Resource allocation with known future |

**(3 marks)** ŌĆö 1 mark for the boundary difference, 1 mark for direction, 1 mark for application context.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting the **boundary condition** ($f_0 = 0$ or $f_{N+1} = 0$). Examiners allot 1ŌĆō2 marks just for this.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Not showing the **state table** explicitly. A blank value table loses 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to do **trace-back** (backtracking). The optimal value alone is incomplete; you must recover the **optimal policy** $(x_1^*, x_2^*, \dots, x_N^*)$.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **forward** vs **backward** recursion ŌĆö examiners test this explicitly.

---

### **Part B ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]`

A project manager must allocate **5 engineers** across **3 tasks** to maximize total productivity. Productivity of each task for $x$ engineers is given below. Find the optimal allocation using **Dynamic Programming (Backward Recursion)**.

| Engineers Allocated ($x$) | Task 1 $g_1(x)$ | Task 2 $g_2(x)$ | Task 3 $g_3(x)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| 2 | 7 | 9 | 11 |
| 3 | 11 | 13 | 15 |
| 4 | 14 | 16 | 18 |
| 5 | 17 | 19 | 21 |

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Formulate as DP and compute $f_1(\lambda_1)$ and $f_2(\lambda_2)$ (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**DP Formulation (2 marks):**

$$Z^{*} = \max_{x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 5} \left[ g_1(x_1) + g_2(x_2) + g_3(x_3) \right]$$

**Recursion (2 marks):**

$$f_k(\lambda_k) = \max_{0 \le x_k \le \lambda_k} \left[ g_k(x_k) + f_{k-1}(\lambda_k - x_k) \right], \quad f_0(\lambda) = 0$$

**Stage 1 Table (1.5 marks):** Since no further stages, $f_1(\lambda_1) = g_1(\lambda_1)$.

| $\lambda_1$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_1(\lambda_1)$ | 0 | 4 | 7 | 11 | 14 | 17 |
| $x_1^{*}$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

**Stage 2 Table (1.5 marks):** $f_2(\lambda_2) = \max_{x_2} \big[ g_2(x_2) + f_1(\lambda_2 - x_2) \big]$.

| $\lambda_2$ | $x_2 = 0$ | $x_2 = 1$ | $x_2 = 2$ | $x_2 = 3$ | $x_2 = 4$ | $x_2 = 5$ | $f_2$ | $x_2^{*}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **0** | 0 |
| 1 | 4 | 5 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **5** | 1 |
| 2 | 7 | 9 | 9 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **9** | 1 or 2 |
| 3 | 11 | 12 | 13 | 13 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **13** | 2 or 3 |
| 4 | 14 | 16 | 17 | 17 | 16 | ŌĆö | **17** | 2 or 3 |
| 5 | 17 | 19 | 21 | 22 | 21 | 19 | **22** | 3 |

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Compute $f_3(5)$ and trace back the optimal policy (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Stage 3 Computation (4 marks):** $f_3(5) = \max_{x_3} \big[ g_3(x_3) + f_2(5 - x_3) \big]$.

$$\begin{aligned}
x_3 = 0 &: \quad 0 + f_2(5) = 0 + 22 = 22 \\
x_3 = 1 &: \quad 6 + f_2(4) = 6 + 17 = 23 \\
x_3 = 2 &: \quad 11 + f_2(3) = 11 + 13 = 24 \\
x_3 = 3 &: \quad 15 + f_2(2) = 15 + 9 = 24 \\
x_3 = 4 &: \quad 18 + f_2(1) = 18 + 5 = 23 \\
x_3 = 5 &: \quad 21 + f_2(0) = 21 + 0 = 21 \\
f_3(5) &= \mathbf{24}, \quad x_3^{*} = 2 \text{ or } 3
\end{aligned}$$

**Trace-Back (2 marks):**

- $f_3(5) = 24$ with $x_3^{*} = 2$ ŌåÆ remaining $= 5 - 2 = 3$.
- $f_2(3) = 13$ with $x_2^{*} = 2$ ŌåÆ remaining $= 3 - 2 = 1$.
- $f_1(1) = 4$ with $x_1^{*} = 1$.

**Optimal Policy (1 mark):**

$$x_1^{*} = 1, \quad x_2^{*} = 2, \quad x_3^{*} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z^{*} = 4 + 9 + 11 = 24$$

**Total Productivity = 24 units (optimal).**

---

### **Part B ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]`

A workshop has **3 jobs** to be processed on **2 machines in the same order (M1 ŌåÆ M2)**. Processing times are given below. Using **Dynamic Programming (equivalent to Johnson's Rule)**, find the sequence that minimizes makespan.

| Job | M1 Time $p_{1j}$ | M2 Time $p_{2j}$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | 2 |
| B | 1 | 6 |
| C | 9 | 7 |

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Set up the DP recursion and the state definition (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**State Definition (2 marks):**
- **Stage $k$** = scheduling decision for the $k$-th job in the sequence.
- **State $s_k = (t_1, t_2)$** = (completion time on M1 so far, completion time on M2 so far) after scheduling $k-1$ jobs.

**Initial state (1 mark):** $s_0 = (0, 0)$.

**Transition (2 marks):**
If job $j$ is scheduled at stage $k$ with current state $(t_1, t_2)$:

$$t_1' = t_1 + p_{1j}, \qquad t_2' = \max(t_1', t_2) + p_{2j}$$

**Objective (1 mark):** Minimize $t_2$ at the terminal state (makespan $C_{\max}$).

**Bellman Recursion (1 mark):**

$$f_k(t_1, t_2) = \min_{j \in \text{unscheduled}} \left[ \max(t_1 + p_{1j}, t_2) + p_{2j} + f_{k+1}(t_1 + p_{1j}, t_2') \right]$$

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Solve using Johnson's Rule (DP-equivalent) and compute the makespan (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Apply Johnson's Rule (4 marks):**

| Job | $p_{1j}$ | $p_{2j}$ | $\min(p_{1j}, p_{2j})$ | Position |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | 2 | **2** (M2) | Last (end) |
| B | 1 | 6 | **1** (M1) | First (start) |
| C | 9 | 7 | **7** (M2) | Middle |

**Johnson's Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Find the job with the smallest processing time across all machines ŌåÆ Job B ($\min = 1$ on M1).
2. Since it is on M1, place B at the **earliest available position** (front).
3. Next smallest: Job A ($\min = 2$ on M2) ŌåÆ place at the **latest position** (end).
4. Remaining: Job C ŌåÆ place in the middle.

**Optimal Sequence:** **B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ A**

**Makespan Computation (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{M1 schedule:} \quad & B: [0,1], \quad C: [1,10], \quad A: [10,15] \\
\text{M2 schedule:} \quad & B: [1,7], \quad C: [10,17], \quad A: [17,19]
\end{aligned}$$

$$C_{\max} = 19 \;\; \text{time units}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- Ō£ģ **DP = Stagewise optimization** with stored sub-problem results.
- Ō£ģ **Bellman's Principle of Optimality** is the *theoretical foundation* ŌĆö must be stated verbatim for full marks.
- Ō£ģ **Recursion types:** Backward (start from last stage, boundary $f_0 = 0$) and Forward (start from first stage, boundary $f_{N+1} = 0$).
- Ō£ģ **State variable** must capture *all* future-relevant information; insufficient states = wrong answer.
- Ō£ģ **Always construct the value table** explicitly; examiners award partial marks per row.
- Ō£ģ **Trace-back is mandatory** ŌĆö without recovering $(x_1^*, x_2^*, \dots)$, you lose 2ŌĆō3 marks.
- Ō£ģ **DP applications in Industrial Scheduling** (KTU 2024 syllabus):
  1. Single-machine weighted completion time sequencing.
  2. Two-machine flow shop (Johnson's rule derivation via DP).
  3. Resource allocation across production lines / projects.
  4. **Wagner-Whitin dynamic lot-sizing model** for production planning.
  5. Aggregate production planning with capacity constraints.
  6. Equipment replacement / maintenance scheduling.
- Ō£ģ **Computational advantage:** DP converts $O(n!)$ enumeration into $O(n \cdot m^2)$ for resource allocation and $O(2^n)$ ŌåÆ $O(n \cdot m)$ for knapsack-type problems.
- Ō£ģ **Common KTU question patterns:**
  - *"Formulate the problem using DP and solve."* ŌåÆ State recursion + boundary, build table, trace back.
  - *"Justify the use of DP over brute force."* ŌåÆ Complexity argument + optimal substructure.
  - *"Compare forward and backward DP."* ŌåÆ Table of differences.
- Ō£ģ **Pitfall to avoid:** Do not confuse DP with *Divide-and-Conquer* ŌĆö DP solves *overlapping* sub-problems, D&C solves *independent* sub-problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Branch & Bound Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Branch & Bound Approach # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Branch and Bound (B&B)** is an exact, enumerative, tree-search algorithm paradigm used to solve discrete and combinatorial optimization problems. In industrial scheduling, it systematically explores the solution space of all possible job sequences (or schedules) by *branching* the search space into smaller sub-problems, computing a *bound* (lower bound for minimization) on the best solution achievable in each sub-problem, and *fathoming* (pruning) any sub-problem whose bound is provably worse than the current best-known feasible solution (the **incumbent**). In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, B&B is the **gold-standard exact method** for the **n-job, m-machine scheduling problem** (e.g., flow shop, job shop) when the search space of $n!$ (or $\binom{n}{k} \cdot k!$ in the case of permutation flow shop) permutations becomes computationally infeasible for direct enumeration. ## 1.2 The Three Sacred Pillars of B&B A Branch and Bound algorithm for scheduling rests on **three pillars** without which it collapses into brute-force enumeration: 1. **Branching** ŌĆō The decision rule that splits a parent node (partial schedule) into a finite set of child nodes (extended schedules) by appending one additional job/decision. 2. **Bounding** ŌĆō The computation of a **lower bound** $LB(S)$ on the optimal objective value of any complete schedule in subtree $S$. The bound must be *admissible* (never over-estimates) and *efficient* (cheap to compute). 3. **Fathoming** ŌĆō The elimination rule that declares a node *dead*. A node is fathomed if **(a)** it is infeasible, **(b)** its lower bound $\geq$ incumbent value, or **(c)** it represents a complete schedule. > [!NOTE] > **Incumbent ($\bar{z}$):** The best (smallest for minimization) feasible objective value discovered so far during the search. Initially $\bar{z} = \infty$ (or estimated via a heuristic like **Johnson's rule** or **SPT**). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "Finding the Best Restaurant in a New City" > [!TIP] > **Analogy ŌĆö *Hunting the best biryani joint in Kochi*:** Imagine you are visiting Kochi and want the cheapest yet tastiest biryani. You cannot try every restaurant (brute force is impractical). Instead: > - **Branch**: Divide the city into 5 zones (branching into sub-problems). > - **Bound**: For each zone, look up the *minimum price* of any restaurant there. This is a *lower bound* on what you will pay in that zone. > - **Fathom**: If Zone A's minimum price is already Ōé╣300, but you have already eaten a Ōé╣250 biryani in Zone C, then Zone A is *fathomed* (pruned) ŌĆö you will never go there. > - **Incumbent**: The current best biryani you have actually eaten (Ōé╣250 in Zone C). > - **Search strategy**: Pick the next zone to explore (BFS by zone, DFS by street, or *best-first* by lowest bound first). > > This is *exactly* how B&B prunes the search tree of $n!$ schedules for an $n$-job problem. ## 1.4 Why B&B Matters in Industrial Scheduling For a **4-job, 2-machine flow shop**, the brute-force permutation count is $4! = 24$ schedules. For a **10-job, 3-machine job shop**, the count explodes to roughly $(10!)^2 \approx 1.3 \times 10^{13}$. B&B routinely prunes **80ŌĆō99 %** of these nodes, making exact solutions tractable for problems with $n \leq 15$ jobs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The student must be able to (i) formulate a B&B tree for an $n$-job permutation flow shop, (ii) compute the lower bound at every node, and (iii) demonstrate the fathoming sequence step by step. This is a high-weightage topic for Part-B (14-mark) questions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The B&B Algorithm ŌĆö Operational Flow The generic B&B algorithm for an $n$-job minimization scheduling problem proceeds as follows: 1. **Initialization**: - Build the root node (empty partial schedule, $k = 0$). - Compute its lower bound $LB_0$. - Obtain an initial incumbent $\bar{z}$ using a fast heuristic (e.g., **Johnson's rule**, **SPT**, **NEH**). - Initialize a candidate list (LIFO stack for DFS, FIFO queue for BFS, or priority queue for best-first). 2. **Selection**: Pop a node $N$ from the candidate list according to the search strategy. 3. **Branching**: From node $N$ (with $k$ jobs fixed and $(n-k)$ remaining), generate child nodes by appending each *unassigned* job at position $(k+1)$. 4. **Bounding**: For each child, compute $LB(N_{\text{child}})$. If $LB(N_{\text{child}}) \geq \bar{z}$, **fathom** (prune) the child. 5. **Fathom Checks**: A node is fathomed if **any** of the following holds: - **Bound failure**: $LB(N) \geq \bar{z}$. - **Infeasibility**: The partial schedule violates a constraint. - **Integrality / Completeness**: Node represents a full schedule ŌĆö update incumbent if better. 6. **Termination**: The candidate list is empty. Return $\bar{z}$ and the corresponding schedule. ## 2.2 Lower Bound Techniques (The Heart of B&B) The *quality* of a B&B algorithm is judged almost entirely by the strength of its **lower bound function**. A weak bound = little pruning = slow algorithm. The standard bounds for the **2-machine permutation flow shop** (Johnson's environment) are: ### 2.2.1 Bound A ŌĆö "Trivial Tail" Bound $$LB_A(N) = c_2(N) + \sum_{j \in R} p_{2j}$$ where $c_2(N)$ is the completion time at Machine 2 of the last job in the partial schedule, and $R$ is the set of remaining unscheduled jobs. **Why it works**: $M_2$ still has $\sum_{j \in R} p_{2j}$ units of work, which cannot start before $c_2(N)$. ### 2.2.2 Bound B ŌĆö "Idle Time on M1" Bound $$LB_B(N) = c_1(N) + \sum_{j \in R} p_{1j} + \max_{j \in R} \left( p_{2j} \right)$$ where $c_1(N)$ is the completion time at Machine 1. **Why it works**: Even if $M_1$ processes all remaining jobs back-to-back, the *last* $M_2$ operation must wait for the final $M_1$ operation to finish (worst case = the job with the largest $p_{2j}$). This bound captures the unavoidable idle time on $M_2$. ### 2.2.3 The Composite Lower Bound (Used in Practice) $$\boxed{LB(N) = \max\!\left( LB_A(N),\; LB_B(N) \right)}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table collects **every formula** a KTU student needs to write a B&B solution on the exam paper. | Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Search space size (permutation)** | $n!$ | Number of candidate schedules for $n$ jobs on $m$ machines (permutation flow shop). | | **Root lower bound** | $LB_0 = \max\!\bigl( \sum_j p_{2j},\; \sum_j p_{1j} + \max_j p_{2j} \bigr)$ | Trivial lower bound on the optimal makespan. | | **Trivial tail bound** $LB_A$ | $c_2(N) + \displaystyle\sum_{j \in R} p_{2j}$ | Lower bound accounting for residual $M_2$ workload. | | **Idle-time bound** $LB_B$ | $c_1(N) + \displaystyle\sum_{j \in R} p_{1j} + \max_{j \in R} p_{2j}$ | Lower bound accounting for unavoidable $M_2$ idle time. | | **Composite bound** | $LB(N) = \max(LB_A, LB_B)$ | Final lower bound used for fathoming. | | **Fathoming rule** | $LB(N) \geq \bar{z} \Rightarrow \text{prune}$ | Discard a node when its bound cannot beat the incumbent. | | **Completion time recursion (1-machine)** | $C_k = C_{k-1} + p_k,\;\; C_0 = 0$ | Standard flow-time recurrence for single-machine problems. | | **Makespan of 2-machine flow shop** | $C_{\max} = \max\!\bigl( \sum_j p_{1j},\; \sum_j p_{2j} \bigr) + \text{idle on } M_2$ | The makespan is bounded below by the larger of total $M_1$ and total $M_2$ work. | | **Best-case idle on $M_2$** | $\max\!\bigl(0,\; p_{1,\text{first}} - p_{2,\text{last}}\bigr)$ | For Johnson's rule, the minimum achievable idle on $M_2$ between jobs. | | **Johnson's heuristic makespan** | Solve by $O(n \log n)$ partition + simulate | Used to seed the incumbent $\bar{z}$ before B&B. | > [!WARNING] > **Do NOT confuse $LB_A$ with $LB_B$ in the exam!** $LB_A$ depends on $c_2$ (current $M_2$ state); $LB_B$ depends on $c_1$ (current $M_1$ state) and the *maximum* $M_2$ time of any *remaining* job. Many students mistakenly sum all $M_2$ times twice or use $c_1$ inside $LB_A$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility B&B is the **computational engine** behind: - **Job-shop schedulers** in CNC flexible manufacturing systems (FMS). - **Aircraft gate-assignment** problems at airports. - **Crew rostering** in airlines and railways. - **VLSI circuit partitioning** and **PCB drilling sequence** optimization. - **Container-terminal crane scheduling** in ports (Cochin Port, Singapore PSA). In all of these, the bound is engineered to exploit the *physical structure* of the problem (e.g., machine availability, precedence, due dates) so that 90 %+ of the search tree is discarded. # 3. Step-by-Step Worked Example & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Problem Statement (A KTU-style Exam Question) > **Solve the following 3-job, 2-machine permutation flow shop scheduling problem using the Branch and Bound method to minimize the makespan $C_{\max}$. Processing times are given in hours.** > > | Job | Machine 1 ($p_{1j}$) | Machine 2 ($p_{2j}$) | > | :---: | :---: | :---: | > | $J_1$ | **2** | **3** | > | $J_2$ | **4** | **1** | > | $J_3$ | **6** | **5** | ## 3.2 Step 1 ŌĆö Brute-Force Enumeration (For Verification) There are $3! = 6$ permutations. The makespan of each is computed by forward simulation: | Sequence | $M_1$ end times | $M_2$ end times | $C_{\max}$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $J_1 \to J_2 \to J_3$ | 2, 6, 12 | 5, 7, 17 | **17** | | $J_1 \to J_3 \to J_2$ | 2, 8, 12 | 5, 13, 14 | **14** Ō£ō | | $J_2 \to J_1 \to J_3$ | 4, 6, 12 | 5, 9, 17 | **17** | | $J_2 \to J_3 \to J_1$ | 4, 10, 12 | 5, 15, 18 | **18** | | $J_3 \to J_1 \to J_2$ | 6, 8, 12 | 11, 14, 15 | **15** | | $J_3 \to J_2 \to J_1$ | 6, 10, 12 | 11, 12, 15 | **15** | **Optimal by enumeration**: $J_1 \to J_3 \to J_2$ with $C_{\max} = 14$ hours. ## 3.3 Step 2 ŌĆö Seed the Incumbent with Johnson's Heuristic Apply **Johnson's 2-machine rule**: - $J_1: p_{11} = 2 \leq p_{21} = 3 \Rightarrow$ Schedule in **Front Set**. - $J_2: p_{12} = 4 > p_{22} = 1 \Rightarrow$ Schedule in **Back Set**. - $J_3: p_{13} = 6 > p_{23} = 5 \Rightarrow$ Schedule in **Back Set**. Front set sorted ascending by $p_1$: $J_1$. Back set sorted descending by $p_1$: $J_3, J_2$. $$\text{Johnson's sequence} = \langle J_1,\; J_3,\; J_2 \rangle \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \bar{z} = 14$$ ## 3.4 Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Root Node Lower Bound At the root: $c_1 = 0,\; c_2 = 0,\; R = \{J_1, J_2, J_3\}$. $$LB_A(\text{root}) = c_2 + \sum_{j \in R} p_{2j} = 0 + (3+1+5) = 9$$ $$LB_B(\text{root}) = c_1 + \sum_{j \in R} p_{1j} + \max_{j \in R} p_{2j} = 0 + 12 + 5 = 17$$ $$LB(\text{root}) = \max(9,\, 17) = 17$$ Since $17 \geq 14 = \bar{z}$ ŌĆö the root **could** still hide a better solution (the root bound is loose), so we must branch. ## 3.5 Step 4 ŌĆö Branch at Level 1 (Choose First Job) Three children are generated: $\{J_1\},\,\{J_2\},\,\{J_3\}$ as the first job. ### Node A ŌĆö First job = $J_1$ Compute state after $J_1$: $c_1 = 2,\; c_2 = 2 + 3 = 5,\; R = \{J_2, J_3\}$. $$LB_A(A) = 5 + (1+5) = 11$$ $$LB_B(A) = 2 + (4+6) + \max(1, 5) = 2 + 10 + 5 = 17$$ $$LB(A) = \max(11, 17) = 17 \geq \bar{z} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Keep (do not prune)}$$ ### Node B ŌĆö First job = $J_2$ $c_1 = 4,\; c_2 = 4 + 1 = 5,\; R = \{J_1, J_3\}$. $$LB_A(B) = 5 + (3+5) = 13$$ $$LB_B(B) = 4 + (2+6) + \max(3, 5) = 4 + 8 + 5 = 17$$ $$LB(B) = 17 \geq \bar{z} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Keep}$$ ### Node C ŌĆö First job = $J_3$ $c_1 = 6,\; c_2 = 6 + 5 = 11,\; R = \{J_1, J_2\}$. $$LB_A(C) = 11 + (3+1) = 15$$ $$LB_B(C) = 6 + (2+4) + \max(3, 1) = 6 + 6 + 3 = 15$$ $$LB(C) = 15 \geq \bar{z} = 14 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Keep}$$ **None of the Level-1 nodes are pruned.** The best-first strategy would next expand Node A (smallest bound = 17, but tied with B). We choose Node A (DFS order). ## 3.6 Step 5 ŌĆö Branch at Level 2 from Node A (Sequence starts with $J_1$) ### Node A1 ŌĆö Sequence $J_1 \to J_2$ $c_1 = \max(2, 4) = 6$ ... wait, recompute correctly. After $J_1$ and $J_2$ on $M_1$: $c_1 = 2 + 4 = 6$. On $M_2$: $J_2$ starts at $\max(c_1,\, c_2) = \max(6, 5) = 6$, so $c_2 = 6 + 1 = 7$. $R = \{J_3\}$. $$LB_A(A_1) = 7 + 5 = 12$$ $$LB_B(A_1) = 6 + 6 + 5 = 17$$ $$LB(A_1) = 17 \geq \bar{z} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Keep}$$ ### Node A2 ŌĆö Sequence $J_1 \to J_3$ After $J_1, J_3$ on $M_1$: $c_1 = 2 + 6 = 8$. On $M_2$: $J_3$ starts at $\max(8, 5) = 8$, so $c_2 = 8 + 5 = 13$. $R = \{J_2\}$. $$LB_A(A_2) = 13 + 1 = 14$$ $$LB_B(A_2) = 8 + 4 + 1 = 13$$ $$LB(A_2) = \max(14, 13) = 14 \geq \bar{z} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Keep (just barely!)}$$ ## 3.7 Step 6 ŌĆö Branch at Level 3 (Leaves) ### Leaf A1.x ŌĆö $J_1 \to J_2 \to J_3$ $M_1$ ends: 2, 6, 12. $M_2$ ends: 5, 7, 17. $C_{\max} = 17 > \bar{z} = 14$. **Discard.** ### Leaf A2.x ŌĆö $J_1 \to J_3 \to J_2$ $M_1$ ends: 2, 8, 12. $M_2$ ends: 5, 13, 14. $C_{\max} = 14 = \bar{z}$. **Tie ŌĆö update not strictly better.** ## 3.8 Step 7 ŌĆö Continue from Node B and Node C Following DFS, expand Node B ŌåÆ Node B1 ($J_2 \to J_1$) and B2 ($J_2 \to J_3$): - **B1 = $J_2 \to J_1$**: $c_1=6, c_2 = \max(6,5) + 3 = 9$. $LB = \max(9+5,\; 6+2+5) = \max(14, 13) = 14 \geq 14 \Rightarrow$ Keep. - **B2 = $J_2 \to J_3$**: $c_1=10, c_2 = \max(10,5)+5 = 15$. $LB = \max(15+3,\; 10+2+3) = \max(18, 15) = 18 \geq 14 \Rightarrow$ Keep but unproductive. Leaves: $J_2 \to J_1 \to J_3 \Rightarrow C_{\max} = 17$ (discard); $J_2 \to J_3 \to J_1 \Rightarrow C_{\max} = 18$ (discard). Similarly for Node C ŌåÆ $J_3 \to J_1$ ($LB=15$ keep) and $J_3 \to J_2$ ($LB=14$ keep). Their leaves give $C_{\max} = 15$ each (discard). ## 3.9 Final Summary of B&B Trace > [!IMPORTANT] > **Optimal Solution**: Sequence $J_1 \to J_3 \to J_2$ with $C_{\max} = 14$ hours. > > **Pruning Statistics**: $0/6$ leaves were discarded by bound (this small problem is too tight), but the B&B tree *systematically organized* the search and would yield massive savings for $n \geq 6$. ## 3.10 Analytical Decision Matrix | Node | Sequence built | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | $R$ | $LB_A$ | $LB_B$ | $LB$ | Action | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Root | $\emptyset$ | 0 | 0 | $\{1,2,3\}$ | 9 | 17 | **17** | Branch | | A | $\{1\}$ | 2 | 5 | $\{2,3\}$ | 11 | 17 | **17** | Branch | | B | $\{2\}$ | 4 | 5 | $\{1,3\}$ | 13 | 17 | **17** | Branch | | C | $\{3\}$ | 6 | 11 | $\{1,2\}$ | 15 | 15 | **15** | Branch | | A1 | $\{1,2\}$ | 6 | 7 | $\{3\}$ | 12 | 17 | **17** | Branch | | A2 | $\{1,3\}$ | 8 | 13 | $\{2\}$ | 14 | 13 | **14** | Branch | | B1 | $\{2,1\}$ | 6 | 9 | $\{3\}$ | 14 | 13 | **14** | Branch | | B2 | $\{2,3\}$ | 10 | 15 | $\{1\}$ | 18 | 15 | **18** | Branch | | C1 | $\{3,1\}$ | 8 | 14 | $\{2\}$ | 15 | 13 | **15** | Branch | | C2 | $\{3,2\}$ | 12 | 12 | $\{1\}$ | 15 | 15 | **15** | Branch | | Leaf A1.x | $\{1,2,3\}$ | 12 | 17 | $\emptyset$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **17** | Update? No | | Leaf A2.x | $\{1,3,2\}$ | 12 | 14 | $\emptyset$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **14** | **Optimal Ō£ō** | | Leaf B1.x | $\{2,1,3\}$ | 12 | 17 | $\emptyset$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **17** | Discard | | Leaf B2.x | $\{2,3,1\}$ | 12 | 18 | $\emptyset$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **18** | Discard | | Leaf C1.x | $\{3,1,2\}$ | 12 | 15 | $\emptyset$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **15** | Discard | | Leaf C2.x | $\{3,2,1\}$ | 12 | 15 | $\emptyset$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **15** | Discard | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Flowchart of the Branch & Bound Algorithm The diagram below illustrates the high-level control flow of a B&B solver for industrial scheduling. ```mermaid flowchart TD A([START B and B Algorithm]) --> B[Initialize root node
c1 = 0, c2 = 0, R = all jobs] B --> C[Compute LB at root
LB = max LA, LB] C --> D[Seed incumbent z-bar
using Johnson or NEH heuristic] D --> E[Push root onto candidate list
LIFO or Priority queue] E --> F{Is candidate list empty?} F -- YES --> G([Return incumbent z-bar and best schedule]) F -- NO --> H[Select node N from list
BFS / DFS / Best-First] H --> I{Is N a complete schedule?} I -- YES --> J{Cmax N less than z-bar?} J -- YES --> K[Update incumbent z-bar = Cmax N
Store schedule] J -- NO --> L[Discard N] K --> F L --> F I -- NO --> M{Is LB N greater or equal z-bar?} M -- YES --> N[Fathom N by bound failure] M -- NO --> O[Generate child nodes
one for each unassigned job] O --> P[Compute c1, c2 and LB for each child] P --> Q[Push non-fathomed children onto candidate list] N --> F Q --> F ``` ## 4.2 B&B Search Tree for the 3-Job Example (Section 3) The next diagram is the *exhaustive tree* showing all 6 leaves, the 3 internal nodes at Level 1, and the lower bounds computed in Section 3.10. The leaf $J_1 \to J_3 \to J_2$ is highlighted as the **optimal incumbent**. ```mermaid flowchart TD R0([ROOT
LB = 17]) R0 --> A1[Node A: J1
c1=2, c2=5
LB=17] R0 --> A2[Node B: J2
c1=4, c2=5
LB=17] R0 --> A3[Node C: J3
c1=6, c2=11
LB=15] A1 --> B1[1,2: LB=17] A1 --> B2[1,3: LB=14] A2 --> C1[2,1: LB=14] A2 --> C2[2,3: LB=18] A3 --> D1[3,1: LB=15] A3 --> D2[3,2: LB=15] B1 --> L1((1,2,3
Cmax 17)) B2 --> L2(((1,3,2
Cmax 14 OPTIMAL))) C1 --> L3((2,1,3
Cmax 17)) C2 --> L4((2,3,1
Cmax 18)) D1 --> L5((3,1,2
Cmax 15)) D2 --> L6((3,2,1
Cmax 15)) ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a B&B Solver ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Module] I1[Job Data: p1j, p2j, dj] I2[Machine Data: m machines] end subgraph HEUR[Heuristic Engine] H1[Johnson Rule for 2 machine] H2[NEH for m machine] end subgraph CORE[B and B Core Engine] B1[Branching Controller] B2[Lower Bound Calculator] B3[Fathoming Logic] B4[Search Strategy Selector] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Module] O1[Optimal Sequence] O2[Optimal Makespan] O3[Number of Nodes Explored] O4[CPU Time and Pruning Rate] end INPUT --> HEUR HEUR --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> OUTPUT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Tip for the Exam:** When asked to draw the B&B tree in the answer sheet, the safest convention is to label every node with the triplet `(c1, c2, LB)` and mark pruned nodes with an `X`. The examiner awards 2 marks specifically for the **bound calculations** at every node. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the Branch and Bound technique. List the three fundamental operations used in B&B for industrial scheduling.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Branch and Bound (B&B)** is an enumerative algorithm that finds the *exact* optimal solution to a combinatorial optimization problem by *intelligently* discarding large portions of the search space. > > The three fundamental operations are: > 1. **Branching** ŌĆō Decomposing a problem (parent node) into a finite set of mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive sub-problems (child nodes), typically by fixing one decision variable (e.g., placing one job at the next position). > 2. **Bounding** ŌĆō Computing a *lower bound* on the best objective value achievable within a sub-problem, used to estimate its potential. > 3. **Fathoming** ŌĆō Eliminating a sub-problem from further consideration when (a) its bound is worse than the current incumbent, (b) it is infeasible, or (c) it is a complete feasible solution. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is an incumbent in a Branch and Bound algorithm? Why is it important to obtain a strong incumbent early?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **incumbent** $\bar{z}$ is the *best (minimum) objective value* among all complete feasible schedules discovered so far in the B&B search. It serves as the **dynamic benchmark** against which all open nodes' lower bounds are compared. A strong incumbent obtained *early* (e.g., via Johnson's rule, Palmer's slope index, or NEH heuristic) is critical because it raises the threshold for fathoming, thereby **pruning more nodes** and accelerating convergence. A weak incumbent forces the algorithm to explore deeper before any pruning becomes possible. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question 1 (A) ŌĆö 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Explain in detail the lower bound calculation methods used in Branch and Bound for the 2-machine permutation flow shop scheduling problem. Derive the two bounds. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** For a 2-machine flow shop with $n$ jobs, each partial schedule $N$ has a known completion time at $M_1$ (denoted $c_1(N)$) and at $M_2$ (denoted $c_2(N)$), and a set $R$ of unscheduled remaining jobs. **Bound A ŌĆö Trivial Tail Bound:** The work that *must* be done on $M_2$ consists of the processing times of all remaining jobs: $$LB_A(N) = c_2(N) + \sum_{j \in R} p_{2j}$$ **Derivation:** $M_2$ cannot finish earlier than its current state $c_2(N)$ plus the sum of all $M_2$ operations still to be performed. This is a *workload* bound, valid for any permutation flow shop. **Bound B ŌĆö Idle Time on M2:** Even under the most optimistic scenario in which $M_1$ processes all remaining jobs back-to-back, the *last* $M_2$ operation must wait for the corresponding $M_1$ operation. The worst case (longest $M_2$ operation going last) yields: $$LB_B(N) = c_1(N) + \sum_{j \in R} p_{1j} + \max_{j \in R} p_{2j}$$ **Derivation:** The term $c_1(N) + \sum_{j \in R} p_{1j}$ is the earliest possible time at which the *last* job can start on $M_1$. Adding the *longest* remaining $M_2$ processing time gives the earliest time at which the *last* job can finish on $M_2$. **Composite Bound:** $LB(N) = \max(LB_A, LB_B)$. **(b) Solve the following 4-job, 2-machine flow shop problem using Branch and Bound (with Johnson's rule to seed the incumbent). Minimize makespan. (7 marks)** | Job | $p_{1j}$ | $p_{2j}$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $J_1$ | **3** | **4** | | $J_2$ | **2** | **6** | | $J_3$ | **5** | **1** | | $J_4$ | **1** | **3** | **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Johnson's Heuristic** - $J_1: 3 \leq 4$ ŌćÆ Front set - $J_2: 2 \leq 6$ ŌćÆ Front set - $J_3: 5 > 1$ ŌćÆ Back set - $J_4: 1 \leq 3$ ŌćÆ Front set Front set sorted ascending by $p_1$: $J_4(1), J_2(2), J_1(3)$. Back set sorted descending by $p_1$: $J_3(5)$. Johnson's sequence: $\langle J_4, J_2, J_1, J_3 \rangle$. Computing makespan: - $M_1$ ends: 1, 3, 6, 11 - $M_2$ ends: 4, 10, 14, 15 Incumbent $\bar{z} = 15$, schedule: $J_4 \to J_2 \to J_1 \to J_3$. **Step 2: Root Node** $c_1 = 0, c_2 = 0, R = \{1,2,3,4\}$. $LB_A = 4+6+1+3 = 14$. $LB_B = 0 + 11 + \max(4,6,1,3) = 11 + 6 = 17$. $LB(\text{root}) = 17 \geq 15$ ŌćÆ **Branch**. **Step 3: Level 1 nodes (only those with $LB < 15$ are kept)** - $J_1$ first: $c_1=3, c_2=7, R=\{2,3,4\}$. $LB_A = 7+10 = 17, LB_B = 3+8+6 = 17$. $LB = 17 \geq 15$ ŌćÆ **Keep, branch.** - $J_2$ first: $c_1=2, c_2=8, R=\{1,3,4\}$. $LB_A = 8+8 = 16, LB_B = 2+9+4 = 15$. $LB = 16 \geq 15$ ŌćÆ **Keep, branch.** - $J_3$ first: $c_1=5, c_2=6, R=\{1,2,4\}$. $LB_A = 6+13 = 19, LB_B = 5+6+6 = 17$. $LB = 19 \geq 15$ ŌćÆ **Keep, branch.** - $J_4$ first: $c_1=1, c_2=4, R=\{1,2,3\}$. $LB_A = 4+11 = 15, LB_B = 1+10+6 = 17$. $LB = 17 \geq 15$ ŌćÆ **Keep, branch.** **Step 4: Continue expanding. The first leaf that equals or beats $\bar{z} = 15$ is $J_4 \to J_2 \to J_1 \to J_3$ itself (already in incumbent). Continuing DFS, the next candidate is $J_4 \to J_2 \to J_3 \to J_1$:** - $M_1$ ends: 1, 3, 8, 11. $M_2$ ends: 4, 10, 11, 15. $C_{\max} = 15$ ŌćÆ Tie. **Final Optimal**: $C_{\max} = 15$ with sequence $J_4 \to J_2 \to J_1 \to J_3$ (Johnson's rule happens to be optimal here). --- ### Question 1 (B) ŌĆö 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Internal Choice)* **(a) Compare Branch and Bound with Dynamic Programming for solving the n-job, 2-machine flow shop scheduling problem. Discuss computational complexity. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Criterion | Branch and Bound | Dynamic Programming | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Search strategy** | Implicit enumeration; explores only promising sub-problems. | Recursive decomposition into overlapping sub-problems; stores and reuses sub-solutions. | | **Optimality guarantee** | Yes (exact method) | Yes (exact method, when state space is finite) | | **Bounding** | Computes lower/upper bounds for pruning. | Computes optimal sub-solution value (Bellman equation). | | **Memory** | Stores only the open node list (stack/queue/priority queue). | Stores entire DP table of size $O(f(n,m))$ ŌĆö exponential in $n$ and $m$ for job shop. | | **Time complexity** | Worst case $O(n!)$; average $O(n! \cdot p)$ with pruning fraction $p \ll 1$. | Worst case exponential in $n$; recurrence $f(j) = \min_k \{f(k) + g(j,k)\}$. | | **Best use case** | Permutation flow shop with strong heuristic bounds. | Problems with small state space (e.g., 2-machine flow shop, $O(n \cdot 2^n)$). | | **Limitation** | Bound weakness leads to near-brute-force behavior. | "Curse of dimensionality" for $m \geq 3$ machines or job shop. | **Conclusion:** For 2-machine flow shop, both are viable; DP is elegant but B&B scales better with strong bounds. For 3+ machines or job shop, B&B with dominance rules is the preferred exact method. **(b) Discuss three search strategies used in B&B: Breadth-First (BFS), Depth-First (DFS), and Best-First. Which is preferred in scheduling and why? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** ŌĆö Uses a FIFO queue. All nodes at depth $k$ are explored before any node at depth $k+1$. *Advantage:* Finds a complete solution quickly to seed incumbent. *Disadvantage:* High memory usage $O(b^d)$ where $b$ is branching factor. 2. **Depth-First Search (DFS)** ŌĆö Uses a LIFO stack. Goes as deep as possible before backtracking. *Advantage:* Low memory $O(b \cdot d)$. *Disadvantage:* May wander into poor branches for a long time before finding a good incumbent. 3. **Best-First Search** ŌĆö Uses a priority queue ordered by $LB$ (ascending). Always expands the most promising node. *Advantage:* Maximally exploits bounds; usually converges fastest to optimum. *Disadvantage:* Requires recomputation on every insertion; slightly more memory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Preferred Strategy:** **Best-First** is theoretically preferred for industrial scheduling because it is *bound-driven* ŌĆö it expands the node most likely to harbor the optimum. In practice, a **hybrid DFS-with-best-bound-initialization** is common: use DFS for memory efficiency, but seed with a good incumbent (Johnson/NEH) so that the bound threshold is high from the start. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the $c_1$ update rule** when a job completes on $M_1$ *before* the previous job finished on $M_2$ (use $c_1 = \text{prev } c_1 + p_{1j}$, NOT $c_1 = c_2$). > 2. **Mixing up $LB_A$ and $LB_B$** ŌĆö $LB_A$ uses $c_2$ and the *sum* of remaining $p_2$; $LB_B$ uses $c_1$ and the *max* of remaining $p_2$. > 3. **Not seeding the incumbent** before starting B&B. Without a good $\bar{z}$, the bound threshold is too low and almost nothing is pruned. Examiners deduct 2 marks. > 4. **Forgetting to mark pruned nodes** with an `X` on the tree diagram. > 5. **Not specifying the search strategy** used (DFS, BFS, or best-first) ŌĆö examiners expect this declaration on the first line of the solution. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for B&B in Industrial Scheduling (KTU Module 2)** - ŌśÉ B&B = **exact method** combining *branching + bounding + fathoming* to enumerate $n!$ permutations smartly. - ŌśÉ The three pillars: **Branch** (split), **Bound** (estimate), **Fathom** (kill). - ŌśÉ **Fathoming triggers**: bound $\geq$ incumbent, infeasibility, or completeness. - ŌśÉ **Incumbent** $\bar{z}$: best feasible solution found so far; **seed it with Johnson's rule** or another polynomial heuristic. - ŌśÉ **Bound A** (workload): $LB_A = c_2 + \sum_{j \in R} p_{2j}$. - ŌśÉ **Bound B** (idle time): $LB_B = c_1 + \sum_{j \in R} p_{1j} + \max_{j \in R} p_{2j}$. - ŌśÉ **Composite bound** $LB = \max(LB_A, LB_B)$ ŌĆö admissible (never over-estimates). - ŌśÉ **Search strategies**: DFS (low memory), BFS (good incumbent), Best-First (fastest convergence). - ŌśÉ **Johnson's rule** partitions jobs into Front ($p_{1j} \leq p_{2j}$) and Back ($p_{1j} > p_{2j}$) sets. - ŌśÉ **Computational saving**: B&B routinely prunes 80ŌĆō99 % of nodes for $n \geq 6$. - ŌśÉ **B&B vs DP**: B&B is *search-driven*; DP is *recurrence-driven* and explodes for $m \geq 3$. - ŌśÉ **Draw the tree with bounds** at every node; mark pruned branches with `X`; write the final incumbent schedule at the bottom of the answer. - ŌśÉ Remember: for 2-machine flow shop, **total processing on $M_1$** $= \sum p_{1j}$ and on $M_2$ $= \sum p_{2j}$ ŌĆö these form a trivial **lower bound on $C_{\max}$**: $C_{\max} \geq \max(\sum p_{1j}, \sum p_{2j})$. - ŌśÉ For the **n-job, m-machine job shop**, B&B with **Giffler-Thompson** active schedule generation is the standard KTU-level approach. - ŌśÉ A weak bound = a slow B&B; a strong bound = a fast B&B. **The bound is the algorithm.**

topics.title: Parallel Identical Processors and Independent Jobs with Preemptions and Without Preemptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Parallel Identical Processors and Independent Jobs with Preemptions and Without Preemptions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (Graham's Notation) In the standard three-field scheduling notation $\alpha \vert \beta \vert \gamma$ introduced by **Graham, Lawler, Lenstra, and Rinnooy Kan (1979)**, the parallel identical processor environment with independent jobs is denoted as: $$P \parallel C_{\max} \quad \text{(without preemption)}$$ $$P \vert pmtn \vert C_{\max} \quad \text{(with preemption)}$$ where the symbols carry precise meaning: | Symbol | Meaning | |:------:|:--------| | $P$ | **P**arallel identical machines (same processing speed) | | $\parallel$ | Jobs are **independent** (no precedence constraints) | | $pmtn$ | **Preemption** is allowed (interruption & resumption of jobs) | | $C_{\max}$ | **Makespan** (the completion time of the last job) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Identical machines** means that if a job $j$ requires $p_j$ time units on one machine, it requires exactly $p_j$ time units on **every** machine. The machines are functionally indistinguishable. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are the **manager of a garment factory** with 4 identical embroidery machines ($m = 4$). A pile of **20 independent orders** arrives on your desk. Each order needs a certain amount of machine time ($p_j$). There are **no dependencies** ŌĆö order B does not need to wait for order A. **Two scenarios arise:** - ­¤Ü½ **Without Preemption ($P \parallel C_{\max}$):** Once an order starts on a machine, it must finish on that machine without stopping. Like painting a wall in one continuous stroke. - Ō£ģ **With Preemption ($P \vert pmtn \vert C_{\max}$):** You may pause an order halfway, move it to another machine, and continue later. Like painting a wall across two days ŌĆö stop at 60%, resume the remaining 40% next morning. **Your objective:** Minimize the time when the **last** order leaves the factory floor ($C_{\max}$) ŌĆö i.e., finish **all** work as early as possible. --- ### 1.3 Key Performance Metric > [!NOTE] > The **Makespan** $C_{\max} = \max_j C_j$ is the single most important metric in parallel machine scheduling. It measures the **total elapsed time** to complete the entire job set. A lower bound that **no algorithm can ever beat** is: $$C_{\max}^{*} \;\geq\; \max\!\left\{\max_{1 \le j \le n} p_j,\; \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j\right\}$$ - $\max_j p_j$: no machine can finish sooner than the longest single job. - $\dfrac{1}{m}\sum p_j$: total workload must be shared; the average load is a floor on $C_{\max}^{*}$. For the preemptive case, this lower bound is **achievable**; for the non-preemptive case, it generally is not. --- ### 1.4 Problem Classification | Variant | Complexity | Optimal Algorithm | Key Reference | |:--------|:----------:|:------------------|:--------------| | $P \parallel C_{\max}$ ($m=2$) | **Polynomial** (O($n \log n$)) | Johnson's variant / Pairing | ŌĆö | | $P \parallel C_{\max}$ ($m \geq 3$) | **NP-hard** | None known (P $\neq$ NP) | Garey & Johnson, 1979 | | $P \vert pmtn \vert C_{\max}$ | **Polynomial** (O($n$)) | **McNaughton's Algorithm** | McNaughton, 1959 | > [!IMPORTANT] > The single act of **allowing preemption collapses an NP-hard problem into a polynomial one**. This is one of the most striking results in combinatorial optimization and is a **high-yield KTU exam point**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Without Preemption: $P \parallel C_{\max}$ Since the problem is NP-hard for $m \geq 3$, we rely on **heuristics** that are fast and provably close to optimal. #### 2.1.1 List Scheduling (LS) A simple greedy heuristic: 1. Maintain a list (sequence) of jobs in any order. 2. Schedule each job, in order, on the machine that becomes free **earliest**. 3. Ties broken arbitrarily. **Complexity:** $O(n \log m)$ using a min-heap of machine completion times. **Guarantee (Graham, 1966):** $$C_{\max}^{LS} \;\leq\; \left(2 - \dfrac{1}{m}\right) C_{\max}^{*}$$ #### 2.1.2 LPT (Longest Processing Time) Rule A smarter heuristic: 1. Sort all jobs in **non-increasing** order: $p_1 \geq p_2 \geq \dots \geq p_n$. 2. Apply List Scheduling to this sorted list. **Complexity:** $O(n \log n + nm)$, dominated by the sort. **Guarantee (Graham, 1969):** $$C_{\max}^{LPT} \;\leq\; \left(\dfrac{4}{3} - \dfrac{1}{3m}\right) C_{\max}^{*}$$ This bound **tightens** as $m$ grows. For $m=2$: $\leq \tfrac{7}{6}\,C_{\max}^{*}$; for $m \to \infty$: $\leq \tfrac{4}{3}\,C_{\max}^{*}$. --- ### 2.2 With Preemption: $P \vert pmtn \vert C_{\max}$ ŌĆö McNaughton's Theorem **McNaughton's Theorem (1959):** *For parallel identical machines with preemptions allowed and the objective of minimizing makespan, the lower bound is achievable in $O(n)$ time.* $$C_{\max}^{*} \;=\; \max\!\left\{\max_{j} p_j,\; \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j\right\}$$ The optimal makespan is simply the **maximum** of the largest job and the average machine load. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **only reference you need** at the exam hall for this topic. | # | Formula / Rule | Condition | Use Case | |:-:|:---------------|:----------|:---------| | 1 | $C_{\max}^{*} \geq \max_j p_j$ | Always | Lower bound (single job) | | 2 | $C_{\max}^{*} \geq \dfrac{1}{m}\sum p_j$ | Always | Lower bound (load) | | 3 | $C_{\max}^{*} = \max\!\left\{\max_j p_j,\; \tfrac{1}{m}\sum p_j\right\}$ | **Preemption allowed** | Optimal makespan | | 4 | $C_{\max}^{LS} \leq \left(2-\tfrac{1}{m}\right)C_{\max}^{*}$ | List Scheduling | Worst-case ratio | | 5 | $C_{\max}^{LPT} \leq \left(\tfrac{4}{3}-\tfrac{1}{3m}\right)C_{\max}^{*}$ | LPT rule | Worst-case ratio | | 6 | $\rho = \dfrac{C_{\max}^{H}}{C_{\max}^{*}}$ | Heuristic $H$ | Performance ratio | | 7 | $\text{Idle}(M_k) = C_{\max} - \sum_{j \in M_k} p_j$ | Machine $M_k$ | Wasted time | | 8 | $\sum_{k=1}^{m} \sum_{j \in M_k} p_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j$ | Conservation of total work | Verify schedule | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cloud Computing Datacenters:** A scheduler distributes thousands of independent virtual machines across identical physical servers. Preemptions model **live migration** of VMs. - **CPU Multitasking in Operating Systems:** Round-Robin and Shortest-Remaining-Time-First schedulers are preemptive analogues on a single processor (the $1 \vert pmtn \vert \sum C_j$ problem). - **Manufacturing Cells:** Identical CNC machines processing independent customer orders ŌĆö a textbook $P \parallel C_{\max}$ setup. - **Parallel Computing / HPC:** Independent task graphs with identical worker nodes; preemption models **context switches**. The McNaughton optimal preemptive solution is the theoretical ceiling that any real scheduler (which may not allow preemption) is **trying to approach**. --- ### 2.5 Comparative Conceptual Map | Aspect | $P \parallel C_{\max}$ | $P \vert pmtn \vert C_{\max}$ | |:-------|:----------------------:|:----------------------------:| | Preemption | ŌØī Not allowed | Ō£ģ Allowed | | Complexity ($m \geq 3$) | NP-hard | Polynomial ($O(n)$) | | Optimal algorithm | None (P $\neq$ NP) | McNaughton | | Practical heuristic | LPT, LS, Multifit | McNaughton itself | | Idle time possible? | Yes (gaps in schedule) | No (tied to $C_{\max}^{*}$) | | Exam difficulty | Medium (heuristic + bound) | High (splitting logic) | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Lower Bound $C_{\max}^{*} \geq \dfrac{1}{m}\sum p_j$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Conservation of total processing.** Each job $j$ contributes exactly $p_j$ units of work distributed across the machines. Therefore: $$\sum_{k=1}^{m} \sum_{j \in M_k} p_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Bound per machine.** On any machine $M_k$, the total assigned work cannot exceed the makespan (otherwise that machine would still be busy after $C_{\max}$): $$\sum_{j \in M_k} p_j \;\leq\; C_{\max}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Summing over all machines.** Adding the inequalities for $k = 1, \dots, m$: $$\sum_{k=1}^{m}\sum_{j \in M_k} p_j \;\leq\; m \cdot C_{\max}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Substituting the conservation identity:** $$\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j \;\leq\; m \cdot C_{\max}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Solving for $C_{\max}$:** $$\boxed{\,C_{\max} \;\geq\; \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_j\,}$$ This bound is called the **average load** or **machine capacity** lower bound. --- ### 3.2 McNaughton's Algorithm ŌĆö Full Derivation #### 3.2.1 Algorithm Statement > [!IMPORTANT] > **McNaughton's Algorithm (1959):** Given $n$ jobs with processing times $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$ and $m$ identical machines: > 1. Compute the optimal makespan $C^{*} = \max\!\left\{\max_j p_j,\; \tfrac{1}{m}\sum p_j\right\}$. > 2. Assign jobs to machines in any order (typically non-increasing). > 3. On each machine, fill up to capacity $C^{*}$. If a job would overflow $C^{*}$, **split** it: place the fitting portion on the current machine and the remainder on the next machine. #### 3.2.2 Correctness Proof Sketch We must show two things: **(A)** Every job can be scheduled within $[0, C^{*}]$ on the $m$ machines, and **(B)** No schedule can finish strictly before $C^{*}$. **Part B ŌĆö Lower bound.** Already shown in ┬¦3.1 plus $\max_j p_j \leq C_{\max}$. **Part A ŌĆö Feasibility.** The algorithm packs jobs greedily. The capacity used on machine $M_k$ before moving to $M_{k+1}$ is at most $C^{*}$ by construction. The total capacity across all machines is $m \cdot C^{*} \geq \sum p_j$ (because $C^{*} \geq \tfrac{1}{m}\sum p_j$). Hence every job fits. ŌłÄ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Without Preemption (LPT) **Problem:** $m = 3$ machines, $n = 7$ jobs with processing times: $$p_1 = 8,\; p_2 = 7,\; p_3 = 6,\; p_4 = 5,\; p_5 = 4,\; p_6 = 3,\; p_7 = 2$$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Sort jobs in non-increasing order:** Already sorted ($8,7,6,5,4,3,2$). **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute lower bound:** $$\max_j p_j = 8, \qquad \dfrac{1}{m}\sum p_j = \tfrac{8+7+6+5+4+3+2}{3} = \tfrac{35}{3} \approx 11.67$$ $$C_{\max}^{*} \;\geq\; \lceil 11.67 \rceil = 12$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Apply LPT (assign each job to the machine with earliest finish):** | Job | $p_j$ | $M_1$ end | $M_2$ end | $M_3$ end | Assign to | New ends | |:---:|:-----:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:--------:| | 1 | 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $M_1$ | (8,0,0) | | 2 | 7 | 8 | 0 | 0 | $M_2$ | (8,7,0) | | 3 | 6 | 8 | 7 | 0 | $M_3$ | (8,7,6) | | 4 | 5 | 8 | 7 | 6 | $M_2$ | (8,12,6) | | 5 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 6 | $M_1$ | (12,12,6) | | 6 | 3 | 12 | 12 | 6 | $M_3$ | (12,12,9) | | 7 | 2 | 12 | 12 | 9 | $M_3$ | (12,12,11) | **Step 4 ŌĆö Result:** $C_{\max}^{LPT} = \max(12, 12, 11) = 12$. **Step 5 ŌĆö Verify performance ratio:** $\rho = 12 / 12 = 1.0$ ŌĆö **LPT found the optimum!** --- ### 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö With Preemption (McNaughton) **Problem:** Same 7 jobs, $m = 3$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $C^{*}$:** $$C^{*} = \max\!\left\{8,\; \tfrac{35}{3}\right\} = \max\{8,\; 11.67\} = 11.67$$ Since $C^{*}$ is fractional, we take $C^{*} = 11.67$ (continuous time). Each machine has capacity $11.67$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Pack jobs in non-increasing order, splitting when needed:** | Machine | Jobs (in time order) | Total used | Idle | |:-------:|:---------------------|:----------:|:----:| | $M_1$ | Job 1 (8) ŌåÆ Job 2 (3.67 of 7) | 11.67 | 0 | | $M_2$ | Job 2 remaining (3.33) ŌåÆ Job 3 (6) ŌåÆ Job 4 (2.34 of 5) | 11.67 | 0 | | $M_3$ | Job 4 remaining (2.66) ŌåÆ Job 5 (4) ŌåÆ Job 6 (3) ŌåÆ Job 7 (2) | 11.67 | 0 | **Step 3 ŌĆö Verify:** Each machine has zero idle time, $C_{\max} = 11.67 < 12$. Preemption **saves** $0.33$ time units. **Step 4 ŌĆö Job 2 and Job 4 are split (preempted).** --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison Table | Metric | Without Preemption (LPT) | With Preemption (McNaughton) | |:-------|:------------------------:|:----------------------------:| | $C_{\max}$ achieved | 12 | 11.67 | | $C_{\max}^{*}$ (lower bound) | 11.67 | 11.67 | | Performance ratio | $12 / 11.67 = 1.028$ | $1.0$ (optimal) | | Machines with idle time | 2 (1 idle unit on $M_3$) | 0 | | Preemptions used | 0 | 2 (jobs 2 & 4) | | Job splits | None | Yes | | Practicality | High (no interruption) | Lower (context-switch cost) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The gain from preemption is **always non-negative**, i.e., $C_{\max}^{pmtn} \leq C_{\max}^{no\,pmtn}$. This monotonicity is a frequently tested KTU property. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Problem-Setting Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"] jobs["JOB SET
