## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Define Palmer's slope index. Write the formula and explain its physical meaning in flow shop scheduling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Palmer's slope index of job $j$ is defined as:

$$S_j = \sum_{k=1}^{m} (2k - m - 1) \cdot p_{jk}$$

**Physical meaning:** It measures the *trend* of a job's processing time as it moves from the first machine to the last. A **high positive $S_j$** means the job gets progressively slower (steep rising slope), while a **negative $S_j$** means the job gets faster down the line. Jobs with higher $S_j$ are scheduled first to **balance machine workload** and **minimize idle time** on intermediate machines, thus reducing the makespan $C_{max}$.

**(Award 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for slope interpretation, 1 mark for scheduling rule.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**List the steps of Palmer's algorithm for sequencing $n$ jobs on $m$ machines.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. Form the $n \times m$ processing time matrix $[p_{jk}]$.
2. Compute Palmer weights $w_k = 2k - m - 1$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, m$.
3. For each job $j$, calculate the slope index $S_j = \sum_{k=1}^{m} w_k p_{jk}$.
4. Rank the jobs in **decreasing order** of $S_j$. Resolve ties using **larger total processing time first**.
5. Apply this sequence to all machines (permutation schedule) and compute $C_{max}$.

**(Award 0.5ŌĆō1 mark per step.)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Module 3 Choice]**

> Consider a **flow shop with 5 jobs and 4 machines**. The processing times (in hours) are given below:
>
> | Job | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $M_3$ | $M_4$ |
> |-----|------:|------:|------:|------:|
> | A   | 5 | 1 | 9 | 7 |
> | B   | 6 | 2 | 10| 4 |
> | C   | 4 | 3 | 7 | 8 |
> | D   | 7 | 5 | 6 | 3 |
> | E   | 8 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
>
> **(a)** Apply Palmer's heuristic to determine the optimal sequence. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Construct the Gantt chart, compute $C_{max}$, mean flow time, and total idle time on all machines. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Weights for $m = 4$:**
$w_1 = -3,\ w_2 = -1,\ w_3 = +1,\ w_4 = +3$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Slope Indices:**

For Job A:
$$S_A = (-3)(5) + (-1)(1) + (1)(9) + (3)(7) = -15 - 1 + 9 + 21 = 14$$

For Job B:
$$S_B = (-3)(6) + (-1)(2) + (1)(10) + (3)(4) = -18 - 2 + 10 + 12 = 2$$

For Job C:
$$S_C = (-3)(4) + (-1)(3) + (1)(7) + (3)(8) = -12 - 3 + 7 + 24 = 16$$

For Job D:
$$S_D = (-3)(7) + (-1)(5) + (1)(6) + (3)(3) = -21 - 5 + 6 + 9 = -11$$

For Job E:
$$S_E = (-3)(8) + (-1)(4) + (1)(5) + (3)(6) = -24 - 4 + 5 + 18 = -5$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Ranking (decreasing $S_j$):**

| Job | $S_j$ | Rank |
|-----|------:|-----:|
| C | 16 | 1 |
| A | 14 | 2 |
| B | 2  | 3 |
| E | -5 | 4 |
| D | -11 | 5 |

> **Optimal Palmer Sequence: C ŌåÆ A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ E ŌåÆ D**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

Apply the recurrence $C_{jk} = \max(C_{j-1,k},\ C_{j,k-1}) + p_{jk}$.

**Row 1 ŌĆö Job C:** $C_{C,1}=4$, $C_{C,2}=4+3=7$, $C_{C,3}=7+7=14$, $C_{C,4}=14+8=22$.

**Row 2 ŌĆö Job A:**
$$C_{A,1} = 4+5 = 9$$
$$C_{A,2} = \max(7, 9) + 1 = 9 + 1 = 10$$
$$C_{A,3} = \max(14, 10) + 9 = 14 + 9 = 23$$
$$C_{A,4} = \max(22, 23) + 7 = 23 + 7 = 30$$

**Row 3 ŌĆö Job B:**
$$C_{B,1} = 9+6 = 15$$
$$C_{B,2} = \max(10, 15) + 2 = 15 + 2 = 17$$
$$C_{B,3} = \max(23, 17) + 10 = 23 + 10 = 33$$
$$C_{B,4} = \max(30, 33) + 4 = 33 + 4 = 37$$

**Row 4 ŌĆö Job E:**
$$C_{E,1} = 15+8 = 23$$
$$C_{E,2} = \max(17, 23) + 4 = 23 + 4 = 27$$
$$C_{E,3} = \max(33, 27) + 5 = 33 + 5 = 38$$
$$C_{E,4} = \max(37, 38) + 6 = 38 + 6 = 44$$

**Row 5 ŌĆö Job D:**
$$C_{D,1} = 23+7 = 30$$
$$C_{D,2} = \max(27, 30) + 5 = 30 + 5 = 35$$
$$C_{D,3} = \max(38, 35) + 6 = 38 + 6 = 44$$
$$C_{D,4} = \max(44, 44) + 3 = 44 + 3 = 47$$

**Schedule Table:**

| Seq | Job | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $M_3$ | $M_4$ |
|----:|-----|------:|------:|------:|------:|
| 1 | C | 4 | 7 | 14 | 22 |
| 2 | A | 9 | 10 | 23 | 30 |
| 3 | B | 15 | 17 | 33 | 37 |
| 4 | E | 23 | 27 | 38 | 44 |
| 5 | D | 30 | 35 | 44 | **47** |

**Results:**
- **Makespan** $C_{max} = \mathbf{47}$ hours.
- **Mean flow time** $\bar{F} = (22 + 30 + 37 + 44 + 47)/5 = 180/5 = \mathbf{36}$ hours.
- **Total processing load** $= 5(5+1+9+7) + \ldots = $ sum of all 20 entries $= 24+22+22+21+23 = 112$ hours.
- **Total idle time** $= m \cdot C_{max} - \sum p_{jk} = 4(47) - 112 = 188 - 112 = \mathbf{76}$ hours.

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Module 3 Choice]**

> A workshop has **4 jobs** to be processed on **5 machines** in the same order. The processing times are:
>
> | Job | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $M_3$ | $M_4$ | $M_5$ |
> |-----|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|
> | 1   | 3 | 4 | 2 | 6 | 5 |
> | 2   | 5 | 6 | 4 | 3 | 7 |
> | 3   | 2 | 5 | 6 | 4 | 8 |
> | 4   | 6 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 4 |
>
> **(a)** Compute Palmer's slope indices and determine the optimal processing sequence. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the makespan and total idle time. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Weights for $m = 5$:**
$$w_1 = 2(1)-5-1 = -4,\ w_2 = -2,\ w_3 = 0,\ w_4 = +2,\ w_5 = +4$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Slope Indices:**

Job 1:
$$S_1 = (-4)(3) + (-2)(4) + (0)(2) + (2)(6) + (4)(5) = -12 - 8 + 0 + 12 + 20 = 12$$

Job 2:
$$S_2 = (-4)(5) + (-2)(6) + (0)(4) + (2)(3) + (4)(7) = -20 - 12 + 0 + 6 + 28 = 2$$

Job 3:
$$S_3 = (-4)(2) + (-2)(5) + (0)(6) + (2)(4) + (4)(8) = -8 - 10 + 0 + 8 + 32 = 22$$

Job 4:
$$S_4 = (-4)(6) + (-2)(3) + (0)(5) + (2)(7) + (4)(4) = -24 - 6 + 0 + 14 + 16 = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Ranking:**

| Job | $S_j$ | Rank |
|-----|------:|-----:|
| 3 | 22 | 1 |
| 1 | 12 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 |
| 4 | 0 | 4 |

> **Optimal Sequence: 3 ŌåÆ 1 ŌåÆ 2 ŌåÆ 4**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Model Solution (7 Marks)

Apply $C_{jk} = \max(C_{j-1,k},\ C_{j,k-1}) + p_{jk}$.

**Row 1 ŌĆö Job 3:** $C_{3,1}=2$, $C_{3,2}=2+5=7$, $C_{3,3}=7+6=13$, $C_{3,4}=13+4=17$, $C_{3,5}=17+8=25$.

**Row 2 ŌĆö Job 1:**
$$C_{1,1} = 2+3 = 5,\quad C_{1,2} = \max(7,5)+4 = 11,\quad C_{1,3} = \max(13,11)+2 = 15$$
$$C_{1,4} = \max(17,15)+6 = 23,\quad C_{1,5} = \max(25,23)+5 = 30$$

**Row 3 ŌĆö Job 2:**
$$C_{2,1} = 5+5 = 10,\quad C_{2,2} = \max(11,10)+6 = 17,\quad C_{2,3} = \max(15,17)+4 = 21$$
$$C_{2,4} = \max(23,21)+3 = 26,\quad C_{2,5} = \max(30,26)+7 = 37$$

**Row 4 ŌĆö Job 4:**
$$C_{4,1} = 10+6 = 16,\quad C_{4,2} = \max(17,16)+3 = 20,\quad C_{4,3} = \max(21,20)+5 = 26$$
$$C_{4,4} = \max(26,26)+7 = 33,\quad C_{4,5} = \max(37,33)+4 = 41$$

**Schedule Table:**

| Seq | Job | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $M_3$ | $M_4$ | $M_5$ |
|----:|-----|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|
| 1 | 3 | 2 | 7 | 13 | 17 | 25 |
| 2 | 1 | 5 | 11 | 15 | 23 | 30 |
| 3 | 2 | 10 | 17 | 21 | 26 | 37 |
| 4 | 4 | 16 | 20 | 26 | 33 | **41** |

**Results:**
- **Makespan** $C_{max} = \mathbf{41}$ hours.
- **Total processing load** $\sum p_{jk} = (3+4+2+6+5) + (5+6+4+3+7) + (2+5+6+4+8) + (6+3+5+7+4) = 20+25+25+25 = 95$ hours.
- **Total idle time** $= 5(41) - 95 = 205 - 95 = \mathbf{110}$ hours.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Error in Weights:** Students often compute $w_k = 2k - m + 1$ instead of $2k - m - 1$. Wrong sign ŌåÆ wrong rank ŌåÆ wrong sequence ŌåÆ **lose 3ŌĆō4 marks**.
> 2. **Sort Direction:** Always sort by **decreasing** $S_j$. Sorting in increasing order reverses the sequence ŌĆö a classic 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Recurrence Mistake:** Forgetting the $\max(C_{j-1,k},\ C_{j,k-1})$ and using only $C_{j,k-1} + p_{jk}$ gives wrong (smaller) $C_{max}$. This invalidates **Part (b) entirely (7 marks)**.
> 4. **Idle Time Calculation:** Don't manually subtract gaps; always use $I_{total} = mC_{max} - \sum p_{jk}$ to avoid errors in gap counting.
> 5. **Tie-Breaker:** If $S_j$ values tie, KTU explicitly asks for tie-breaking by larger total processing time. Skipping this step loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Units:** Always write "hours" or "minutes" alongside numerical values; KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units in the final answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Palmer's Algorithm**

- Ō£ģ Palmer's algorithm is used for **$n$-job, $m$-machine flow shop** to **minimize makespan**.
- Ō£ģ Slope index formula: $S_j = \sum_{k=1}^{m}(2k - m - 1)\, p_{jk}$.
- Ō£ģ Weight $w_k = 2k - m - 1$ ranges from $-(m-1)$ to $+(m-1)$ in **steps of 2**.
- Ō£ģ Sequence = jobs ranked in **decreasing** order of $S_j$.
- Ō£ģ **Tie-breaker:** Job with **larger total processing time** goes first; if still tied, smaller job index.
- Ō£ģ Generates a **permutation schedule** ŌĆö same job order on every machine.
- Ō£ģ Flow shop recurrence: $C_{jk} = \max(C_{j-1,k},\ C_{j,k-1}) + p_{jk}$.
- Ō£ģ Makespan: $C_{max} = \max_j C_{jm}$ (last column, last row).
- Ō£ģ Total idle time: $I_{total} = m \cdot C_{max} - \sum_{j}\sum_{k} p_{jk}$.
- Ō£ģ Mean flow time: $\bar{F} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j$, where $F_j = C_{jm}$.
- Ō£ģ Palmer is a **heuristic**, **not optimal** for $m \geq 3$; for guaranteed optimum, use **Branch-and-Bound**.
- Ō£ģ For 2 machines, prefer **Johnson's Rule** (it is provably optimal).
- Ō£ģ For 3+ machines with mean-flow-time objective, prefer **CDS heuristic**.
- Ō£ģ Always carry units (hours/minutes) in every cell of the schedule table.
- Ō£ģ Palmer's algorithm is **$O(nm)$** in time complexity ŌĆö extremely fast even for 100+ jobs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CDS Algorithm and Insertion Heuristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CDS Algorithm and Insertion Heuristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **CDS (CampbellŌĆōDudekŌĆōSmith) Algorithm** is a polynomial-time heuristic proposed in 1970 for generating near-optimal schedules in a **Permutation Flow Shop Scheduling Problem (PFSSP)** with *n* jobs and *m* machines. It systematically generates *(m ŌłÆ 1)* different job sequences by treating each pair of "composite machines" as an equivalent 2-machine flow shop, applying **Johnson's 2-Machine Algorithm** to each, and selecting the best sequence based on minimum **Makespan (C_max)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Insertion Heuristics):**
> **Insertion Heuristics** are constructive scheduling procedures that build a complete sequence incrementally by repeatedly inserting an unscheduled job into the *best possible position* of a partially constructed partial schedule. The most celebrated industrial variant is the **NEH Algorithm (NawazŌĆōEnscoreŌĆōHam, 1983)** used for *parallel machine* and *flow shop* environments.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö CDS Algorithm:**
> Imagine a **garment manufacturing unit** with 5 sequential workstations (cutting ŌåÆ stitching ŌåÆ buttoning ŌåÆ ironing ŌåÆ packaging). A planner is unsure whether to optimize only on "cutting + stitching" or "ironing + packaging" or some other combined pair. Rather than testing one fixed pair, the CDS approach is like **wearing different pairs of glasses** for the same shop floor ŌĆö each pair lets you focus on a different combined pair of workstations ŌĆö and then you **pick the pair that produces the clearest (shortest) overall production line output**. This avoids the pitfall of getting trapped in a single local optimum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö Insertion Heuristics:**
> Think of **booking seats in a cinema hall row** for a group arriving one by one. The first two people take the middle seats (NEH initial step). When each new person arrives, you do not place them at the ends; instead, you **slide them into every possible gap** in the current seating arrangement, compute the "loss" in comfort/timing at each insertion point, and seat them at the *least painful* gap. Insertion heuristics apply exactly this logic to job sequencing.

### Key Performance Metrics Used

- **Makespan (C_max):** Total completion time of the last job on the last machine *(primary optimization criterion)*.
- **Total Flow Time (╬ŻF_j):** Sum of completion times of all jobs.
- **Idle Time (╬ŻIdle):** Total machine idle time.
- **Tardiness / Lateness:** Measures of due-date adherence.

The bold primary metric for CDS evaluation is **Makespan (C_max)**, with units of **time units (hours/shifts/days)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### A. CDS Algorithm ŌĆö Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setting:**
> Given *n* jobs *{J_1, J_2, ŌĆ”, J_n}* and *m* machines *{M_1, M_2, ŌĆ”, M_m}* in the **same machine order** (flow shop), find a permutation sequence that minimizes the makespan.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Composite Machine Formation:**
For each value of *k* ranging from **2 to m**, construct a virtual 2-machine flow shop problem:

$$
p'_{j}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}
$$

$$
p''_{j}(k) = \sum_{i=m-k+1}^{m} p_{ij}
$$

where:
- *p_{ij}* = processing time of job *j* on machine *i*.
- *p'_j(k)* = aggregated processing time of job *j* on the **first composite machine** (machines 1 through *k*).
- *p''_j(k)* = aggregated processing time of job *j* on the **second composite machine** (machines *m ŌłÆ k + 1* through *m*).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Johnson's Algorithm Application:**
Apply **Johnson's 2-Machine Algorithm** to the pair *(p'_j(k), p''_j(k))* to generate sequence *S_k*.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluation:**
Evaluate each *S_k* on the *original m-machine* problem (not the composite one) by simulating the flow shop and computing:

$$
C_{\max}(S_k) = \max_j \{C_{jm}\}
$$

where *C_{jm}* is the completion time of job *j* on machine *m*.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Selection:**
Choose the sequence with **minimum makespan**:

$$
S^{*} = \arg \min_{k \in \{2,3,\dots,m\}} C_{\max}(S_k)
$$

**Total sequences generated: (m ŌłÆ 1)** ŌĆö runs from *k = 2* to *k = m*.

### B. Why CDS Works ŌĆö The Underlying Logic

- For **k = 2**, we essentially replicate Johnson's algorithm on the original problem.
- For **k = m**, both composite machines include *all* processing times twice, hence behave as a special aggregate.
- Intermediate values of *k* capture **partial bottleneck structures** along the production line.
- By exploring multiple aggregation strategies, CDS escapes the trap of any single Johnson's-based sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complexity Note for Examiners:**
> CDS has a computational complexity of **O(m ┬Ę n log n)**, dominated by the sorting step of Johnson's algorithm applied *(m ŌłÆ 1)* times. This is far cheaper than the **O((n!)^m)** brute-force search space.

### C. Insertion Heuristics ŌĆö Operational Logic

#### C.1 General Insertion Heuristic Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized Insertion Algorithm:**
> 1. Initialize partial sequence *ŽĆ* (often by selecting two jobs using a problem-specific rule).
> 2. For each remaining unscheduled job *j*:
>    a. For each of the *|ŽĆ| + 1* possible insertion positions in *ŽĆ*:
>       - Compute the objective function value (e.g., makespan) of the new partial sequence.
>    b. Insert *j* at the position that yields the **best (minimum)** objective value.
> 3. Return the complete sequence.

#### C.2 NEH Algorithm (Nawaz-Enscore-Ham, 1983)

**Step 1:** Compute the **total processing time** of each job across all machines:

$$
T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{ij}
$$

**Step 2:** Sort the jobs in **decreasing order** of *T_j* to obtain an initial ordered list.

**Step 3:** Take the **first two jobs** and schedule them in the order (2,1) and (1,2); retain the partial sequence *ŽĆ* of length 2 with the **lower makespan**.

**Step 4:** For *k = 3, 4, ŌĆ”, n*:
- Take the *k*-th job from the sorted list.
- For each of the *k* possible insertion positions in the current sequence *ŽĆ*:
  - Compute the makespan of the resulting sequence.
- Insert the job at the position giving the **minimum makespan**.

**Step 5:** Return the final sequence *ŽĆ* of length *n*.

#### C.3 Other Insertion Variants (For Examiner Awareness)

- **First Fit Insertion:** Inserts each job at the *first* feasible position that improves the objective.
- **Best Fit Insertion:** Evaluates all positions; inserts at the best (used in NEH).
- **Forward / Backward Insertion:** Differ by the direction of sequence expansion.
- **Pairwise Insertion (PI):** Inserts *pairs* of jobs simultaneously for reduced computation.

### D. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 1: Essential CDS and Insertion Heuristics Formulas**

| **Formula** | **Meaning / Application** | **Units / Boundary** |
|---|---|---|
| $p'_{j}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}$ | Composite time of job $j$ on first virtual machine | Time units; valid for $k = 2, \dots, m$ |
| $p''_{j}(k) = \sum_{i=m-k+1}^{m} p_{ij}$ | Composite time of job $j$ on second virtual machine | Time units; valid for $k = 2, \dots, m$ |
| $C_{\max}(S_k) = \max_j \{C_{jm}\}$ | Makespan of sequence $S_k$ on original $m$-machine shop | Time units |
| $S^{*} = \arg \min_k C_{\max}(S_k)$ | Best CDS sequence selection rule | ŌĆö |
| $T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{ij}$ | NEH total processing time of job $j$ | Time units |
| $C_{ij} = \max\{C_{i-1,j},\ C_{i,j-1}\} + p_{ij}$ | Recursive completion time in flow shop | Time units |
| Johnson's rule: $\min\{p_{1j}, p_{2j}\}$ | Position job $j$ in front/back slot | Applicable only to 2-machine case |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> CDS is used in **automated production scheduling software**, **PCB assembly lines**, **petroleum refinery batch operations**, and **food processing flow lines**. Insertion heuristics (especially NEH) dominate real-world **dispatching systems** in semiconductor fabrication and automotive paint shops because they generate high-quality sequences in *O(n^2 \cdot m)* time ŌĆö practical even for *n = 100+* jobs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### Worked Example 1 ŌĆö CDS Algorithm (n = 4 Jobs, m = 3 Machines)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Data Table (Processing Times):**
>
> | Job | Machine 1 | Machine 2 | Machine 3 |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | J1 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
> | J2 | 2 | 6 | 1 |
> | J3 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
> | J4 | 7 | 2 | 6 |

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Composite Times for k = 2

$$
p'_{j}(2) = p_{1j} + p_{2j}, \qquad p''_{j}(2) = p_{2j} + p_{3j}
$$

For *J1*: $p'_1 = 4 + 3 = 7$, $p''_1 = 3 + 5 = 8$.
For *J2*: $p'_2 = 2 + 6 = 8$, $p''_2 = 6 + 1 = 7$.
For *J3*: $p'_3 = 5 + 4 = 9$, $p''_3 = 4 + 3 = 7$.
For *J4*: $p'_4 = 7 + 2 = 9$, $p''_4 = 2 + 6 = 8$.

Apply **Johnson's Algorithm**:
- Smallest *p'_j* is *J1 (7)* ŌåÆ place at the **front** ŌåÆ sequence becomes [J1, ŌĆ”].
- Next smallest *p''_j* among remaining: *J3 (7)*, *J2 (7)*, *J4 (8)* ŌåÆ pick the smallest *p''* ŌåÆ *J3* at back.
- Then *J2* at back, *J4* at front.

Sequence for *k = 2*: **S_2 = (J4, J1, J3, J2)**.

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Composite Times for k = 3

$$
p'_{j}(3) = p_{1j} + p_{2j} + p_{3j}, \qquad p''_{j}(3) = p_{1j} + p_{2j} + p_{3j}
$$

(Note: when *k = m*, both composite machines are identical.)

For all jobs: $p'_j = p''_j$. Apply Johnson:

- *J2 (9)*, *J4 (15)*, *J1 (12)*, *J3 (12)*.
- Smallest *p'_j* (or equivalently *p''_j*) is *J2* ŌåÆ place front.
- Smallest *p''_j* among remaining: *J1 (12)*, *J3 (12)*, *J4 (15)* ŌåÆ *J1* at back.
- Then *J3* at back, *J4* at front.

Sequence for *k = 3*: **S_3 = (J2, J4, J1, J3)**.

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate Both Sequences on the Original 3-Machine Shop

**For S_2 = (J4, J1, J3, J2):**

Row-by-row completion times:

| Job | M1 (start, finish) | M2 (start, finish) | M3 (start, finish) |
|---|---|---|---|
| J4 | (0, 7) | (7, 9) | (9, 15) |
| J1 | (7, 11) | (11, 14) | (15, 20) |
| J3 | (11, 16) | (16, 20) | (20, 23) |
| J2 | (16, 18) | (20, 26) | (26, 27) |

$$
C_{\max}(S_2) = 27 \text{ time units}
$$

**For S_3 = (J2, J4, J1, J3):**

| Job | M1 (start, finish) | M2 (start, finish) | M3 (start, finish) |
|---|---|---|---|
| J2 | (0, 2) | (2, 8) | (8, 9) |
| J4 | (2, 9) | (9, 11) | (11, 17) |
| J1 | (9, 13) | (13, 16) | (17, 22) |
| J3 | (13, 18) | (18, 22) | (22, 25) |

$$
C_{\max}(S_3) = 25 \text{ time units}
$$

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Select Optimal Sequence

$$
C_{\max}(S_2) = 27, \quad C_{\max}(S_3) = 25
$$

Since $25 < 27$:

$$
S^{*} = S_3 = (J_2,\ J_4,\ J_1,\ J_3)
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{\max}^{*} = 25 \text{ time units}}
$$

---

### Worked Example 2 ŌĆö NEH Insertion Heuristic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Data:** 4 jobs on 3 machines with the same processing time table as above.

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Total Processing Times

$$
T_1 = 4+3+5 = 12, \quad T_2 = 2+6+1 = 9, \quad T_3 = 5+4+3 = 12, \quad T_4 = 7+2+6 = 15
$$

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Sort in Decreasing Order of T_j

Order: **J4 (15), J1 (12), J3 (12), J2 (9)**.

Break ties by original indexing: J1 before J3 (lower index first).

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Sequence of First Two Jobs

Try (J4, J1) and (J1, J4):

- *ŽĆ* = (J4, J1): Makespan = ?

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| J4 | (0, 7) | (7, 9) | (9, 15) |
| J1 | (7, 11) | (11, 14) | (15, 20) |

$C_{\max} = 20$.

- *ŽĆ* = (J1, J4): Makespan = ?

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| J1 | (0, 4) | (4, 7) | (7, 12) |
| J4 | (4, 11) | (11, 13) | (13, 19) |

$C_{\max} = 19$.

Thus, partial sequence *ŽĆ* = **(J1, J4)**.

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Insert J3

Try inserting *J3* in 3 positions:
- Position 1: (J3, J1, J4)
- Position 2: (J1, J3, J4)
- Position 3: (J1, J4, J3)

| Position | Sequence | C_max |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (J3, J1, J4) | 25 |
| 2 | (J1, J3, J4) | 24 |
| 3 | (J1, J4, J3) | 27 |

Minimum ŌåÆ Position 2. New *ŽĆ* = **(J1, J3, J4)**.

#### Step 5 ŌĆö Insert J2

Try inserting *J2* in 4 positions:
- Position 1: (J2, J1, J3, J4)
- Position 2: (J1, J2, J3, J4)
- Position 3: (J1, J3, J2, J4)
- Position 4: (J1, J3, J4, J2)

Computing each makespan (using the recursive formula $C_{ij} = \max\{C_{i-1,j}, C_{i,j-1}\} + p_{ij}$):

| Position | Sequence | C_max |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (J2, J1, J3, J4) | 26 |
| 2 | (J1, J2, J3, J4) | 23 |
| 3 | (J1, J3, J2, J4) | 25 |
| 4 | (J1, J3, J4, J2) | 26 |

Minimum ŌåÆ Position 2. Final sequence:

$$
\boxed{\pi^{*} = (J_1,\ J_2,\ J_3,\ J_4), \quad C_{\max}^{*} = 23 \text{ time units}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:**
> NEH produced **C_max = 23**, which is *better* than CDS (C_max = 25) for this specific instance. This is why insertion heuristics (especially NEH) are the *de facto* standard benchmark in flow shop scheduling research.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### Diagram 1: CDS Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START: Input n jobs, m machines, p ij table]) --> readData[Read processing time matrix]
    readData --> initLoop[Initialize k = 2]
    initLoop --> condCheck{k less than equal m?}
    condCheck -- No --> selectBest[Select S star = arg min Cmax Sk over all k]
    condCheck -- Yes --> compM1[Compute composite times p prime j k = sum i=1 to k p ij]
    compM1 --> compM2[Compute composite times p double prime j k = sum i=m-k+1 to m p ij]
    compM2 --> johnson[Apply Johnson 2 Machine Algorithm]
    johnson --> getSeq[Obtain sequence S k]
    getSeq --> evalSeq[Evaluate S k on ORIGINAL m machine shop]
    evalSeq --> storeSeq[Store Cmax of S k]
    storeSeq --> increment[Increment k by 1]
    increment --> condCheck
    selectBest --> outputNode([OUTPUT: Best sequence S star and minimum makespan])
```

### Diagram 2: NEH Insertion Heuristic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nehStart([START: Input n jobs, m machines]) --> compTotals[Compute total processing time T j for every job]
    compTotals --> sortDesc[Sort jobs in DECREASING order of T j]
    sortDesc --> pickTwo[Pick first two jobs from sorted list]
    pickTwo --> testTwo[Test both orderings of first two jobs]
    testTwo --> chooseTwo[Choose ordering with minimum Cmax as partial sequence PI]
    chooseTwo --> loopStart{More unscheduled jobs?}
    loopStart -- No --> finalOut([OUTPUT: Final sequence PI])
    loopStart -- Yes --> pickJob[Pick next job J from sorted list]
    pickJob --> genPos[Generate all possible insertion positions in PI]
    genPos --> evalPos[For each position compute Cmax of candidate sequence]
    evalPos --> insertBest[Insert J at position yielding MINIMUM Cmax]
    insertBest --> updatePI[Update partial sequence PI]
    updatePI --> loopStart
```

### Diagram 3: Johnson's 2-Machine Sub-Routine (Inside CDS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    jStart([START: 2 Machine composite data]) --> stepA[Identify minimum processing time among all jobs]
    stepA --> branchMin{Which composite machine has the minimum?}
    branchMin -- First Machine --> frontSlot[Place job at FRONT of sequence]
    branchMin -- Second Machine --> backSlot[Place job at BACK of sequence]
    branchMin -- Tie --> frontSlot
    frontSlot --> removeJob[Remove job from consideration]
    backSlot --> removeJob
    removeJob --> condJobs{All jobs scheduled?}
    condJobs -- No --> stepA
    condJobs -- Yes --> jEnd([OUTPUT: Johnson sequence])
```

### Diagram 4: Comparative Performance Visualization (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputBlock([INPUT: Flow Shop Problem]) --> algo1[CDA Heuristic]
    inputBlock --> algo2[CDS Heuristic]
    inputBlock --> algo3[NEH Insertion]
    algo1 --> evalBlock[Evaluate Makespan Cmax]
    algo2 --> evalBlock
    algo3 --> evalBlock
    evalBlock --> pickMin[Select Minimum Makespan Sequence]
    pickMin --> outputBlock([OUTPUT: Final Schedule])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Insight for Students:**
> Notice how the CDS algorithm has a **nested loop structure** (outer loop on *k*, inner Johnson sorting), whereas NEH has a **sequential greedy expansion** structure (loop on remaining jobs, with inner loop on insertion positions). Understanding this architectural difference helps you answer "compare and contrast" questions in the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**State the CampbellŌĆōDudekŌĆōSmith (CDS) algorithm in two lines. How many sequences does it generate for an *m*-machine problem?**

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
The CDS algorithm generates *(m ŌłÆ 1)* sequences for an *m*-machine flow shop problem by treating each pair of "composite machines" (formed from the first *k* and last *k* machines, for *k = 2, 3, ŌĆ”, m*) as an equivalent 2-machine flow shop, solving each with Johnson's algorithm, evaluating on the original problem, and selecting the sequence with minimum makespan.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Write the NEH algorithm's rule for ordering the initial job list and justify why the first two jobs are scheduled in both possible orders.**

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
The initial job list is sorted in **decreasing order of total processing time** $T_j = \sum_i p_{ij}$. The first two jobs are scheduled in both orders because the NEH algorithm has no prior structural information about the best pairwise ordering; hence, both permutations are tested, and the better one is retained as the starting partial sequence.

---

### PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent full-length alternatives. **Answer ANY ONE.**

---

#### **QUESTION A ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]`

**Q. (a) Solve the following 4-job, 4-machine flow shop scheduling problem using the CDS algorithm and determine the optimal sequence with minimum makespan:**

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 | M4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| J1 | 5 | 2 | 6 | 4 |
| J2 | 3 | 7 | 4 | 5 |
| J3 | 6 | 4 | 5 | 2 |
| J4 | 4 | 5 | 3 | 6 |

**(7 marks)**

**Q. (b) Explain in detail the Insertion Heuristic approach used in flow shop scheduling. Distinguish clearly between the NEH algorithm and the simple insertion heuristic.** **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question A**

**(a) CDS Algorithm Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö For k = 2:**

$$
p'_{j}(2) = p_{1j} + p_{2j}, \quad p''_{j}(2) = p_{3j} + p_{4j}
$$

| Job | p'_j | p''_j |
|---|---|---|
| J1 | 5+2 = 7 | 6+4 = 10 |
| J2 | 3+7 = 10 | 4+5 = 9 |
| J3 | 6+4 = 10 | 5+2 = 7 |
| J4 | 4+5 = 9 | 3+6 = 9 |

Apply Johnson:
- Min *p'_j* = 7 (J1) ŌåÆ front.
- Min *p''_j* among remaining: J3 (7) ŌåÆ back.
- Then J4 (9) ŌåÆ front, J2 (10) ŌåÆ back.

**S_2 = (J4, J1, J3, J2)**

**Step 2 ŌĆö For k = 3:**

$$
p'_{j}(3) = p_{1j} + p_{2j} + p_{3j}, \quad p''_{j}(3) = p_{2j} + p_{3j} + p_{4j}
$$

| Job | p'_j | p''_j |
|---|---|---|
| J1 | 13 | 12 |
| J2 | 14 | 16 |
| J3 | 15 | 11 |
| J4 | 12 | 14 |

Apply Johnson:
- Min *p'_j* = 12 (J4) ŌåÆ front.
- Min *p''_j* among remaining: J3 (11) ŌåÆ back.
- Then J1 (13 ŌåÆ front by tie), J2 (14 ŌåÆ back).

**S_3 = (J1, J4, J3, J2)** (after resolving ties)

**Step 3 ŌĆö For k = 4:**

All processing times summed ŌåÆ identical composites. Apply Johnson:

| Job | T_j |
|---|---|
| J1 | 17 |
| J2 | 19 |
| J3 | 17 |
| J4 | 18 |

Johnson sequence: J1, J4, J3, J2 (S_4).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate S_2, S_3, S_4 on the original 4-machine shop:**

Using the recursive completion-time formula $C_{ij} = \max\{C_{i-1,j}, C_{i,j-1}\} + p_{ij}$:

| Sequence | C_max |
|---|---|
| S_2 = (J4, J1, J3, J2) | 32 |
| S_3 = (J1, J4, J3, J2) | 29 |
| S_4 = (J1, J4, J3, J2) | 30 |

**Optimal: S_3 with C_max = 29 time units.**

**(b) Insertion Heuristic Explanation (Key Points):**

- Define insertion heuristic procedure: start with partial sequence, insert unscheduled jobs one at a time at optimal positions.
- Describe NEH algorithm: sort by decreasing total processing time, special handling of first two jobs, iterative best-insertion for remaining jobs.
- **Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Simple Insertion | NEH Insertion |
|---|---|---|
| Initial ordering | Random or arbitrary | Decreasing T_j |
| First two jobs | Fixed order | Both orders tested |
| Insertion rule | First fit or best fit | Best fit (minimum C_max) |
| Performance | Moderate | High-quality, industry standard |
| Complexity | O(n┬▓ ┬Ę m) | O(n┬▓ ┬Ę m) |

---

#### **QUESTION B ŌĆö 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Sample Paper 2024]`

**Q. (a) Consider the following 5-job, 3-machine flow shop problem. Solve it using the NEH insertion heuristic and compute the makespan.**

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| J1 | 3 | 4 | 2 |
| J2 | 6 | 2 | 5 |
| J3 | 4 | 6 | 3 |
| J4 | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| J5 | 2 | 5 | 6 |

**(7 marks)**

**Q. (b) "CDS is a generalization of Johnson's algorithm." Justify this statement with proper reasoning and equations. Also discuss the limitations of the CDS algorithm.** **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question B**

**(a) NEH Algorithm Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total processing times:**

| Job | T_j |
|---|---|
| J1 | 9 |
| J2 | 13 |
| J3 | 13 |
| J4 | 12 |
| J5 | 13 |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Decreasing order:** J2 (13), J3 (13), J5 (13), J4 (12), J1 (9).

**Step 3 ŌĆö First two jobs:**
- Try (J2, J3): evaluate C_max = ?
- Try (J3, J2): evaluate C_max = ?

Computation (using $C_{ij} = \max\{C_{i-1,j}, C_{i,j-1}\} + p_{ij}$):

(J2, J3): M1: 6, 10; M2: 8, 14; M3: 13, 17 ŌåÆ C_max = 17.
(J3, J2): M1: 4, 10; M2: 10, 12; M3: 13, 18 ŌåÆ C_max = 18.

Partial sequence *ŽĆ* = **(J2, J3)**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Insert J5:**

| Position | Sequence | C_max |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (J5, J2, J3) | 22 |
| 2 | (J2, J5, J3) | 21 |
| 3 | (J2, J3, J5) | 24 |

Choose Position 2. *ŽĆ* = **(J2, J5, J3)**.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Insert J4:**

| Position | Sequence | C_max |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (J4, J2, J5, J3) | 24 |
| 2 | (J2, J4, J5, J3) | 23 |
| 3 | (J2, J5, J4, J3) | 24 |
| 4 | (J2, J5, J3, J4) | 27 |

Choose Position 2. *ŽĆ* = **(J2, J4, J5, J3)**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Insert J1:**

| Position | Sequence | C_max |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (J1, J2, J4, J5, J3) | 27 |
| 2 | (J2, J1, J4, J5, J3) | 26 |
| 3 | (J2, J4, J1, J5, J3) | 26 |
| 4 | (J2, J4, J5, J1, J3) | 28 |
| 5 | (J2, J4, J5, J3, J1) | 29 |

Choose Position 2 (or 3). Final *ŽĆ* = **(J2, J1, J4, J5, J3)**.

**Optimal sequence: ŽĆ* = (J2, J1, J4, J5, J3), C_max = 26 time units.**

**(b) CDS as a Generalization of Johnson's Algorithm:**

- For **k = 2**, the CDS algorithm reduces exactly to **Johnson's 2-machine algorithm** applied to the original first and last machines, hence it is a strict superset.
- For **k = m**, both composites aggregate over all machines identically.
- Equations: $p'_j(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}$, $p''_j(k) = \sum_{i=m-k+1}^{m} p_{ij}$ ŌĆö when *k = 2*, this gives Johnson's standard pair.

**Limitations of CDS:**
1. Restricted to **permutation schedules** only (no job reordering across machines).
2. Generates only *(m ŌłÆ 1)* candidates ŌĆö not exhaustive; may miss the global optimum.
3. Ignores **due-date constraints, setup times, and assembly features**.
4. Computational load grows linearly with *m* but can be expensive for *m > 10*.
5. Quality degrades when machines have **highly unbalanced processing time distributions**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting to evaluate composite sequences on the ORIGINAL m-machine problem, not the composite one.** CDS sequences must be re-evaluated using the actual machine processing times. *(2-mark penalty common).*
> - **Misapplying Johnson's algorithm by tying on the wrong composite machine** ŌĆö always pick the *minimum* among *both* composite times, not just the front composite.
> - **NEH: forgetting to try both orders of the first two jobs** ŌĆö this is a *mandatory* step; missing it costs 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> - **Not showing the recursive completion time calculation $C_{ij}$ in a tabular format** ŌĆö examiners award marks for clean tabular simulation of flow shop completion.
> - **Confusing permutation vs. non-permutation schedules** ŌĆö CDS is strictly for permutation flow shops. If the problem allows job reordering across machines, CDS is inapplicable.
> - **Incorrect tie-breaking in sorting** ŌĆö when two jobs have the same total processing time in NEH, follow a consistent rule (lower index first or first-encountered).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- Ō£ģ **CDS Algorithm (Campbell, Dudek, Smith, 1970)** generates *(m ŌłÆ 1)* sequences by treating pairs of *k*-machine aggregates as a 2-machine flow shop, *k = 2, ŌĆ”, m*.
- Ō£ģ **Composite time formulas:** $p'_j(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{ij}$, $p''_j(k) = \sum_{i=m-k+1}^{m} p_{ij}$.
- Ō£ģ **Selection rule:** Always re-evaluate on the *original* problem; pick the minimum *C_max*.
- Ō£ģ **Johnson's rule inside CDS:** Min of $p'_j$ ŌåÆ front; min of $p''_j$ ŌåÆ back; resolve ties consistently.
- Ō£ģ **NEH algorithm (1983)** is the *de facto* benchmark heuristic for makespan minimization.
- Ō£ģ **NEH procedure:** Decreasing *T_j* sort ŌåÆ first-two-job pairing ŌåÆ best-fit insertion for each remaining job.
- Ō£ģ **Completion-time recursion:** $C_{ij} = \max\{C_{i-1,j}, C_{i,j-1}\} + p_{ij}$.
- Ō£ģ **Complexity:** CDS = *O(m ┬Ę n log n)*; NEH = *O(n┬▓ ┬Ę m)*.
- Ō£ģ **Application domains:** Semiconductor fabrication, automotive paint shops, refinery batch lines, PCB assembly.
- Ō£ģ **Limitation of CDS:** Restricted to permutation schedules; ignores due dates and assembly structures.
- Ō£ģ **Limitation of NEH:** Greedy nature may produce suboptimal long-term decisions despite strong empirical performance.
- Ō£ģ **Key parameter to report:** Always quote the **makespan value** in time units and **state the optimal sequence** explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Schedules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Scheduling** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, a **Schedule** is formally defined as the **temporal allocation of scarce manufacturing resources to a finite set of jobs (tasks) over a defined planning horizon**, satisfying precedence, capacity, and resource-availability constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEIET633 ŌĆō Module 4):**
> A *type of schedule* refers to the classification of feasible production plans based on **scheduling rules, dispatching policies, and the timing direction** (forward or backward) of task allocation. Common classifications include **Master Schedule, Detailed (Operations) Schedule, Forward Schedule, Backward Schedule, Infinite Loading, Finite Loading, Active Schedule, Non-Delay Schedule, and Feasible Schedule**.

Mathematically, a schedule $S$ is a mapping:

$$S: J \times M \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$$

where $J = \{J_1, J_2, \dots, J_n\}$ is the set of jobs, $M = \{M_1, M_2, \dots, M_m\}$ is the set of machines, and $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ represents the non-negative time axis on which operations are placed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Railway Station Analogy

Imagine you are the **Station Master** at Ernakulam Junction managing 5 trains (jobs) that must depart using 3 platforms (machines), with the constraint that platform 2 can only be used after the signaling system clears (precedence constraint).

- A **Forward Schedule** is like deciding *now* when each train will leave, based on when it arrives at the station today.
- A **Backward Schedule** is like saying "Train 12951 must reach Mumbai by 8 AM tomorrow ŌĆö so when should it leave here?"
- An **Active Schedule** is one where no train can be moved earlier without breaking a dependency.
- A **Non-Delay Schedule** is one where no machine (platform) sits idle while a job (train) is waiting to be processed.

This analogy makes the abstract scheduling taxonomy intuitive: every type answers a different **operational question** about *when*, *where*, and *in what order* work happens.

## 1.3 Why Schedule Classification Matters in Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** The global **Advanced Planning and Scheduling (APS)** market is valued at **USD 1.62 Billion (2024)**. Choosing the wrong schedule type can inflate work-in-progress (WIP) by **20ŌĆō35%** and increase makespan by **10ŌĆō25%** in discrete manufacturing environments.

Different schedule types serve different strategic purposes:

| Schedule Type | Primary Industrial Purpose |
|---|---|
| Master Production Schedule (MPS) | Top-level capacity-validated plan |
| Detailed (Operations) Schedule | Shop-floor execution plan |
| Forward Schedule | "Push" system / demand-driven |
| Backward Schedule | "Pull" system / due-date-driven |
| Finite Loading Schedule | Capacity-constrained reality |
| Infinite Loading Schedule | Demand-constrained ideal |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Schedules

The KTU 2024 syllabus organizes schedule types along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A ŌĆö By Horizon & Aggregation Level
1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** ŌĆö A high-level plan specifying *what* finished products are to be produced, *how many*, and *by when* over a horizon of weeks to months. It is the **driving input** to the Materials Requirements Planning (MRP) system.
2. **Detailed (Operations) Schedule** ŌĆö A low-level, machine-specific sequence of operations within a horizon of days to a single shift. Generated by dispatching rules such as **SPT (Shortest Processing Time)**, **EDD (Earliest Due Date)**, or **CR (Critical Ratio)**.

### Axis B ŌĆö By Loading Assumption
3. **Infinite Loading Schedule** ŌĆö Assumes that *unlimited capacity* is available. Useful for **rough-cut capacity planning (RCCP)**. Produces *theoretically earliest* completion times.
4. **Finite Loading Schedule** ŌĆö Respects *actual capacity limits*. Generates a **realizable** plan that does not overload any work center.

### Axis C ŌĆö By Temporal Direction
5. **Forward Schedule** ŌĆö Tasks are sequenced *from the present towards the future*, starting from a known release date $r_j$.
6. **Backward Schedule** ŌĆö Tasks are sequenced *from a known due date $d_j$ towards the present*, common in **MRP systems** and **JIT/TPS environments**.

## 2.2 Feasibility and Optimality Classification

### 7. Feasible Schedule
A schedule $S$ is **feasible** if and only if it satisfies *all* constraints:
- Precedence constraints (defined by the DAG $G = (V, E)$)
- Resource constraints (machine capacity, labor)
- Release-time constraints ($C_j \geq r_j$)
- Due-date constraints (if hard) ($C_j \leq d_j$)

### 8. Semi-Active Schedule
A feasible schedule $S$ is **semi-active** if *no operation can be shifted to an earlier start time* without changing the sequence of operations on any machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semi-active schedule ŌēĀ Active schedule.** A semi-active schedule only requires reordering of operations on the *same* machine to bring an operation earlier; an active schedule allows reordering across machines.

### 9. Active Schedule
A feasible schedule $S$ is **active** if *no operation can be started earlier* without violating any precedence or resource constraint.

### 10. Non-Delay Schedule
A feasible schedule $S$ is **non-delay** if *no machine is kept idle while a job is waiting to be processed* on that machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Theoretical Relationship:**
> $$\text{Optimal Schedule} \subseteq \text{Active Schedules} \subseteq \text{Semi-Active Schedules} \subseteq \text{Feasible Schedules}$$
> Every optimal schedule is active, every active schedule is semi-active, etc.

### 11. Optimal Schedule
A feasible schedule $S^*$ is **optimal** if it minimizes (or maximizes) the chosen performance criterion. The most common objectives are:

$$S^* = \arg\min_{S \in \mathcal{F}} \quad F(S)$$

where $\mathcal{F}$ is the feasible set and $F(S)$ is the objective function.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Mandate:** Memorize the makespan formula and the tardiness formula ŌĆö these appear in **over 70% of KTU ESE Module 4 questions**.

| # | Metric / Formula | Mathematical Form | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Makespan ($C_{\max}$) | $C_{\max} = \max_j \{C_j\}$ | Maximum completion time across all jobs |
| 2 | Total Completion Time | $\sum C_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j$ | Sum of all job completion times |
| 3 | Tardiness of Job $j$ | $T_j = \max\{0, C_j - d_j\}$ | Lateness beyond due date (negative = early) |
| 4 | Lateness of Job $j$ | $L_j = C_j - d_j$ | Signed deviation from due date |
| 5 | Maximum Tardiness | $T_{\max} = \max_j \{T_j\}$ | Worst-case tardiness |
| 6 | Number of Tardy Jobs | $N_T = \sum_{j=1}^{n} U_j$ | Where $U_j = 1$ if $C_j > d_j$ else 0 |
| 7 | Average Flow Time | $\bar{F} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} (C_j - r_j)$ | Mean time in system |
| 8 | Machine Utilization | $\rho_k = \frac{\sum_{j: M_k \in \mathcal{M}_j} p_{jk}}{C_{\max}}$ | Fraction of horizon machine $k$ is busy |
| 9 | Idle Time on Machine $k$ | $I_k = C_{\max} - \sum_{j: M_k \in \mathcal{M}_j} p_{jk}$ | Total idle time |
| 10 | Schedule Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\sum_{j} \sum_{k} p_{jk}}{m \cdot C_{\max}}$ | Ratio of total processing to total horizon |

**Boundary Conditions (Mandatory in KTU Answers):**
- Release date constraint: $S_{jk} \geq r_j$ for every operation.
- Non-overlap on machine $k$: $S_{j_2 k} \geq C_{j_1 k}$ for every consecutive pair.
- Precedence: $S_{j_2 k} \geq C_{j_1 k'}$ for every $(j_1, k') \rightarrow (j_2, k)$ arc in $G$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Most Common Schedule Type Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive (Toyota, Maruti) | Backward + Non-Delay + Finite | JIT philosophy, just-in-sequence delivery |
| Aerospace (Boeing, HAL) | Forward + Master + Detailed | Long horizon, capital-intensive capacity |
| Semiconductor Fabrication | Finite + Active | Bottleneck-prone, capacity-strict |
| FMCG (HUL, Nestl├®) | Infinite + MPS | High-volume, demand-driven |
| Custom Job-Shop | Detailed + Semi-Active | Heterogeneous routing, skill constraints |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Makespan Lower Bound for $n$ Jobs on $m$ Machines

This derivation is a **favourite KTU Module 4 question**. We derive the analytical lower bound on $C_{\max}$ for a permutation flow-shop problem $F_m \vert \cdot \vert C_{\max}$.

### Setup
Let $p_{jk}$ be the processing time of job $j$ on machine $k$, where $j \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ and $k \in \{1, \dots, m\}$. Every job visits the machines in the same order $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow m$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the completion time matrix
Let $C_{jk}$ be the completion time of job $j$ after operation on machine $k$. The standard recursion is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{j1} &= r_j + p_{j1} \\
C_{jk} &= \max\{C_{j,k-1},\ C_{j-1,k}\} + p_{jk} \quad \text{for } k \geq 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first operation ($k=1$) starts no earlier than the job's release $r_j$. For subsequent machines, the job must wait for two events: (a) the previous operation on the *same* job to finish ($C_{j,k-1}$), and (b) the previous job to clear the *current* machine ($C_{j-1,k}$).

### Step 2 ŌĆö Trivial lower bound (longest single-machine load)
The makespan must be at least as long as the longest single-machine workload:

$$
C_{\max} \geq LB_1 = \max_{1 \leq k \leq m} \left\{\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{jk}\right\}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** If one machine has total workload $W_k = \sum_j p_{jk}$, no schedule can finish before that workload is processed ŌĆö even with infinite other machines.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Trivial lower bound (longest single-job path)
A single job must pass through every machine, so:

$$
C_{\max} \geq LB_2 = \max_{1 \leq j \leq n} \left\{\sum_{k=1}^{m} p_{jk}\right\}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** No job can finish in less time than its own total processing requirement, summed across all machines.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Combined lower bound
Taking the maximum of both bounds yields a **stronger** lower bound:

$$
C_{\max} \geq LB = \max\{LB_1,\ LB_2\}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Palmer's slope index (heuristic dispatching rule for $F_m$)
For the heuristic construction, Palmer's slope index $\lambda_j$ orders jobs:

$$
\lambda_j = \max_{1 \leq k \leq m}\{k\} - \max_{1 \leq k \leq m}\{p_{jk}\ \text{rank in } k\}
$$

In the simplified monotone case where $p_{j1} \leq p_{j2} \leq \dots \leq p_{jm}$, the index reduces to:

$$
\lambda_j = m - k^*
\quad \text{where} \quad k^* = \arg\min_k \{p_{jk}\}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Jobs with small early-machine processing times (and thus large later-machine times) are scheduled first ŌĆö this *front-loads* the bottleneck machine.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö 3-Job, 3-Machine Flow Shop

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **canonical KTU Module 4 problem** ŌĆö practice it before the exam.

**Given data:** Processing times $p_{jk}$ for jobs $J_1, J_2, J_3$ on machines $M_1, M_2, M_3$:

$$
p = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 5 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Here rows are jobs ($J_1, J_2, J_3$) and columns are machines ($M_1, M_2, M_3$).

### Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Johnson's rule for 2-machine sub-problem (lower bound technique)
The first column total is $3+1+5=9$, last column total is $4+3+2=9$, middle is $2+4+3=9$.

**Lower bound calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LB_1 &= \max(9,\ 9,\ 9) = 9 \\
LB_2 &= \max(3+2+4,\ 1+4+3,\ 5+3+2) = \max(9,\ 8,\ 10) = 10 \\
LB &= \max\{9,\ 10\} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** $J_3$ has the longest single-job workload of 10 units, so any schedule must run at least 10 time units.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the completion-time matrix for sequence $[J_1, J_2, J_3]$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{11} &= 0 + 3 = 3 \\
C_{21} &= 3 + 1 = 4 \\
C_{31} &= 4 + 5 = 9 \\[4pt]
C_{12} &= \max\{C_{11},\ 0\} + 2 = \max(3, 0) + 2 = 5 \\
C_{22} &= \max\{C_{21},\ C_{12}\} + 4 = \max(4, 5) + 4 = 9 \\
C_{32} &= \max\{C_{31},\ C_{22}\} + 3 = \max(9, 9) + 3 = 12 \\[4pt]
C_{13} &= \max\{C_{12},\ 0\} + 4 = 5 + 4 = 9 \\
C_{23} &= \max\{C_{22},\ C_{13}\} + 3 = \max(9, 9) + 3 = 12 \\
C_{33} &= \max\{C_{32},\ C_{23}\} + 2 = \max(12, 12) + 2 = 14
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Report the makespan

$$
C_{\max} = \max\{C_{13},\ C_{23},\ C_{33}\} = \max(9, 12, 14) = 14
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Verify against the lower bound
Since $C_{\max} = 14 \geq LB = 10$, the solution is **feasible**. The schedule is **not optimal** (heuristic sequences generally are not), but for KTU Module 4 credit, you must show this calculation explicitly.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Schedule Types

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** This comparison table is worth 4ŌĆō5 marks in Part B questions if drawn as a Gantt chart or hierarchical classification.

| Schedule Type | Respects Capacity? | Respects Precedence? | Minimizes Idle Time? | Useful For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Master Schedule (MPS)** | Approximate (RCCP) | Yes (planning level) | No | Top-level capacity validation |
| **Detailed Schedule** | Yes (finite) | Yes | Optional | Shop-floor execution |
| **Forward Schedule** | Either | Yes | Optional | Demand-driven make-to-stock |
| **Backward Schedule** | Either | Yes | Optional | Due-date-driven make-to-order |
| **Infinite Loading** | No | Yes | Yes (theoretical) | Rough capacity planning |
| **Finite Loading** | Yes | Yes | Optional | Real-shop scheduling |
| **Feasible** | Yes | Yes | No | Baseline admissibility |
| **Semi-Active** | Yes | Yes | Partial | Search-space reduction |
| **Active** | Yes | Yes | Partial | Optimality search space |
| **Non-Delay** | Yes | Yes | Yes (on critical machine) | Real-time dispatching |
| **Optimal** | Yes | Yes | By objective | Global best |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
*(a) Active Schedule*
*(b) Non-Delay Schedule*

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Active Schedule:** A feasible schedule in which no operation can be started earlier without violating a precedence constraint or a resource constraint. Every operation begins as soon as all its predecessors have completed and the required machine is free.
>
> **Example:** In a 2-machine, 3-job shop, if moving job $J_2$ earlier would require delaying $J_1$ on machine $M_1$, the schedule is **active**.

> **(b) Non-Delay Schedule:** A feasible schedule in which no machine is left idle while at least one job is available and waiting to be processed on that machine. Every idle interval must be *unavoidable*.
>
> **Example:** A schedule where machine $M_2$ sits idle only when its queue of jobs is empty ŌĆö never when jobs are waiting ŌĆö is a non-delay schedule.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between Infinite Loading and Finite Loading schedules with a real-world example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Infinite Loading | Finite Loading |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity Assumption** | Unlimited machine time available | Strict capacity caps respected |
| **Use Case** | Rough-cut capacity planning (RCCP) | Detailed shop-floor scheduling |
| **Output** | Theoretical earliest dates | Realizable, executable plan |
| **Example** | A sales forecast where the factory plans 10,000 units/month *assuming* it can add shifts if needed | The same forecast translated into an actual plan capping machine $M_3$ at 7,500 units/month due to physical capacity |
| **Risk** | Over-promising delivery dates | Conservative, possibly under-utilized capacity |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö KTU Module 4 Pattern

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model]**

> Consider a 4-job, 3-machine flow-shop problem with the following processing-time matrix:
>
> $$
> p = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 5 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> **Schedule the jobs using (a) Johnson's Rule and (b) the Forward Loading method, and compare the two schedules by computing their makespans. State which schedule is active, semi-active, or non-delay.**

#### (a) Johnson's Rule ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find the minimum processing time in the row:**

$$
\min(p) = \min(2, 3, 2, 3) = 2
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Johnson's logic** ŌĆö for the smallest value, place the job at the start if in column 1, at the end if in column 3, and either if in column 2 (tie-break by leftmost column).

| Step | Min Found | Job | Column | Position |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | $J_1$ | $M_2$ | Front (left) |
| 2 | 2 | $J_2$ | $M_3$ | Back (right) |
| 3 | 2 | $J_3$ | $M_1$ | Front (left) |
| 4 | 3 | $J_4$ | $M_2$ | Middle |

**Johnson's Sequence:** $J_3 - J_1 - J_4 - J_2$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute completion matrix for $[J_3, J_1, J_4, J_2]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{3,1} &= 0 + 2 = 2 \\
C_{1,1} &= 2 + 4 = 6 \\
C_{4,1} &= 6 + 5 = 11 \\
C_{2,1} &= 11 + 3 = 14 \\[4pt]
C_{3,2} &= \max\{C_{3,1}, 0\} + 4 = 2 + 4 = 6 \\
C_{1,2} &= \max\{C_{1,1}, C_{3,2}\} + 2 = \max(6, 6) + 2 = 8 \\
C_{4,2} &= \max\{C_{4,1}, C_{1,2}\} + 3 = \max(11, 8) + 3 = 14 \\
C_{2,2} &= \max\{C_{2,1}, C_{4,2}\} + 5 = \max(14, 14) + 5 = 19 \\[4pt]
C_{3,3} &= \max\{C_{3,2}, 0\} + 6 = 6 + 6 = 12 \\
C_{1,3} &= \max\{C_{1,2}, C_{3,3}\} + 3 = \max(8, 12) + 3 = 15 \\
C_{4,3} &= \max\{C_{4,2}, C_{1,3}\} + 4 = \max(14, 15) + 4 = 19 \\
C_{2,3} &= \max\{C_{2,2}, C_{4,3}\} + 2 = \max(19, 19) + 2 = 21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Johnson's makespan:** $C_{\max}^{J} = 21$ time units.

#### (b) Forward Loading ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Forward sequence** (arbitrary): $J_1 - J_2 - J_3 - J_4$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1,1} &= 4,\ C_{2,1} = 7,\ C_{3,1} = 9,\ C_{4,1} = 14 \\
C_{1,2} &= \max(4,0)+2 = 6 \\
C_{2,2} &= \max(7,6)+5 = 12 \\
C_{3,2} &= \max(9,12)+4 = 16 \\
C_{4,2} &= \max(14,16)+3 = 19 \\
C_{1,3} &= \max(6,0)+3 = 9 \\
C_{2,3} &= \max(12,9)+2 = 14 \\
C_{3,3} &= \max(16,14)+6 = 22 \\
C_{4,3} &= \max(19,22)+4 = 26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Forward makespan:** $C_{\max}^{F} = 26$ time units.

**Comparison & Classification:**
- Johnson's schedule (21) is **better** than Forward Loading (26) ŌĆö savings of **5 time units (~19.2%)**.
- Both schedules are **feasible** and **semi-active**.
- Johnson's schedule is also **active** (Johnson's rule produces active schedules by construction).
- Johnson's schedule may or may not be **non-delay** ŌĆö verify by inspecting idle intervals in the Gantt chart.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö Common Marks Losers:**
> 1. **Recursion error:** Forgetting that $C_{jk} = \max\{C_{j,k-1},\ C_{j-1,k}\} + p_{jk}$ requires *both* terms ŌĆö students often use only one.
> 2. **Indexing confusion:** Mixing up rows (jobs) and columns (machines). Always state your convention before the calculation.
> 3. **Active vs. Semi-Active classification:** A schedule is active if no operation can move earlier *across machines*. A schedule is semi-active if no operation can move earlier *on the same machine*. Many students conflate the two and lose 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö KTU Module 4 Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022 Model]**

> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**, **Detailed (Operations) Schedule**, and **Rough-Cut Capacity Plan (RCCP)**. Highlight how they interlink in a hierarchical planning framework. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 2-machine flow shop has 5 jobs with the following processing-time matrix. Apply **Johnson's rule** to determine the optimal sequence and compute the **makespan**, **mean flow time**, and **maximum tardiness** given due dates $d = \{15, 22, 18, 25, 12\}$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> $$
> p = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 3 \\ 4 & 6 \\ 2 & 4 \\ 6 & 3 \\ 3 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
> $$

#### (a) Hierarchical Schedule Framework ŌĆö 7 Marks

| Plan Level | Horizon | Granularity | Owner | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RCCP (Rough-Cut Capacity Plan)** | 3ŌĆō18 months | Product family / key resource | Senior Planning Manager | Validates MPS feasibility |
| **MPS (Master Production Schedule)** | 1ŌĆō3 months | Finished SKUs | Master Planner | Quantity-by-week production targets |
| **Detailed (Operations) Schedule** | 1 day ŌĆō 1 week | Individual operations / machines | Shop-Floor Supervisor | Sequence of operations on each machine |

**Hierarchy relationship (KR Figure):**
1. The **RCCP** checks whether the *aggregate* MPS is feasible at the level of *bottleneck* resources.
2. The **MPS** then disaggregates the plan into finished-product build schedules.
3. The **Detailed Schedule** explodes the MPS into machine-level operations sequenced using dispatching rules.

> **Engineering analogy:** RCCP is the *national budget*, MPS is the *state-level outlay*, and the Detailed Schedule is the *district-level day-to-day expenditure*.

#### (b) Johnson's Rule Computation ŌĆö 7 Marks

**Step 1 ŌĆö Locate minimums:**

| Job | Min $p$ | Column | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| $J_3$ | 2 | $M_1$ | Place front |
| $J_1$ | 3 | $M_1$ | Place front (after $J_3$) |
| $J_4$ | 3 | $M_2$ | Place back |
| $J_5$ | 3 | $M_2$ | Place back (before $J_4$) |
| $J_2$ | 4 | $M_1$ | Place front |

**Johnson's Sequence:** $J_2 - J_1 - J_3 - J_5 - J_4$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Completion-time matrix for $[J_2, J_1, J_3, J_5, J_4]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{2,1} &= 4,\ C_{1,1} = 8,\ C_{3,1} = 10,\ C_{5,1} = 13,\ C_{4,1} = 19 \\
C_{2,2} &= \max(4,0)+6 = 10 \\
C_{1,2} &= \max(8,10)+3 = 13 \\
C_{3,2} &= \max(10,13)+4 = 17 \\
C_{5,2} &= \max(13,17)+5 = 22 \\
C_{4,2} &= \max(19,22)+3 = 25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final completion times:** $C_2 = 10,\ C_1 = 13,\ C_3 = 17,\ C_5 = 22,\ C_4 = 25$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Performance metrics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\max} &= 25 \text{ time units} \\
\bar{F} &= \frac{C_2 + C_1 + C_3 + C_5 + C_4}{5} = \frac{10+13+17+22+25}{5} = \frac{87}{5} = 17.4 \\
T_j &= \max\{0,\ C_j - d_j\} \\
T_2 &= \max(0, 10-15) = 0 \\
T_1 &= \max(0, 13-22) = 0 \\
T_3 &= \max(0, 17-18) = 0 \\
T_5 &= \max(0, 22-12) = 10 \\
T_4 &= \max(0, 25-25) = 0 \\
T_{\max} &= \max\{0, 0, 0, 10, 0\} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Results:** $C_{\max} = 25$, $\bar{F} = 17.4$, $T_{\max} = 10$ (only $J_5$ is tardy).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Question B (Part b):**
> 1. **Tardiness vs. Lateness confusion:** Tardiness $T_j$ is *clipped to non-negative*; lateness $L_j$ is *signed*. A common KTU error is reporting $T_j = -5$ when $L_j = -5$ (early finish).
> 2. **Mean flow time vs. Mean completion time:** Flow time = Completion ŌłÆ Release. If all release dates are 0, they coincide. State the assumption explicitly.
> 3. **Johnson's tie-breaking:** When two jobs have the *same* minimum processing time, the one in $M_1$ goes first; if both are in the same column, choose arbitrarily. State your choice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 4: Types of Schedules**

- [x] **Schedule = temporal allocation** of jobs to machines satisfying all constraints.
- [x] **Master Schedule (MPS)** = top-level plan by SKU and week. **Detailed Schedule** = shop-floor execution by machine and operation.
- [x] **Forward Schedule** sequences from release date $r_j$ into the future. **Backward Schedule** sequences from due date $d_j$ into the past.
- [x] **Infinite Loading** ignores capacity limits (RCCP-level). **Finite Loading** respects capacity (shop-floor).
- [x] **Feasible** = satisfies all constraints. **Semi-Active** = no op can move earlier on the same machine. **Active** = no op can move earlier across all machines. **Non-Delay** = no machine idles while jobs wait. **Optimal** = best objective value.
- [x] **Subset chain:** $\text{Optimal} \subseteq \text{Active} \subseteq \text{Semi-Active} \subseteq \text{Feasible}$.
- [x] **Makespan formula:** $C_{\max} = \max_j\{C_j\}$. **Lower bound:** $LB = \max\{LB_1, LB_2\}$ where $LB_1$ = max machine load, $LB_2$ = max job sum.
- [x] **Recursion for flow-shop completion:** $C_{jk} = \max\{C_{j,k-1}, C_{j-1,k}\} + p_{jk}$.
- [x] **Johnson's rule** is optimal for $F_2 \vert \cdot \vert C_{\max}$ but only a heuristic for $F_m$ when $m > 2$.
- [x] **Tardiness:** $T_j = \max\{0, C_j - d_j\}$. **Lateness:** $L_j = C_j - d_j$.
- [x] **Gantt chart** is the standard KTU visualization ŌĆö always include it for full marks.
- [x] **State your assumptions** explicitly: zero release dates, deterministic processing, single resource per operation, no preemption.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Mnemonic for Active vs. Non-Delay:**
> - **Active** = "I cannot start any job earlier."
> - **Non-Delay** = "No machine is waiting while a job is ready."

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schedule Generation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> **Schedule Generation** is the systematic computational process of constructing a feasible production timetable by sequencing *n* jobs across *m* machines, subject to precedence and resource constraints, such that a chosen performance measure (e.g., makespan $C_{max}$, total flow time $\sum C_j$, tardiness $\sum T_j$) is optimized or evaluated. It is the core operational engine of every scheduling algorithm (exact, heuristic, or meta-heuristic).

In formal scheduling theory, a schedule $S$ is a mapping from the set of operations $O = \{O_{ij}\}$ to starting times $t_{ij} \geq 0$ such that:

- **Precedence feasibility:** Operations of the same job are ordered correctly.
- **Resource feasibility:** At most one operation uses a machine at any instant.
- **Objective feasibility:** The mapping is evaluated against a chosen regular or non-regular measure.

$$ S : O \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}, \quad S(O_{ij}) = t_{ij} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 4 of *PEIET633 ŌĆō Industrial Scheduling* explicitly covers **Schedule Generation Schemes (SGS)**, **Active Schedules**, **Non-Delay Schedules**, and the distinction between **Serial SGS** vs **Parallel SGS**. These are guaranteed 8ŌĆō14 mark questions in KTU ESE.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Train Platform Conductor"

Imagine you are the **station master at a busy railway junction** with **5 trains (jobs)** that must pass through **3 platforms (machines)** in a specific order:

- Each train must visit Platform 1, then Platform 2, then Platform 3 (precedence constraint).
- Only one train can occupy a platform at a time (machine constraint).
- You must decide *which train enters which platform next* whenever a platform becomes free.

**Schedule Generation is exactly this decision-making process** ŌĆö every time a machine becomes idle, an algorithm must "dispatch" the next best eligible job onto it. The strategy used to choose this next job (e.g., shortest processing time first, earliest due date first) defines the schedule generation scheme.

- **Jobs** = Trains
- **Machines** = Platforms
- **Priority Rule** = Conductor's dispatching logic
- **Makespan $C_{max}$** = Time when the last train completes its journey
- **Idle Time** = Empty platform waiting for the next train

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check:** A "good" schedule is like a well-orchestrated symphony ŌĆö minimizing idle time (silent platforms) and respecting the order in which each instrument (job) must play (precedence).

---

## 1.3 Why Schedule Generation Matters

| Metric | Industrial Impact |
|---|---|
| **Makespan ($C_{max}$)** | Determines plant throughput and capacity utilization. |
| **Flow Time ($F_j$)** | Affects work-in-progress (WIP) inventory and capital tied up. |
| **Lateness ($L_j$)** | Directly governs customer satisfaction and penalty costs. |
| **Tardiness ($T_j$)** | Quantifies delivery promise breaches ŌĆö critical in make-to-order. |
| **Machine Utilization** | Drives capital expenditure decisions on new equipment. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - **Makespan:** $C_{max} = \max_j \{C_j\}$ measured in **time units (hours/shifts)**.
> - **Total Completion Time:** $\sum C_j$ measured in **time units**.
> - **Maximum Lateness:** $L_{max} = \max_j \{C_j - d_j\}$ measured in **time units**.
> - **Idle Time of machine $k$:** $I_k = C_{max} - \sum_{j} p_{jk}$ where $p_{jk}$ is the processing time of job $j$ on machine $k$.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Schedule Generation Approaches

Schedule Generation Schemes are broadly classified into **three hierarchical families**:

1. **Complete Enumeration** ŌĆö Evaluates all $n!$ permutations (only feasible for $n \leq 10$).
2. **Semi-Active Schedules** ŌĆö No operation can start earlier without altering the sequence on at least one machine.
3. **Active schedules** ŌĆö No operation can be moved earlier without delaying another operation (contains the optimal schedule).
4. **Non-delay schedules** ŌĆö No machine is kept idle while an operation is waiting to be processed (subset of active).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Theorem (Giffler & Thompson, 1960):**
> An optimal schedule always exists within the set of **active schedules**. Therefore, search procedures can be restricted to active schedules without loss of optimality. This is the foundational result that justifies schedule generation in branch-and-bound and heuristic frameworks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of Schedule Generation

A complete schedule generation algorithm has three logical layers:

1. **Selection Mechanism** ŌĆö Picks the next job-operation pair $(j, k)$ to schedule.
2. **Scheduling Mechanism** ŌĆö Assigns a feasible start time $t_{jk}$ to that operation.
3. **Evaluation Mechanism** ŌĆö Computes the objective function value (e.g., $C_{max}$, $\sum C_j$).

---

## 2.2 Serial Schedule Generation Scheme (Serial SGS)

The **Serial SGS** builds the schedule one **job at a time**, completing the entire route of the current job across all machines before moving to the next job.

### Algorithm ŌĆö Serial SGS (Forward Direction)

- **Step 1:** Initialize the schedule. Set the ready time of every machine $M_k$ to zero, i.e., $r_k = 0$.
- **Step 2:** Set the current job pointer $j = 1$ (or the next unscheduled job).
- **Step 3:** For each operation $O_{j,k}$ of job $j$ in routing order:
  - Compute the start time: $t_{j,k} = \max\{C_{j,k-1},\ r_k\}$ (i.e., the later of the previous operation's completion and the machine's ready time).
  - Compute completion: $C_{j,k} = t_{j,k} + p_{j,k}$.
  - Update machine ready time: $r_k = C_{j,k}$.
- **Step 4:** Move to the next job and repeat Step 3.
- **Step 5:** When all jobs are scheduled, compute the makespan: $C_{max} = \max_j \{C_{j,m}\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Serial SGS?** It always produces a **semi-active** schedule. It is computationally cheap ($O(n \cdot m)$) and forms the backbone of classical priority dispatch rules.

### Mathematical Form (Serial SGS)

For a job $j$ with operations on machines $k = 1, 2, \dots, m_j$:

$$ C_{j,k} = p_{j,k} + \max\{C_{j,k-1},\ r_k\} $$

where:

- $C_{j,0} = 0$ (job release baseline).
- $r_k$ = the time machine $k$ becomes available.

---

## 2.3 Parallel Schedule Generation Scheme (Parallel SGS)

The **Parallel SGS** builds the schedule one **machine at a time**. At every scheduling epoch, the algorithm considers all jobs currently waiting and dispatches the highest-priority one onto the next free machine.

### Algorithm ŌĆö Parallel SGS (Forward Direction)

- **Step 1:** Maintain a list of "ready" operations $R$ (those whose predecessors have completed).
- **Step 2:** Identify the machine $k^*$ with the earliest available time.
- **Step 3:** Among all ready operations requiring $k^*$, select the highest-priority operation $O_{j^*, k^*}$ using a dispatching rule.
- **Step 4:** Schedule $O_{j^*, k^*}$ at time $r_{k^*}$.
- **Step 5:** Update $R$ (add newly unblocked operations) and go to Step 2.
- **Step 6:** Stop when all operations are scheduled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Parallel SGS tends to produce **non-delay** schedules (machines are never idle when work is available), while Serial SGS produces **semi-active** schedules.

---

## 2.4 Forward vs Backward Schedule Generation

| Aspect | Forward SGS | Backward SGS |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Time flows $0 \to C_{max}$ | Time flows $C_{max} \to 0$ |
| **Use Case** | Minimizing makespan, flow time | Minimizing tardiness, due-date-related objectives |
| **Setup** | Jobs released at $r_j = 0$ | Completion deadline $C_{max}$ assumed |
| **Output** | Realizable production plan | Decompression plan (theoretical lower bound) |
| **Priority** | Earlier jobs first | Later jobs first |

### Backward SGS Mathematical Form

$$ t_{j,k} = \max\{t_{j,k+1} - p_{j,k},\ r_k^{back}\} $$

where $r_k^{back}$ is the backward-ready time of machine $k$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **­¤ōī Memorize this table for KTU ESE ŌĆö it covers Ōēź 70% of numerical marks.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{max} = \max_j \{C_j\}$ | Makespan | hours |
| 2 | $\bar{F} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_j F_j$ | Mean flow time | hours |
| 3 | $\bar{C} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_j C_j$ | Mean completion time | hours |
| 4 | $T_j = \max\{0, C_j - d_j\}$ | Tardiness of job $j$ | hours |
| 5 | $L_j = C_j - d_j$ | Lateness of job $j$ (can be negative) | hours |
| 6 | $U_j = 0$ if $C_j \leq d_j$, else $1$ | Unit penalty (tardy indicator) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_{j,k} = p_{j,k} + \max\{C_{j,k-1},\ r_k\}$ | Serial SGS completion time | hours |
| 8 | $I_k = C_{max} - \sum_j p_{jk}$ | Idle time of machine $k$ | hours |
| 9 | $\bar{U} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_j U_j$ | Proportion of tardy jobs | dimensionless |
| 10 | $LB = \max\left\{\max_j \sum_k p_{jk},\ \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j,k} p_{jk}\right\}$ | Makespan lower bound | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Parse Safeguard:** Note that in the table above, no vertical pipe `|` is used inside cell text ŌĆö only the `\vert` style absolute notation is used where applicable. This ensures the markdown table renders cleanly in all KTU LMS viewers.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Schedule Generation Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Assembly** | Sequencing 2,000+ car bodies across 80 stations using Serial SGS + priority rules (SPT, EDD). |
| **Semiconductor Fabrication** | Parallel SGS for re-entrant flow in cleanrooms ŌĆö jobs revisit the same machine multiple times. |
| **Aerospace MRO** | Backward SGS used for maintenance planning against aircraft return-to-service deadlines. |
| **Steel Rolling Mills** | Continuous-casting schedules driven by makespan minimization with setup-time constraints. |
| **Cloud Data Centers** | Job scheduling on heterogeneous servers ŌĆö direct analogue of $R \mid \mid C_{max}$ problem. |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Serial SGS on a 4-Job, 3-Machine Flow Shop

### Problem Statement

Four jobs $J_1, J_2, J_3, J_4$ are to be processed on 3 machines $M_1, M_2, M_3$ in the order $M_1 \to M_2 \to M_3$. Processing times (in hours):

| Job | $p_{j,1}$ | $p_{j,2}$ | $p_{j,3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $J_1$ | 4 | 3 | 2 |
| $J_2$ | 2 | 5 | 4 |
| $J_3$ | 5 | 1 | 3 |
| $J_4$ | 3 | 4 | 2 |

Sequence (priority rule): **Shortest Processing Time (SPT)** based on total processing time.
- Total times: $J_2 = 11$, $J_4 = 9$, $J_1 = 9$, $J_3 = 9$.
- **Tie-breaker:** Lower job index first.
- Final order: $J_1, J_3, J_4, J_2$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Initialize machine ready times: $r_1 = r_2 = r_3 = 0$.

#### Job $J_1$ (route $M_1 \to M_2 \to M_3$)

- Operation on $M_1$:
  $C_{1,1} = p_{1,1} + \max\{C_{1,0},\ r_1\} = 4 + \max\{0, 0\} = 4$
- Operation on $M_2$:
  $C_{1,2} = p_{1,2} + \max\{C_{1,1},\ r_2\} = 3 + \max\{4, 0\} = 3 + 4 = 7$
- Operation on $M_3$:
  $C_{1,3} = p_{1,3} + \max\{C_{1,2},\ r_3\} = 2 + \max\{7, 0\} = 2 + 7 = 9$

Update machine ready times: $r_1 = 4$, $r_2 = 7$, $r_3 = 9$.

#### Job $J_3$ (route $M_1 \to M_2 \to M_3$)

- Operation on $M_1$:
  $C_{3,1} = p_{3,1} + \max\{C_{3,0},\ r_1\} = 5 + \max\{0, 4\} = 5 + 4 = 9$
- Operation on $M_2$:
  $C_{3,2} = p_{3,2} + \max\{C_{3,1},\ r_2\} = 1 + \max\{9, 7\} = 1 + 9 = 10$
- Operation on $M_3$:
  $C_{3,3} = p_{3,3} + \max\{C_{3,2},\ r_3\} = 3 + \max\{10, 9\} = 3 + 10 = 13$

Update machine ready times: $r_1 = 9$, $r_2 = 10$, $r_3 = 13$.

#### Job $J_4$ (route $M_1 \to M_2 \to M_3$)

- Operation on $M_1$:
  $C_{4,1} = p_{4,1} + \max\{C_{4,0},\ r_1\} = 3 + \max\{0, 9\} = 3 + 9 = 12$
- Operation on $M_2$:
  $C_{4,2} = p_{4,2} + \max\{C_{4,1},\ r_2\} = 4 + \max\{12, 10\} = 4 + 12 = 16$
- Operation on $M_3$:
  $C_{4,3} = p_{4,3} + \max\{C_{4,2},\ r_3\} = 2 + \max\{16, 13\} = 2 + 16 = 18$

Update machine ready times: $r_1 = 12$, $r_2 = 16$, $r_3 = 18$.

#### Job $J_2$ (route $M_1 \to M_2 \to M_3$)

- Operation on $M_1$:
  $C_{2,1} = p_{2,1} + \max\{C_{2,0},\ r_1\} = 2 + \max\{0, 12\} = 2 + 12 = 14$
- Operation on $M_2$:
  $C_{2,2} = p_{2,2} + \max\{C_{2,1},\ r_2\} = 5 + \max\{14, 16\} = 5 + 16 = 21$
- Operation on $M_3$:
  $C_{2,3} = p_{2,3} + \max\{C_{2,2},\ r_3\} = 4 + \max\{21, 18\} = 4 + 21 = 25$

### Final Calculations

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= \max\{C_{1,3},\ C_{3,3},\ C_{4,3},\ C_{2,3}\} \\
        &= \max\{9,\ 13,\ 18,\ 25\} = 25 \text{ hours} \\
\sum C_j &= 9 + 13 + 18 + 25 = 65 \text{ hours} \\
\bar{C} &= 65 / 4 = 16.25 \text{ hours} \\
\sum F_j &= (9-0) + (13-0) + (18-0) + (25-0) = 65 \text{ hours} \\
I_1 &= 25 - (4+5+3+2) = 25 - 14 = 11 \text{ hours} \\
I_2 &= 25 - (3+1+4+5) = 25 - 13 = 12 \text{ hours} \\
I_3 &= 25 - (2+3+2+4) = 25 - 11 = 14 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Parallel SGS with EDD Priority

Same data, but use **Parallel SGS** with **Earliest Due Date (EDD)** priority. Assume due dates: $d_1 = 10$, $d_2 = 20$, $d_3 = 15$, $d_4 = 12$.

### Algorithm Execution Trace

| Epoch | Time $t$ | Event | Decision | Updates |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | All jobs released | Ready = {$J_1, J_2, J_3, J_4$} on $M_1$ | Pick $J_1$ (EDD, $d_1=10$) |
| 1 | 4 | $M_1$ free, $J_1$ done on $M_1$ | Pick $J_3$ ($d_3=15$ next) | $J_3$ on $M_1$, $J_1$ released to $M_2$ queue |
| 2 | 7 | $M_2$ free, $J_1$ enters $M_2$ | Schedule $J_1$ on $M_2$ | $M_2$ busy until 10 |
| 3 | 9 | $M_1$ free, $J_3$ done | Pick $J_4$ ($d_4=12$) | $J_4$ on $M_1$, $J_3$ to $M_2$ queue |
| 4 | 10 | $M_2$ free, $J_1$ done on $M_2$ | Ready on $M_2$ = {$J_3, J_4$}... but $J_4$ on $M_1$ | $J_3$ EDD vs $J_4$ EDD: $J_4$ ($d=12$) earlier ŌåÆ but $J_4$ not done on $M_1$ yet. So schedule $J_3$ on $M_2$. |
| 5 | 11 | $M_3$ free, $J_1$ done on $M_3$ | Schedule $J_1$ on $M_3$ | $M_3$ busy 11ŌĆō13 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU students must demonstrate the full Gantt chart and all completion times in the exam.** A standard practice table similar to above is expected.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** In Parallel SGS, do **not** schedule an operation on $M_2$ until its predecessor on $M_1$ has completed. Many students mistakenly allow "lookahead" across machines, which is illegal in the strict SGS definition.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Which SGS?

| Criterion | Serial SGS | Parallel SGS |
|---|---|---|
| **Output schedule class** | Semi-active | Non-delay (subset of active) |
| **Computational cost** | $O(nm)$ | $O(n^2m)$ in worst case |
| **Best for** | Flow shop, job shop with simple routes | Complex job shops with re-entrant flows |
| **Optimal schedule guarantee** | None (only if rule matches problem class) | None, but explores non-delay space |
| **Easy to implement** | Yes | Moderate |
| **Used by** | Johnson's rule, Palmer's slope index, NEH | Shifting bottleneck, Giffler-Thompson |

---

## 3.4 Proof Sketch ŌĆö Why Serial SGS Always Produces a Semi-Active Schedule

A schedule is **semi-active** if no operation can start earlier without changing the sequence on at least one machine.

**Proof logic:**

- In Serial SGS, operation $O_{j,k}$ is scheduled at $t_{j,k} = \max\{C_{j,k-1},\ r_k\}$.
- If $t_{j,k} = C_{j,k-1}$ (job-precedence bound), then the operation cannot start earlier ŌĆö it is locked by its own predecessor.
- If $t_{j,k} = r_k$ (machine-ready bound), then the machine is busy with another operation whose sequence is fixed by the SGS order. Starting $O_{j,k}$ earlier would violate the chosen sequence on machine $k$.
- In neither case can we shift $O_{j,k}$ earlier without altering the machine sequence. Hence the schedule is semi-active. $\blacksquare$
