- **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):** $F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$.
- **Expectation:** $E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f(x) \, dx$.
- **Variance:** $Var(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$.
- **$k^{th}$ Moment:** $E[X^k] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^k f(x) \, dx$.

For a discrete random variable $X$ taking values $x_i$ with probabilities $p_i$:

- **Expectation:** $E[X] = \sum_i x_i \, p_i$.
- **Variance:** $Var(X) = \sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2 \, p_i$.

### 2.2.2 Common Probability Distributions (KTU Exam Hot-Spots)

| Distribution | PDF / PMF | Mean ($E[X]$) | Variance ($Var(X)$) | Engineering Application in Scheduling |
|:-------------|:----------|:--------------|:--------------------|:--------------------------------------|
| **Exponential** ($\lambda$) | $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \, x \geq 0$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | Inter-arrival times of jobs (Poisson process) |
| **Normal** ($\mu, \sigma^2$) | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | Processing times (CLT-based approximation) |
| **Uniform** $(a,b)$ | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{b-a}$ | $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$ | Random variate generation; bounded noise |
| **Poisson** ($\lambda$) | $P(X=k) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k}{k!}$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | Number of job arrivals in fixed interval |
| **Weibull** ($k, \lambda$) | $f(x) = \dfrac{k}{\lambda}\left(\dfrac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}$ | $\lambda \, \Gamma\!\left(1+\tfrac{1}{k}\right)$ | $\lambda^2\!\left[\Gamma\!\left(1+\tfrac{2}{k}\right) - \Gamma^2\!\left(1+\tfrac{1}{k}\right)\right]$ | Machine reliability, time-to-failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memoryless Property of the Exponential Distribution:**
> For $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, $P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$.
> This is the **only continuous distribution with this property** and is fundamental to Markovian queueing models used in scheduling.

### 2.2.3 Renewal Process Fundamentals

A **renewal process** $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ counts the number of renewals up to time $t$, with inter-arrival times $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ being i.i.d. non-negative random variables with CDF $F(x)$.

**Key Quantities:**

- **Renewal Function:** $M(t) = E[N(t)]$ ŌĆö the expected number of renewals in $[0,t]$.
- **Renewal Density:** $m(t) = \dfrac{d M(t)}{dt}$.
- **Elementary Renewal Theorem:** As $t \to \infty$, $\dfrac{M(t)}{t} \to \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ where $\mu = E[X]$.
- **Renewal Equation:** $M(t) = F(t) + \int_0^t M(t-x) \, dF(x)$ (Volterra integral equation of convolution type).

### 2.2.4 Little's Law (Queueing Backbone)

> [!NOTE]
> **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda \cdot W$
> - $L$ = long-run average number of customers in the system.
> - $\lambda$ = long-run average arrival rate.
> - $W$ = long-run average time spent in the system.

This law is **distribution-free** and is the single most cited result in queueing-based scheduling analyses.

### 2.2.5 Random Variate Generation ŌĆö Inverse Transform Method

To generate a sample from a continuous distribution with CDF $F(x)$:

1. Generate $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$.
2. Compute $X = F^{-1}(U)$.

This is the **workhorse algorithm** for translating mathematical distributions into simulation inputs.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Simulation** lets plant managers test scheduling rules (FCFS, SPT, EDD, Critical Ratio) before live deployment.
- **Stochastic Models** quantify expected delays, WIP accumulation, and utilization ŌĆö critical inputs to lean manufacturing and Theory of Constraints (TOC) applications.
- **Renewal Theory** underpins preventive maintenance scheduling for machines (replacing components before failure).
- **Markov Chains** model machine states: *Working*, *Idle*, *Broken*, *Under Repair* ŌĆö used in reliability-aware scheduling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Mean and Variance of the Exponential Distribution

> **Given:** $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ with PDF $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \geq 0$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Mean $E[X]$:**

$$
E[X] = \int_0^{\infty} x \cdot f(x) \, dx = \int_0^{\infty} x \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx
$$

Apply integration by parts with $u = x \Rightarrow du = dx$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda x} dx \Rightarrow v = -e^{-\lambda x}$:

$$
E[X] = \left[-x e^{-\lambda x}\right]_0^{\infty} + \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x} \, dx
$$

The boundary term at $\infty$ is 0 (exponential decay dominates), and at 0 it is 0:

$$
E[X] = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda x} \, dx = \left[-\dfrac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda x}\right]_0^{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $E[X^2]$ for the Variance:**

$$
E[X^2] = \int_0^{\infty} x^2 \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \, dx
$$

Use the Gamma function identity $\int_0^{\infty} x^n e^{-\lambda x} dx = \dfrac{n!}{\lambda^{n+1}}$ for integer $n$:

$$
E[X^2] = \lambda \cdot \dfrac{2!}{\lambda^3} = \dfrac{2}{\lambda^2}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Variance:**

$$
Var(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = \dfrac{2}{\lambda^2} - \left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \dfrac{2}{\lambda^2} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}
$$

> **Result:** $E[X] = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ and $Var(X) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$. This is a **KTU favourite** for numerical substitution problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö The Renewal Function for a Poisson Process

> **Given:** Inter-arrival times $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ i.i.d. ŌåÆ Counting process $N(t)$ is a **Poisson process** with rate $\lambda$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Distribution of $S_n$ (Time of the $n^{th}$ Renewal):**

The sum of $n$ i.i.d. $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$ random variables follows a Gamma/Erlang distribution:

$$
S_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \sim \text{Gamma}(n, \lambda), \quad f_{S_n}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Relate $N(t)$ to $S_n$:**

By definition, the $n^{th}$ renewal occurs on or before time $t$ if and only if $N(t) \geq n$:

$$
\{N(t) \geq n\} = \{S_n \leq t\}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(N(t) = n) = P(N(t) \geq n) - P(N(t) \geq n+1) = P(S_n \leq t) - P(S_{n+1} \leq t)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Renewal Function $M(t) = E[N(t)]$:**

Using the identity $E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} P(N(t) \geq n)$ and substituting $P(N(t) \geq n) = P(S_n \leq t)$:

$$
M(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} P(S_n \leq t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \int_0^t \dfrac{\lambda^n s^{n-1} e^{-\lambda s}}{(n-1)!} \, ds
$$

Swap the order of summation and integration (Fubini's theorem applies since all terms are non-negative):

$$
M(t) = \int_0^t e^{-\lambda s} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{\lambda^n s^{n-1}}{(n-1)!} \, ds = \int_0^t e^{-\lambda s} \cdot \lambda e^{\lambda s} \, ds
$$

Simplify the integrand: $e^{-\lambda s} \cdot \lambda e^{\lambda s} = \lambda$:

$$
M(t) = \int_0^t \lambda \, ds = \lambda t
$$

> **Result:** For a Poisson process, the renewal function is **linear**: $M(t) = \lambda t$. This is the cleanest case of the **Elementary Renewal Theorem**, which states $\lim_{t \to \infty} M(t)/t = 1/\mu = \lambda$ ŌĆö perfectly consistent.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Inverse Transform Method for Exponential Distribution

> **Goal:** Generate a sample $X$ from $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$ using a uniform random number $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Derive the CDF:**

$$
F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_0^x \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Invert the CDF:**

Set $U = F(X) = 1 - e^{-\lambda X}$ and solve for $X$:

$$
1 - U = e^{-\lambda X} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(1 - U) = -\lambda X \quad \Rightarrow \quad X = -\dfrac{\ln(1 - U)}{\lambda}
$$

Since $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$, $1 - U$ is also $\text{Uniform}(0,1)$, so this simplifies to:

$$
X = -\dfrac{\ln(U)}{\lambda}
$$

> **Algorithm:** Draw $U$ from a standard uniform RNG ŌåÆ compute $X = -\ln(U)/\lambda$ ŌåÆ $X$ is an exponentially distributed sample.
> This is the **foundational algorithm** for simulating job arrivals in a dynamic job shop.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Key Stochastic Quantities (At a Glance)

| Concept | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Interpretation |
|:--------|:-----------------------|:---------------------------|
| Expected Value | $E[X] = \int x \, dF(x)$ | Long-run average behaviour |
| Variance | $Var(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$ | Spread / unpredictability of the system |
| Coefficient of Variation | $C_v = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu}$ | Relative variability (use $\sqrt{\lambda^2}/\lambda=1$ for Exp) |
| Renewal Rate | $r(t) = m(t) = \dfrac{dM(t)}{dt}$ | Instantaneous frequency of events |
| Little's Law | $L = \lambda W$ | ThroughputŌĆōWIPŌĆōLatency trade-off |
| Memoryless Property | $P(X>s+t \mid X>s) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Markovian scheduling assumption |
| Steady-State Probability | $\pi_j = \lim_{n \to \infty} P(X_n = j)$ | Long-run fraction of time in state $j$ |
| Mean Time to Failure (MTTF) | $MTTF = E[X]$ for $X$=time to failure | Drives preventive maintenance scheduling |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Style Tag]**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Module 4]**
**Differentiate between Static and Dynamic Job Shop scheduling. Why is simulation the preferred analytical tool for dynamic job shops? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Job Shop | Dynamic Job Shop |
|:-------|:----------------|:-----------------|
| Job Information | All jobs known at time zero | Jobs arrive randomly over time |
| Arrivals | Deterministic / pre-listed | Stochastic (random) |
| Solver Approach | Combinatorial optimization (Johnson's, NEH, CDS) | Simulation, queueing theory, stochastic control |
| Tractability | Closed-form heuristics exist | Closed-form solutions mostly intractable |

**Simulation Preference (Ōēł1 mark):** Because the system has stochastic arrivals, multiple machines, complex routings, and competing performance metrics, the *state-space* is too large for analytical methods. Simulation allows the scheduler to **experiment with policies** (FCFS, SPT, EDD, CR) under controlled randomness, observe performance, and *compare alternatives* before live implementation. It is also **non-invasive** ŌĆö the real shop is not disrupted.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**State and explain the memoryless property of the exponential distribution. Give one engineering example relevant to scheduling. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **memoryless property** of the exponential distribution states that the conditional probability of surviving an additional time $t$, given survival up to time $s$, depends only on $t$ and not on $s$:

$$
P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad s, t \geq 0
$$

**Derivation Sketch:**

$$
P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = \dfrac{P(X > s + t)}{P(X > s)} = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Engineering Example:** In a dynamic job shop, if the **inter-arrival time** of jobs follows an exponential distribution with rate $\lambda$, then the time until the *next* job arrives is independent of how long we have already waited. This is the cornerstone of the **M/M/1 and M/M/c queueing models** widely used in machine-scheduling analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write the formula as $P(X > s+t) = P(X > t)$, which is **wrong** ŌĆö the conditional bar $\mid X > s$ on the LHS is mandatory. **Loss of 1 mark** is routine for this error.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Format, Module 4]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Sample QP Pattern, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the **renewal function** $M(t) = E[N(t)]$ for a Poisson process whose inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential with rate $\lambda$. Show every step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The processing times of jobs on Machine M1 follow an exponential distribution with mean **6 minutes**. The arrival of jobs follows a Poisson process with rate **$\lambda = 10$ jobs/hour**. Use the **Inverse Transform Method** to derive the formula for generating a sample of (i) the inter-arrival time and (ii) the processing time. If a uniform random number $U = 0.40$ is drawn, compute the corresponding exponential sample. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Renewal Counting Process:**

For inter-arrival times $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ i.i.d. $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$:

$$
S_n = X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n, \quad N(t) = \max\{n : S_n \leq t\}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Distribution of $S_n$ (Gamma/Erlang):**

$$
f_{S_n}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Relate $N(t)$ to $S_n$:**

$$
P(N(t) \geq n) = P(S_n \leq t)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Express the Renewal Function:**

$$
M(t) = E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} P(N(t) \geq n) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} P(S_n \leq t)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute and Swap SumŌĆōIntegral (Fubini):**

$$
M(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \int_0^t \dfrac{\lambda^n s^{n-1} e^{-\lambda s}}{(n-1)!} \, ds = \int_0^t e^{-\lambda s} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{\lambda^n s^{n-1}}{(n-1)!} \, ds
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Recognize the Series as a Shifted Exponential:**

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{\lambda^n s^{n-1}}{(n-1)!} = \lambda \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{(\lambda s)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!} = \lambda e^{\lambda s}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final Integration:**

$$
M(t) = \int_0^t e^{-\lambda s} \cdot \lambda e^{\lambda s} \, ds = \int_0^t \lambda \, ds = \lambda t
$$

> **Conclusion:** $M(t) = \lambda t$. The renewal function is **linear** with slope $\lambda$. **(7 marks ŌĆö full credit if all steps are shown clearly.)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**(i) Inter-arrival Time Generation:**

Arrival rate $\lambda_a = 10$ jobs/hour $= \dfrac{1}{6}$ jobs/minute. So $\lambda_a = \dfrac{1}{6}$ per minute.

CDF of exponential: $F(x) = 1 - e^{-x/6}$. Inverse transform:

$$
T_a = -6 \ln(U) \text{ minutes}
$$

For $U = 0.40$:

$$
T_a = -6 \ln(0.40) = -6 \times (-0.9163) = 5.498 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(ii) Processing Time Generation:**

Mean processing time $= 6$ min ŌåÆ rate $\lambda_p = \dfrac{1}{6}$ per minute.

CDF: $F(x) = 1 - e^{-x/6}$. Inverse transform:

$$
T_p = -6 \ln(U) \text{ minutes}
$$

For $U = 0.40$:

$$
T_p = -6 \ln(0.40) = 5.498 \text{ minutes}
$$

> **Note:** Both random variates use the same formula because both distributions are exponential with the same mean. In general, the rate parameter must be carefully identified from the given mean.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Sample QP Pattern, Module 4]**

**(a)** State **Little's Law**. In a dynamic job shop, the average number of jobs in the system is observed to be 12, and the average arrival rate is 4 jobs per hour. Compute the average time a job spends in the system. Comment on the practical utility of this result. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **five phases of a dynamic job shop simulation study**. Mention at least two performance measures commonly used as outputs. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö State Little's Law:**

$$
L = \lambda \cdot W
$$

where $L$ = long-run average number in system, $\lambda$ = long-run average arrival rate, $W$ = long-run average time in system.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute Given Values:**

$L = 12$ jobs, $\lambda = 4$ jobs/hour.

$$
12 = 4 \cdot W \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Practical Utility Comment:**

Little's Law is **distribution-free** ŌĆö it holds regardless of arrival pattern, service-time distribution, number of servers, or queue discipline. It enables the scheduler to:
- Predict WIP levels from throughput targets (or vice versa).
- Identify bottlenecks: if a station's $L$ is disproportionately high, it is a candidate for capacity expansion.
- Justify lean initiatives: reducing $L$ directly reduces $W$ at constant $\lambda$, accelerating deliveries.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**The Five Phases (Mermaid Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PH1[1 PROBLEM FORMULATION] --> PH2[2 CONCEPTUAL MODEL]
    PH2 --> PH3[3 DATA COLLECTION AND INPUT MODELING]
    PH3 --> PH4[4 MODEL TRANSLATION AND VERIFICATION]
    PH4 --> PH5[5 VALIDATION AND OUTPUT ANALYSIS]

    style PH1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PH2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PH3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PH4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PH5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Description of Phases (Ōēł5 marks):**

1. **Problem Formulation:** Define objectives (minimize makespan? tardiness?) and constraints (machine availability, due dates).
2. **Conceptual Model:** Identify entities (jobs, machines, transporters), attributes (job type, due date), and events (arrival, completion, breakdown).
3. **Data Collection & Input Modeling:** Gather historical data, fit distributions (Exponential for arrivals, Normal/Lognormal for processing times).
4. **Model Translation & Verification:** Code the model in Arena / FlexSim / ProModel. Debug to ensure logic is bug-free.
5. **Validation & Output Analysis:** Compare model output with real system. Use statistical tests. Run replications; build confidence intervals.

**Performance Measures (Ōēł2 marks):**
- **Average Flow Time:** Time from job arrival to completion.
- **Machine Utilization:** Fraction of busy time per machine.
- **Tardiness / Lateness:** Deviation from due date.
- **WIP (Work-in-Process):** Number of jobs in the system at any instant.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing $N(t)$ with $M(t)$:** $N(t)$ is the *count* (random variable), $M(t) = E[N(t)]$ is the *renewal function* (deterministic expectation). Examiners deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the memoryless condition bar:** In $P(X > s+t \mid X > s)$, omitting $\mid X > s$ is a *direct* loss of 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up $E[X]$ and $1/\lambda$:** Always write the units explicitly (per hour, per minute) ŌĆö KTU valuation strictly checks dimensional consistency.
> 4. **Skipping the Fubini swap justification in renewal derivations:** Awarded 1 mark separately for noting that the swap is valid because all integrands are non-negative.
> 5. **In Inverse Transform, writing $X = -\ln(U)/\lambda$ without showing the inversion steps** ŌĆö examiners want $U = 1 - e^{-\lambda X}$ ŌåÆ $X = -\ln(1-U)/\lambda$ ŌåÆ simplification to $-\ln(U)/\lambda$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:**

- ŌśÉ **Dynamic Job Shop** = stochastic arrivals + online scheduling. Distinguish clearly from the *static* (deterministic) case.
- ŌśÉ **Simulation** is the *only* practical analytical tool when the state-space is combinatorially explosive.
- ŌśÉ The **Five Phases** of simulation: Problem Formulation ŌåÆ Conceptual Model ŌåÆ Input Modeling ŌåÆ Translation/Verification ŌåÆ Validation/Output Analysis.
- ŌśÉ A **Stochastic Model** describes a system under probabilistic laws; its *preliminaries* are RVs, distributions, and stochastic processes.
- ŌśÉ **Exponential distribution** has mean $1/\lambda$, variance $1/\lambda^2$, and the **memoryless property** ŌĆö KTU's most-tested distribution.
- ŌśÉ **Normal distribution** is used for processing times (via CLT) ŌĆö mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$.
- ŌśÉ **Poisson process** has inter-arrival times i.i.d. $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$; its **renewal function is linear**: $M(t) = \lambda t$.
- ŌśÉ **Little's Law** $L = \lambda W$ is **distribution-free** and universal in queueing-based scheduling.
- ŌśÉ **Inverse Transform Method** generates samples via $X = F^{-1}(U)$ where $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$.
- ŌśÉ **Renewal Equation** (Volterra): $M(t) = F(t) + \int_0^t M(t-x) \, dF(x)$.
- ŌśÉ **Elementary Renewal Theorem:** $\lim_{t \to \infty} M(t)/t = 1/\mu$ where $\mu = E[X]$.
- ŌśÉ For KTU numericals: always convert **per-hour to per-minute** or vice versa before substituting into $\lambda$.
- ŌśÉ For the inverse transform, **show the inversion algebra explicitly** ŌĆö partial marks depend on the working.
- ŌśÉ **Verification ŌēĀ Validation:** Verification = model is bug-free; Validation = model represents reality.
- ŌśÉ Use **Mermaid block diagrams** (or neatly labelled rectangles in the answer script) for "describe the phases" type questions.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stochastic Scheduling Based on Expectation** is a class of scheduling problems in which the processing times of jobs are **random variables** (not deterministic constants) characterized by known probability distributions. The decision-maker constructs schedules using the **mathematical expectation** $E[P_j]$ (also written as $\bar{p}_j$) of these random variables as the primary decision parameter, aiming to optimize an expected performance metric such as $E[C_{max}]$, $E[\sum C_j]$, $E[\sum w_j C_j]$, or $E[L_{max}]$.

In the standard three-field notation $\alpha \mid \beta \mid \gamma$, stochastic scheduling problems are often denoted as:

$$
1 \mid P_j \sim \text{stochastic} \mid E[\gamma]
$$

where $P_j$ represents the processing time distribution of job $j$.

## 1.2 Key Assumptions

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Assumptions in Stochastic Expectation-Based Scheduling:**
> 1. **Independence:** Job processing times $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$ are mutually independent random variables.
> 2. **Known Distributions:** Each $P_j$ has a fully specified probability distribution with known mean $E[P_j] = \bar{p}_j$ and known variance $\text{Var}(P_j) = \sigma_j^2$.
> 3. **Non-negativity:** $P_j \geq 0$ almost surely.
> 4. **Static Release:** All jobs are available at time zero ($r_j = 0$).
> 5. **No Preemption:** (Unless explicitly allowed) A job, once started, runs to completion.
> 6. **Deterministic Sequence:** Although processing times vary in realization, the **sequence** of jobs is fixed at time zero.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Hospital Triage Counter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Multi-Specialty Hospital Outpatient Department**
>
> Imagine you are the hospital administrator deciding the calling order of patients. You do **not** know the exact consultation time for each patient ŌĆö that depends on the complexity of the diagnosis, the patient's cooperation, the doctor's pace, etc. However, from historical data, you know the **expected consultation time** for a cardiology patient is 20 min, for an orthopedic patient is 35 min, and for a general checkup is 10 min.
>
> Now, if your objective is to **minimize the total waiting time of all patients combined**, what order do you call them in? You would call the **shortest expected consultation first** (general checkup ŌåÆ cardiology ŌåÆ orthopedics), exactly as the **SEPT rule** prescribes. The expected value acts as a "best statistical proxy" for the unknown future reality ŌĆö a rational decision when repeated many times.

## 1.4 Decision Metrics ŌĆö The Three Pillars

For any stochastic schedule $\pi$, the three most studied expectation-based metrics are:

$$
E[C_{max}(\pi)] = E\left[\max_j C_j(\pi)\right]
$$

$$
E[F(\pi)] = E\left[\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j(\pi)\right] \quad \text{(expected total flow time)}
$$

$$
E[TW(\pi)] = E\left[\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j C_j(\pi)\right] \quad \text{(expected weighted flow time)}
$$

where $w_j > 0$ is the importance weight assigned to job $j$.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Stochastic Scheduling Rules

When processing times are stochastic, deterministic rules (like Johnson's or CDS) may fail to be optimal. Three expectation-based rules dominate the single-machine literature:

### 2.1.1 SEPT ŌĆö Shortest Expected Processing Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SEPT Rule (Shortest Expected Processing Time):**
> Sequence the jobs in **non-decreasing order of $E[P_j]$**, i.e.,
>
> $$
> E[P_{(1)}] \leq E[P_{(2)}] \leq \dots \leq E[P_{(n)}]
> $$
>
> This rule is **optimal** for the problem $1 \mid \mid E[\sum C_j]$ when processing times are independent.

### 2.1.2 WSEPT ŌĆö Weighted Shortest Expected Processing Time (Stochastic Smith's Rule)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WSEPT Rule (Stochastic Extension of Smith's Rule):**
> Sequence the jobs in **non-increasing order of the ratio $\dfrac{w_j}{E[P_j]}$** (the stochastic Smith's index):
>
> $$
> \frac{w_{[1]}}{E[P_{(1)}]} \geq \frac{w_{[2]}}{E[P_{(2)}]} \geq \dots \geq \frac{w_{[n]}}{E[P_{(n)}]}
> $$
>
> This rule is **optimal** for the problem $1 \mid \mid E[\sum w_j C_j]$ under stochastic dominance assumptions.

### 2.1.3 EDD ŌĆö Earliest Due Date

> [!NOTE]
> **EDD Rule in Stochastic Setting:**
> Sequence jobs by **non-decreasing due dates** $d_j$. Under conditions of **stochastic ordering** (when the processing time distributions of jobs with earlier due dates stochastically dominate those with later due dates), EDD minimizes $E[L_{max}]$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Application |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Expected Completion Time of job in position $k$ | $E[C_{(k)}] = \sum_{i=1}^{k} E[P_{(i)}]$ | SEPT/WSEPT schedules |
| 2 | Expected Total Flow Time | $E[\sum C_j] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (n-k+1) \cdot E[P_{(k)}]$ | Single-machine, any order |
| 3 | Expected Makespan (single machine) | $E[C_{max}] = \sum_{j=1}^{n} E[P_j]$ | Independent of sequence |
| 4 | Expected Weighted Flow Time (SEPT order) | $E[\sum w_j C_j] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_{(k)} \sum_{i=1}^{k} E[P_{(i)}]$ | After applying WSEPT |
| 5 | Variance of Makespan | $\text{Var}(C_{max}) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{Var}(P_j)$ | Assuming independence |
| 6 | WSEPT Priority Index | $\rho_j = \dfrac{w_j}{E[P_j]}$ | Higher value = earlier position |
| 7 | Expected Lateness | $E[L_j] = E[C_j] - d_j$ | For job $j$ in any schedule |
| 8 | Smith's Index (deterministic) | $\rho_j^{S} = \dfrac{w_j}{p_j}$ | Reference comparison |
| 9 | Jensen's Inequality (convex $f$) | $E[f(P)] \geq f(E[P])$ | Lower bound on stochastic cost |
| 10 | Gittins Index (geometric band) | $g(\tau) = p$ (constant) | Multi-armed bandit scheduling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The fact that $E[C_{max}] = \sum E[P_j]$ for a single machine (rule #3) is a powerful insight ŌĆö the **expected** makespan is **sequence-independent**. However, the **variance** (rule #5) grows with $\sum \sigma_j^2$ and is *also* independent of the order. This is unique to single-machine problems. For parallel machines, **expected makespan is minimized by LPT (Longest Processing Time first)** under stochastic dominance.

## 2.3 Why These Rules Work ŌĆö The "Why" Behind the Logic

| Step | Logic | Consequence |
|------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Each job contributes to flow time of all jobs that follow it. | A short job placed early "spoils" fewer later jobs. |
| 2 | Job $j$ in position $k$ contributes $w_j$ to each of the $n-k+1$ weighted completion times that follow. | Position weight grows toward the front. |
| 3 | When processing times are random, only the **mean** is a known decision input. | Expected value replaces deterministic $p_j$. |
| 4 | Independence of $P_j$ allows $E[\sum] = \sum E[\cdot]$. | Linear expected cost becomes a tractable sum. |
| 5 | Pairwise interchange argument: a swap of two adjacent jobs is a complete local search. | Any optimal schedule has its neighbors in a "correct" relative order. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor Wafer Fabrication:** Wafer processing times have high variance; SEPT-type rules are deployed in lithography queueing.
- **Cloud Computing Task Scheduling:** Job durations follow heavy-tailed distributions; WSEPT generalizes to priority-weighted tasks.
- **Operating Systems:** CPU time-slice allocation uses Gittins-index style decisions when job sizes are unknown.
- **Healthcare Operations:** Triage systems are precisely SEPT implementations with stochastic consultation times.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Expected Completion Time of Job in Position $k$

Consider a single machine processing $n$ jobs in the fixed sequence $\pi = (1, 2, \dots, n)$. The completion time of the $k^{th}$ job in $\pi$ is the sum of all processing times up to and including position $k$:

$$
C_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i
$$

Applying the expectation operator to both sides, and using **linearity of expectation**:

$$
E[C_k] = E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{k} E[P_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{p}_i
$$

This is the **fundamental building block** of all subsequent expected-cost calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why linearity matters:** We did *not* require independence to take the sum outside. However, to compute the **variance** of $C_k$ (which is *not* sequence-independent in general), we *do* need independence.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Expected Total Flow Time $E[\sum C_j]$

The total flow time is the sum of completion times of all jobs:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j = \sum_{k=1}^{n} C_k = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i
$$

We can re-express this double sum by counting how many times each $P_i$ appears. Job $i$ in position $i$ contributes to the completion time of itself and every job after it, i.e., it appears in $C_i, C_{i+1}, \dots, C_n$ ŌĆö a total of $(n - i + 1)$ times.

Therefore:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (n - i + 1) \cdot P_i
$$

Taking expectations:

$$
E\left[\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (n - i + 1) \cdot E[P_i]
$$

This is the **sequence-dependent expected cost** that SEPT minimizes.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö SEPT Optimality via Pairwise Interchange (Full Proof)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Rothkopf & Smith, 1984; classical result):**
> For $n$ independent jobs on a single machine with random processing times $P_1, \dots, P_n$, the schedule that minimizes $E[\sum C_j]$ sequences jobs in non-decreasing order of $E[P_j]$.

**Proof by Pairwise Interchange Argument:**

Assume an optimal schedule exists. Consider any two **adjacent** jobs in this schedule, say jobs $i$ (in position $k$) and $j$ (in position $k+1$).

Let $S$ denote the sum of processing times of all jobs scheduled **before** position $k$. Then:

**Sub-case A: $i$ before $j$ (current order)**

$$
C_i = S + P_i
$$

$$
C_j = S + P_i + P_j
$$

The contribution of these two jobs to the total flow time is:

$$
F_A = C_i + C_j = (S + P_i) + (S + P_i + P_j) = 2S + 2P_i + P_j
$$

**Sub-case B: $j$ before $i$ (swapped order)**

$$
C_j' = S + P_j
$$

$$
C_i' = S + P_j + P_i
$$

The contribution now is:

$$
F_B = C_j' + C_i' = (S + P_j) + (S + P_j + P_i) = 2S + 2P_j + P_i
$$

**Difference Analysis:**

$$
F_A - F_B = (2S + 2P_i + P_j) - (2S + 2P_j + P_i) = P_i - P_j
$$

Now take expectations. By linearity:

$$
E[F_A] - E[F_B] = E[P_i] - E[P_j] = \bar{p}_i - \bar{p}_j
$$

**Case 1:** If $\bar{p}_i > \bar{p}_j$, then $E[F_A] - E[F_B] > 0$, meaning $F_A > F_B$ in expectation. So the **swapped** order is strictly better.

**Case 2:** If $\bar{p}_i < \bar{p}_j$, then $E[F_A] < E[F_B]$, and the **current** order is better.

**Conclusion:** For the schedule to be optimal, we must have $\bar{p}_i \leq \bar{p}_j$ for every adjacent pair, which means the schedule must be sorted in non-decreasing order of $E[P_j]$ ŌĆö i.e., **SEPT**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö WSEPT Optimality for $E[\sum w_j C_j]$

The proof follows the same interchange logic but with weights. The contribution of jobs $i$ (position $k$) and $j$ (position $k+1$) becomes:

$$
F_A^{(w)} = w_i(S + P_i) + w_j(S + P_i + P_j) = (w_i + w_j)S + (w_i + w_j)P_i + w_j P_j
$$

$$
F_B^{(w)} = w_j(S + P_j) + w_i(S + P_j + P_i) = (w_i + w_j)S + (w_i + w_j)P_j + w_i P_i
$$

**Difference:**

$$
F_A^{(w)} - F_B^{(w)} = w_j P_i - w_i P_j
$$

Taking expectations:

$$
E[F_A^{(w)}] - E[F_B^{(w)}] = w_j \bar{p}_i - w_i \bar{p}_j = w_i w_j \left(\frac{\bar{p}_i}{w_i} - \frac{\bar{p}_j}{w_j}\right)
$$

For this difference to be non-positive (i.e., the current order $i$ before $j$ to be optimal), we need:

$$
\frac{\bar{p}_i}{w_i} \leq \frac{\bar{p}_j}{w_j} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{w_i}{\bar{p}_i} \geq \frac{w_j}{\bar{p}_j}
$$

Thus, the optimal order sequences jobs in **non-increasing order of $w_j / E[P_j]$** ŌĆö the **WSEPT** rule. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Expected Makespan of $n$ Jobs on a Single Machine

On a single machine, the makespan is the completion time of the **last** job:

$$
C_{max} = C_n = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j
$$

Taking expectations:

$$
E[C_{max}] = \sum_{j=1}^{n} E[P_j] = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \bar{p}_j
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** $E[C_{max}]$ is **independent of the schedule order**! Whether you process the shortest job first or the longest job first, the expected makespan is the same. This is a unique property of single-machine problems. However, the **distribution** of $C_{max}$ (and its variance) is not necessarily order-independent in *all* distributions ŌĆö only when $P_j$ are independent does the sum's distribution simplify.

The variance (assuming independence) is:

$$
\text{Var}(C_{max}) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{Var}(P_j) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sigma_j^2
$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6 ŌĆö Jensen's Inequality Bound for Convex Costs

If the objective function $f(\cdot)$ is **convex** in the processing time, then:

$$
E[f(P_j)] \geq f(E[P_j]) = f(\bar{p}_j)
$$

This means the **stochastic expected cost** is at least the cost computed with the deterministic mean. For example, for variance-regularized objectives like:

$$
E[C_{max}^2] = \text{Var}(C_{max}) + (E[C_{max}])^2 = \sum_j \sigma_j^2 + \left(\sum_j \bar{p}_j\right)^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convexity Corollary:** Any convex objective function is *harder* in the stochastic case than in the deterministic case. This is why stochastic scheduling is fundamentally more challenging than its deterministic counterpart ŌĆö the average scenario is never the worst scenario for convex costs.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Three jobs with processing time distributions:
- Job 1: $P_1 \sim$ Exponential$(\lambda_1 = 1/4)$, so $E[P_1] = 4$
- Job 2: $P_2 \sim$ Exponential$(\lambda_2 = 1/2)$, so $E[P_2] = 2$
- Job 3: $P_3 \sim$ Exponential$(\lambda_3 = 1/5)$, so $E[P_3] = 5$

Objective: Minimize $E[\sum C_j]$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Sort by $E[P_j]$ (SEPT order):**

$$
E[P_2] = 2 < E[P_1] = 4 < E[P_3] = 5
$$

Optimal SEPT order: $(2, 1, 3)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute expected completion times in SEPT order:**

$$
E[C_2] = E[P_2] = 2
$$

$$
E[C_1] = E[P_2] + E[P_1] = 2 + 4 = 6
$$

$$
E[C_3] = E[P_2] + E[P_1] + E[P_3] = 2 + 4 + 5 = 11
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute expected total flow time:**

$$
E[\sum C_j] = 2 + 6 + 11 = 19
$$

**Verification with alternative order $(1, 2, 3)$:**

$$
E[C_1] = 4, \quad E[C_2] = 6, \quad E[C_3] = 11
$$

$$
E[\sum C_j] = 4 + 6 + 11 = 21 > 19 \quad \checkmark
$$

Thus, SEPT yields a strictly better schedule. $\blacksquare$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stochastic Scheduling Decision Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the decision logic a scheduler follows when processing times are random and expectations govern the choice of rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: n Jobs with Stochastic Pj] --> B{Identify Objective Function}
    B -->|Minimize E Cm| C[Order Does Not Matter]
    B -->|Minimize E Sum Cj| D[Apply SEPT Rule]
    B -->|Minimize E Sum wj Cj| E[Apply WSEPT Rule]
    B -->|Minimize E Lmax| F{Weights Available?}
    F -->|Yes, Due Dates Known| G[Apply EDD Rule]
    F -->|No| H[Apply Heuristic Dispatching]
    C --> I[Schedule: Any Permutation]
    D --> J[Sort: Increasing E Pj]
    E --> K[Sort: Decreasing wj / E Pj]
    G --> L[Sort: Increasing dj]
    I --> M[END: Schedule Ready]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    H --> M
    L --> M
```
