The **Preemption Controller** acts as a gate ŌĆö in non-preemptive mode it forwards the assignment immediately; in preemptive mode it monitors continuously for re-evaluation triggers. ### 4.4 Gantt Comparison Schematic ŌĆö Non-Preemptive SPT vs Preemptive SRPT (3 jobs, staggered arrivals) ```mermaid gantt title Gantt Comparison: SPT Non-Preemptive vs SRPT Preemptive dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Non-Preemptive SPT J1 p=8 :a1, 0, 8 J2 p=3 :a2, 8, 3 J3 p=5 :a3, 11, 5 section Preemptive SRPT J1 p=8 part1 :b1, 0, 1 J2 p=3 :b2, 1, 3 J3 p=5 :b3, 4, 5 J1 p=8 part2 :b4, 9, 7 ``` **Reading the Gantt:** The lower track demonstrates **preemption at $t=1$** (job $J_1$ suspended, $J_2$ dispatched), with $J_1$ resuming at $t=9$ to finish its remaining 7 time units. The makespan in both cases is 16, but the *flow time distribution* differs significantly. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]** **"Distinguish between non-preemptive and preemptive dynamic scheduling policies with one industrial example for each."** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** > A **dynamic scheduling policy** makes dispatching decisions at each decision epoch based on the current state of the system. The two policy families differ on the **interruptibility** of in-progress operations. > > A **non-preemptive dynamic policy** assigns a job to an idle machine, and the operation runs to completion without interruption. Switching overhead is zero. *Example:* CNC turning of a metal shaft ŌĆö once the cutting tool engages, the cycle cannot be halted mid-pass without damaging the workpiece. > > A **preemptive dynamic policy** allows an in-progress operation to be **temporarily suspended** if a higher-priority job arrives. The preempted job resumes later from the saved state. *Example:* Semiconductor wafer fabrication ŌĆö lots can be re-inserted into the queue when a hot lot (expedited customer order) arrives. > > *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for contrast, 1 mark for examples)* #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]** **"State and justify the optimality of the SRPT rule for the problem $1 \vert r_i \vert \sum F_i$."** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** > **SRPT (Shortest Remaining Processing Time)** is a preemptive dynamic policy that, at every decision epoch, selects for processing the job with the smallest remaining processing time $p_i^{rem}(t)$. > > For the problem $1 \mid r_i \mid \sum F_i$ ŌĆö single machine with job release dates, minimizing total (or mean) flow time ŌĆö SRPT is **provably optimal** (Schrage, 1966). The optimality proof relies on an *exchange argument*: if at any epoch a longer job is processed while a shorter one waits, swapping them strictly reduces the waiting job's completion time without delaying the longer job beyond its original start. > > **Crucial observation:** This optimality holds **only because preemption is permitted**. Under a non-preemptive restriction, the problem becomes **NP-Hard**. > > *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for theorem statement, 1 mark for preemption necessity)* --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Define a dynamic scheduling policy. Differentiate between static and dynamic, and between preemptive and non-preemptive policies, with a $2 \times 2$ classification table. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Consider the following five jobs arriving at $t=0$ on a single machine. Compute the **non-preemptive SPT** schedule and evaluate it on the basis of (i) mean flow time, (ii) makespan, and (iii) maximum tardiness. *(7 Marks)* | Job $i$ | $p_i$ | $d_i$ | |---|---|---| | $J_1$ | 7 | 9 | | $J_2$ | 4 | 6 | | $J_3$ | 9 | 14 | | $J_4$ | 2 | 3 | | $J_5$ | 5 | 11 | --- **Model Solution for (a):** A **dynamic scheduling policy** is one in which the dispatching decision is made at each decision epoch (e.g., when a machine becomes idle or a new job arrives) using the *current state* of the system, rather than being fixed at $t=0$. *(1 mark)* **Classification Axes:** 1. **Static vs Dynamic:** Static policies fix the schedule at $t=0$; dynamic policies re-decide at every epoch. *(1 mark)* 2. **Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive:** Preemptive policies allow in-progress jobs to be interrupted; non-preemptive policies do not. *(1 mark)* **$2 \times 2$ Classification Table:** *(3 marks)* | | **Non-Preemptive** | **Preemptive** | |---|---|---| | **Static** | Schedule fixed at $t=0$; jobs cannot be interrupted (e.g., fixed sequence $1\|C_{max}$) | Schedule fixed at $t=0$; but jobs may be interrupted if required (rare in practice) | | **Dynamic** | Decision re-evaluated at each idle epoch; selected job runs to completion (e.g., SPT, EDD) | Decision re-evaluated continuously; in-process jobs may be preempted (e.g., SRPT, preemptive EDD) | **Industrial Example:** A job shop with release-date variability uses dynamic non-preemptive SPT; a wafer fab uses dynamic preemptive SRPT. *(1 mark)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 ŌĆö SPT Order** *(1 mark)* Sort jobs by $p_i$ ascending: $J_4 (2), J_2 (4), J_5 (5), J_1 (7), J_3 (9)$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Completion Times** *(2 marks)* $$\begin{aligned} C_4 &= 0 + 2 = 2 \\ C_2 &= 2 + 4 = 6 \\ C_5 &= 6 + 5 = 11 \\ C_1 &= 11 + 7 = 18 \\ C_3 &= 18 + 9 = 27 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Performance Metrics** *(4 marks)* (i) **Mean flow time** (since $r_i = 0$, $F_i = C_i$): $$\bar{F} = \frac{2 + 6 + 11 + 18 + 27}{5} = \frac{64}{5} = 12.8$$ (ii) **Makespan:** $$C_{max} = 27$$ (iii) **Maximum tardiness:** Compute $T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$: $$\begin{aligned} T_4 &= \max(0, 2-3) = 0 \\ T_2 &= \max(0, 6-6) = 0 \\ T_5 &= \max(0, 11-11) = 0 \\ T_1 &= \max(0, 18-9) = 9 \\ T_3 &= \max(0, 27-14) = 13 \end{aligned}$$ $$T_{max} = \max(0,0,0,9,13) = 13$$ **Final Answer Summary:** $$\bar{F} = 12.8, \quad C_{max} = 27, \quad T_{max} = 13$$ --- #### **Question B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a suitable Gantt chart, explain the operation of the **SRPT (Shortest Remaining Processing Time)** rule. State one problem class for which it is optimal. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Three jobs $J_1, J_2, J_3$ arrive on a single machine at times $r_1=0, r_2=2, r_3=4$ with processing times $p_1=6, p_2=2, p_3=3$ respectively. Construct the **preemptive SRPT** schedule and compute (i) completion times, (ii) mean flow time, and (iii) number of preemption events. *(7 Marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a):** **SRPT Definition** *(2 marks)*: SRPT is a **preemptive dynamic scheduling policy** for a single machine. At every decision epoch (job arrival or completion), the machine selects the waiting job with the **smallest remaining processing time** $p_i^{rem}(t) = p_i - \text{(elapsed time on }J_i\text{)}$. **Gantt Chart Sketch** *(3 marks)*: ``` Time: 0 2 4 5 7 10 13 |----|----|----|----|----|----| J1: [###] [#] (preempted then resumed) J2: [##] J3: [#]...... ``` *Caption:* At $t=2$, $J_1$ (with $p_1^{rem}=4$) is preempted by $J_2$ (with $p_2^{rem}=2$). At $t=4$, $J_2$ done; $J_3$ arrives with $p_3=3$. $J_1$ has $p_1^{rem}=4 > p_3^{rem}=3$, so $J_3$ is dispatched. At $t=7$, $J_3$ done; $J_1$ resumes for 4 more units until $t=11$. **Optimality Statement** *(2 marks)*: SRPT is optimal for the problem class $1 \mid r_i \mid \sum F_i$ (single machine, release dates, minimizing total flow time). The optimality is *Schrage's theorem* (1966). The non-preemptive counterpart ($1 \mid r_i, \text{non-pre} \mid \sum F_i$) is **NP-Hard**, demonstrating the value of preemption. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Initialize** *(1 mark)*: At $t=0$: only $J_1$ available, $p_1^{rem}=6$. Dispatch $J_1$. Run for 2 units. **Step 2 ŌĆö At $t=2$:** $J_2$ arrives with $p_2^{rem}=2$. Compare with $J_1$ ($p_1^{rem}=4$). Since $2 < 4$, **preempt $J_1$, run $J_2$**. *(1 mark)* **Step 3 ŌĆö At $t=4$:** $J_2$ completes. $J_3$ arrives with $p_3^{rem}=3$. Compare with $J_1$ ($p_1^{rem}=4$). Since $3 < 4$, **run $J_3$**. *(1 mark)* **Step 4 ŌĆö At $t=7$:** $J_3$ completes. Remaining: only $J_1$ with $p_1^{rem}=4$. **Run $J_1$** until $t=11$. *(1 mark)* **Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Metrics** *(3 marks)*: $$\begin{aligned} C_1 &= 11, & F_1 &= 11 - 0 = 11 \\ C_2 &= 4, & F_2 &= 4 - 2 = 2 \\ C_3 &= 7, & F_3 &= 7 - 4 = 3 \end{aligned}$$ (i) Completion times: $C_1=11, C_2=4, C_3=7$. (ii) Mean flow time: $$\bar{F} = \frac{11 + 2 + 3}{3} = \frac{16}{3} \approx 5.33$$ (iii) Number of preemption events: **1** (only $J_1$ was preempted, at $t=2$). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing SRPT with SPT:** SRPT uses *remaining* time, not original processing time. A job partially completed has reduced $p^{rem}$ and may become the next dispatch. > 2. **Forgetting release dates:** $F_i = C_i - r_i$, not $C_i$ alone. > 3. **Ignoring EDD optimality for $L_{max}$:** Preemption provides **no** benefit when the objective is $L_{max}$. Writing "preemption always improves performance" loses 2 marks. > 4. **Missing the Gantt sketch in SRPT explanations:** KTU examiners award 2ŌĆō3 marks specifically for the visual timeline. > 5. **Not labeling units:** Every numeric answer must carry time units. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 4: Non-Preemptive & Preemptive Dynamic Policies** - ŌśÉ A **dynamic policy** re-decides at every decision epoch; a **preemptive** policy allows interruption. - ŌśÉ The **four families**: Static-Non-Pre, Static-Pre, Dynamic-Non-Pre, Dynamic-Pre. - ŌśÉ **SPT** (non-preemptive) minimizes $\sum F_i$ when all jobs are released at $t=0$. - ŌśÉ **SRPT** (preemptive) is **optimal** for $1 \mid r_i \mid \sum F_i$ ŌĆö Schrage's Theorem. - ŌśÉ **EDD** is optimal for $1 \mid \mid L_{max}$ and $1 \mid r_i \mid L_{max}$ regardless of preemption. - ŌśÉ **Critical Ratio** $CR_i = (d_i - t)/p_i^{rem}$ is the workhorse of modern MES software. - ŌśÉ **Slack** $S_i = d_i - t - p_i^{rem}$ triggers dispatch when it falls below zero. - ŌśÉ Preemption carries **switching overhead $\sigma$**; only beneficial if time saved $>\sigma$. - ŌśÉ **Performance bound:** $\sum F_i^{SRPT} \leq 1.5 \times \sum F_i^{OPT}$ in worst case. - ŌśÉ **NP-Hardness relief:** Preemption converts $1 \mid r_i \mid \sum F_i$ from NP-Hard to polynomial. - ŌśÉ Always draw a **Gantt chart** in long-answer solutions ŌĆö KTU awards 2ŌĆō3 marks for it. - ŌśÉ **Mean flow time** is sensitive to preemption; **maximum tardiness** is not. - ŌśÉ Starvation of long jobs is a risk under SRPT ŌĆö counter with aging or hybrid rules in practice. - ŌśÉ **Notation $1 \mid r_i \mid \sum F_i$** is Graham's three-field notation ŌĆö practice writing it. - ŌśÉ For makespan in parallel machines, **LPT** is a classic heuristic; preemption does not improve $C_{max}$.

topics.title: Stochastic Single Machine Models - Arbitrary Distributions Without Preemptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stochastic Single Machine Models - Arbitrary Distributions Without Preemptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stochastic Single Machine Models** are scheduling frameworks in which a set of $n$ jobs must be processed on a **single machine (one processor at a time)**, where the processing time of each job $i$, denoted $P_i$, is a **random variable** drawn from a known probability distribution. The qualifier **"Arbitrary Distributions"** means the $P_i$'s need not be exponential, normal, or any other specific parametric family ŌĆö only the **expected value** $E[P_i]$ (and sometimes the variance) is assumed known. The qualifier **"Without Preemptions"** means that once a job begins processing on the machine, it must run to completion without interruption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> A stochastic non-preemptive single machine scheduling problem is defined by the tuple $(1 \mid \text{stochastic}; p_i \sim F_i; r_i, d_i \mid \gamma)$ where $\gamma$ is the objective function (e.g., $\sum E[C_j]$, $\sum E[T_j]$, $E[L_{max}]$).

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **single teller counter at a small bank branch** on a busy day. Customers arrive with varied transactions (cash deposit, loan inquiry, cheque clearing). The teller cannot predict *exactly* how long each transaction will take ŌĆö sometimes a withdrawal takes 2 minutes, sometimes 8 minutes depending on technical issues. The teller also cannot stop a customer's transaction midway to serve someone else (**no preemption**). The bank manager wants to **minimize the average time customers spend waiting in the queue** (expected flow time). The rule "serve the customer with the *shortest expected* transaction first" intuitively feels right ŌĆö and mathematically, it is optimal. This is the essence of the **SEPT/WSPT** rule in stochastic single machine scheduling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Deterministic Scheduling**
> In deterministic scheduling, processing times $p_i$ are known constants. In stochastic scheduling, we optimize over **expected values of random outcomes**, which requires tools from probability theory (expectation, Wald's identity, stochastic dominance).

### 1.3 Performance Metrics Used in This Topic

| Symbol | Metric | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $C_i$ | Completion time | Random variable, time when job $i$ finishes |
| $F_i = C_i - r_i$ | Flow time | Time from release to completion |
| $T_i = \max\{0, C_i - d_i\}$ | Tardiness | Amount of time past due date |
| $L_i = C_i - d_i$ | Lateness | Signed difference from due date |
| $U_i$ | Unit penalty | 1 if tardy, 0 otherwise |
| $E[C_i]$ | Expected completion | Mean value of completion time |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Examiner Insight:** KTU questions in this module almost always ask for **(a) the expected value of the optimal schedule's objective** and **(b) the optimal sequencing rule**. Memorize WSPT, SEPT, and Moore's rule for stochastic extensions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of Stochastic Single Machine Problems

**Block 1 ŌĆö Random Processing Times**
Each job $i$ has $P_i \sim F_i(\cdot)$ with $E[P_i] = p_i$ (expected value). The actual realization is revealed **only when the job is completed** (or sometimes when it begins ŌĆö depending on the model). The expectation operator $E[\cdot]$ is the cornerstone of all analysis.

**Block 2 ŌĆö No Preemption Constraint**
A job, once started at time $S_i$, runs continuously until its actual completion. This means:

$$C_i = S_i + P_i \quad \text{(where $P_i$ is the realized random processing time)}$$

**Block 3 ŌĆö Objective Function**
A function $\gamma(\cdot)$ of the completion times (or derived quantities) that we seek to minimize in expectation. Common objectives: $\sum E[C_i]$, $\sum w_i E[C_i]$, $\sum E[T_i]$, $E[L_{max}]$, $E[\sum U_i]$.

### 2.2 The Three Canonical Sequencing Rules

**Rule 1: SEPT ŌĆö Shortest Expected Processing Time**
Order jobs in non-decreasing order of $E[P_i] = p_i$. Optimal for $1 \mid \mid \sum E[C_i]$ when all $r_i = 0$ and the objective is to minimize expected total (unweighted) completion time.

**Rule 2: WSPT ŌĆö Weighted Shortest Processing Time**
Order jobs in non-decreasing order of $\frac{p_i}{w_i}$. Optimal for $1 \mid \mid \sum w_i E[C_i]$.

**Rule 3: Hodgson's Algorithm (Stochastic Moore's Rule)**
For $1 \mid \mid E[\sum U_i]$: schedule in SEPT order, then iteratively remove the job with the largest probability of being tardy if it falls at the end. Optimal among all single-machine non-preemptive rules for stochastic due dates.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E[C_i] = E\left[\sum_{k \le i} P_{\sigma(k)}\right] = \sum_{k \le i} p_{\sigma(k)}$ | Expected completion of $i$-th job in sequence $\sigma$ | Foundation for all expected flow analyses |
| 2 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} E[C_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(n-i+1)\, p_{\sigma(i)}$ | Expected total completion time | When SEPT is optimal |
| 3 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i E[C_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{\sigma(i)} \sum_{k=1}^{i} p_{\sigma(k)}$ | Expected weighted sum of completions | WSPT optimality proof |
| 4 | Wald's Eqn: $E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i\right] = E[N]\cdot E[X]$ | For iid $X_i$, stopping time $N$ | When completion triggers next job |
| 5 | $E[L_{max}] = E[\max_i\{C_i - d_i\}]$ | Expected maximum lateness | Hard to optimize; use bounds |
| 6 | $P(T_i > 0) = 1 - F_i(C_i)$ | Probability of being tardy | Used in stochastic $U_i$ problems |
| 7 | $E[U_i] = P(C_i > d_i)$ | Expected unit penalty | Equivalent to $E[\sum U_i] = \sum P(T_i > 0)$ |
| 8 | $F_i^{\text{opt}} = E[C_i] - r_i$ | Expected flow time | Subject to minimization |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In stochastic scheduling, $E[\max\{X,Y\}] \neq \max\{E[X], E[Y]\}$. Jensen's inequality tells us $E[\max\{X,Y\}] \geq \max\{E[X],E[Y]\}$. Always use the actual distribution, not the max of expectations, for $L_{max}$-type objectives.

### 2.4 Why These Rules Are Optimal ŌĆö The "Why" Behind the Math

The optimality of SEPT/WSPT in stochastic settings rests on an **interchange argument**: if two adjacent jobs $i$ and $j$ in a sequence have $p_i > p_j$ (or $p_i/w_i > p_j/w_j$), swapping them reduces the expected sum of completion times. Critically, this argument works because **expectation is linear** ($E[aX+bY] = aE[X]+bE[Y]$) ŌĆö a property that does not hold for $\max$ or other non-linear functions. This is why the $L_{max}$ objective is *much* harder in stochastic settings.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Semiconductor wafer fabrication:** Lot processing times are stochastic; sequencing determines wafer cycle time.
- **Job-shop bottleneck machines:** Often modeled as a single stochastic server (G/G/1 queue approximations).
- **Hospital operating rooms:** Surgery durations are random; minimizing patient flow time improves throughput.
- **Cloud computing task schedulers:** Job execution times are unknown in advance; WSPT-like heuristics are used.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Expected Completion Time in Position $k$

Let $\sigma$ be a sequence of $n$ jobs, where $\sigma(i)$ denotes the job occupying position $i$. Let $P_{\sigma(i)}$ be the random processing time of that job. The completion time of the job in position $i$ is:

$$C_{\sigma(i)} = \sum_{k=1}^{i} P_{\sigma(k)}$$

Taking the expectation on both sides, and using **linearity of expectation** (a cornerstone theorem of probability ŌĆö the expected value of a sum of random variables equals the sum of their individual expected values, *regardless of dependence*):

$$E[C_{\sigma(i)}] = E\left[\sum_{k=1}^{i} P_{\sigma(k)}\right] = \sum_{k=1}^{i} E[P_{\sigma(k)}] = \sum_{k=1}^{i} p_{\sigma(k)}$$

This shows the expected completion time of the $i$-th job in a sequence is the **sum of the expected processing times of itself and all jobs before it**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Expected Completion Time $\sum E[C_j]$

We want $\sum_{i=1}^{n} E[C_{\sigma(i)}]$. Substituting the result above:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} E[C_{\sigma(i)}] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{i} p_{\sigma(k)}$$

To simplify, we recognize that the job in position $k$ contributes to the completions of all jobs in positions $k, k+1, \ldots, n$. That is, $p_{\sigma(k)}$ appears in $E[C_{\sigma(i)}]$ for all $i \geq k$. Therefore, the number of times $p_{\sigma(k)}$ is counted is $(n - k + 1)$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{i} p_{\sigma(k)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (n - k + 1)\, p_{\sigma(k)}$$

In terms of the original job indices, this is:

$$\sum_{j \in \text{jobs}} (n - \text{pos}(j) + 1)\, p_j$$

where $\text{pos}(j)$ is the position of job $j$ in the sequence. **This formula shows: jobs placed earlier contribute more times to the total cost, so jobs with small $p_j$ should be placed early.**

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Proof of SEPT Optimality (Interchange Argument)

**Claim:** For the problem $1 \mid p_i \text{ stochastic}; \text{ no preempt} \mid \sum E[C_j]$, the SEPT sequence is optimal.

**Proof by Interchange:**

Consider any sequence $\sigma$ that is *not* in SEPT order. Then there exist two jobs $i$ and $j$ in adjacent positions $k$ and $k+1$ such that:

$$p_i > p_j \quad \text{(i.e., they are out of SEPT order)}$$

We compare the contribution of these two positions to $\sum E[C_j]$ in two scenarios: **(A) original order $(i, j)$** and **(B) swapped order $(j, i)$**.

**Scenario A ŌĆö Original order $(i, j)$:**
- Job at position $k$ is $i$, contributing $p_i$ to the completion of itself and all later jobs.
- Job at position $k+1$ is $j$, contributing $p_j$ to the completion of itself and all later jobs (excluding position $k$).
- The relevant portion of $\sum E[C_j]$ involving these two jobs is (ignoring jobs before them, which are identical in both cases):

$$A = (n-k+1)\, p_i \;+\; (n-k)\, p_j$$

**Scenario B ŌĆö Swapped order $(j, i)$:**
$$B = (n-k+1)\, p_j \;+\; (n-k)\, p_i$$

**Difference $A - B$:**

$$A - B = (n-k+1)\, p_i + (n-k)\, p_j - (n-k+1)\, p_j - (n-k)\, p_i$$

Grouping $p_i$ and $p_j$ terms:

$$A - B = \left[(n-k+1) - (n-k)\right] p_i + \left[(n-k) - (n-k+1)\right] p_j$$

$$A - B = (1)\, p_i + (-1)\, p_j = p_i - p_j$$

Since by assumption $p_i > p_j$, we conclude $A - B > 0$, i.e., $A > B$.

**Conclusion:** The swapped order is *strictly better* (smaller expected total completion time) than the original order. Repeating this argument removes all inversions, and the SEPT order (no inversions remain) is therefore optimal. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Expected Weighted Sum and WSPT

For the weighted problem $\sum w_i E[C_j]$, the analogous derivation yields:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i E[C_{\sigma(i)}] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{\sigma(i)} \sum_{k=1}^{i} p_{\sigma(k)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} p_{\sigma(k)} \sum_{i=k}^{n} w_{\sigma(i)}$$

For the interchange argument with adjacent jobs $i$ at position $k$ and $j$ at position $k+1$, the relevant contribution is:

$$A_{\text{weighted}} = p_i \cdot \left[w_i + \sum_{i' \geq k+1} w_{i'}\right] + p_j \cdot \sum_{i' \geq k+2} w_{i'}$$

After simplification, the change in objective from swapping is:

$$\Delta = A - B = \left(\sum_{i' \geq k+1} w_{i'}\right) \cdot (p_i - p_j)$$

Since $\sum_{i' \geq k+1} w_{i'} > 0$, we need $p_i < p_j$ to make $\Delta < 0$ (i.e., the swap improves the objective). Equivalently, we should place the job with the smaller $p_j / w_j$ *ratio* earlier. This is the **WSPT rule**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Wald's Equation for Stopping Times

**Wald's Equation (1938):** If $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ are iid random variables with $E[|X_i|] < \infty$ and $N$ is a stopping time with $E[N] < \infty$, then:

$$E\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i\right] = E[N] \cdot E[X]$$

**Application to Single Machine:** If the number of jobs $N$ completed in a fixed horizon $T$ is treated as a stopping time, the total processing time used is $\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$ and its expectation is $E[N] \cdot \bar{p}$. This is foundational in the analysis of $G/G/1$ queue approximations of single machine stochastic systems.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Which Rule

| Objective | Optimal Rule | Conditions | KTU 2024 Module Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\sum E[C_j]$ | **SEPT** | $r_i = 0$, deterministic/stochastic $p_i$ | Core |
| $\sum w_i E[C_j]$ | **WSPT** | $r_i = 0$, arbitrary $w_i$ | Core |
| $E[\sum U_j]$ | **Hodgson's algorithm** | Stochastic due dates | Module 4 |
| $E[L_{max}]$ | **Branch & Bound** (no polynomial rule) | General case | Advanced |
| $E[\max C_i]$ | **Minimize makespan**; use SPT in expectation | $\bar{p} = \max p$ approx | Reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hodgson's Algorithm Steps (Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. Sequence jobs in **SEPT order**.
> 2. Calculate $P(T_i > 0)$ for each job assuming SEPT order.
> 3. Iteratively remove the job with the **highest $P(T_i > 0)$** until removing more jobs increases total expected penalty.
> 4. Append removed jobs at the end (in any order ŌĆö they're expected to be tardy anyway).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of a Stochastic Single Machine Scheduling System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Jobs Arrive with Stochastic Processing Times P sub i] --> B[Release Time r sub i Known]
    B --> C[Decision: Available Job Set Available]
    C --> D[Scheduler Selects Next Job using SEPT or WSPT rule]
    D --> E[Machine Starts Job at Time S sub i]
    E --> F{P_i Realized During Execution}
    F --> G[No Preemption Constraint Enforced]
    G --> H[Job Completes at C sub i = S sub i + P sub i]
    H --> I[Compute Metrics: T sub i, L sub i, U sub i]
    I --> J{C_i > d sub i?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Tardiness T sub i = C sub i - d sub i]
    J -- No --> L[U sub i = 0, on time]
    I --> M[Update Expected Objective: Sum E of C sub j, etc.]
    M --> N[Output Optimal Schedule sigma]

    classDef def fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef hi fill:#ffe0b0,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef st fill:#d0ffd0,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    class A,B,C def
    class D,E,F,G,H hi
    class I,J,K,L,M,N st
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow for Selecting the Sequencing Rule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Objective Function] --> B{Type of Objective?}
    B -- Sum of E of C sub j unweighted --> C[Use SEPT Rule]
    B -- Sum of w sub i times E of C sub j --> D[Use WSPT Rule]
    B -- E of Sum of U sub j --> E[Use Hodgson Algorithm]
    B -- E of L sub max --> F[Use Branch and Bound or Heuristics]
    C --> G[Order by increasing p sub i]
    D --> H[Order by increasing p sub i over w sub i]
    E --> I[Start SEPT, then iteratively prune highest P T sub i > 0]
    F --> J[Enumerate candidate sequences, compute expected L sub max]
    G --> K[Output Optimal Sequence sigma]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Validate No Preemption: C sub i = S sub i + P sub i]

    classDef rule fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000
    classDef obj fill:#e0e0ff,stroke:#333399,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    class A,B,F,J,L out
    class C,D,E,G,H,I obj
    class K rule
```

### 4.3 State Transition Diagram for a Job's Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Waiting
    Waiting --> Ready: r sub i reached
    Ready --> Processing: Scheduler selects
    Processing --> Processing: No preemption
    Processing --> Completed: P sub i realized
    Completed --> Tardy: C sub i > d sub i
    Completed --> OnTime: C sub i <= d sub i
    Tardy --> [*]: U sub i = 1
    OnTime --> [*]: U sub i = 0

    note right of Processing: Cannot be interrupted\nStochastic duration P sub i
    note right of Tardy: T sub i = C sub i - d sub i
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Why SEPT/WSPT Work

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
    I1[Job set J = 1 to n]
    I2[Distributions F sub i for P sub i]
    I3[Expected values p sub i = E of P sub i]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Layer]
    P1[Linearity of Expectation]
    P2[Interchange Argument]
    P3[Pairwise Swap Comparison]
    P4[No Preemption Constraint]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
    O1[Optimal Sequence sigma star]
    O2[Expected Objective: sum w sub i E of C sub i]
    O3[Bounds on E of L sub max]
    end
    I1 --> P2
    I2 --> P3
    I3 --> P1
    P1 --> P3
    P3 --> P2
    P2 --> O1
    P4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a stochastic single machine scheduling problem with arbitrary distributions. What is meant by "no preemption" in this context?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A stochastic single machine scheduling problem involves $n$ jobs to be processed on a single machine, where the processing time $P_i$ of each job $i$ is a **random variable** with a known (but arbitrary, i.e., not necessarily exponential or normal) probability distribution $F_i$. Only the expected value $p_i = E[P_i]$ is typically assumed known. **"No preemption"** means that once a job starts processing on the machine at time $S_i$, it must run continuously to completion ŌĆö it cannot be interrupted to process another job and resumed later. Thus, the completion time is the sum $C_i = S_i + P_i$, where $P_i$ is the realized (random) processing time. The objective is to find a sequence $\sigma$ that minimizes an expected-value function such as $\sum E[C_j]$, $\sum w_i E[C_j]$, or $E[L_{max}]$.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the SEPT sequencing rule and the optimality condition for which it is the optimal solution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**SEPT (Shortest Expected Processing Time) Rule:** Sequence the jobs in **non-decreasing order of their expected processing times** $p_i = E[P_i]$, i.e., $p_{\sigma(1)} \le p_{\sigma(2)} \le \cdots \le p_{\sigma(n)}$.

**Optimality Condition:** SEPT is optimal for the problem $1 \mid p_i \text{ stochastic}; \text{ no preemption} \mid \sum E[C_j]$ when all jobs are available at time zero ($r_i = 0$ for all $i$). It minimizes the expected total (unweighted) completion time. The proof uses an interchange argument: swapping any two out-of-order jobs strictly reduces the objective, hence the SEPT order is optimal.

---

### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each, with internal choice. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks. Choose ONE out of two alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Consider 4 jobs to be processed on a single machine. The processing times are stochastic with the following expected values and probability distributions:**

| Job $i$ | $E[P_i] = p_i$ | $P(P_i = 1)$ | $P(P_i = 2)$ | $P(P_i = 3)$ | Due date $d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0 | 0.2 | 0.6 | 0.2 | 4 |
| 2 | 1.5 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.0 | 3 |
| 3 | 2.5 | 0.0 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 6 |
| 4 | 1.0 | 0.7 | 0.3 | 0.0 | 2 |

**(a)** Find the optimal sequence to minimize the **expected total completion time** $\sum E[C_j]$. Compute the optimal expected value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the optimal sequence to minimize the **expected number of tardy jobs** $E[\sum U_j]$ using Hodgson's algorithm. Show the iterative steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

### Part (a) ŌĆö SEPT Optimization (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the expected processing times:
$p_1 = 2.0,\ p_2 = 1.5,\ p_3 = 2.5,\ p_4 = 1.0$

**Step 2:** Apply SEPT rule ŌĆö sort in non-decreasing order of $p_i$:

$$p_4 = 1.0 < p_2 = 1.5 < p_1 = 2.0 < p_3 = 2.5$$

So the optimal sequence is $\sigma^* = (4, 2, 1, 3)$.

**Step 3:** Compute expected completion times:
- $E[C_4] = p_4 = 1.0$
- $E[C_2] = p_4 + p_2 = 1.0 + 1.5 = 2.5$
- $E[C_1] = p_4 + p_2 + p_1 = 1.0 + 1.5 + 2.0 = 4.5$
- $E[C_3] = p_4 + p_2 + p_1 + p_3 = 1.0 + 1.5 + 2.0 + 2.5 = 7.0$

**Step 4:** Total expected completion time:

$$\sum E[C_j] = 1.0 + 2.5 + 4.5 + 7.0 = 15.0$$

Using the formula $\sum (n - k + 1) p_{\sigma(k)}$:

$$4 \cdot 1.0 + 3 \cdot 1.5 + 2 \cdot 2.0 + 1 \cdot 2.5 = 4.0 + 4.5 + 4.0 + 2.5 = 15.0 \checkmark$$

**Answer:** Optimal sequence $(4, 2, 1, 3)$ with $\sum E[C_j] = 15.0$.

---

### Part (b) ŌĆö Hodgson's Algorithm (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Begin with the SEPT order: $\sigma_0 = (4, 2, 1, 3)$.

**Step 2:** Compute the probability of tardiness for each job assuming this sequence. We approximate by using the **expected completion time** as the actual completion time for the probability calculation (this is the standard assumption when the full joint distribution is unavailable):

| Job | $E[C_i]$ | $d_i$ | $C_i > d_i$? | $P(T_i > 0)$ (approximate using $C_i$ and $F_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 1.0 | 2 | No | 0 |
| 2 | 2.5 | 3 | No | 0 |
| 1 | 4.5 | 4 | Yes | $P(P_1 \ge 1) = 1$ |
| 3 | 7.0 | 6 | Yes | $P(P_3 \ge 0) = 1$ |

More precisely, $P(T_i > 0) = P(C_i > d_i)$. Assuming $C_i \approx E[C_i]$:
- Job 4: $P(C_4 > 2) = P(P_4 > 2) = 0$
- Job 2: $P(C_2 > 3) = P(P_4 + P_2 > 3) = P(P_2 > 2) = 0$ (since $P_2 \in \{1, 2\}$)
- Job 1: $P(C_1 > 4) = P(P_4 + P_2 + P_1 > 4) = P(P_1 > 1) = 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.4$
- Job 3: $P(C_3 > 6) = P(P_4 + P_2 + P_1 + P_3 > 6) = 1.0$ (always tardy)

**Step 3:** Identify the job with the highest $P(T_i > 0)$: **Job 3** with $P = 1.0$.

**Step 4:** Move Job 3 to the end. New sequence: $(4, 2, 1)$, with Job 3 appended at the end.

**Step 5:** Re-evaluate the sequence $(4, 2, 1, 3)$:
- $E[C_4] = 1.0,\ E[C_2] = 2.5,\ E[C_1] = 4.5,\ E[C_3] = 7.0$
- Expected number of tardy jobs: $E[\sum U_j] \approx 0 + 0 + 0.4 + 1.0 = 1.4$

**Step 6:** Check if removing Job 1 helps. Move Job 1 to end: $(4, 2, 3, 1)$:
- $E[C_4] = 1.0,\ E[C_2] = 2.5,\ E[C_3] = 5.0,\ E[C_1] = 7.0$
- Tardy jobs: Job 1 ($P=0.4$), Job 3 ($P \approx 1.0$); Job 2 still on time. Expected tardy: $0.4 + 1.0 = 1.4$.

No improvement ŌĆö stop.

**Answer:** Optimal stochastic sequence $\sigma^* = (4, 2, 1, 3)$ with $E[\sum U_j] \approx 1.4$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Five jobs are to be processed on a single machine. The expected processing times and weights are:**

| Job $i$ | $p_i$ | $w_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 |
| 3 | 5 | 1 |
| 4 | 3 | 4 |
| 5 | 1 | 5 |

The processing times are stochastic with arbitrary distributions (only $p_i$ known). All jobs are available at $t=0$.

**(a)** Determine the **optimal sequence** to minimize $\sum w_i E[C_j]$ using the WSPT rule. Compute the optimal expected weighted completion time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove, using the interchange argument, why WSPT is optimal for the stochastic problem $1 \mid p_i \text{ stochastic} \mid \sum w_i E[C_j]$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

### Part (a) ŌĆö WSPT Sequence (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the ratio $p_i / w_i$ for each job:

| Job $i$ | $p_i$ | $w_i$ | $p_i / w_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 2 | 2.000 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | 0.667 |
| 3 | 5 | 1 | 5.000 |
| 4 | 3 | 4 | 0.750 |
| 5 | 1 | 5 | 0.200 |

**Step 2:** Sort in non-decreasing order of $p_i / w_i$:

$$\frac{p_5}{w_5} = 0.200 < \frac{p_2}{w_2} = 0.667 < \frac{p_4}{w_4} = 0.750 < \frac{p_1}{w_1} = 2.000 < \frac{p_3}{w_3} = 5.000$$

**Optimal WSPT sequence:** $\sigma^* = (5, 2, 4, 1, 3)$.

**Step 3:** Compute expected weighted completion times:

- $E[C_5] = p_5 = 1$
- $E[C_2] = p_5 + p_2 = 1 + 2 = 3$
- $E[C_4] = p_5 + p_2 + p_4 = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6$
- $E[C_1] = p_5 + p_2 + p_4 + p_1 = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10$
- $E[C_3] = p_5 + p_2 + p_4 + p_1 + p_3 = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 15$

**Step 4:** Compute $\sum w_i E[C_i]$:

$$\sum w_i E[C_i] = 5(1) + 3(3) + 4(6) + 2(10) + 1(15) = 5 + 9 + 24 + 20 + 15 = 73$$

**Answer:** WSPT sequence $(5, 2, 4, 1, 3)$ with $\sum w_i E[C_i] = 73$.

---

### Part (b) ŌĆö Proof of WSPT Optimality (7 marks)

**Theorem:** WSPT is optimal for $1 \mid p_i \text{ stochastic}, \text{ no preempt} \mid \sum w_i E[C_j]$.

**Proof:**

Suppose sequence $\sigma$ is optimal but is **not** in WSPT order. Then there exist two jobs $i$ and $j$ in adjacent positions $k$ and $k+1$ with $p_i / w_i > p_j / w_j$, i.e., the job with the higher ratio is scheduled before the job with the lower ratio.

Let $S = \sum_{l < k} p_{\sigma(l)}$ be the expected processing time of all jobs before position $k$ (same in both original and swapped sequences). The expected completion times in the **original** order $(i, j)$ at positions $k$ and $k+1$ are:

$$E[C_i^{(\text{orig})}] = S + p_i, \quad E[C_j^{(\text{orig})}] = S + p_i + p_j$$

In the **swapped** order $(j, i)$ at positions $k$ and $k+1$:

$$E[C_j^{(\text{swap})}] = S + p_j, \quad E[C_i^{(\text{swap})}] = S + p_j + p_i$$

The remaining jobs in positions $k+2, \ldots, n$ are unaffected. So the change in objective is:

$$\Delta = \left[w_i E[C_i^{(\text{swap})}] + w_j E[C_j^{(\text{swap})}]\right] - \left[w_i E[C_i^{(\text{orig})}] + w_j E[C_j^{(\text{orig})}]\right]$$

$$\Delta = w_i (S + p_j + p_i) + w_j (S + p_j) - w_i (S + p_i) - w_j (S + p_i + p_j)$$

$$\Delta = w_i p_j + w_j p_j - w_j p_i - w_j p_j = w_i p_j - w_j p_i$$

Since the original had $p_i / w_i > p_j / w_j$, cross-multiplying (with $w_i, w_j > 0$) gives $p_i w_j > p_j w_i$, so:

$$\Delta = w_i p_j - w_j p_i = p_j w_i - p_i w_j < 0$$

This means the swap **strictly decreases** the objective ŌĆö contradicting the optimality of $\sigma$. Therefore, an optimal sequence has no such inversions, i.e., it is in WSPT order. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Misapplying WSPT when due dates exist:** If $d_i$ are non-trivial, WSPT does **not** minimize tardiness. Use Hodgson's algorithm.
> 2. **Confusing $E[\max X_i]$ with $\max E[X_i]$:** For $L_{max}$ objectives, you cannot just take max of expectations. Jensen's inequality is your enemy here.
> 3. **Forgetting the no-preemption constraint:** When computing $E[C_i]$, always sum *all* preceding $p$'s, never use remaining-time shortcuts.
> 4. **Wrong WSPT ratio direction:** Some students order by $w_i / p_i$ instead of $p_i / w_i$. **Smaller ratio = scheduled earlier.**
> 5. **Ignoring linearity of expectation:** Using $E[XY] = E[X]E[Y]$ when $X$ and $Y$ are dependent. This is a fatal error; the derivation only works because of $E[\sum] = \sum E[\cdot]$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section as a one-page revision sheet before your exam!**

- **Stochastic single machine** = one machine, $n$ jobs, $P_i$ random, **no preemption** allowed.
- Three key objectives to remember: $\sum E[C_j]$, $\sum w_i E[C_j]$, $E[\sum U_j]$.
- **SEPT** = sort by $p_i \uparrow$ ŌåÆ minimizes $\sum E[C_j]$.
- **WSPT** = sort by $p_i / w_i \uparrow$ ŌåÆ minimizes $\sum w_i E[C_j]$.
- **Hodgson's Algorithm** = SEPT first, then iteratively remove highest $P(T_i > 0)$ ŌåÆ minimizes $E[\sum U_j]$.
- **Wald's Equation:** $E[\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i] = E[N] \cdot E[X]$ for iid $X_i$ and stopping time $N$.
- **Interchange argument** = the proof technique; works only because of **linearity of expectation**.
- The formula $\sum E[C_{\sigma(i)}] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (n - k + 1)\, p_{\sigma(k)}$ is the **master equation** for SEPT-type problems.
- $E[\max\{X, Y\}] \geq \max\{E[X], E[Y]\}$ ŌĆö beware of this for $L_{max}$ problems.
- The optimal expected value (e.g., $\sum w_i E[C_i] = 73$ in the WSPT example) is **not** the actual realized value in any single run ŌĆö it is the **average over many realizations**.
- For **arbitrary distributions**, only $E[P_i]$ is needed for the SEPT/WSPT results ŌĆö no distributional form is required.
- **KTU trick question alert:** "What is the optimal sequence for $E[L_{max}]$ with stochastic processing times?" ŌåÆ No polynomial rule exists; use heuristics or B&B.
- **Mnemonic:** "**S**EPT = **S**hortest **E**xpected **P**rocessing **T**ime" and "**W**SPT = **W**eighted **SPT**" ŌĆö same logic, just add weights in the denominator.
- **Last-line defense in exam:** If you forget the rule, write down the interchange argument generically ŌĆö it earns partial marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arbitrary Distributions with Preemptions - the Gittins Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Arbitrary Distributions with Preemptions - the Gittins Index ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gittins Index (Definition):** For a stochastic job whose remaining processing time is governed by an *arbitrary* (general) distribution, the **Gittins Index** $G(x)$ of the job when it is in state $x$ is defined as the *maximum expected discounted reward obtainable per unit of remaining processing time* when the job is processed until a (possibly random) stopping time chosen optimally, divided by the expected remaining processing time. Mathematically, for a job currently in state $x$ with a known (or partially observed) history: $$G(x) \;=\; \max_{\tau \,\geq\, 0} \;\; \frac{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\int_{0}^{\tau} e^{-\beta t}\, dt \;\Bigm|\; x\,\right]}{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\tau \;\Bigm|\; x\,\right]}$$ where $\beta > 0$ is the **discount rate**, $\tau$ is a stopping time measurable with respect to the filtration generated by the job's evolution, and the expectation is taken conditional on state $x$. > [!NOTE] > **Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) Problem:** A classic sequential decision-making problem in which a decision-maker (the "player") must choose, at each decision epoch, one of $N$ independent stochastic processes (the "arms") to observe, receiving a *reward* proportional to the time spent on that arm. Each arm is a job with an unknown/arbitrary processing-time distribution. > [!NOTE] > **Indexability:** A bandit (or job) is called **indexable** if, for every discount factor $\beta$, the optimal stationary policy can be described by a *single scalar function* of the current state ŌĆö namely, the **Gittins Index** $G(x)$. Indexability guarantees that an index-based greedy policy is globally optimal. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **casino manager** with $N$ slot machines (one-armed bandits). Each machine is in some internal "state" ŌĆö perhaps it is *hot* (about to pay) or *cold* (unlikely to pay). At each moment, you can place exactly one coin in one machine. The catch: each machine's payout time follows a *completely unknown* (arbitrary) distribution, and you may have already observed part of the play. - The **Gittins Index** is, intuitively, the **"wager-priority"** of each machine: it tells you *which single machine to play next* in order to maximize total discounted reward. - Higher index ŌåÆ "more attractive" ŌåÆ play first. - The remarkable result (Gittins, 1979) is that this **greedy myopic** policy is, in fact, **globally optimal** ŌĆö even though each machine's future evolution is correlated with its past. In the scheduling context: each **job** is a bandit; the **state** is the elapsed processing time (or any sufficient statistic of the residual service-time distribution). Choosing the job with the *largest Gittins index* at every preemptive decision epoch minimizes the total expected (discounted or long-run average) completion-related cost. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric ŌĆō Discounted Criterion:** A continuous-time discount rate **$\beta > 0$** is used so that a unit of reward earned at time $t$ is worth $e^{-\beta t}$ in present-value terms. > > **Standard Metric ŌĆō Long-Run Average Criterion:** When $\beta \to 0$, the discounted problem reduces to a **time-average (ergodic) cost minimization** problem. > > **No physical constants** appear in this purely probabilistic/operational-research construction; the only "constants" are the discount rate $\beta$ and the prior hyperparameters of each job's service-time distribution. ### 1.4 Why Preemptions? > [!NOTE] > **Preemption** means that processing of a job may be interrupted at any time (without cost) and resumed later, possibly on a different machine, with all past work preserved. Under preemptions, the **Gittins Index** policy becomes a *non-anticipating* index rule that depends only on the *current* state of each job ŌĆö making the otherwise intractable joint dynamic programming problem separable and solvable in polynomial time for the single-machine case. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Multi-Armed Bandit Model ŌĆö Formal Setup Consider $N$ independent stochastic jobs, indexed $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$, each with a service-time distribution that is *partially* known through a sufficient statistic $x_i \in \mathcal{X}_i$. The system evolves in continuous time: 1. At every decision epoch $t$, the scheduler selects **one** job $i(t)$ to process. 2. The chosen job $i(t)$ earns reward at unit rate while it is being served; all other jobs earn nothing. 3. The scheduler may **preempt** the running job at any time and switch to another; upon resumption, the chosen job continues from where it left off, with its internal state unchanged. 4. The objective is to **maximize** the expected total discounted reward (or, equivalently, minimize a flow-time-like cost). ### 2.2 Indexability and the Gittins Index Theorem **Indexability Condition (per job $i$):** A job $i$ with state space $\mathcal{X}_i$ is **indexable** if there exists a non-negative function $G_i : \mathcal{X}_i \to \mathbb{R}_{+}$ such that, for every threshold $\lambda \geq 0$, the policy "*process job $i$ iff $G_i(x_i) \geq \lambda$*" is the optimal policy for the **single-job** problem in which the job earns reward at unit rate while being processed and pays cost $\lambda$ per unit time, until an optimal stopping time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gittins Index Theorem (1979):** *If every job in the system is indexable, then the following index policy is optimal for the joint multi-armed bandit problem: at every decision epoch, process a job with the largest Gittins Index, breaking ties arbitrarily.* This is one of the deepest results in stochastic optimization ŌĆö it converts a **coupled, high-dimensional** dynamic program into a **collection of independent, one-dimensional** calculations, one per job. ### 2.3 The Two Equivalent Definitions of the Gittins Index The Gittins Index admits two complementary characterizations: **Definition A ŌĆö "Fair Game" / Marginal Productivity (most common):** $$G(x) \;=\; \min_{\tau \,\geq\, 0} \;\; \frac{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\int_{0}^{\tau} e^{-\beta t}\, dt \;\Bigm|\; x\,\right]}{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,1 - e^{-\beta \tau} \;\Bigm|\; x\,\right]}$$ where $\tau$ ranges over *all stopping times* and the *minimum* is taken. The index equals the smallest "fair price" $\lambda$ at which the discounted-reward "investment" in continuing the job equals the discounted "option value" of stopping. **Definition B ŌĆö Virtual Delay / Reset Interpretation:** The Gittins index of a state $x$ equals the **expected discounted delay** until the next "fictitious" stopping epoch, under the optimal policy that *resets* the job to a known terminal state at the stopping time. This is sometimes called the **"cake-eating"** representation. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Domain | |---|---|---| | $\beta$ | Continuous-time discount rate | $\beta > 0$, units: $1/\text{time}$ | | $x$ | Current state of a job (e.g., elapsed time, sufficient statistic) | $\mathcal{X}$ | | $\tau$ | Optimal stopping time | $\tau \geq 0$ (random) | | $G(x)$ | Gittins Index of a job in state $x$ | $\mathbb{R}_{+}$ | | $N$ | Number of independent jobs (arms) | $\mathbb{N}$ | | $V_{i}(x)$ | Optimal value function for job $i$ in state $x$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | | $C_{i}(x)$ | Optimal continuation cost for job $i$ | $\mathbb{R}_{+}$ | | $\lambda$ | Threshold (fair-game price) | $\mathbb{R}_{+}$ | | $F(\cdot)$ | CDF of the service-time distribution | $[0,1]$ | | $\bar{F}(\cdot)$ | Survivor function $\bar{F}(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | $[0,1]$ | | $h(t)$ | Hazard rate $h(t) = f(t) / \bar{F}(t)$ | $\mathbb{R}_{+}$ | | $m$ | Mean service time $\mathbb{E}[T]$ | time | | $\gamma$ | Completion-cost coefficient (flow-time weight) | $\mathbb{R}_{+}$ | ### 2.5 Boundary & Limiting Conditions - **No-preemption limit:** As the service-time distribution becomes degenerate (deterministic), the Gittins Index reduces to the **Smith's rule (SRPT-like)** priority: shortest-remaining-processing-time (SRPT) is optimal in the deterministic case. - **Exponential limit:** For *exponential* service times, the memoryless property implies the Gittins Index is *constant* (state-independent); the optimal policy reduces to the classical **$c\mu$-rule** of Klimov / Buyukkoc. - **Deterministic limit ($\beta \to 0$):** The discounted criterion converges to the **long-run average** criterion; the index remains well-defined as the "expected delay per unit service" under an optimal stopping rule. - **Asymptotic / heavy-tailed case:** For distributions with regularly-varying tails, the Gittins Index exhibits a **state-dependent drift**; jobs deep in their tails acquire *lower* indices and are de-prioritized ŌĆö providing a rigorous justification for the "discriminate" scheduling of heavy-tailed workloads. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Cloud Computing** | VM consolidation, multi-tenant scheduling, job-shop dispatch with unknown job lengths | | **Manufacturing** | Preemptive scheduling on a single bottleneck machine with stochastic processing times | | **Wireless Networks** | Opportunistic scheduling in time-varying channels (see further: Whittle's index for restless bandits) | | **Healthcare / OR** | Patient prioritization in triage with unknown treatment durations | | **Operations Research** | Foundation for **restless bandit** theory (Whittle, 1988) and index-based heuristics in $N$-machine settings | | **Queueing Theory** | Optimal control of the $M/G/1$ queue and its multi-class generalizations | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Gittins Index as a Stopping-Problem Ratio We start with a single job in state $x$. Define the *one-job* problem: at each instant, we either *process* the job (earning discounted reward at rate $e^{-\beta t}$) or *stop* (earning nothing more from this job). Let $\tau$ be the (random) time at which we stop, measurable with respect to the natural filtration $\mathcal{F}_t$ generated by the job's evolution. **Step 1 ŌĆö Express the discounted reward obtained by processing until $\tau$.** The total expected discounted reward is the expected integral of the reward rate from $0$ to $\tau$: $$R(\tau \mid x) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\int_{0}^{\tau} e^{-\beta t}\, dt \;\Bigm|\; x\,\right]$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Express the expected "time" (elapsed wall-clock) spent on the job before stopping.** $$T(\tau \mid x) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\tau \;\Bigm|\; x\,\right]$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Form the ratio, and choose the optimal stopping time.** The Gittins index is the *largest* such ratio ŌĆö i.e., the best average discounted reward obtainable per unit of "real time" consumed: $$G(x) \;=\; \sup_{\tau \,\geq\, 0} \;\; \frac{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\int_{0}^{\tau} e^{-\beta t}\, dt \;\Bigm|\; x\,\right]}{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\tau \;\Bigm|\; x\,\right]}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Equivalent "fair-game" (min) form via the optional-stopping theorem.** Let $M_t = e^{-\beta t}$ denote the discount factor. By the *optional-sampling identity*, for any stopping time $\tau$ and any integrable process $Z_t$, $$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,M_\tau Z_\tau\,\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\int_{0}^{\tau} M_t\, dZ_t\,\right] + \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,M_0 Z_0\,\right]$$ Choosing $Z_t = 1$ (constant) gives $$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{-\beta \tau}\,\right] \;=\; 1 - \beta\,\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\int_{0}^{\tau} e^{-\beta t}\, dt\,\right]$$ Substituting into the ratio, the **sup** (over $\tau$) is equivalent to a **min** over a complementary quantity: $$G(x) \;=\; \min_{\tau \,\geq\, 0} \;\; \frac{1 - \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{-\beta \tau} \;\bigm\vert\; x\,\right]}{\beta\,\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\tau \;\bigm\vert\; x\,\right]}$$ This is the **"price-of-a-continuation-option"** interpretation: $G(x)$ is the smallest $\lambda$ for which the job's expected discounted remaining reward *exactly* compensates the discounted cost of the wall-clock time consumed. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Indexability of Deterministic Residual Times Consider the simplest non-trivial case: a job whose *remaining* service time is a known constant $r > 0$ (a "deterministic" job in the middle of execution). The job is processed at unit rate; once the work is done, the job leaves the system. The Gittins index should intuitively equal **1** (the job always earns reward at unit rate until completion). **Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the stopping problem.** We may choose to stop at any time $\tau \in [0, r]$. Beyond $r$ the job no longer exists, so the natural optimal stopping time is $\tau = r$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the numerator.** $$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\int_{0}^{r} e^{-\beta t}\, dt\,\right] \;=\; \left[\,-\tfrac{1}{\beta} e^{-\beta t}\,\right]_{0}^{r} \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-\beta r}}{\beta}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the denominator.** $$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\tau\,\right] \;=\; r$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Form the index.** $$G_{\text{det}}(r) \;=\; \frac{(1 - e^{-\beta r}) / \beta}{r} \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-\beta r}}{\beta r}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Take limits to confirm the boundary behaviour.** - As $\beta \to 0$: expand $e^{-\beta r} \approx 1 - \beta r + \tfrac{1}{2}\beta^2 r^2 - \cdots$ to get $$G_{\text{det}}(r) \;\longrightarrow\; 1 \quad \text{as } \beta \to 0$$ - As $\beta \to \infty$: $G_{\text{det}}(r) \to 0$ (heavy discounting makes near-term processing worthless). **Interpretation:** A job with a *short* remaining deterministic residual time has a *higher* index than one with a *long* residual time ŌĆö recovering, in the limit $\beta \to 0$, the celebrated **SRPT (Shortest Remaining Processing Time)** rule for deterministic service times. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Gittins Index for the Exponential Case (Memoryless Limit) Suppose the *residual* service time of a job is exponentially distributed with rate $\mu > 0$, i.e., the job is "fresh" with rate $\mu$ from any state. Then by the memoryless property, the Gittins index must be **constant** in $x$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Choose the natural stopping time.** For an exponential residual of rate $\mu$, the job completes at an exponential time $T \sim \text{Exp}(\mu)$. There is no informative stopping time other than the job's natural completion, so set $\tau = T$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Numerator.** $$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\int_{0}^{T} e^{-\beta t}\, dt\,\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\frac{1 - e^{-\beta T}}{\beta}\,\right] \;=\; \frac{1}{\beta}\!\left[\,1 - \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,e^{-\beta T}\,\right]\,\right]$$ For $T \sim \text{Exp}(\mu)$: $\mathbb{E}[e^{-\beta T}] = \mu / (\mu + \beta)$. So the numerator is $$\frac{1}{\beta}\!\left[\,1 - \frac{\mu}{\mu + \beta}\,\right] \;=\; \frac{1}{\mu + \beta}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Denominator.** $$\mathbb{E}[T] \;=\; \frac{1}{\mu}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Form the index.** $$G_{\text{exp}}(\mu) \;=\; \frac{1/(\mu + \beta)}{1/\mu} \;=\; \frac{\mu}{\mu + \beta}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Limit $\beta \to 0$: $G_{\text{exp}}(\mu) \to 1$.** This is exactly the **$c\mu$-rule** (Klimov, 1974) in disguise: under exponential service times, the Gittins index reduces to a constant, and the index policy degenerates to "always serve the job with the highest $\mu$" ŌĆö i.e., the job whose service is most efficient. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö The Dynamic-Programming / Whittle Recursion For an *arbitrary* residual-time distribution with known CDF $F$ and known age $a$, the Gittins index can be computed by the following **dynamic-programming recursion** (Varaiya, Walrand, Buyukkoc, 1985; also used in Nino-Mora, 2007): **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the value of continuing to process the job from age $a$ for an additional $s$ seconds and then stopping.** $$V(a, s) \;=\; \int_{0}^{s} e^{-\beta t}\, dt \;+\; e^{-\beta s}\!\left[\,F(a+s) \cdot 0 \;+\; \int_{0}^{\infty} V(a+s+t, t)\, dF(t \mid a+s)\,\right]$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö The "continuation cost" of age $a$.** $$C(a) \;=\; \min_{s \,\geq\, 0} \;\; V(a, s)$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö The Gittins index as the marginal cost of "one more second" of processing.** $$G(a) \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial s}\bigg|_{s=0}\, V(a, s) \quad \text{(subject to discretization)}$$ For a discretized, tabular residual-time distribution $\{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$ (with $p_k$ = probability that the *remaining* service is exactly $k$ units), the recursion collapses to a *finite* set of linear equations solvable in $O(n^2)$ time per job. ### 3.5 Summary Table ŌĆö Index by Distribution Family | Residual-time distribution | Closed-form Gittins Index $G(x)$ | Limiting policy | |---|---|---| | Deterministic, remaining $r$ | $(1 - e^{-\beta r})/(\beta r)$ | SRPT ($\beta \to 0$) | | Exponential, rate $\mu$ | $\mu / (\mu + \beta)$ | $c\mu$-rule ($\beta \to 0$) | | Hyperexponential (mixture) | Convex combination of component indices | Weighted $c\mu$ | | Uniform on $[0, b]$ | $2(1 - e^{-\beta b}(1 + \beta b)) / (\beta^2 b^2)$ | Threshold on $b - a$ | | General (arbitrary) | Computed via DP recursion (no closed form) | Index policy (greedy) | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Architecture of the Gittins Index Decision Framework ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["JOB i ENTERS SYSTEM"] --> nodeB["INITIAL STATE x_i
e.g. age a = 0"] nodeB --> nodeC["MAINTAIN HAZARD
RECORD h_i t"] nodeC --> nodeD{"DECISION EPOCH t"} nodeD --> nodeE["COMPUTE G_i x_i
for every job i"] nodeE --> nodeF["GLOBAL ARGMIN / ARGMAX
i star = argmax G_i"] nodeF --> nodeG{"PREEMPTION
NEEDED?"} nodeG -- YES --> nodeH["SWITCH TO JOB i_star"] nodeG -- NO --> nodeI["CONTINUE CURRENT JOB"] nodeH --> nodeJ["PROCESS UNTIL NEXT
DECISION EPOCH"] nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK["UPDATE STATE
x_i to x_i prime"] nodeK --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeL["SINGLE JOB DP
STOPPING RECURSION"] nodeL --> nodeE ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Gittins Index Computation ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["INPUT
Residual-time distribution F
Current state x, discount beta"] --> nodeB["SUFFICIENT STATISTIC
e.g. age a, hazard h a"] nodeB --> nodeC["VALUE FUNCTION V a s
Process s more units then stop"] nodeC --> nodeD["OPTIMAL STOPPING
s star = argmin V a s"] nodeD --> nodeE["MARGINAL REWARD
dV a s star divided by ds"] nodeE --> nodeF["GITTINS INDEX G a
output scalar"] nodeF --> nodeG["INDEX TABLE
G a for a in X"] nodeG --> nodeH["INDEX POLICY
Serve i with max G a"] ``` ### 4.3 Comparison: Gittins Index vs. Classical Heuristics ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["SINGLE-MACHINE PREEMPTIVE
SCHEDULING PROBLEM"] --> nodeB{"SERVICE-TIME
DISTRIBUTION?"} nodeB -- "DETERMINISTIC" --> nodeC["SRPT
Shortest Remaining
Processing Time"] nodeB -- "EXPONENTIAL" --> nodeD["c MU RULE
Highest service
rate first"] nodeB -- "ARBITRARY /
UNKNOWN" --> nodeE["GITTINS INDEX POLICY
Greedy on G x"] nodeC --> nodeF["OPTIMAL"] nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeA --> nodeG["RESTLESS BANDITS
Multi-machine non-idling"] nodeG --> nodeH["WHITTLE INDEX POLICY
Heuristic; asymptotically optimal"] ``` ### 4.4 Information-Flow Diagram of the MAB Indexability Proof ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["JOBS i = 1 ... N
Arbitrary distributions"] --> nodeB["FOR EACH JOB i
solve 1D stopping problem"] nodeB --> nodeC["COMPUTE G_i x_i
scalar function of state"] nodeC --> nodeD{"INDEXABILITY
VERIFIED?"} nodeD -- "YES" --> nodeE["APPLY GITTINS THEOREM
Greedy = globally optimal"] nodeD -- "NO" --> nodeF["USE WHITTLE INDEX
or other heuristic"] nodeE --> nodeG["DYNAMIC POLICY
i star t = argmax G_i x_i t"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 4]** *Define the Gittins Index for a stochastic job with an arbitrary residual service-time distribution. State precisely the Gittins Index Theorem (1979) and the indexability condition.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Gittins Index Definition:** For a job in state $x$, the Gittins index is the *supremum*, over all stopping times $\tau \geq 0$, of the ratio of the expected discounted reward obtainable by processing the job until $\tau$ to the expected wall-clock time $\mathbb{E}[\tau]$: > $$G(x) \;=\; \sup_{\tau \,\geq\, 0}\;\frac{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\int_{0}^{\tau} e^{-\beta t}\, dt \;\bigm\vert\; x\,\right]}{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\,\tau \;\bigm\vert\; x\,\right]}$$ > > **Gittins Index Theorem:** *If every job in the multi-armed bandit is indexable, then the policy that, at every decision epoch, processes a job with the largest Gittins Index, is globally optimal for the discounted (and, by taking $\beta \to 0$, the long-run average) criterion.* > > **Indexability:** A job is *indexable* if, for every threshold $\lambda \geq 0$, the policy "*process iff $G(x) \geq \lambda$*" is optimal for the corresponding single-job threshold problem. (3 marks ŌĆö one each for definition, theorem, and indexability.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 4]** *With a suitable state space, derive the Gittins Index for a job whose remaining service time is exponentially distributed with rate $\mu$. What well-known scheduling rule does this special case reduce to as $\beta \to 0$?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Setting $\tau \sim \text{Exp}(\mu)$ and using $\mathbb{E}[e^{-\beta T}] = \mu/(\mu+\beta)$: > > **Numerator:** $\frac{1}{\beta}\!\left(1 - \frac{\mu}{\mu+\beta}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu+\beta}$ > > **Denominator:** $\frac{1}{\mu}$ > > **Index:** $G(\mu) = \frac{\mu}{\mu+\beta}$ > > In the limit $\beta \to 0$: $G(\mu) \to 1$, and the index policy reduces to the **$c\mu$-rule (Klimov, 1974)** ŌĆö always serve the job with the highest service rate. (1.5 marks for derivation, 1.5 marks for limit identification.) --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each; Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model, Module 4]** **(a)** *Formulate the single-machine preemptive scheduling problem as a continuous-time multi-armed bandit (MAB). State the Gittins Index Theorem with its precise hypotheses. Show, by direct calculation, that the Gittins Index of a deterministic job with remaining processing time $r$ is $G(r) = (1 - e^{-\beta r})/(\beta r)$, and verify that the index-policy reduces to the SRPT rule in the limit $\beta \to 0$.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Consider a stochastic job whose remaining service time follows a mixture-of-exponentials distribution: with probability $p$ it is $\text{Exp}(\mu_1)$ and with probability $1 - p$ it is $\text{Exp}(\mu_2)$. State and justify the form of the Gittins Index for this job, and describe how the index policy behaves when $\mu_1 \gg \mu_2$ and $p$ is very small. What does this tell you about index-based scheduling of "rare-but-fast" jobs?* **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution** **(a) ŌĆö 7 marks** **Formulation (2 marks):** At each instant the scheduler selects one of $N$ jobs to process, earning reward at unit rate on the chosen job and zero on the others; preemptions are allowed at no cost; each job's residual service-time distribution evolves according to its own stochastic law; the objective is to maximize the expected total discounted reward $\mathbb{E}\left[\,\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\beta t}\, r(t)\, dt\,\right]$ where $r(t) \in \{0,1\}$ indicates which job (if any) is being served. **Gittins Theorem (1 mark):** *If all jobs are indexable, the greedy index policy is optimal.* **Deterministic-job derivation (3 marks):** With $\tau = r$ (the only sensible stopping time), the numerator is $\int_0^r e^{-\beta t} dt = (1 - e^{-\beta r})/\beta$ and the denominator is $r$. Hence $$G_{\text{det}}(r) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-\beta r}}{\beta r}.$$ For $\beta \to 0$, expand $e^{-\beta r} = 1 - \beta r + O(\beta^2 r^2)$ to obtain $G_{\text{det}}(r) \to 1$. Since the index is *constant* (= 1) in this limit, the index policy is degenerate in absolute terms ŌĆö but the **ranking** is preserved by the *time to completion*, recovering **SRPT**. (1 mark) **(b) ŌĆö 7 marks** **Index form (3 marks):** By linearity of expectation over the mixture, the Gittins index is the *mixture* of the component indices: $$G_{\text{mix}} \;=\; p \cdot \frac{\mu_1}{\mu_1 + \beta} \;+\; (1 - p) \cdot \frac{\mu_2}{\mu_2 + \beta}.$$ This follows from the fact that the stopping problem splits across the two mixture components, and the index is the sup of a ratio whose numerator and denominator are linear in the mixture weights. **Behaviour for $\mu_1 \gg \mu_2$, $p \ll 1$ (2 marks):** When $\beta \to 0$, $G \approx p + (1-p) = 1$, so the index is *dominated* by the slow component whenever $1 - p \gg p$. The fast-but-rare job contributes a *small* index $p$ and is de-prioritized; the index policy is *not* myopically "favour the fast job." This is the **fundamental distinction** from the $c\mu$-rule. **Scheduling insight (2 marks):** Index-based scheduling **automatically hedges** against statistical outliers: a job is only given high priority if it is *consistently* fast, not if it is merely *occasionally* fast. This is the rigorous mathematical foundation of **delay-based** scheduling policies used in modern cloud-computing systems (e.g., Hadoop's fair scheduler, delay-based TCP variants). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model, Module 4]** **(a)** *Explain the concept of indexability. Show that any exponential job is indexable, and compute its Gittins Index. What is the computational complexity of computing the Gittins Index for a job with a discretized residual-time distribution on $n$ states? Justify.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Compare and contrast the Gittins Index policy (for non-restless bandits) with Whittle's Index policy (for restless bandits) in terms of (i) the cost structure assumed, (ii) the optimality guarantees, and (iii) one application domain where each is most appropriate.* **(7 marks)** ##### **Model Solution** **(a) ŌĆö 7 marks** **Indexability explained (1.5 marks):** A job is indexable if there exists a scalar function $G(x)$ such that, for every threshold $\lambda$, the "process iff $G(x) \geq \lambda$" rule is optimal for the single-job threshold problem. This is the *separability* property that allows the joint MDP to decompose. **Exponential indexability (2 marks):** For an $\text{Exp}(\mu)$ job, the optimal stopping time is the natural completion (no informative alternative, by memorylessness), so the threshold problem has a *single* decision ŌĆö process vs. stop ŌĆö and the resulting policy is trivially of "process iff $G \geq \lambda$" form. Hence the job is indexable with $$G \;=\; \frac{\mu}{\mu + \beta}.$$ **Computational complexity (2 marks):** For a discretized residual-time distribution on $n$ states, computing the Gittins index requires solving $n$ separate one-dimensional stopping problems. Each problem is a *single* scalar optimization over $n$ possible stopping states, giving an $O(n)$ inner cost, and the index value at each state requires an $O(n)$ scan of the future. The total complexity is therefore $O(n^2)$ per job, or $O(N n^2)$ for $N$ jobs. **Practical remark (1.5 marks):** The algorithm of VaraiyaŌĆōWalrandŌĆōBuyukkoc (1985) achieves $O(n^2)$ by amortized dynamic programming; the algorithm of Nino-Mora (2007) reaches $O(n \log n)$ via a Lagrangian-relaxation-based "index calculus" with a "forest" data structure. **(b) ŌĆö 7 marks** | Aspect | Gittins Index (Non-Restless) | Whittle Index (Restless) | |---|---|---| | **(i) Cost structure** | At most one job processed at any instant ("passive" arms) | A fixed number of jobs processed at every instant (e.g., one per machine) | | **(ii) Optimality** | **Provably optimal** (Gittins, 1979) for indexable jobs | **Asymptotically optimal** under technical conditions (Weber & Weiss, 1990) | | **(iii) Best application** | Single-bottleneck scheduling, opportunistic wireless (one user at a time), cloud VM dispatch | Multi-channel wireless, multi-machine job shops, inventory with re-order points, health-care resource allocation | **Discussion (1 mark each aspect, 1 mark synthesis):** The Gittins index solves the *single-machine* problem with absolute optimality. The Whittle index is its *restless* generalization (Whittle, 1988), where every machine must remain busy with some job. Optimality is only asymptotic in the number of arms, but the index is *computable* for a much wider class of practical settings. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on the Gittins Index Topic:** > 1. **Confusing the sup and the min:** Many students write the Gittins index as a *minimum* without realizing that the sup-over-$\tau$ and min-over-$\tau$ are two *equivalent* definitions linked by the optional-stopping theorem. Examiners expect you to know *both* forms. (ŌłÆ1 mark) > 2. **Forgetting to state indexability:** A statement of the Gittins Index Theorem *without* the indexability hypothesis is incomplete and may lose a mark. (ŌłÆ1 mark) > 3. **Mixing up the discount rate's role:** Confusing the discounted criterion with the long-run average criterion; the limit $\beta \to 0$ must be invoked *explicitly* when transitioning. (ŌłÆ0.5 to ŌłÆ1 mark) > 4. **Arithmetic slip on the exponential case:** Forgetting that $\mathbb{E}[e^{-\beta T}] = \mu/(\mu+\beta)$ for $T \sim \text{Exp}(\mu)$ leads to a wrong index; examiners watch for this. (ŌłÆ1 mark) > 5. **Using "c╬╝-rule" and "Gittins Index" interchangeably:** They coincide *only* in the exponential, $\beta \to 0$ limit. In general, the Gittins index is *state-dependent* and the $c\mu$-rule is not. (ŌłÆ0.5 mark) > 6. **Skipping the residual-time state variable:** A full definition must include the *state* $x$ (age, sufficient statistic); writing the index as a bare number (i.e., $G = \mu/(\mu+\beta)$) without qualifying it as a function of state loses a mark in Part A. (ŌłÆ0.5 mark) --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Gittins Index Definition:** A *state-dependent scalar* $G(x)$ defined as the *sup over stopping times* of the ratio of expected discounted reward to expected processing time. Equivalently, the smallest "fair-game" price of continuing the job. - **Gittins Index Theorem (1979):** For an indexable MAB, the *greedy* policy (process the job with the largest index) is *globally optimal* for the discounted criterion. - **Indexability:** A job is indexable if for every threshold $\lambda$, the threshold policy is optimal for the one-job problem. Indexability is the *separability* condition that makes the theorem work. - **Exponential Special Case:** $G = \mu/(\mu+\beta)$; reduces to the **$c\mu$-rule** as $\beta \to 0$. - **Deterministic Special Case:** $G(r) = (1 - e^{-\beta r})/(\beta r)$; reduces to **SRPT** as $\beta \to 0$. - **Two Equivalent Definitions:** Sup-form (Definition A) and Min-form (Definition B) ŌĆö linked by the optional-stopping theorem. - **Computation:** $O(n^2)$ per job for a discretized $n$-state distribution; $O(n \log n)$ achievable with advanced data structures (Nino-Mora, 2007). - **Restless Generalization:** Whittle's index (1988) extends the idea to the case where arms cannot be left idle; optimality is *asymptotic* in $N$. - **Preemption Assumption:** The Gittins framework assumes *cost-free preemptions* and the ability to switch jobs at *any instant*. Without preemptions, the index policy is no longer optimal in general. - **Discounted vs. Long-Run Average:** The discounted criterion ($\beta > 0$) yields well-defined indices; the long-run average is recovered as $\beta \to 0$, with the index becoming a pure "throughput per unit time." - **Real-world meaning:** The Gittins index is a *dynamic, information-aware* priority ŌĆö a job is prioritized not just on its expected service time, but on *how much of it has already been observed* and *what its residual-time distribution looks like conditional on that history*. - **The "fictitious" delay interpretation:** The Gittins index equals the expected discounted "delay" of an optimal stopping policy on the *residual* time axis ŌĆö a beautiful equivalence that connects scheduling to classical optimal-stopping theory. ---

topics.title: Likelihood Ratio Ordered Distributions and Exponential Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET633 - INDUSTRIAL SCHEDULING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Likelihood Ratio Ordered Distributions and Exponential Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Likelihood Ratio (LR) Ordered Distributions

In industrial scheduling, we rarely know the **exact processing time** of a job. Instead, we deal with **random variables** representing processing times. To compare two such random variables, we need a formal notion of "ordering." The **Likelihood Ratio (LR) order** is the strongest and most useful stochastic ordering used in scheduling theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (LR Order):**
> Let $X$ and $Y$ be two non-negative continuous random variables with probability density functions $f_X(x)$ and $f_Y(x)$. Then $X$ is said to be **larger than $Y$ in the likelihood ratio order** (written $X \geq_{lr} Y$) if and only if the ratio
> $$\frac{f_X(t)}{f_Y(t)}$$
> is a **non-decreasing function** of $t$ over the entire support.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is LR Order Powerful?**
> LR ordering is the **strongest** among all stochastic orderings (LR > HR > ST > ICX). If $X \geq_{lr} Y$, then $X$ automatically dominates $Y$ in every weaker order (hazard rate, stochastic, and convex order).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine two machines, M1 and M2, processing identical batches of jobs. Suppose we are told that for any given processing duration $t$, the *probability that M1 takes longer than $t$ compared to M2* grows as $t$ increases. That is, M1's processing time distribution is "stretched to the right" relative to M2's. This is exactly what LR ordering captures ŌĆö M1's processing time is systematically larger than M2's, not just on average, but in a stronger distributional sense.

### 1.2 Exponential Distribution

The **Exponential Distribution** is the single most important distribution in stochastic scheduling. It is the natural model for inter-arrival times, processing times, and machine breakdowns because of its unique **memoryless property**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow an Exponential Distribution with parameter $\lambda > 0$ if its probability density function (PDF) is:
> $$f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$$
> The parameter $\lambda$ is called the **rate parameter** and its reciprocal $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ is the **mean (expected processing time)**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a CNC machine cutting a metal workpiece. The probability that the machine completes its current cut within the next few seconds is *completely independent of how long it has already been running*. It does not "tire out" or "warm up" ŌĆö every instant is a fresh start. This is precisely the memoryless behavior captured by the exponential distribution. The rate parameter $\lambda$ controls how fast jobs typically finish: a higher $\lambda$ means a faster, more productive machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Scheduling:**
> The exponential distribution is the *only* continuous distribution that is both **memoryless** and **likelihood-ratio ordered with respect to its rate parameter**. That is, if $X_1 \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_1)$ and $X_2 \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_2)$, then $X_1 \geq_{lr} X_2$ if and only if $\lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2$. This single property is what makes exponential processing times tractable for proving optimality of scheduling rules like **WSEPT** and **Smith's Rule**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Steps Behind LR Ordering

To verify $X \geq_{lr} Y$ rigorously, the following structured logic applies:

1. **Identify the densities** $f_X(t)$ and $f_Y(t)$ of the two random variables (e.g., two different exponential rates, or two normal distributions).
2. **Form the likelihood ratio** $R(t) = \dfrac{f_X(t)}{f_Y(t)}$ for all $t$ in the common support.
3. **Compute the derivative** $R'(t)$ or inspect the ratio's algebraic structure.
4. **Check monotonicity**: If $R'(t) \geq 0$ for all $t$ (or equivalently $R(t)$ is non-decreasing), then $X \geq_{lr} Y$.
5. **Consequences cascade automatically**:
   - $X \geq_{lr} Y \implies X \geq_{hr} Y$ (hazard rate order)
   - $X \geq_{lr} Y \implies X \geq_{st} Y$ (stochastic order)
   - $X \geq_{lr} Y \implies X \geq_{cx} Y$ (convex order, under non-negativity)

### 2.2 Logical Steps Behind Exponential Distribution Properties

1. **Memoryless Property** derivation from conditional probability $P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$.
2. **Minimum of Independent Exponentials** yields another exponential: if $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_i)$ independently, then $\min(X_1, \dots, X_n) \sim \text{Exp}\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)$.
3. **Hazard Rate** is **constant**: $h(t) = \lambda$ for all $t$.
4. **LR Ordering Criterion**: For $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_i)$, we have $X_1 \geq_{lr} X_2 \iff \lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Job-Shop Manufacturing** | Modeling random processing times of CNC, lathe, and milling operations |
| **Queueing Systems** | Inter-arrival times of customer orders and service times of machines |
| **Reliability Engineering** | Time-to-failure of components with constant failure rate |
| **Project Management** | Stochastic durations of repetitive sub-tasks |
| **Supply Chain** | Lead times with constant hazard of disruption |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LR Order Condition | $X \geq_{lr} Y \iff \frac{f_X(t)}{f_Y(t)}$ is non-decreasing in $t$ | Ratio must be monotonic |
| 2 | Exponential PDF | $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \; x \geq 0$ | $\lambda$ is rate (jobs/hr) |
| 3 | Exponential CDF | $F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ | ŌĆö |
| 4 | Exponential Mean | $E[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Time units |
| 5 | Exponential Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | Time$^2$ units |
| 6 | Memoryless Property | $P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$ | Only for exponential |
| 7 | Hazard Rate | $h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)} = \lambda$ | Constant for exponential |
| 8 | Minimum of $n$ Exponentials | $\min(X_1, \dots, X_n) \sim \text{Exp}\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)$ | Independent case |
| 9 | Sum of $n$ Exponentials | $\sum X_i \sim \text{Erlang}(n, \lambda)$ | Same rate $\lambda$ |
| 10 | LR Order for Exponentials | $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_i): \; X_1 \geq_{lr} X_2 \iff \lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2$ | Critical for scheduling |
| 11 | ST Order Implication | $X \geq_{lr} Y \implies X \geq_{st} Y$ | Stronger $\Rightarrow$ Weaker |
| 12 | Conditional Mean | $E[X \mid X > a] = a + \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Shifted by $a$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The KTU board frequently tests the **equivalence in Row 10**. Memorize it ŌĆö it is the bridge between abstract LR ordering and concrete exponential scheduling.
