## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Memoryless Property of Exponential Distribution

**Statement:** For $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ and any $s, t \geq 0$,
$$P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the conditional probability definition.**
By definition of conditional probability,
$$P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = \frac{P(X > s+t \text{ and } X > s)}{P(X > s)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Simplify the intersection.**
Since $s+t > s$, the event $\{X > s+t\} \subseteq \{X > s\}$, hence
$$P(X > s+t \text{ and } X > s) = P(X > s+t)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the survival function.**
For exponential, $P(X > x) = 1 - F(x) = e^{-\lambda x}$. Therefore
$$P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = \frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Algebraic cancellation.**
$$= \frac{e^{-\lambda s} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = e^{-\lambda t} = P(X > t)$$

This proves the memoryless property. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum of Two Independent Exponentials

**Statement:** If $X_1 \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_1)$ and $X_2 \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_2)$ are independent, then
$$Z = \min(X_1, X_2) \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Use the survival function approach.**
$$P(Z > t) = P(\min(X_1, X_2) > t) = P(X_1 > t \text{ and } X_2 > t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply independence.**
$$= P(X_1 > t) \cdot P(X_2 > t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 t}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Combine exponents.**
$$= e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2) t}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Recognize the form.**
This is precisely the survival function of an Exponential distribution with rate $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2$. Hence
$$Z \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2) \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation: LR Order Verification for Two Exponentials

**Problem:** Show that $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_1)$ and $Y \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_2)$ satisfy $X \geq_{lr} Y$ if and only if $\lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the likelihood ratio.**
$$R(t) = \frac{f_X(t)}{f_Y(t)} = \frac{\lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_1 t}}{\lambda_2 e^{-\lambda_2 t}} = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} \cdot e^{-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)t}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the derivative.**
$$R'(t) = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} \cdot [-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)] \cdot e^{-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)t}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sign analysis.**
Since the prefactor $\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} \cdot e^{-(\lambda_1-\lambda_2)t} > 0$, the sign of $R'(t)$ is determined by $-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)$:
- If $\lambda_1 < \lambda_2$, then $-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) > 0$, so $R'(t) > 0$ ŌćÆ $R(t)$ is increasing ŌćÆ $X \geq_{lr} Y$. Ō£ō
- If $\lambda_1 > \lambda_2$, then $-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) < 0$, so $R'(t) < 0$ ŌćÆ $R(t)$ is decreasing ŌćÆ $X \leq_{lr} Y$. Ō£Ś
- If $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2$, then $R(t)$ is constant, so $X$ and $Y$ are LR-equivalent. 

Hence, $X \geq_{lr} Y \iff \lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Derivation: Hazard Rate of Exponential Distribution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Hazard rate definition.**
$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{P(X > t)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute exponential PDF and survival.**
$$h(t) = \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda t}} = \lambda$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Interpretation.**
The hazard rate is **constant** and equal to the rate parameter $\lambda$. This is the only continuous distribution with a constant hazard rate, and it is why the exponential is the canonical model for processes with no aging effect (e.g., radioactive decay, random arrivals under the simplest Poisson model). $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Property in Scheduling Problems

| Scheduling Question | Relevant Property | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Which job to process first? | LR order $X \geq_{lr} Y$ | Smith's Rule, WSEPT |
| Will exponential jobs overlap? | Minimum of exponentials | Machine availability analysis |
| Expected wait time of a job? | Memoryless property | $E[X \mid X > a] = a + \frac{1}{\lambda}$ |
| Reliability of a machine? | Constant hazard rate | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) |
| Probability of completion by deadline $D$? | CDF of exponential | $P(X \leq D) = 1 - e^{-\lambda D}$ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
*Define Likelihood Ratio (LR) ordering between two non-negative continuous random variables. State one key property that LR ordering implies for hazard rate and stochastic ordering.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Let $X$ and $Y$ be two non-negative continuous random variables with densities $f_X(t)$ and $f_Y(t)$. Then $X \geq_{lr} Y$ if and only if the ratio
$$\frac{f_X(t)}{f_Y(t)}$$
is a **non-decreasing function of $t$** for all $t$ in the common support.

**Key Property (1 mark):**
LR ordering is the strongest among the major stochastic orderings. Specifically:
$$X \geq_{lr} Y \implies X \geq_{hr} Y \implies X \geq_{st} Y$$
That is, LR order implies hazard rate order, which in turn implies ordinary stochastic order.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the memoryless property of the exponential distribution and write the PDF of an exponential random variable with mean 5 time units.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Memoryless Property (2 marks):**
For $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ and any $s, t \geq 0$,
$$P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$
This means the residual lifetime after observing survival up to time $s$ has the same exponential distribution as the original.

**PDF (1 mark):**
Given mean $= \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 5$, we get $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{5}$. Therefore
$$f(x) = \frac{1}{5} e^{-x/5}, \quad x \geq 0$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024 Model]

**a)** Define LR ordering between two non-negative continuous random variables $X$ and $Y$. Show that if $X_1 \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_1)$ and $X_2 \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_2)$ with $\lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2$, then $X_1 \geq_{lr} X_2$. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Two jobs $J_1$ and $J_2$ have independent exponential processing times with rates $\lambda_1 = 1/4$ and $\lambda_2 = 1/2$ jobs per hour respectively. Compute the expected processing time of the job that finishes first, and find the probability that $J_1$ finishes before $J_2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
$X \geq_{lr} Y$ if and only if the likelihood ratio
$$R(t) = \frac{f_X(t)}{f_Y(t)}$$
is non-decreasing in $t$ for all $t \geq 0$.

**Setup (1 mark):**
With $X_1 \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_1)$ and $X_2 \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_2)$, the densities are $f_{X_1}(t) = \lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_1 t}$ and $f_{X_2}(t) = \lambda_2 e^{-\lambda_2 t}$.

**Form the likelihood ratio (2 marks):**
$$R(t) = \frac{\lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_1 t}}{\lambda_2 e^{-\lambda_2 t}} = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} e^{-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)t}$$

**Derivative test (2 marks):**
$$R'(t) = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} \cdot [-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)] \cdot e^{-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)t}$$

Since $\lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2$, we have $-(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) \geq 0$, so $R'(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$. Hence $R(t)$ is non-decreasing, proving $X_1 \geq_{lr} X_2$. $\blacksquare$

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Given (1 mark):** $\lambda_1 = \frac{1}{4}$, $\lambda_2 = \frac{1}{2}$, both per hour.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Minimum of two independent exponentials (2 marks):**
The finishing time of the first job is $Z = \min(X_1, X_2) \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2) = \text{Exp}(3/4)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expected finishing time (1 mark):**
$$E[Z] = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{4}{3} \text{ hours} \approx 1 \text{ hr } 20 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Probability that $J_1$ finishes first (3 marks):**
By the minimum property of exponentials, the probability that a particular exponential is the minimum is proportional to its rate. Therefore
$$P(J_1 \text{ finishes first}) = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{1/4}{1/4 + 1/2} = \frac{1/4}{3/4} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Verification (1 mark):** Alternatively, using integration:
$$P(X_1 < X_2) = \int_0^\infty f_1(t) \cdot P(X_2 > t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty \frac{1}{4} e^{-t/4} \cdot e^{-t/2} \, dt = \frac{1/4}{3/4} = \frac{1}{3}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]

**a)** State and prove the memoryless property of the exponential distribution. Hence show that if $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, then $E[X \mid X > a] = a + \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A machine has three components whose lifetimes (in hours) are independent exponentials with rates $\lambda_1 = 0.01$, $\lambda_2 = 0.02$, and $\lambda_3 = 0.02$ per hour. The machine fails as soon as any one component fails. Find: (i) the expected time to failure, and (ii) the probability that component 1 is the first to fail. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Statement (1 mark):** For $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ and $s, t \geq 0$,
$$P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$$

**Proof (4 marks):**
By definition of conditional probability,
$$P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = \frac{P(\{X > s+t\} \cap \{X > s\})}{P(X > s)}$$
Since $\{X > s+t\} \subseteq \{X > s\}$, the numerator equals $P(X > s+t)$. Hence
$$= \frac{P(X > s+t)}{P(X > s)} = \frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = e^{-\lambda t} = P(X > t) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Conditional Expectation (2 marks):**
By the memoryless property, if $X \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, then $X - a \mid X > a \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ as well. Therefore
$$E[X \mid X > a] = E[(X - a) + a \mid X > a] = a + E[X - a \mid X > a] = a + \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Given (1 mark):** $\lambda_1 = 0.01$, $\lambda_2 = 0.02$, $\lambda_3 = 0.02$ per hour.

**Part (i) ŌĆö Expected time to failure (2 marks):**
The time to failure is $T = \min(X_1, X_2, X_3)$, which is exponential with rate
$$\lambda_{\text{total}} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 0.01 + 0.02 + 0.02 = 0.05$$
Therefore
$$E[T] = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (ii) ŌĆö Probability that component 1 fails first (4 marks):**
By the minimum-of-exponentials property, the probability that the minimum corresponds to component $i$ is proportional to $\lambda_i$:
$$P(\text{Component 1 fails first}) = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3} = \frac{0.01}{0.05} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20$$

**Conclusion:** Expected time to failure = **20 hours**, probability that component 1 fails first = **0.20** (or 20%).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse *likelihood ratio order* ($\geq_{lr}$) with *stochastic order* ($\geq_{st}$). Remember: LR is **much stronger** and requires monotonicity of the **ratio of densities**, not just the CDFs. Showing only $F_X(t) \leq F_Y(t)$ does not prove LR order ŌĆö it only proves ST order. **(Loses 2ŌĆō3 marks)**
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** For the minimum of exponentials, students sometimes write the rate as $\min(\lambda_i)$ instead of $\sum \lambda_i$. Always **add** the rates. **(Loses 2 marks)**
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** For conditional expectation $E[X \mid X > a]$, students forget to add the offset $a$. The correct answer is $a + \frac{1}{\lambda}$, **not** just $\frac{1}{\lambda}$. **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** When verifying LR order for exponentials, students forget to check the *sign of the derivative* explicitly. The KTU board requires both the derivative computation **and** the sign analysis. **(Loses 1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LR Order:** The strongest stochastic order; defined by the monotonicity of the ratio $f_X(t)/f_Y(t)$.
- **Cascade Property:** $X \geq_{lr} Y \Rightarrow X \geq_{hr} Y \Rightarrow X \geq_{st} Y$ (and under non-negativity, also $\geq_{cx} Y$).
- **Exponential PDF:** $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \geq 0$; mean is $\frac{1}{\lambda}$; variance is $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$.
- **Memoryless Property:** $P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$ ŌĆö the signature property of the exponential distribution.
- **Minimum of Exponentials:** Independent exponentials with rates $\lambda_i$ yield a minimum with rate $\sum \lambda_i$.
- **LR Order Criterion for Exponentials:** $X_1 \geq_{lr} X_2 \iff \lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2$ ŌĆö **memorize this equivalence**, it is the cornerstone of stochastic scheduling proofs.
- **Hazard Rate:** Constant at $\lambda$ for the exponential; this is what makes it the canonical model for memoryless processes.
- **Scheduling Implication:** Under exponential processing times, stochastic scheduling rules (Smith's Rule for $1 \mid \sum C_i$, WSEPT for $1 \mid \sum w_j C_j$) can be proved optimal *without* assuming deterministic processing times.
- **Conditional Mean Shortcut:** $E[X \mid X > a] = a + \frac{1}{\lambda}$ ŌĆö useful for "remaining time" computations in queues and reliability.
- **Key Insight:** Whenever a KTU problem mentions "exponential processing times" together with a "stochastic comparison," immediately think of LR ordering and use the rate-comparison criterion.
